## HG Amitasana Dasa

### ESCAPE THE CAGE - BEFORE WE AGE | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, entrapment, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So today the session is all about what is this cage in which the Atma is trapped, how it is trapped and how we can escape from this cage, this entrapment."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of escaping the cage of material existence, which traps the soul in a cycle of birth, death, old age, and disease, and outlines the session's focus on understanding this entrapment and how to achieve liberation.

Hare Krishna, a warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Life session. The topic for the day is, escape the cage before you age. Many of you must be wondering, what is the cage we are talking about? As we have been discussing in our previous sessions, Atma, the soul, keep the main thing, the main thing. So the main thing is the Atma, the soul, without which when that Atma leaves the body, we cremate the body or bury the body. Body minus Atma is a dead body. So this Atma is encaged, is entrapped, entrapped in a material body, made of five elements, earth, air, water, fire and ether, and in one sense, trapped in this body. Although the Atma is eternal, never dying, because it is trapped in the body and identified with the body, it experiences death, old age, diseases and rebirth. So the cage what we are talking about is getting trapped in body life after life, punar api janma, punar api marana, much against our wish, in different species of life, as plants, as birds, as aquatics, reptiles, animals, and after many many lifetimes, human beings, which also is not a permanent life form. And this Atma is transmigrating. We all don't want death, but we are forced to experience death, forced to experience old age. Much against our wish, we are forced to experience diseases of various kinds, and nobody is an exception. So today the session is all about what is this cage in which the Atma is trapped, how it is trapped and how we can escape from this cage, this entrapment. So before we get into the details, let's start the session with prayers as usual.

#### spiritual practice, age, social pressure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=442
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "People don't say that it is wrong, but they say that this is too early."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the common misconception that spiritual practice is only suitable for old age, after fulfilling worldly responsibilities, and challenges this notion.

So the first principle which we are going to focus on is the right age to start. I am sure many of you might have encountered this once we start practicing Krishna consciousness, when we start wearing a Kanthi Mala or putting a Tilak or start chanting on the beads and especially if a person is young in his twenties, thirties, forties, invariably you will hear comments like this is not the right time. Yes spirituality is important. Yes religion is important, but you will definitely hear this, but this is not the right time. There is time for everything. This is a time when you have to grow in your career, make a lot of money, buy a good house, settle yourself in life, get married, have nice children, take care of their good upbringing. And yes, post retirement, post seventy years, seventy five years, maybe when your grandchildren have got sick, they have got settled comfortably, they've got married and you are relatively free from all so-called family responsibilities, then you can practice spiritual life. This is the common understanding. People don't say that it is wrong, but they say that this is too early.

#### spiritual education, childhood, Vedic culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=650
**Verse References:** SB 7.6.1
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** Using the example of Socrates and Prahlad Maharaj, the speaker emphasizes the importance of starting spiritual education from a very young age (Kaumaram), highlighting the rarity and value of human life for understanding and escaping material entrapment.

Lady, rush, rush, you're already five years late. Prahlad Maharaj in the Srimad Bhagavatam seventh canto, in his instructions he says, Kaumaram acharedh pragno dharman bhagvatan yah, Kaumaram from the very childhood, acharedh dharman bhagvatan yah, this understanding has to be taught from Kaumara age. Kaumaram is right from the very childhood. This human form of life, he continues saying that this human form of life is very rarely achieved. And other life forms, as I mentioned, there are 84 lakh species, plants, birds, innumerable species of plants, birds, reptiles, aquatics, underwater, 84 lakh species. Although this human form of life is temporary, it is not that eternally we get this human form of life, which is very valuable. Arthadam. It is very, very meaningful. We can achieve something substantial as far as coming out of the cage is concerned. Although this human form of life is temporary, but it is very meaningful because it affords us a wonderful opportunity to understand about who we are, why we are encaged and how to come out of this cage. And when does this learning this understanding starts from the very childhood that's why in vedic times there were gurukul systems where a child including pandavas krishna himself went and learned this science brahmavidya under the guidance of a guru and learn the art of controlling the mind controlling the senses because which is very important for us to understand this great science when our mind is bewildered by boga and aishwarya.

#### mind control, sense control, brahmacharya, Vedic stages of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSk1fo0bmmE?t=798
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the first 25 years was predominantly focused on controlling the mind controlling the senses, learning those principles which was predominantly called brahmacharya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that when our senses are uncontrolled and the mind is rattled with materialistic thoughts, it is not possible to have steady intelligence to understand God. He describes the Vedic system of brahmacharya, vanaprastha, and sannyasa, emphasizing the importance of controlling the mind and senses in the early stages of life to prepare for detachment and focus on spiritual liberation.

When our senses are uncontrolled mind is rattled with so many materialistic thoughts it is not possible steady intelligence whereby we can understand about god think of god remember god will not come about so the first 25 years was predominantly focused on controlling the mind controlling the senses learning those principles which was predominantly called brahmacharya and the next 25 years a person would get into family lives would get so to say exposed to worldly pleasures but in a regulated way more so because of the training which he has undergone in the first and knowing that ultimate goal of life is not to eat drink be merry and enjoy life and settle down comfortably at the age of 50 people would voluntarily start detaching themselves from family life and that stage was called vanaprastha and after 25 years of preparing for that so-called renunciation a person would completely give up all connections with the family and that life was called sannyasa so that with single-mindedness with no distraction whatsoever the person could focus on extricating the soul from this body or escaping the cage.

So this is the first point i wanted to share with all of you the right age to start and what is the right age the right age is koumaram acharit pragna from the very childhood i've shared this example from the very childhood i've shared this example let's say this pen represents our life this represents the day we came in this world the day we were born and let's say this end represents the day we are going to leave this world which is inevitable one was taken birth is sure to die for one who has died is sure to take birth again so I have used this example in many of my talks many of you may be familiar with it if this pen represents our life this is the day we took birth this is the day we're going to leave this world so if I ask somebody and I've done this practically where are you currently placed as far as your life is concerned so people quickly calculate if they are 40 years so you know if I call someone they'll come and point it here no assuming that our life is for hundred years so or 80 years so if I call someone they'll come and point it here assuming that our life is for 100 years so or 80 years average lifespan so 40 years mean somewhere in between and then I ask the audience is that answer correct can we be very confident of it people start laughing some of them have understood where I am heading we start laughing because we cannot be sure we cannot be sure that today if I am 40 years I am halfway through it could be could be the last year of my life it could be the last day of my life.

**Content Type:** philosophy

**Key Quote:** "One who uses his life for the right purpose for understanding why we are engaged and how we can liberate ourselves extricate ourselves from this janma mrityu jarab yadi punarapi janma punarapi marna such a person is called a brahmana."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that just because we are engaged in activities on a different scale does not make us different. He quotes scriptures that such human beings are called dvipada pashu (two-legged animals). He contrasts brahmana (one who uses life for the right purpose) with kripana (a miserly person).

**Key Quote:** "Therefore, human form of life is an opportunity to purify our desires."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Lord will give us another life to fulfill our material desires. He notes that even in heavenly planets, there is repetition of birth, death, old age, and disease. Therefore, human life is an opportunity to purify our desires.

The Lord being very kind and merciful will give us another life, another life form to fulfill that desire. They're saying first deserve, then desire. If we do not have the pious Christ, we will not be able to fulfill that desire

We will begin the discussion on chapter 16, text numbers 17 and 18. This verse talks about the demoniac mentality. In this chapter, we have been talking about how the demons think and act. ## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Escape Cupid's Arrows: Turn to Madan Mohan | Sri Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### lust, attraction, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jE9IMGCtwkw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we have experienced real love, real beauty of the Lord, then we will experience that satisfaction."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that people are enchanted by Cupid's arrows, which represent lusty desires, but Krishna, or Madan Mohan, is more attractive than Cupid and surrendering to Him can free one from these desires and lead to real love and satisfaction.

World over, people are enchanted by Cupid, arrows of Cupid. Cupid shoots arrows, pierces our heart. We have that picture of a heart being pierced by an arrow, lusty desires. And Cupid is called Madan, and Krishna is called Madan Mohana, who enchants the Cupid, who is much, much more attractive, millions of times more attractive than the Cupid, so-called beauty in this material world. So if we get enchanted by Madan Mohana, if we worship Madan Mohan, if we surrender to Madan Mohan, if we follow the instructions of Krishna, Madan Mohan, which is given in the Bhagavad Gita, then Cupid has no place in our lives, will not be tormented, will not be shot at by the Cupid. If we have experienced real love, real beauty of the Lord, then we will experience that satisfaction. We will experience that higher taste, and the lower taste, false beauty, illusory beauty of this material world will not enchant us.

#### Arjuna, battlefield, dilemma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=623
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And just when the battle is about to begin, Arjuna requests Krishna, Krishna, can you please draw the chariot and place it in between the two armies so that I can have a closer look of my opponents, my enemy with whom we are going to fight."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa recounts the beginning of the Bhagavad Gita, describing Arjuna's dilemma on the battlefield of Kurukshetra as he faces the prospect of fighting his own kinsmen.

So let's see from Bhagavad Gita's perspective. Bhagavad Gita starts, as we all know, the two armies have assembled in the battlefield in Kurukshetra. On one side it's the Pandavas, the other side is the Kauravas. And just when the battle is about to begin, Arjuna requests Krishna, "Krishna, can you please draw the chariot and place it in between the two armies so that I can have a closer look of my opponents, my enemy with whom we are going to fight." So Arjuna, based on the request of Arjuna, Krishna drew the chariot and placed it in between the two armies. And when Arjuna saw the opponents, the enemy against whom he has to fight, it's his own kinsmen, own brothers, own relatives, near and dear ones, Bhishma Pitamah was there, Dronacharya was there, his teacher, spiritual master, Karna, his cousins, his brothers, although they were willing to fight against him, but still they were relatives, they were his own kinsmen. So when Arjuna saw them, he was completely bewildered, and was into a very confused state of mind. The dilemma he had was, should he really fight this battle? Because if he fights this battle, even if he wins the battle, still he won't be able to enjoy the kingdom because it will be tainted with the blood of his own relatives.

---
#### victory, relatives, stress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=753
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What is the use of having a victory and our near and dear ones are not there to celebrate that moment, to celebrate that victory?"
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa illustrates Arjuna's dilemma by comparing it to the joy of celebrating a victory with loved ones, highlighting the futility of victory tainted by the loss of relatives, and describes Arjuna's resulting stress and confusion.

Generally when we achieve something, we want to enjoy relishing that victory or achievement with our near and dear ones. What is the use of having a victory and our near and dear ones are not there to celebrate that moment, to celebrate that victory? Just like even the World Cup when Indian team wins, they come back and then there are many many functions organized to celebrate the victory. And that really gives the whole victory another wonderful perspective or dimension. So here Arjuna gets into a very big dilemma, in fact a very perplexing situation. Even if he wins, actually it's a loss for him, according to what Arjuna thought. And anyway when he loses the battle, it's anyway it's loss. So just like we have win-win solution, here was a scenario where it was a lose-lose scenario. Even if he wins the battle, he is going to lose. If he loses the battle, anyway he is lost. And seeing his own kinsmen, own relatives, own brothers, elders, his mind starts reeling. He becomes very confused. And then towards the end of first chapter, Arjuna tells Krishna, "Krishna, my mind is reeling. I am unable to stand any longer. My bow, Gandiva, is slipping from my hands." So these are all symptoms of a person who is in a very stressful situation.

---
#### stress, Arjuna, battle
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=882
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And then towards the end of first chapter, Arjuna gives up his Gandiva bow and says, Krishna, I will not fight."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa continues to describe Arjuna's symptoms of stress and confusion, culminating in his decision to give up his bow and refuse to fight.

My hairs are standing on end. My mind is reeling. I am unable to think properly. I am not sure what I am supposed to do. My legs are trembling. And then towards the end of first chapter, Arjuna gives up his Gandiva bow and says, "Krishna, I will not fight anymore."

---
#### Krishna, Bhagavad Gita, solution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rTeNoKbfNE?t=990
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And after hearing the message of Gita, the instructions of Lord Sri Krishna, we know what happened. All the doubts, all the confusions, all the dilemmas, all the misgivings, all the fear, all the anxiety which Arjuna had, everything got wiped out."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa contrasts Arjuna's overwhelming challenge with the relatively smaller challenges people face today, and poses the question of how Krishna responded to Arjuna's confusion, revealing that Krishna simply smiled and spoke the Bhagavad Gita, which resolved all of Arjuna's doubts and fears.

Now, a question to all of you. After hearing these statements of Arjuna, after seeing that he is completely confused, what did Krishna do? Did he take him through a crash course on stress management? He simply smiled. And macro level, although he spoke many things without getting into details as to what he spoke right now, macro level, he spoke the Bhagavad Gita. And after hearing the message of Gita, the instructions of Lord Sri Krishna, we know what happened. All the doubts, all the confusions, all the dilemmas, all the misgivings, all the fear, all the anxiety which Arjuna had, everything got wiped out. Arjuna got up, took his bow Gandiva, fought that battle very enthusiastically and won the battle. So, in other words, the message of Gita has the potency, has the power, irrespective of what situation we may face.

### FAMILY MEMBERS AND HAPPINESS - CASE STUDY | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### application of principles, individuality, family dynamics

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces a case study and emphasizes the importance of applying Krishna consciousness principles intelligently and with consideration for individual circumstances, avoiding alienation within families.

So today we will start with the case study. Before we start with this case study, let me explain to you how we are going to handle this. So I'm going to share with you a particular case. Now, everyone's circumstances are unique, am I right? So in Krishna consciousness, we learn the principles and we have to use our intelligence to apply those principles in respective circumstances, am I right? In that sense, we cannot give canned solutions. In other words, suppose in a particular family they are used to eating non-veg and somebody has started practicing Krishna consciousness and he is intending to be a vegetarian and there is some element of friction in the family. Now, there are so many parameters involved in that equation, am I right? What are the various parameters involved which may vary from one family to another? Can anyone help me with an answer? What are the parameters involved? Let's say this particular scenario, somebody has given up non-veg, but the entire family regularly takes non-veg and there is an element of some kind of a friction, misunderstanding. You should take it. No, I will not take it. What is wrong? Why are you going there? What's wrong? All along we have been taking, it's part of our family tradition. So what are the parameters which may vary from family to family? Give me examples. It could be, let's say the family, the entire family right from generations have been non-vegetarians, all of a sudden one person is becoming. So how deeply they are entrenched in that habit could be one. There could be some families who have just started eating because of social listing few years back and it's okay if somebody gives up. So that is one parameter. What else? Health-wise, okay. So because they believe that, okay, they believe that it is optional, so it's not so deeply entrenched in a family. So every family situation will be unique. So we have to use our intelligence, how to apply those principles intelligently, so that we don't completely alienate the environment. We have to live there, right? We cannot just completely step out of the family and say, okay. So how do we intelligently maneuver and apply the principles? Now, when we are going to discuss a particular case, although we might suggest that this person can do like this, or he should do like this, we have to still use intelligence before executing it in our scenario, right? Understood?

#### family conflict, spiritual practice, detachment

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker presents the case study of Vishal, who has embraced Krishna consciousness, leading to conflict with his non-vegetarian, party-going family and friends, who feel alienated by his new lifestyle.

Is this concept clear? Why I am giving this disclaimer is somebody should not say, Prabhuji, aapne bola tha, kya ho gaya. So every circumstance is unique, every person is unique. Just like Krishna says, there are five factors of action. So even in applying the principle, there are many parameters which are involved. How you deal with that situation, with what tone you explain. One can be very rigid, no, I will not do it, and speak in that. Other things, with a little more trying to accommodate, trying to understand, trying to make them understand, like that. So the tone with which a person speaks, how we try to think of, you know, without alienating them. No doubt there could be some element of friction. So we will deal with this case. So let's start. Vishal is regularly attending Gita Live sessions on Sundays. His family is not very happy with this, as he has started spending more time chanting and reading Bhagavad Gita and watching devotional videos. Vishal has also turned vegetarian when his entire family are non-vegetarians. He has also taken a commitment to chant 16 rounds daily and spend time with his family and spends close to two hours daily in his commitment to chant 16 rounds. However, his family and friends feel alienated from him and he is no longer a part of the parties and get together. Even when he joins them, he finds himself lonely as he no longer enjoys such parties. His friends and family try to talk him out of this. His wife and kids are concerned of Vishal's behavior as he is slowly getting detached and also has started contributing funds to HKM. Sone pe suhaga. Tough case. In spite of the stress and tensions at home, Vishal seems to be more happy and content to follow the spiritual path. He doesn't want to give up his family or friends, but there is a clear disconnect and he is being considered as abnormal. Is he on the right track? Should he do what his family wants and keeps his spiritual journey for after 60 years? He is just 45 years right now. Does he listen to his friends who say, God has made life to enjoy, let's party by drinking and eating? How can Vishal maintain both his spiritual growth and his family and friends' happiness?

#### patience, understanding, communication
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AQyKZSLr9Os?t=752
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Seek first to understand then be understood."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the principle of 'seek first to understand, then be understood,' emphasizing the importance of patience and understanding in dealing with family members who may not share the same spiritual views.

I agree with this. When we find some kind of an alienation, we have to be little patient. There is a principle called seek first to understand then be understood. This is one of the principles. It's from a book called Seven Habits. Just for your information, I am not a big fan or follower of Stephen Covey. But nevertheless let me tell you how this book became popular. Stephen Covey, he along with his wife went on a sabbatical and stayed next to a sea beach for two years and his objective was to introspect, to ruminate, to study and to also figure out what he did. Along with him, he took 200 years of success literature. You know, self-help books, how to win happy, good friends, how to influence people and this thing and so on. So many, Think and Grow Rich, Napoleon Hill, so many bestsellers who have been there. The last 200 years, all the books he took along with him and two years it was all about reading, digesting, introspecting, walking on the beach, trying to understand what are the common underlying principles in all the books. So in that sense, it was not just, you know, study something somewhere, little bit apply mind. So it was a lot of deep introspection, trying to figure out what is the underlying common thread in all these books and that's how this book came out, Seven Habits of Effective People. And one of the habit, the very fact that it is mentioned as one of the habit means that this is something which is common in all the self-help books which is a very important principle and that is seek first to understand then be understood.

This person was regularly coming to our session and even started chanting 16 rounds. His wife was not at all interested, it seems. It appeared so and only on festival time, sometime she might come and she also had some inclination on classical music and all that. So this person, although he was coming, he was quite patient with his wife and once in a while he would say, this is the thing, what is happening in the house, keep us informed and all that, but he was little patient, didn't really force his ideologies or his thinking upon her and once in a while, you know, kept calling her for the festivals and all that. And one thing he did, which later on he told me was, everyday he would, because in the house he cannot offer food because he is not cooking food, he is going for office. So everyday morning in this thing he would keep a glass of water for offering to the Lord in the altar with Tulsi and very religiously he would mix that water in that water can which was a drinking water can where the whole family would drink and slowly, little by little, there was a change and she started involving in some kind of seva in the temple. You know, in festivals she would sing and all that and today, you won't believe. You won't believe. She chants 16 rounds and she is more committed than him. This time when I went to Vrindavan, she was there. Then for me it was, it was a big shock. I mean, with Tilak and with this thing and chanting Mala, Prabhuji, how are you? You miss, you know, you used to come to this thing. Do you recognize? All the kind of things. So then I said, how this... And then she also remembered, Prabhuji, you remember when we came and met you. You were trying to somehow indirectly nudge me to chant and all that and I was quite reluctant. So slowly, sometimes we have to give little time.

---
#### individuality, spiritual evolution, patience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AQyKZSLr9Os?t=1031
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Every Atma is at a different level of their spiritual journey."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the uniqueness of each soul and its individual level of spiritual evolution, cautioning against forcing Krishna consciousness on family members and stressing the need for patience.

We cannot trust our understanding on other people because every Atma is unique. Every Atma is at a different level of their spiritual journey. There are four lakh species. Their basis, the evolution of consciousness. We cannot force an Atma which is at this level. No, you have to come to this level. So we have to give time and if we try to be very pushy, if we try to be very pushy, this is what I want and this is what will happen in the house and come what may, I will do this. If we try to be pushy, there could be an element of alienation which can result in lot of confusion, more resistance. You know, people. So we have to be little patient with our family members. With family members, we should not try to force them also to be Krishna conscious. We can do our duty. As a matter of duty, we can tell them. The other day I mentioned Prabhupada's wife was not Krishna conscious. She was drinking tea, coffee. She sold the manuscripts of Bhagavad Gita. She sold the manuscripts of Bhagavad Gita which she had written before it had to go for printing as a Raddhi. Can you believe that? So Prabhupada was patient with her. So there will be such scenarios because now the principles are correct, mind you. What we are teaching, what we are talking is an authorized thing. Chanting 16 rounds is an authorized thing. To be a vegetarian is a right thing, but what happens when in a family they are so all are individual souls. What happens if all of them are at a very different levels of consciousness? To maintain parity, either this person should come down.

#### spiritual consciousness, levels of consciousness, human form of life

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** General public
**Key Quote:** "There are 84 lakh species. Out of 84 lakh species, four lakhs are human beings."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the concept of different levels of spiritual consciousness, explaining that there are four lakhs gradations of consciousness within the human form of life, ranging from uncultured individuals to pure, God-conscious Paramahamsas.

Now, you may say that I can explain to that person, but person cannot understand why you are doing what you are doing. I gave this example. This is a very important example. Please hear carefully. When a child is playing in the house, what he generally does, like the child who is just about, what is his age? Three years. Three years. Okay. Okay. Much lesser, much younger. Maybe one year old. So just keeps moving around, crawling in the house and puts thing, everything in the mouth. Okay. Pen is there. Put the pen in the mouth. You see here, time piece you put in the mouth. So for the child, one year old who is putting everything in the mouth and tasting the, whatever the item, if he puts the time piece in the mouth and if he were to give his judgment about what is the time piece, good or bad, what judgment will he pass? It is tasteless. It is bad. It is tasteless. It is bad and he will throw it away because he didn't enjoy it, am I right? That valuable time piece, he will throw away because it doesn't taste well. He is a one year old child. He just throws away. For him, a lollipop is much more valuable than a time piece because it has taste. Now, can a father explain to that one year old child that this time piece is more valuable than this lollipop? Can he explain? According to child, that is waste. This is more valuable, more interesting, more tasty. He is not mature enough to understand that this time piece is much more valuable. You throw, I am just giving an example, again a crude example. You throw a 500 rupee note in front of a pig. He is also an atma. No way he can value that. You give halwa to a pig, it will run away towards the stool because that is his consciousness. So this alienation happens mainly because there is a big gap and to a large extent, you cannot bridge that gap. You cannot bridge the gap. Like Prahlad Maharaj, he was a great devotee but his father was a demon. He says, why are you chanting the names of God? And to some extent, he disobeyed the father and he is called a Mahajana. I am talking about a principle. Are you with me on this? Why this alienation happens? No doubt a person seeks first to understand, then be understood. We can apply that principle, but in spite of a person trying to explain his point of view, his perspective, his understanding, the other person is way below, way below, way below. He cannot understand. Just like a child cannot understand that this time piece is valuable. Then the problem comes. Now very intelligently, knowing, first we should know theoretically why this gap is coming. Then knowing that this gap exists because of this reason, we very intelligently maneuver and do the best we can in that scenario. Neither this person is wrong because what he is doing is authorized. It is recommended in our scriptures. It is meant to be done in human form of life. To be a vegetarian is not wrong. In the parties where everyone is drinking and, you know, womanizing or doing something, this thing, a person will feel uncomfortable because he has risen to another level of thinking and consciousness. For him, for him it looks quite childish and frivolous. No doubt being in a family, he should attend that party. I cannot completely alienate, cannot completely say no to it, but I am just saying why this difference exists and to large extent, the family members may not be able to understand even if he tries to explain. That's how this problem arises. Okay, having any other point, any other thing you would like to share? Other people. So the point here she is making is that the person should not act fanatically with half-baked knowledge and with that half-baked knowledge, try to push his understanding on others. So himself have a good understanding and then try to convince them, but in case of Vishal, it is quite evident that he has started getting very deep into it. Okay, tell me one thing. How do we define fanatism? Chanting 16 rounds is fanatism? No, you can do it. You can do it all, but you know, lot of people show it to the world that I am doing this. You can do it silently. If I want to do 16 rounds, I don't have to advertise in front of my family that I am doing 16 rounds, but family is seeing. Family is seeing. No, you have to plan your time accordingly. Hide and seek, but yeah, many a times that is also needed for me. That is also needed because you are not causing harm to anyone. I agree with this. Sometimes, for example, there was a time when I used to just without anything go on the terrace and chat or go out for a walk and chat. Little bit we will have to consider the persons. Actually, you know what I told in the beginning, it is very important because everyone's situation is unique. There is so much dynamics involved. Everyone's family, everyone's situation will be unique. Now, for example, we can confidently say he can just recuse himself and do independently, but there could be a scenario where family is not allowing that to happen. While we do that, we should not try to force our views and understanding on others. We should not try to force because as I mentioned everyone is at a different level. They will take time. We cannot short circuit that process of the evolving. Good point. So, this triggers a very important discussion. Initially, just like when marriage also happens, there is a phase in which you are understanding the other person's likes, dislikes, what is acceptable to him, what is not acceptable to him and after that so called, I have not gone through it but nevertheless I have heard. After the so called honeymoon period, one year can be turbulent where, you know, he is not breathing. All that adjustment is happening and all that. Again, it is theory for me and then we try to reconcile and accept that he is like this. He needs more sugar in his tea. Give it to him. Don't fight with him. So, you accept and then slowly you, things fall in place, am I right? So, even in Krishna consciousness when we come, there will be a phase where there will be misunderstandings. Now, many times the problem comes when we think that this emotional misunderstanding or that trauma or whatever you call it will last for eternity. It will not last. Even this confusion, slowly the Vishal family members will understand that it is his priority. This is how he is. This is what he likes. Give him that space. Initially they would like to pull him. Why not? That they are doing their duty and slowly as past passage of time they will understand that this is what he wants. Sunday he wants to go, let him go. Morning with two hours he wants to go, let him go and they will also understand. Okay, I am sure they will not want to change. I will not speak to them. Why unnecessarily agitate them by always talking to them and making them come for the session. They don't want to come. Leave them. Just like Prabhupada. Okay, she wants to drink tea. Okay, let her drink. So, I am sure there won't be daily fights happening. So, there is a phase where for family also why he is doing, what is he doing, they are not able to understand. For this person also, you know, on one side he is getting convinced of this message. The other side, you know, they have a family who is at a different level of spiritual growth. So, there would be some confusion for a time. With understanding, I think, it might settle down. Then they eventually put it on Bhagwan. Yes, this is very interesting. When Bhagwan will make us do something, we will do it. Why don't you come for Gita like, if God calls me, I will come. Why don't you come for Gita like, if God calls me, I will come. He didn't call you. He didn't call me. See, one very important thing which we have to learn is when we try to argue or for example, explain to someone, we have to very, very intelligently judge what is his... Is he listening to understand or he is listening to refute? Sometimes, it's not the problem with your arguments. It's a problem that you have not assessed the person properly. In other words, the person... There are people whom you say... Okay, why are you not taking non-metrics? Oh, is it? Is it? It's written in scriptures. Can you please show me that? That person may be in a mood to understand and there are people where no matter how... While you are speaking and giving an argument... See, Tolstoy also was this thing. Mahatma Gandhi also was this thing. He is getting ready with his next point. So, there are three kinds of devotees which are mentioned in our scriptures. One is called Uttama Adhikari, pure devotees. One is called Madhyama Adhikari. Madhyama means middle and then one is called Kanishadhikari. Kanishadhikari means he is neophyte. Neophyte devotees, Kanishadhikari faith is very feeble. Now, most of us are which Adhikaris? How many of us are Uttama Adhikari? Please raise your hands. How many of us are Madhyama Adhikaris? How many of us are Kanishadhikari? See, so humble souls are here. Okay. So, Kanishadhikari... Faith is very Komala, tender. It is recommended that when a person is Kanishadhikari, first he should work on consolidating his faith because sometimes when a person who is very forceful in his arguments, we can get swayed. So, Kanishadhikari, he should first consolidate practice, grow himself, be more convinced. He reaches a faith where he has conviction in what he is doing. He has a right understanding. He has a right conviction. Now, Madhyama Adhikari, he actually is a person who discriminates. There are four kinds of people. One is non-devotees, Athis. He discriminates. He discriminates between the devotees. They are devotees. They are non-devotees and very intelligently he preaches to them or relates with them. Uttama Adhikari, the highest devotee of Paramahamsa, actually does not discriminate. He thinks that everyone is a devotee. I am not a devotee. He thinks everyone is Pandita, Samadhi. Everyone is Darshina. Everyone is equal. Everyone is good. So, Uttama Adhikari devotee actually, for the sake of preaching, comes to the level of Madhyama Adhikari and preaches because when we have to preach, some element of discretion or discrimination is required. I think I gave this example. One time, there was a person in our temple here in Bangalore. He came and, you know, he was a Christian who came to the temple. A lot of visitors come, tourists come. So, he visited. I was taking him around and showed him, you know, Narsimha temple and then Balaji temple and then we went upstairs and showed him Radhakrishna. At one point of time, I could see he was getting little worked up. He says, you know, you have five temples. Why don't you have a temple for Jesus also here? And I said, okay, here we have. You know, like that there are so many gods. You cannot have everyone. No, but Jesus is very important God. So then, instead of arguing, I said, yes, yes, in the next phase, we are planning. So, he was quiet. Next expansion will happen, we are planned actually. So, he was quiet. I mean, there's no point in now opening the whole thing and says, why we can't have a Jesus temple there? So, little bit we'll have to discriminate who is at what level, what he will be able to fathom, what he can understand, what he will not understand. Little bit we have to use our intelligence. We cannot have a canned solution. Canned solution means, go and say this. It will be seen. You will say that in one family, there will be some other result. In the other family, there will be some other result. Yes. Yoni does not mean, you are not stagnant to one species in one lifetime. That is real progress. All other material progress is like horizontal progress. You are in the same species, but oscillating left, right, left, right. Your wealth is increasing. Your social status is increasing, but everything is temporary. It is like a pendulum. It seems to be moving, but no progress. It's just going horizontally, left, right, left, right. So, all material progress is like a pendulum. One life you are a millennial, next life you are a pauper. Next life you are a millennial, next life you are a pauper. You are PhD, next life LKG. PhD, LKG. What is real growth is, in 4 lakhs, where have you headed? What is real growth is, you started off with 3 lakhs, 23,000, whatever that number in terms of species, and you have moved up. That is growth because that growth is permanent growth. It comes with you, life after life. Next life, you start from that level and move ahead. Yeah. So, good point. It says that, we talked about different levels in the 12th chapter where, a person is completely surrendered to Krishna, always remembering. Then Krishna says, we cannot do this thing. You know, be my devotee and, you know, do my work. If you cannot do this, okay, give in charity, all those things. Again, you can classify in different ways. You can classify in different ways. Like, for example, now the IPL is going on. You know, okay, here, this is Chennai, this thing. Next game, they will be classified differently. Okay, these are Indians, these are Australians. You get what I am saying? So, just I thought of this example. It may not be the right, accurate example, but just I am saying, you can classify things differently at different times. Broadly speaking, consciousness wise, just I gave you example. Even in this hall, although we are, most of us are regular to Krishna consciousness, how many levels are there? Am I right? Of our commitment? Some are chanting four rounds, following Gagadashi. Some are following Gagadashi, not chanting. Some are, you know, chanting one round. Some are chanting two rounds, but we watch TV, we watch IPL, we watch everything. Some are chanting four rounds, but we watch this, we don't watch that. We eat this, we don't eat that. So, so many levels are there. Like that, there are four lakh levels. The bigger the gap and more so in the same family, bigger the problem and that's why, if in the same family, both the members are practicing more or less at the same level, it becomes very congenial. Point here is, what if, like, we hear that we have to chant rounds, we have to also read, we have to also follow Gagadashi Vrata, we have also, we have to, what else? What else we hear? We have to follow the four regulative principles, we should not take non-veg, we should also avoid intoxicants, you know. So, is it okay that one can chant 16 rounds and once in a while take beer and, you know, social this thing and, may not, okay, no intoxication kar nahi follow kiya and Ekadashi me bhi also we take grains, it's okay, but chanting bhi chal raha hai, Gita life bhi chal raha hai. So, can we do that or we have to follow everything in total? Okay. So, I'll first complete this thing. Now, we have been prescribed some principles. Okay, do this, do this, do this, don't do this, don't do this, don't do this. See, just like igniting fire, if we want to ignite fire and have good fire, what do we do? We follow the dos and don'ts. One is, if we have to ignite fire, we put ghee at the right interval, at the right time, right quantity, am I right? There are certain don'ts also. What are the don'ts? Don't put water in it. Now, all the rules which have been prescribed in our scriptures, these are basically to ignite the fire of bhakti or devotion to God. So, there are some dos, some don'ts. Now, if we do not follow, we follow only some dos, for example, we are igniting fire and we are fanning it and putting ghee also and we are also somehow not convinced of the idea that we should not put water and we decide, it is okay, let us put water. Then, it can undo what you are doing and it can, you might find, I have ignited so much fire, I have smoked so much, finally it is all smoke. There is no fire and family members will say, what, you have been going for so many years, you are still the same because, it is like pouring water, that is why we have to be little clear what we want in life. We do this, we do that, we do this, we do that, but nothing happens because, you are trying to pour water in the bucket and bucket has big, big holes, finally there is no water in the bucket. People will say, why did you burn the tap? It is leaking. What are the leakages? Breaking the principles are leakages. We eat a little and say, okay, what is there to do in the party, non-vegetarian, I have not killed a chicken, it was already dead. So, what is wrong, what will happen if you eat once? Tomorrow I will chant additional two rounds, what is that? The chanting in

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### FEAR: HOW TO CONQUER? HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### fear, Bhagavad Gita, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There are many tips in the Bhagavad Gita, many insights which we can gather from the instructions of Lord Shri Krishna, where we can go through these fearful situations without getting bewildered, without getting overwhelmed, with confidence, with faith in the Lord."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of conquering fear through the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita and invites the audience to join in prayers for blessings before the discussion.

Hare Krishna, a warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is fear, how to conquer it. All of us will go through situations, circumstances when we will be having some kind of fear or fearful situations. There are many tips in the Bhagavad Gita, many insights which we can gather from the instructions of Lord Shri Krishna, where we can go through these fearful situations without getting bewildered, without getting overwhelmed, with confidence, with faith in the Lord. And this is what we are going to discuss today in the session. So before we get into the discussion, I request all of you to join me in offering the prayers. Let us seek the blessings of Shri Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Shri Krishna and with their blessings get into the discussion. So please fold your hands if possible and join me in offering these prayers. Let us pray together.

---
#### illusion, fear, dreams
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=276
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The only option is he wakes up that person knowing that he is having a dream, and once the person wakes up, there is no tiger, there is no fear."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about a man having a nightmare about a tiger chasing him, illustrating that waking up from the dream is the only way to escape the fear, setting up the first principle of the lecture.

Once there were two friends staying together. In fact, they decided to stay overnight together. And while one of them was sleeping, the other one noticed that he is blurting out, "Save me, save me," somewhat screaming, maybe he is having a fearful dream. "Save me, save me, tiger is chasing, save me, save me," he is screaming. Mind you, when we dream, although the dream may be unreal, but the experience is real. That's why at times we even perspire. We wake up and then we feel a sense of relief that after all it was a dream. The experience is real. So this person was screaming, the tiger is chasing me, help me, help me. He was trying to run fast in his dream. And this friend of his was standing next to him, next to his bed, watching him screaming because the tiger was chasing him in the dream. Now obviously when you are a friend of someone and someone is in danger, naturally you will like to pitch in to help that person. So now this person was in a dilemma. What should he do to help this friend? To help this friend of his who was being chased by a tiger in his dream? The two options were, one, he can take a stick or arm himself with some kind of a gun or a pistol to kill the tiger and get into the dream world of his friend with that gun, with that stick, kill the tiger, and save his friend so that his friend can then relax and feel peaceful. That is option one. Option two, he wakes that friend of his and the moment the friend wakes up, he realizes that it was a dream and all of a sudden without no extraneous effort, all the fear, all the horror, all the stress what he was going through completely goes off. Obviously the first option is not possible. He cannot get into the dream world of a person with a stick and try to kill the tiger to give him the relief. The only option is he wakes up that person knowing that he is having a dream, and once the person wakes up, there is no tiger, there is no fear.

---
#### awakening, Maya, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=468
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The principle is wake up, wake up."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the first principle: 'Wake up, wake up,' drawing from a shloka and explaining that we are all sleeping on the lap of Maya, experiencing illusory fears and anxieties.

So the first point which I want to make today, the first principle is, please make a note of it, the principle is wake up, wake up. I repeat, wake up, wake up. This comes from a shloka which says utishta, utishta, oh wake up, oh wake up. Jeeva jago, Jeeva jago, Gaurachandra bole. Gaurachandra is Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. He is begging, oh wake up, oh Jeeva, wake up. How long you will be sleeping on the lap of Maya? We all are sleeping on the lap of Maya. And because we are sleeping on the lap of Maya, we are part of the illusory world with all kinds of illusory experiences, sometimes which are full of fear, anxiety, stress.

---
#### soul, eternal life, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=533
**Verse References:** BG 14.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "From the perspective of the soul, even this life is like a dream, because this is also temporary, and when we leave this body, we take up another body, just like when we come out of the dream world, we get into another world."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that our natural state is joyful, eternal, full of knowledge and bliss, and that from the soul's perspective, this life is like an extended dream, temporary and unreal.

In fact, Bhagavad Gita says, brahma bhuta prasannatma, our natural state is that we are prasannatma, we are joyful, we are sat, we are eternal, we are chit, we are full of knowledge, and ananda, we are blissful. Our scriptures also say, just like we have a dream in the night, and then we wake up, the dream is gone. Dream is for two hours, three hours, four hours, at best maybe for the length of the night. And when we wake up, we realize that it was a dream, it was unreal. We went into a forest, we were being chased by a tiger, it was unreal. Or something very inauspicious happened to us, it was unreal. When we wake up, all that inauspiciousness is gone. So from the perspective of the soul, even this life is like a dream, because this is also temporary, and when we leave this body, we take up another body, just like when we come out of the dream world, we get into another world. So this life, you can call it as an extended dream or a daydream. Because from the perspective of soul, this is not our eternal life. We are not eternally Indian, we are not eternally men, we are not eternally, let's say, into a particular business. It's like a dream world. Now in the dream world, just like in a dream world, you can have an inauspicious dream, a horrid dream in which we could experience a lot of fear.

---
#### knowledge, soul, fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=643
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we realize that I am a pure soul, I am not the body, I am eternal servant of Krishna, that waking up eliminates all inauspiciousness, all fear from our lives."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the experience of fear when identifying with the body with the fearlessness that comes from realizing oneself as a pure soul and eternal servant of Krishna.

Likewise, we go through a lot of good and bad instances in this life. If we are very convinced, and we are living with that understanding that I am the body, we have no option but to experience fear, pain, agony, but what Bhagavad Gita is saying that when we wake up, utishta, utishta, jagrata, when we wake up, when we realize that I am a pure soul, I am not the body, I am eternal servant of Krishna, that waking up eliminates all inauspiciousness, all fear from our lives.

#### fear, death, anxiety
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=693
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "I wanted to take ten only, but the balance twenty people died because of fear, because of stress, because of anxiety."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about Yamaduta, the lord of death, who came to take ten people from a village, but thirty people died, with the extra twenty dying from fear and anxiety.

That's why Krishna in the Gita says, brahma bhuta prasannatma, he becomes joyful. So, one time it so happened that Yamaduta, Yamadutas we all know are representatives of Lord Yamaraja, the lord of death. So he was entering a particular village. So a person who had lot of mystic powers, siddhis, he intercepted Yamaduta and said, why are you entering this village? Somehow he could recognize Yamaduta, and Yamaduta being a representative of Yamaraja, told that person that today, as per the task assigned to me, I have to take ten people from this village. In other words, ten people are going to die, and I have to take them to Yamaraja. So the person said, this is not done. You can't take anyone from this village. Just like here in present day pandemic, we are trying our best to minimize the casualties, deaths. So this person also went to the village and alerted everyone, beware, beware, Yamaduta is entering. Just like in present day time, people are being cautioned how to take precautions, how not to get infected. But in the evening, to his surprise, when he again intercepted Yamaduta, exiting from that village, he said, this is not fair. Morning when you met me, you told me that you are going to take ten people from this village, but why is it that today thirty people died? You told me ten, but the count is thirty. He said, I wanted to take ten only, but the balance twenty people died because of fear, because of stress, because of anxiety.

#### knowledge, fear, understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=841
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we have to overcome the fear or be confident and not get bewildered and overwhelmed in fearful situations, we have no option but to come to knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that fear, anxiety, and stress can accelerate death, and to overcome fear and be confident, one must awaken to the real understanding of who we are.

It's a fact. Many people die because of fear, anxiety, stress, even in present times. No doubt in present pandemic, because of coronavirus, obviously because of the infection, but otherwise there's a lot of fear, a lot of anxiety. What if, what if? And that also accelerates the process of death. So if we have to overcome the fear or be confident and not get bewildered and overwhelmed in fearful situations, we have no option but to come to knowledge. Wake up, wake up with the real understanding of who we are.

---
#### Bhagavad Gita, Arjuna, fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=886
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "After hearing that, all that fear, all that inauspiciousness, all that anxiety which Arjuna was going through got dissipated. Arjuna rose up, fought valiantly and won the battle."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Arjuna in the Bhagavad Gita, who was overwhelmed with fear and anxiety, but after hearing Krishna's message, overcame his fear and won the battle.

In the first chapter of Bhagavad Gita, Arjuna, when he saw his own kinsmen, own relatives, own friends assembled in the battlefield, as we all know, towards the end of the first chapter, he becomes overwhelmed with fear, with dilemma and says, Krishna, I'm not going to fight. And he leaves his Gandiva bow, his hands trembling, his mind reeling. He says, I'm unable to stand any longer. These are all symptoms of a person who is overwhelmed with fear, anxiety, stress. And then finally he says, I'm not going to fight. Now Krishna, after hearing all these explanations from Arjuna about what he is going through, what he is feeling, he spoke the message of Gita. He did not change the circumstances. He did not say, okay, you can go home and I will fight the battle for you. He just started the immortal message of Bhagavad Gita. And after hearing that, all that fear, all that inauspiciousness, all that anxiety which Arjuna was going through got dissipated. Arjuna rose up, fought valiantly and won the battle.

#### faith, dependence, confidence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=1048
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So he was very confident that so long as my father is there, the ticket collector will not be able to take me out of the tram cart."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about Srila Prabhupada and his son on a tram, where the son's confidence in his father's presence illustrates the importance of faith and dependence on a higher power.

One time Srila Prabhupada was travelling with his son in a tram cart in Calcutta. The son was quite small, and as usual, the ticket conductor, the ticket checker, he came, and the son was quite small. Sometimes, you know, they play with the child. So the ticket collector pointed to the child and says, give me money. Give me money for the ticket. So the child very innocently said, I don't have. So the ticket seller said, if you don't have money, if you don't give money, get out. Get out. Get out from the tram cart. So the moment the ticket collector said, get out, get out, the child looked at his father and told the ticket collector, my father. My father. So he was very confident that so long as my father is there, the ticket collector will not be able to take me out of the tram cart. Because if I need to pay for the thing, my father will pay.

#### faith, Lord Krishna, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=1119

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker draws an analogy between the child's confidence in his father and the faith one should have in Lord Krishna, emphasizing that feeling the Lord's presence provides protection and eliminates fear.

So just like a father, a child is very, very confident because of the presence of his father. Likewise, if we have the presence of our all-knowing, all-powerful father, the Supreme Lord, Lord Shri Krishna, if we feel that presence, just like the child, for that, it was very obvious for him that father is next to me. So the ticket collector cannot ask me to get down from the tram cart. So likewise, if we have that faith, if we have that conviction, if we have that confidence that Lord is with me, the Lord is my protector, the Lord will protect me, then we will not have any kind of fear. Now, that faith, that conviction, that relationship has to be built.

#### dependence, surrender, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=1233
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Catch hold of my hand because it's quite risky. In case, in case you slip, catch hold of my hand so that in case you slip, we are safe."
**Summary:** The speaker tells a story about a father and daughter crossing a dilapidated bridge, where the daughter insists that the father hold her hand, illustrating the deeper meaning of surrender and dependence on the Lord.

One time, a father and his daughter were trying to cross a bridge. And just below the bridge, it was a very makeshift bridge made of some wooden planks tied to some rope. And it was quite dilapidated, quite old bridge. And below that bridge was a river with a lot of currents. And this father and daughter had no option but to cross this bridge, even though it was risky, because they had no option if they have to, and they couldn't reach their village. So just before crossing the bridge, the father told the daughter, catch hold of my hand because it's quite risky. In case you slip, catch hold of my hand so that in case you slip, we are safe. But the daughter refused, says, no, father. So the father was quite alarmed. After all, what he was asking was quite normal. You know, they were about to cross a bridge, the bridge made of wooden planks, bamboos and, you know, with a rope, quite risky. So the daughter said, no, father, instead, you catch hold of my hand. Father was again surprised. Father was again surprised. What's the difference? The father is asking the daughter to catch hold of his hand and daughter is saying, no, father, I will not catch. And the daughter is saying, father, you catch my hand. So the father was quite surprised with this proposition from his daughter and asked, what is the difference?

#### surrender, dependence, divine will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wp2jExojDDU?t=1360
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If I catch your hand and in case I slip or in case we go through some challenging times, there is a possibility that I might stop holding your hand. But in case you hold the land, my hand, then you, out of love for me, will never leave my hand."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the daughter's reasoning: that her grip on her father's hand might falter, but his love would never let him release her hand, illustrating the deeper meaning of surrender and dependence on the Lord.

Whether I catch your hand or you catch my hand, what's the difference? One and the same thing. After all, what I'm asking is just catch hold of the hand. So he says, the daughter says, that if I catch your hand and in case I slip or in case we go through some challenging times, there is a possibility that I might stop holding your hand. I might let loose. I might let loose the grip from your hand. But in case you hold the land, my hand, then you, out of love for me, will never leave my hand. It's a very fine difference. So likewise, one thing is we depend on the Lord and catch hold of the Lord's lotus feet. Catch hold of the Lord's hand and beg the Lord for Lord's protection. That is one thing. A devotee of the Lord can also pray to the Lord. Oh, Lord, please, catch hold of me. Catch hold of my hand. Do not let me go away from your lotus feet into the lap of Maya. Catch hold of me. Lead my life. Lead my life according to your will. That is the meaning of surrender. So when a person is surrendered to the Lord, when he is dependent on the Lord, when he is leading a life where he is begging the Lord, Lord, catch hold of my hand. Lead my way. Guide my way.

Very practical tips. Surrender to the Lord is not something vague. Chanting the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra is one form of surrender. You are surrendering your time. You are surrendering your energy. Following the four regulative principles is one form of surrender, because the mind is saying, eat something which is not good for your soul, but say that, no, I will follow the instructions what Prabhupada has given. That is one form of surrender. So when we surrender to the Lord, when we obey his instructions, when we depend on the Lord.

If you surrender 100%, then Krishna's reciprocation is also 100%.

We have example of Draupadi. She completely let go of her saree. And then Krishna released unlimited supply of saree, and she was protected. So ee vishwasa palana, that faith has to be cultivated. And it is not a blind faith, with knowledge, with understanding, with examples of great devotees, how the Lord protected them.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### FINDING TIME FOR KRISHNA | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, prayer, purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xy0njl-QK-A?t=19
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** introduction
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So that we can have a very, very meaningful discussion today and we can learn some important insights which if practiced in our day to day lives, can enable us to spend more time with the Lord."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa welcomes the audience to the Gita Live session, introduces the topic of finding time for Krishna, and explains the session's structure, emphasizing practical principles and tips, followed by a prayer seeking blessings for a meaningful discussion.

Hare Krishna! Warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is finding time for Krishna. Many of us want to spend more time with the Lord, devote more time for spiritual activities, but there is always a sense of guilt. I am not able to do as much as I want to. So today's session, we will be talking about some principles, some tips, some do's and don'ts, which can enable us to find more time for the Lord. So before we start our discussion, let us all pray together. Seek the blessings of Srila Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya, Lord Shri Krishna, so that we can have a very, very meaningful discussion today and we can learn some important insights which, if practiced in our day to day lives, can enable us to spend more time with the Lord. So we will start with prayers. Request all of you to also fold your hands and join me in offering these prayers. We can offer them together.

Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Prishtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gaurabhakta Vrinda Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratushe Namah Namo Brahmane Devaya Go Brahmane Hithaya Cha Jagadhithaya Krishnaya Govindaya Namo Namah He Krishna Karuna Sindhu Deena Bandhu Jagatpate Gopesha Gopika Kaanta Radha Kaanta Namostute Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye Vansha Kalpata Rubhyasya Kripa Sindhu Bhya Evachya Patita Naam Paavne Bhyo Vaishnave Bhyo Namo Namaha Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare

**Key Quote:** "So I would request all of you if possible, in fact I would reinforce those key points so that maybe you can make a note of it and after every session if you could just note these three points so that even if we were to remember what did I learn from the session, if we just remember these three key words, three key points, it can help us recollect many things which were discussed in the session."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa outlines the structure of the lecture, focusing on three key principles for finding time for Krishna, encouraging the audience to note these points for better retention and recollection, and requests that questions be specific to the current discussion.

So today, as part of the discussion on the topic finding time for Krishna, I'll share with you three principles, not two, not five, not four, just three principles. I was thinking rather than talking about so many things, let's talk less, in other words, less number of points so that after the session, if you were to remember, it is relatively easy. Many times we remember that yes, I attended some session, but what was there in that session, what were the key takeaways, we hardly remember. So I would request all of you if possible, in fact I would reinforce those key points so that maybe you can make a note of it, and after every session, if you could just note these three points so that even if we were to remember what did I learn from the session, if we just remember these three key words, three key points, it can help us recollect many things which were discussed in the session. So the first principle which I would like to mention, for each principle I'll give a heading, and in fact as part of today's discussion, I would like all of you to also type the principle in the comments box where we are asking questions. Before I get into the discussion, there was a feedback by one of the participant, if you are writing questions, if you are asking questions, try as far as possible to ask questions pertaining to the discussion which we are having today. Say for example, I am speaking something and it is not clear or you want to ask something on that particular point, either you want to clarify or you want to ask something specific to that point, you can ask. Try not to ask questions which are general in nature and not related to what we are discussing today.

#### surrender, release, entanglement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xy0njl-QK-A?t=407
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All of us in this world are trapped in the web of illusion, the degree varies from person to person."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the first principle: 'Pray for Release,' explaining that everyone is entangled in the material world to varying degrees, ranging from those completely bound to those liberated, like Narada Muni, emphasizing the need to pray for release from this entanglement.

So the first principle is, please make a note of it, I will be asking this and request all of you to type later on at certain point of time. The first principle is pray for release. I repeat, pray for release. Now what is this principle? All of us are actually entangled and bound to different degrees in this material world. All of us in this world are trapped in the web of illusion, the degree varies from person to person. On one side of the spectrum are people who are completely bound, completely entangled. If they want to take the name of God, there is no opportunity, there is no time, just for putting food in their mouth they have to work for 16 hours a day. There are people like that, so that's one end of the spectrum. They may be pious, they may be religious, they may be God fearing, they may want to worship Lord but there is no opportunity, they are so badly entangled, so that's one end of the spectrum. The other end of the spectrum are Paramahamsa's, 24 hours they are engaged in Lord's service, Lord's work, chanting the names of the Lord, completely liberated. Example I will give you of Narada Muni, he is just in his Veena traveling, glorifying the Lord, chanting the names of the Lord, liberated soul. We also have examples of Paramahamsa's, we also have examples of great devotees, 24 hours, even while they are sleeping they are serving the Lord. So we have a lot of difference, all of us are entangled or liberated to different degrees.

---
#### equality, freedom, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xy0njl-QK-A?t=565
**Verse References:** BG 9.29
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All of us figuratively, like this example, are bound by ropes."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that Krishna is equal to everyone, using the analogy of a cow tied to a pole with varying rope lengths to illustrate the degrees of freedom and entanglement in the material world, emphasizing that everyone is bound by invisible ropes of the three gunas.

And Krishna in the Gita says, Samoham Sarva Bhootesho, I am equal to everyone. In this world we see people who are filthy rich and we are having people who live in abject poverty, they don't have even food to eat, the basic necessities of life and Krishna says, I am equal to everyone, I am not partial to anybody, I don't favor anyone. Another example I will give you, just like a cow, I have spoken about this many, many times in many, many sessions, for some of you it might be a repetition but nevertheless it is a very good example. A cow which is tied to a pole with a rope, now visualize, just imagine the length of the rope is 3 meters, the cow is free to move around the pole in a radius of 3 meters. It is free to move around the pole in a radius of 3 meters. It is free to move around the pole in a radius of 3 meters. It is free, it can move front, back within that 3 meter radius but beyond the 3 meter if the cow wants to move the rope will not allow. So in other words the cow is bound to the pole with a rope and the length of the rope is 3 meters, that is the freedom which the cow has. Now imagine a shorter rope, let's say the length of the rope is 1 meter only, the cow still has freedom, the cow still can move but it can move in a radius of 1 meter only, the freedom is relatively compared to the earlier case restricted. It is not as free as the case where the cow has the rope which is 3 meters long. Now imagine another case where the cow is tied so tightly to the pole that the length of the rope is practically zero. In other words the neck is tied to the pole, although we haven't seen a scenario like this but just visualize, visualize that the cow is tied to the pole and the length of the rope is practically zero, the cow cannot move. Cannot move an inch. Now let's take the other example, the length of the rope is 8 meters, the cow can relatively move very freely, 6 meters radius it can move front back, sit down, whatever it can, it can do relatively lot of things. So all of us figuratively, like this example, are bound by ropes. In our case the ropes are invisible and the ropes are the ropes of gunas, three gunas. Guna also means ropes, just like ropes you have seen, ropes are made of strands which are intertwined, there are three strands which are generally intertwined, it can be thicker rope, many more strands all intertwined. Likewise this rope which binds us to this material world, the three gunas, that rope is made of three strands and the three strands are the ropes. So if the rope is made of three strands then we can say that the length of the rope is little longer.

---
#### gunas, bondage, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/xy0njl-QK-A?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In either case, whether it is gold, silver, or iron, it is a chain and it's binding us."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa elaborates on how the three gunas (tamo guna, sattva guna, and rajo guna) influence the length of the rope binding individuals, comparing them to chains of iron, silver, and gold, respectively, emphasizing that all three bind us to the material world.

If tamo guna is predominant, if a person is predominantly influenced by mode of ignorance then we can say the length of the rope is less than the length of the rope. That's why when a person is influenced by tamo guna, ignorance, laziness, he wants to just sleep, doesn't want to do anything, inertia. When a person is influenced by sattva guna, enlightenment, knowledge, freedom, contentment, these are all the feelings which a person will experience. But Prabhupada says even if a person is predominantly influenced by sattva guna you can compare it to a golden rope, a chain made of gold. A person predominantly influenced by rajo guna you can call it a chain made of iron. In either case, whether it is gold, silver, or iron, it is a chain and it's binding us. So all of us are bound.
