#### hearing, taste, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uS09kQJ_FIU?t=2101
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we have done proper Shravanam, if we have done proper hearing, then what will happen? We will naturally get taste, we will naturally get an inspiration, we will naturally get motivated to do Bhajan Kriya."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that proper hearing (Shravanam) leads to taste, inspiration, and motivation for devotional service (Bhajan Kriya), while insufficient hearing results in mechanical and tasteless spiritual activities.

And if we have done proper Shravanam, if we have done proper hearing, then what will happen? We will naturally get taste, we will naturally get an inspiration, we will naturally get motivated to do Bhajan Kriya. If we have not sufficiently heard, even our chanting will become mechanical, it will become tasteless. Our spiritual activities will lose a context, why we are doing what we are doing, we will not know and we will not be able to sustain. Moment something interesting comes up in our life, we will start pursuing that and we will get defocused here.

#### mercy, perfection, spiritual life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uS09kQJ_FIU?t=5344
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that perfection in spiritual life can be achieved through both personal effort (Sadhna Siddha) and the mercy of the Lord and his devotees (Kripa Siddha), emphasizing the importance of prayer and seeking blessings.

And by the mercy of the Lord, there is two kind of Siddha, Siddhi we can get, perfection. One is through our Sadhna, Sadhna Siddha and there is another thing it's called Kripa Siddha, by the mercy of the Lord, by the mercy of his pure devotees. The best part in spiritual life, whatever we are lacking, we can pray, we can beg, we can ask for and the best part is that the Lord is very merciful, pure devotees are very merciful.

#### chanting, attentiveness, pace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uS09kQJ_FIU?t=5391
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhupada said chanting fast or slow is secondary, the main thing is to hear attentively."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses a question about the correct timing for chanting one round, quoting Prabhupada as saying that attentiveness is more important than speed, but suggesting a reasonable pace of 7.5 to 9 minutes per round.

I live in my one round of chanting takes ten minutes is it correct timings? Prabhupada said chanting fast or slow is secondary, the main thing is to hear attentively, but generally reasonable pace if we chant not too fast, not too slow a person can finish this round, one round hundred eight times in eight to nine minutes, seven and a half to eight to nine minutes that is the range, some people finish it eight minutes.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### God of Gods | Bhagavad Gita Chapter 10 Verses 12 - 16 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Introduction, Bhagavad Gita Chapter 10, Opulences of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** introduction
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So today we'll start the discussion we are going to discuss 12th to 16th verse from the 10th chapter of the Gita in which as I mentioned the 10th chapter is about the opulences of the Lord."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa begins the lecture by introducing the topic: verses 12-16 from the 10th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, focusing on the opulences of the Lord, based on Srila Prabhupada's teachings.

So today we'll start the discussion. We are going to discuss 12th to 16th verse from the 10th chapter of the Gita, in which, as I mentioned, the 10th chapter is about the opulences of the Lord. It's based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada, the founder acharya of worldwide Hare Krishna movement. We will chant this verse together. It's the 12th verse. Please repeat after me.

---
#### Verse Recitation, Arjuna's Understanding, Supreme Brahman
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=30
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Arjuna said, you are the supreme Brahman, the ultimate made, the supreme abode and purifier, the absolute truth and the eternal divine person. You are the primal God, transcendental and original and you are unborn and all-pervading beauty."
**Summary:** The speaker leads the audience in reciting the 12th verse of the 10th chapter, where Arjuna acknowledges Krishna as the Supreme Brahman, the ultimate abode, purifier, absolute truth, eternal divine person, primal God, transcendental, original, unborn, and all-pervading beauty.

Translation: Arjuna said, you are the supreme Brahman, the ultimate abode, the supreme abode and purifier, the absolute truth and the eternal divine person. You are the primal God, transcendental and original, and you are unborn and all-pervading beauty.

---
#### Krishna as Source, Understanding and Devotion, Budha Bhava Samanvita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=103
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Having understood that Krishna is the absolute truth, the cause of all causes, everything emanates from him, a person who is wise, who comes to this understanding, he worships me with bhava, with love, with devotion."
**Summary:** The speaker references a previous verse where Krishna declares Himself as the source of everything. He explains that those who understand this truth, who are 'Budha bhava samanvita' (intelligent, wise, and filled with love and devotion), worship Krishna.

The previous verse Krishna says, 'Aham sarvasya prabhavo matta sarvam pravartate.' We covered it last week. I am the source of everything, material and spiritual. Everything emanates from me. Anyone remembers the next two lines? 'Aham sarvasya prabhavo matta sarvam pravartate. Iti matva bhajante maam.' 'Iti matva' means, with this understanding, 'matva' means understanding. 'Bhajante maam,' worship me, glorify me. 'Budha bhava samanvita,' budha means one who is intelligent, one who is wise, one who has realized this understanding, who has come to this conclusion. Budha bhava, bhava means with feelings, with emotions, with love, with devotion. Samanvita means along with, together with. Having understood that Krishna is the absolute truth, the cause of all causes, everything emanates from him, a person who is wise, who comes to this understanding, he worships me with bhava, with love, with devotion.

---
#### Arjuna's Confirmation, Sages' Testimony, Universal Truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=181
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Arjuna having understood that, is saying, you are the supreme Brahman, the ultimate, the supreme abode and the purifier, absolute truth and the eternal divine person... Just to clarify, he says, it is not just my understanding. All the great sages like Narada, Asita, Devala, Vyasa, proclaim this of you and now you yourself are declaring it to me."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Arjuna, having understood Krishna's supreme position, confirms it by stating Krishna's attributes. To emphasize that this understanding isn't solely Arjuna's, he points out that great sages like Narada, Asita, Devala, and Vyasa also proclaim the same truth.

So now here, Arjuna having understood that, is saying, you are the supreme Brahman, the ultimate, the supreme abode and the purifier, absolute truth and the eternal divine person. You are primal God, transcendental and original, and you are unborn, all-pervading beauty. Now, somebody may say, Arjuna is saying that because he is friend of Krishna and, you know, because he is in the battle of Kurukshetra, he has the understanding, maybe Krishna is God. Just to clarify, he says, it is not just my understanding. 'Ahustam rishya sarve,' rishya means sages. Sarve means all. Who are those prominent sages? All the great sages like Narada, Asita, Devala, Vyasa, proclaim this of you and now you yourself are declaring it to me.

---
#### Krishna's Declaration, Arjuna's Agreement, Confirmation of Truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=244
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna, Arjuna says, yes, I agree. You are the supreme Brahman. You are the supreme purifier. You are the absolute truth. And this is not just my understanding. Even great sages have. I have been talking about it and now today you yourself are proclaiming this truth."
**Summary:** The speaker highlights Arjuna's agreement with Krishna's self-declaration as the Supreme Brahman, purifier, and absolute truth. He reiterates that this understanding is supported by great sages and Krishna's own proclamation.

So Krishna is saying, Krishna, Arjuna says, yes, I agree. You are the supreme Brahman. You are the supreme purifier. You are the absolute truth. And this is not just my understanding. Even great sages have been talking about it and now today you yourself are proclaiming this truth. Now, after hearing the essential four verses, Arjuna is fully clear. He is no longer in any doubt and he is not flattering him. And just to confirm that this understanding is not just his, he says that even the great sages like Narada, Asita, proclaim this about you. Now, what is supreme Brahman? Anyone can help me with an answer. What is Brahman?

---
#### Understanding Brahman, Impersonal Aspect, Analogy of the Sun
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=300
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahman is impersonal, all pervading aspect. The aspect of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker initiates a discussion on the nature of Brahman, contrasting it with the concept of Brahmana. He uses the analogy of the sun, sun god, sun planet, and sunshine to explain that Brahman is the impersonal, all-pervading aspect of the Supreme Lord, similar to how sunshine is the energy emanating from the sun.

'Aham Brahmasmi.' You heard this statement. What is Brahman? Is it same as Brahmana? Yes, Sunil. Okay. Very close to the right answer. What is Brahman? What is Brahman? Brahman. Vipratishtha aham. Krishna says, I am that supreme Brahman. What is Brahman? 'Aham Brahmasmi.' Have you heard this statement? How many of us heard this? 'Aham Brahmasmi.' The supreme Lord, many times people say, is energy. Right? This understanding is not wrong, but it is not the complete understanding. Just like you have the sun. You have the sun god, you have sun planet and you have sunshine. When sunshine enters the house, we say sun has come. In one sense it is not wrong, because sunshine is energy of sun, emanating from sun. Sunshine has no place without sun. There cannot be sunshine without sun. So, Brahman is impersonal, all pervading aspect. Brahman is impersonal, all pervading aspect. The aspect of the Supreme Lord.

#### Brahman and Omnipresence, Purity of the Lord, Purification of Consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=381
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahman is all-pervading impersonal aspect of the Supreme Lord... The Lord is said to be purest of all pure... When we chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, our consciousness will get purified."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that Brahman is the all-pervading, impersonal aspect of the Supreme Lord, similar to sunshine being the all-pervading aspect of the sun. He emphasizes the purity of the Lord and how chanting the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra purifies consciousness by removing negative qualities like kama, krodha, lobha, greed, lust, pride, and envy.

Is that clear to everyone? Brahman is all-pervading, Lord is also said omnipresent. Am I right? Brahman is all-pervading impersonal aspect of the Supreme Lord. Just like sunshine is all-pervading aspect of sun. So everything is resting on Brahman, because everything, all pervading, it's all Brahman. 'Aham Brahmasmi,' I am also that Brahman. So Supreme Brahman is the resting place for everything. And the Lord is said to be purest of all pure. 'Pavitram Uttamam.' Uttamam means highest. Now we say when we chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, our consciousness will get purified. We will get purified. All the anarthas, all the dirty things, kama, krodha, lobha, greed, lust, pride, envy, all of us know it's not good qualities. We want to get rid of all this. We know that greed is not bad. We know that lust is not bad. We know anger is not bad. And many times we strive for getting rid of all these, base qualities.

#### Spiritual Advancement, Purification Process, Association with the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=469
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Genuine advancement is accompanied by purification of consciousness... The Lord who is all pure, descends in the form of his holy name, and the holy name of the Lord and the Lord are non-different... When I associate with the Lord, specifically in this age, by chanting the holy names of the Lord, I contact, I touch, I associate with the Lord who is all-pure... I also become pure."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that genuine spiritual advancement involves purification of consciousness. This happens through association with the Lord, particularly by chanting His holy names, which are non-different from Him. He uses the analogy of an iron rod becoming red hot when placed in fire to illustrate how association with the pure Lord leads to experiencing sat, chit, and ananda, and ultimately, purification.

Sometimes we try some tools, techniques. They say when you get angry, count backwards, 100 to 1. How many of us have tried some of these things? When we get angry, first thing we forget to count all these things, right? So in spiritual life, genuine advancement, is accompanied by purification of consciousness. How does that happen? What is the process? The Lord who is all pure, descends in the form of his holy name, and the holy name of the Lord and the Lord are non-different. 'Naam, Naamino, Abhinatvam' are non-different. When I touch fire, then I become, I feel the heat of fire. When I put an iron rod in fire, a time comes when the iron rod becomes red hot, almost becomes like fire. So likewise, when I associate with the Lord, specifically in this age, by chanting the holy names of the Lord, I contact, I touch, I associate with the Lord who is all-pure, who is reservoir of sat, chit and ananda. Just like that iron becomes red hot, likewise when I also contact the Lord who is all-pure, who is reservoir of sat, chit and ananda, I also experience that sat, chit and ananda. I also become pure.

#### Importance of Chanting, Duration of Association, Cause of All Causes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=585
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The longer you keep, more heat gets transferred to that fire... He is the cause of all causes. When there was nothing, He was there. And everything is emanating from Him."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of prolonged chanting, comparing it to keeping an iron rod in fire for a longer time to make it red hot. He then discusses Krishna as the supreme origin and cause of all causes, using the riddle of the chicken and the egg to illustrate that everything emanates from Him.

So this is the reason why we chant. Somebody says, why one hour chanting? Why not just five minutes? Why not two minutes? Just like an iron rod, you can put it in fire and take it out. It doesn't happen, nothing much happens to it. You keep it in fire, it becomes red hot. The longer you keep, more heat gets transferred to that fire. So likewise, daily we touch the Supreme Lord in the form of His Holy Name, and we start experiencing purification of consciousness. Then Arjuna says, you are the supreme origin. He has no cause, for He is the cause of all causes, and everything is emanating from Him. Sometimes people wonder, egg came first or the chicken came first? What is the answer? Egg came first or chicken came first? Or hen came first? What is the answer? Srinivas, what came first? What came first? Both of them came from the Lord. He is the cause of all causes. When there was nothing, He was there. And everything is emanating from Him. We can have that perfect knowledge and faith and that understanding only by the grace of Supreme Lord.

There is element of faith, there is element of surrender, and Krishna blesses with Divya Gyan Hride Prakashiva. The same is true for the Prasita. Prakashita means, it shines from within the heart. Once there was a Brahmin, who was engaged in different kinds of spiritual activities, rituals, penances, austerities. And he happened to meet Narada, who was on his way to Vaikuntha. How many of you have heard this story? Ok, briefly I will share. So, this Brahmin was striving for salvation, liberation. So he knew that Narada is very close to Lord Vishnu, Narayana. He keeps going to Vaikuntha. So he said, when you meet Narayana, you ask him this question, this Brahmin, he is striving hard for mukti or liberation. When will he get his mukti? Narada said, okay, I will ask.

#### Narada's Journey, Questions about Liberation, The Lord's Response
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=757
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "On his way to Vaikuntha, he also happened to meet a cobbler. The cobbler also asked the same question. If you happen to meet Narayana, please ask him, when will I get my liberation?... The Lord said, he may have to wait for many, many lifetimes... after he finishes this life, he is going to come back to me."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, mentioning that Narada also encounters a cobbler who asks the same question about liberation. Upon asking Narayana, Narada learns that the Brahmin will have to wait many lifetimes, while the cobbler will be liberated in the next life. Narayana instructs Narada to tell them that He was trying to pass an elephant through the eye of a needle.

On his way to Vaikuntha, he also happened to meet a cobbler. The cobbler also asked the same question. If you happen to meet Narayana, please ask him, when will I get my liberation? So Narada Muni went to Vaikuntha. He happened to meet Narayana after meeting him, after paying obeisances, after all that interaction. He asked this question. I met this Brahmin who is engaged in lot of austerities and penances. He has requested me to ask this question, when is he going to get his liberation? So the Lord said, he may have to wait for many, many lifetimes. So he was surprised. Then he asked, by the way, I met the cobbler also. He also asked the same question, when is he going to get his liberation? So he said, after he finishes this life, he is going to come back to me. So Narada was surprised. The cobbler, day in and day out, he is working with leather, leather shoes and all those kind of, not a very quote unquote, a glorious profession. He is going to come the very next life and Brahmin who is chanting mantras, sacrifices, rituals, is going to wait for many, many lifetimes. So he said, I am not able to fathom, I am not able to understand, why so? So he says, when you go back and in case they inquire what the Lord was doing, just tell them, one thing. If they ask what the Lord was doing in Vaikuntha, tell them that the Lord was trying to pass an elephant through a needle, the eye of a needle. So Narada said, okay.

#### Faith vs. Doubt, Conviction in the Lord, The Cobbler's Realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=868
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The Brahmana said, how is that possible? What an absurd thing. How can elephant pass through an eye of a needle?... Such a wonderful Lord. How glorious he is. Anything and everything is possible by him."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, explaining how the Brahmin doubts the Lord's ability to pass an elephant through the eye of a needle, while the cobbler expresses unwavering faith, believing that anything is possible for the Lord. The cobbler uses the example of a banyan tree growing from a tiny seed to illustrate the Lord's limitless power.

So first he went to Brahmana. So he, first question, do you meet Narayana? Yes, yes, I met him. What was Narayana doing there? So he was saying, he was trying to pass an elephant through an eye of a needle. The Brahmana said, how is that possible? What an absurd thing. How can elephant pass through an eye of a needle? No, no, but the Lord was trying to do that. No, no, come on, don't joke. How is it possible? So Narada said, okay. That is his conviction about the Lord. Then he went to the cobbler. The cobbler asked the same question, what was Narayana doing? He said, he was trying to pass an elephant through an eye of a needle. Such a wonderful Lord. How glorious he is. Anything and everything is possible by him. So Narada said, wait a minute. Is it really possible? Do you think the Lord can pass an elephant through an eye of a needle? Is it really possible? He said, why not? You see, I am sitting underneath this banyan tree and it has so many fruits. All of them are falling. Each of them has hundreds of seeds and each seed, has the potency to grow into a full banyan tree. If the Lord can do that, each and every seed has the potential to grow into a whole big banyan tree. I don't think it's difficult for the Lord to pass an elephant through an eye of a needle. My Lord is so great. My Lord is so wonderful. So Narada understood what the Lord meant when he said that he'll come back. So the main important thing is the conviction and faith. And that comes by the blessing and mercy of the Lord.

#### Jnana vs. Vijnana, Realization and Faith, Arjuna's Nishtha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=976
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We can be a big pandit. We could know, verbatim the entire Bhagavad Gita. We can quote all the shlokas. But deep within that conviction, that faith, that the Lord is the supreme controller... May not be there... Vijnana means realization."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that true understanding goes beyond theoretical knowledge (Jnana) and requires realization (Vijnana). One can be a scholar and quote scriptures, but without deep conviction and faith in the Lord, true understanding is lacking. Arjuna possessed this 'nishtha' (steadfast faith).

We can be a big pandit. We could know, verbatim the entire Bhagavad Gita. We can quote all the shlokas. But deep within that conviction, that faith, that the Lord is the supreme controller, He is the cause of all causes, may not be there. Jnana and another thing is called Vijnana. Vijnana means realization. We can have theoretical knowledge but that conviction, that faith, that nishtha, we are blessed with. And that we have to pay a price for that nishtha. And that price, what that price is, we'll discuss as we move forward. So Arjuna had that nishtha. He's saying, yes Krishna, I agree. You are supreme Brahman. You are the supreme pure. You are the absolute truth. I agree with that.

#### Acceptance of Krishna, Envy of God, Understanding Gita

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna, I totally accept as truth all that you have told me... Because deep within, we are envious of God... That's why it is very important that we try to understand Gita not from professional reciters or not read Gita written by anybody and everybody."
**Summary:** The speaker highlights Arjuna's complete acceptance of Krishna's words. He points out that people often feel envious of God. He stresses the importance of understanding the Gita from authentic sources, not from those who lack conviction in Krishna's supreme position.

Krishna, I totally accept as truth all that you have told me. Now you see, many of us when we hear these statements, we feel, you know, why Krishna, Krishna, Krishna, all over Krishna, Krishna. And next few verses Krishna is going to say, I am this, I am that of the mountains, I am the Everest. He is glorifying himself a lot. Because deep within, we are envious of God. I totally accept as truth all that you have told me. Neither the God nor demons, O Lord, know thy personality. Persons of faithless and demoniac nature cannot understand Krishna. He is not even known by the demigods, so what to speak of so-called scholars of the mundane material world. That's why it is very important that we try to understand Gita not from professional reciters or not read Gita written by anybody and everybody. There are many scholars, there are many philosophers, there are many politicians who have written Bhagavad Gita. In their heart, there is no conviction that Krishna is the Supreme Lord. So the original intent of Bhagavad Gita, the transformation Krishna wants to bring about by telling the Gita to Arjuna, that transformation doesn't come about.

#### Authenticity of Knowledge, Analogy of a Mic, Spiritual Ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=1125
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The same words, the same Gita, the same Shloka, apparently the same translation, but there is no change of heart... Likewise, spiritually speaking also, we are born ignorant of these realities."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a disconnected microphone to illustrate that even if the words of the Gita are recited or translated, without the proper connection (conviction and faith), there will be no transformation of the heart. He emphasizes that we are born spiritually ignorant and need education.

In other words, this mic has a wire, this electrical signal getting transmitted because it's connected to the amplifier and the power source. The same wire, if you disconnect it, looks the same wire, but it won't work. If I unplug this mic from the amplifier, it won't work. It looks the same wire. The same words, the same Gita, the same Shloka, apparently the same translation, but there is no change of heart. So now, generally we see children going to school. Every parent, as the children grow up, we send them to kindergartens, nursery and then school because we understand the importance of education. We all are born ignorant. We need to get educated. Likewise, spiritually speaking, spiritually speaking also, we are born ignorant of these realities. That's why our Shastra says, especially in human form of life, Tamasi Ma Jyotir Gamaya. Tamas of darkness. Tamas of ignorance. Tamasi Ma, give up and Jyotir Gamaya. Come to light, come to knowledge.

#### Importance of Gita, Timing of Study, Gita as a Manual
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=1215
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Gita is a very important book... Actually, Gita is like a manual... the best time to refer to manual is, what is the best time? Before you even install the product. Before you even start using the product."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the common tendency to keep the Gita sealed and unread until retirement. He argues that the Gita is like a manual for life and should be consulted before starting life's journey, just as one reads a product manual before using it.

And many times people say, Yes, Swamiji, what you are saying is true. Gita is a very important book. Last time we discussed, everyone has, how many of us have Gita in the house? In Indian's house, the biggest challenge we face when we do book distribution, Swamiji, we have it. We have it. Where is it? In the puja room. Where is it? It is sealed and kept so that no pest should come, no nothing should touch it. And the belief is that everything has a time. Yes or no? And when will the time for Gita be read? When will it be? Post-retirement. When the grandchildren settle, they get married and get married. And, you know, everything is all settled. Then is the time when you can peacefully read Gita. Actually, Gita is like a manual. It guides us how we should go and lead our lives. Just like when we buy a product, the best time to refer to manual is, what is the best time? Before you even install the product, before you even start using the product. Because, if you don't refer to manual, quite possible that you may not utilize the product efficiently or effectively.

#### Importance of Early Education, Analogy of Aristotle, Purpose of Life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=1331
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "You're already five years late... this knowledge about the Lord, about who we are, what is the purpose of life... should be exposed to right from the very beginning."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of early spiritual education, drawing an analogy to Aristotle's advice to a mother who was hesitant to enroll her five-year-old child in school. He argues that knowledge about the Lord, the purpose of life, and genuine success should be imparted from a young age.

Because every time, as you can see, the essential part of learning is you strive as long as possible. You have to keep a clear vision before you. It's my dream that he should get educated in your school. He said, very good. Please admit him. He says, but Aristotle, I have a dilemma. I have a doubt. So Aristotle wondered, what's that doubt? He says, I'm just wondering, is this the right age? He's barely five years old. Is it the right age for him to get admitted? Is it too young for getting trained under you? So Aristotle said, rush, lady, rush. You're already five years late. Just like as parents, we realize that we don't even wait for first standard. Am I right? These days, even you have pre-play school, what is that? Pre-play school. Toddlers and all kinds of play schools. So we want the child at the very youngest, three years, to start getting trained. So likewise, this knowledge about the Lord, about who we are, what is the purpose of life, what is the meaningful thing which we should strive to achieve, what is genuine success, this all knowledge which is there in the Gita should be exposed to right from the very beginning.

#### Parampara System, Disciplic Succession, Distortion of Knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/goTACIX3zuw?t=1443
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavad Gita should be understood in a parampara system... Parampara is the knowledge is spoken by the Lord. It is delivered as it is to the next person in the chain of disciplic succession... In due course of time, kala means time. It got the original intent, the original message. It got this. It got distorted."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding the Bhagavad Gita through the parampara system, which is the disciplic succession. He explains that knowledge is passed down unchanged from the Lord through a chain of teachers. Over time, however, the original intent can be distorted, necessitating re-establishment of the parampara.

Bhagavad Gita should be understood in a parampara system. As I mentioned, Krishna, when he spoke the Gita, there was an intent, specific message, a specific transformation, a specific insight which Arjuna, he was trying to give to Arjuna. And that message, in truth, In Tattvataha we get when we understand Gita in a parampara system. What is parampara? Parampara is the knowledge is spoken by the Lord. It is delivered as it is to the next person in the chain of disciplic succession, to the next person, to the next person, like that. Just like a letter changes many hands. You put in the post box, it goes to the post office, it gets sorted. Next hand, next hand, next hand. Finally it gets delivered to the person who is the recipient, but it is unchanged. That's why Krishna says, I spoke this knowledge to sun god Vivasvan. Vivasvan spoke to Manu. Manu spoke to Ikshwaku. It was coming in a parampara. In due course of time, kala means time. It got, the original intent, the original message, it got distorted. That's why he says, The same knowledge what he spoke to sun god Vivasvan, who spoke to Manu, Manu spoke to Ikshwaku, was coming in a parampara. But because as it is, it was lost, it got distorted.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Goodness Passion Ignorance  | BG 14.05 - 14.09 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### modes of nature, conditioning, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the living entity comes in contact with nature, he becomes conditioned by these modes."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the three modes of material nature (goodness, passion, and ignorance) and explains how contact with these modes conditions the living entity, contrasting this with the unconditioned state in the spiritual world.

So goodness, passion, and ignorance, based on the teachings of His Divine Grace A. C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. This is text 5 of the 14th chapter. Material nature consists of three modes: goodness, passion, and ignorance. When the living entity comes in contact with nature, he becomes conditioned by these modes. In the spiritual world, in our original state, we are not under the grip or the clutches of these modes.

---
#### influence of modes, individuality, variety
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=60
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He loses his original individuality and he gets goaded by the type of modes influencing him."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on how the modes of nature influence behavior and create variety in tastes and inclinations, even within the same family.

Goodness, passion, and ignorance in Sanskrit is also called Sattva Guna, Raja Guna, and Tamo Guna. So when the living entity comes in contact with this material world, material nature, Prakriti, he gets conditioned. He gets influenced. He loses his original individuality in the sense and he gets, so to say, goaded by the type of modes influencing him. If it is Raja Guna which is predominantly influencing him, he acts in a particular way. If it is Tamo Guna, he acts in another way. So we will see that as we move forward. So the living entity is under the spell. Just like they say, magic is done on him, and the person starts behaving. Black magic is done on him. He starts behaving in a weird way. What have you done to him? So under the spell of the modes. Now, this is a very good example. How all of us have different natures, different inclinations, different likes, different dislikes. Even in a family, somebody likes eating a particular vegetable, another person does not like. Someone likes fruits, someone doesn't like. Do we see that characteristic difference? Even in a family. Somebody likes rock music. Somebody likes pop music. Somebody likes classical music. Different kinds of tastes are there.

---
#### personal experience, variety of tastes, cultural differences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=160
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What is nectar for one person is poison for another."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal anecdote about his college days to illustrate the variety of tastes and preferences, extending the discussion to cultural programs and different forms of music.

What is nectar for the body? What is nectar for one person is poison for another. When I was in college, we were three of us sharing one room. And one of my friends, we were sharing the room. He was from Mumbai. REC, you know, it has students from all states. So he was a great fan of rock music. And I have no clue, no idea, no inclination whatsoever of rock music. And in hostel when you come, you have the freedom, you can play the music the way you want. So he got, he made a, you know, some makeshift sound system with that. You know, you have that matkas. You put the speaker inverted and you know, so he made that and he would really start playing that heavy metal. Anyone has heard of heavy metal? I mean, sometimes you wonder how it is called music. What is there to appreciate? But there are people who are so fond of it. So how people have so much variety, varieties of taste. For example, now classical music, not everyone likes it. Somehow I am not a good fan of, I don't have taste much for music. And many times we have these cultural programs in Bangalore and then tha, thi, tha, thi, thi, tha. So it doesn't make any sense to me. Sometimes I wonder why, again, with all due respects, I know that I don't have a taste, but sometimes I wonder why are they wasting time? If they have to depict something, let them tell what is happening and at least we can understand what's happening, but the dance is going on and then, you know, the whole lot of, but there is a great science. People are not wasting their time. So how these different tastes are there? Why there is so much variety in this world?

#### printing analogy, modes of nature, color separation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=305
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All this picture is actually made of these four colors only."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of color printing with CMYK (cyan, magenta, yellow, black) to illustrate how the three modes of nature combine to create diverse experiences and inclinations.

People with different inclinations, people with different likes, even in the same family, sometimes children are there, they pester that I want to eat this. Parents, no, it's not good. No, you should not eat it. We know that it is wrong, but still, you cannot avoid. He has that like. He has that taste. So here we have an example that how all the colors what we see printed in different ways, let's say in the form of calendars, brochures, different kinds of leaflets, they all are printed using only four colors. We discussed in the last session, C, M, Y, K, cyan, magenta, black and yellow. Now you see the picture here, it has colorful fruits, different kinds of colors are there, yellow, red, green, purple, all that. All this picture is actually made of these four colors only. In fact, when this is called color separation, plates are made, some of you may be familiar with the printing technology, plates are made, and when the paper passes through a roller,

---
#### printing analogy, modes of nature, variety of experience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=460
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The density of dots and the thickness of the dots, that determines different colors."
**Summary:** Continuing the printing analogy, the speaker explains how the density and thickness of CMYK dots create different colors, relating this to how the varying influence of the three modes creates diverse tastes and inclinations.

Now the density of dots and the thickness of the dots, that determines different colors. For example now here, it looks little blackish, so here you have k dots, that is black dots, little more in number, and little more bigger in size. Another example, now this is, this aspect if it is blown up, this is blue color, you see the blue dots are more in number, and these four dots are there, but depending on the size of the dot, how closely they are packed, different colors manifest. Now why I am sharing this with you, I am not trying to teach printing, or share more about what is happening in printing industry. Just like with four colors, you can have unlimited shades of colors, likewise, the influence of these three modes, on our consciousness, can create so many varieties of tastes, inclinations, desires, initiatives. Just like you have yellow, in yellow you have hundreds of yellow shades, in blue you have hundreds of blue shades, likewise, predominantly, somebody may be in sattva guna, but in that also there is variety. Broadly speaking, it is sattva guna, raja guna, and tamo guna, but with these combinations, 80% sattva guna, 16% of raja guna, and 4% of tamo guna, the behavior, inclination, taste is one thing. You change the percentage, you have another variation.

Now, these modes are influencing us, there is a spell on us. Just like these colors, when printed on the paper, leaves a different color impression. The paper looks green, the paper looks bluish. Likewise, these modes, when they influence us, impact our thinking, feeling, willing, our desires, our inclinations, everything. Now, this is another similar example, just to take you further on what we discussed. Now, you see, this is yellow, this image drawn in yellow. Now, this image is yellow plus magenta. It is looking little orangish. So, when the paper passes through these two rollers, it will look something like this. And then, we have the third roller, which is blue color, the image looks something like this. And then, it passes through a black roller, it looks something like this. So, this image is drawn in yellow, magenta and cyan, like that. So, these modes, which are influencing us, depending on which mode is predominant. Now, a question to all of you. A picture is static. You know, I see a picture, it is yellowish, or it is bluish. Is the mode, which is influencing us, the percentage of Satwaguna, Tamoguna, Rajoguna, is it static? Is it fixed? Yes? No? How many of us say, it is fixed? Based on our birth, based on which family, which caste we have taken birth, it is fixed? How many of us say, it is fixed? How many of us say, it varies? How many of us say, it is fixed? How many of us say, it is fixed? How many of us say, it is fixed? How many of us say, it varies? The influence of the mode varies. The influence of the mode, we will cover in the next few verses. It is very dynamic.

#### modes and time, goodness, sinful reactions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=709
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The mode of goodness, being purer than the others, is illuminating and it frees one from sinful reaction."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how the modes are generally associated with different times of the day and describes the mode of goodness as illuminating and freeing one from sinful reactions, while also hinting at a potential danger within goodness itself.

In the morning time, which mode is predominantly influencing us? Satwaguna. As time passes, as the day passes, it is Rajaguna which becomes, in general, in general. Some people will be Rajasik throughout the day, possible. Or, Tamsik throughout the day, possible. Kumbhakarna, six months. So, by and large, morning time is predominantly Satwaguna. Day time predominantly is Rajaguna. And, as the sun sets, darkness, night, it is Tamoguna which sets in. That's why you will see, everywhere, temples open early in the morning. Bars and pubs open late in the evening. You don't have anybody going to bar at 4.30 in the morning. You don't have people going to the temple. No doubt there are some busy temples. Generally, the temple darshan closes by 8, 8.39. O sinless one, the mode of goodness, being purer than the others, is illuminating. And it frees one from sinful reaction. If we are under the influence of Satwaguna, or mode of goodness, the symptom, the effect, the spell of that mode on us will be, it will be illuminating. We'll have knowledge. We'll be able to see things, relatively more clearer. See things as they are, relatively speaking, compared to the influence of other modes. And it frees one from sinful reactions. Other modes are very entangling. If suppose somebody is in Tamoguna or Rajoguna, he is goaded, he is forced, he is impelled to act sinfully and gets entangled. Those situated in modes, in that mode, that is mode of goodness, develop knowledge. But they become conditioned by the concept of happiness. There's a danger of even mode of goodness. We'll talk about it as we move forward. Although it is better than Rajoguna and Tamoguna, but still, there is a danger.

#### modes and species, wisdom, simple living
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=876
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The effect of developing the mode of goodness in the material world is that one becomes wiser than those otherwise conditioned."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how the modes influence the variety of life forms and highlights the wisdom and regulated lifestyle associated with the mode of goodness, emphasizing simple living and high thinking.

The living entities conditioned by material nature are of various types. That's how we have 4 lakh species. Out of 84 lakhs, 4 lakhs species, different types, it is based on the influence of these modes. The effect of developing the mode of goodness in the material world is that one becomes wiser than those otherwise conditioned. So who is among the three, who is the wisest? Satwaguna, Rajoguna and Tamoguna. Who is the wisest? Satwaguna. Who is influenced by Satwaguna? Next? Rajoguna. Next? Tamoguna. A man in mode of goodness is not so much affected by material miseries as he has a sense of advancement in material knowledge. So he is in knowledge. He is under the influence of Satwaguna. He is relatively regulated. He has less greed, hankering, bombarded with various desires. Relatively that element is subdued. One is more or less free from sinful reactions. His life is simple, simple living, high thinking. That becomes the mode of living. He will eat Satvik food. The lifestyle will be, be good, do good. Do not unnecessarily trouble others. That is his mindset for a person who is predominantly in Satwaguna. In the Vedic literature, it is said that the mode of goodness means greater knowledge and greater sense of happiness.

#### happiness, spirituality, mode of goodness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=1010

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the understanding of happiness in the modes of passion and goodness, asserting that true enjoyment requires intelligence and that spiritualists also experience happiness, albeit of a different kind.

Compared to other modes, there is a greater sense of happiness. Sometimes people think, especially those who are in passion, they think happiness means, you know, you have to really squeeze out happiness. Actually, to enjoy life also requires very fine intelligence. Sometimes people think that spirituality means, spiritual, those who are practicing spirituality or spiritualist means, they have rejected happiness or they are against happiness. What's wrong? They say, what's wrong in enjoying life? What's wrong? Have you gone mad? What's wrong? In fact, a pure spiritualist also will think, what's wrong? What is day for a materialist, is a night for a spiritualist. What is night for a materialist, is a day for a spiritualist. So even the spiritualist is enjoying happiness, but a different kind of happiness. And in fact, in passion, a person who is in Rajoguna, he thinks that he is happy, but that very happiness actually is very entangling. It's illusory. It's momentary. It's fleeting. It is not satisfying. It brings in more and more hankering, more and more desires, triggers more and more desires. The difficulty is that when the person is situated in mode of goodness, he has a sense of well-being, a sense of satisfaction, sense of, I am now very peacefully and nicely situated. And hence, a person can get carried away. Can anyone help me with an answer? What's wrong if a person has a sense of satisfaction? I am very happy now. He is simple living, high thinking. He is morally, ethically upright. He doesn't trouble anyone. He does his job very ethically. What's wrong with that kind of a mindset, if one thinks that that is what life is? What's wrong?

So, there are people who say that, Yes, I don't trouble anyone. I help others. I am an orphan. I do this. I donate. We have a camp. I don't believe in God. #### purpose of creation, reviving relationship, danger of goodness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=1330
**Verse References:** BG 7.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This creation is made where an opportunity is given for a person to revive that relationship."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the material world is designed to provide an opportunity to revive our relationship with the Lord and that even a person in the mode of goodness can miss this point by trying to settle down peacefully in a place not meant for that.

This world is not designed for that purpose. That's why all our attempts to settle down peacefully gets frustrated. Yes or no? We make attempts to settle down, to have permanence, to have permanent relationships, to have good health always. The world is not designed like that. It is not meant for that. A toilet is not meant where you can go down and settle down comfortably. It's meant for you to do something and come out. Finish your business and come out. So, this world is designed for a purpose. And the purpose is that we have messed up our life, completely forgetful of our relationship with the Lord. And this creation is made where an opportunity is given for a person to revive that relationship. bahunam janmanam ante gyanvan mam prapadyante vasudevam sarvam iti sa mahatma su-durlabhah. So, a person who is in goodness, can miss that point. He can try to settle down peacefully in a place which is not meant to settle down peacefully. Not designed to settle down peacefully.

#### destination, illusion, modes as chains
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=1421
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Chain is a chain. If a person is bound, it doesn't matter what is the chain which is binding him."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of driving to a destination to illustrate how one can become so focused on avoiding problems that they forget the purpose of life. He then compares the modes to different types of chains, emphasizing that even the 'golden chain' of goodness is still a form of bondage.

He is not absolved of the real challenges or real problems of material existence which is janma, mrityu, jara, vyadhi. He is not absolved of. A person, let's say he is a philosopher, he is a scientist, he is very knowledgeable, he is very, he is not into all this socializing, drinking, boozing. He is simple, simple lifestyle. He is completely absorbed in his work. He is not passionate. He is just minding his own business. But he can miss the main point. So, that's the danger. Now, a person may be so focused in riding smoothly. Let's say we have a car and a person is so focused that he should not meet with an accident, he should not violate any traffic law and he is driving so smoothly but he can forget the destination. He can go in circles. You are driving, you are driving for a purpose to go from point A to point B. What is the point in just driving, having a smooth ride and not going to the destination? So, that can happen in mode of goodness. Due to illusion of material energy, one thinks that sort of life is pleasant. I am happy. I am contented. So, example is given. If mode of ignorance is compared to an iron chain, passion is compared to a silver chain, goodness is compared to a gold chain. Chain is a chain. If a person is bound, it doesn't matter what is the chain which is binding him. So, Satva Guna is, relatively speaking, a costlier chain, a better chain, a more expensive chain, but it is still entangling.

**Summary:** The speaker transitions to discussing the mode of passion, highlighting its connection to unlimited desires and the entanglement in material fruitive activities that results from it.

The mode of passion is born of unlimited desires and longings. O son of Kunti! And because of this, one is bound to material fruitive activities. Now, we see lot of passion, ye bhi karna, ye bhi karna, ye bhi karna, ye bhi karna, ye bhi karna, so many desires. It is because of mode of passion. Unbounded desires. It goads a person, never satisfied. And because of this, one is bound to material fruitive activities. He entangles himself in innumerable material activities. Actually, you know, we all have to work. No one can sit idle. It is not recommended. It is not recommended also. We all have to earn money. For that, we have to get educated. But, it is a means to an end. When a person has an ignorance and passion, that means becomes an? Means become an end. That is the danger. For a person, for a person who is very passionate, the means become an end. The mode of passion is characterized by intense attraction between man and woman. So, passion incites lust. For sense gratification, a man in the mode of passion wants honor in the society and wants to have a happy family with nice children, wife, house. He wants to make nice arrangements. Palace shell house, good social circle, people will come, will glorify, wow. They may not say, but then, that appreciation, he is hankering for that.

#### material enjoyment, hope and chase, fleeting fulfillment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=1717
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The nature of material enjoyment is, predominantly lot of time gets spent in these four stages. And fulfilment is very, very fleeting."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the stages of material enjoyment (hoping, chasing, capturing, indulging, and fulfillment), emphasizing that fulfillment is fleeting and leads to more desires.

So, all of us have heard of this, this famous, we have heard in different places. So, we all are, actually there is a template in our mind. We are hoping for enjoyment. What is the second stage after we hope? We chase. Just like here we have, there is a hope that I will get this carrot and it is running, chasing. Then what happens? Next stage, hoping, chasing, then capture. You get it. And fourth stage is indulge. You try to indulge in it. And fifth stage is fulfilment. The nature of material enjoyment is, predominantly lot of time gets spent in these four stages. And fulfilment is very, very fleeting. Very, very temporary. It does not satisfy. That is how, when we get something, suddenly we have ten more desires which are triggered. Hoping, chasing, capturing, indulging and then fulfilment. Because that fulfilment is missing, constantly we are hoping, desiring, chasing, the life becomes a chase.

Intense desires. Always wanting more, more, more and more. Hoping that, yes, I will get fulfilled. But fulfilment, real fulfilment is missing. Modern civilization is considered to be advanced in the standard of mode of passion. Present civilization is extremely passionate. There is lot of passion in the air. #### pavarga, materialistic life, dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-Wb_rWgHqww?t=1900
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dharma hi apavargasya, that which negates this materialistic life of Pavarga."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of 'pavarga' (parishram, phena, bandhan, bhaya, mriti) as representing the characteristics of materialistic life and defines dharma as that which negates pavarga.

We all have heard of pavarga, pavarga in pa, pha, bha, bha, ma. Each of this word, represents something. Dharmasya hi, what is dharma? What is true religious principles? What are they trying to achieve? Dharmasya hi apavargasya. Apavarga means, it's nullify, negates pavarga. What is pavarga? Pa means, anyone can help me with an answer. Pa means what? Parishram. Intense hard work. You see, characterized by in today's world, intense hard work. There was a lady who was regularly attending our Gita live session, very regular. Both husband, wife, I don't want to name now. And for last four months, she has not been coming. So I asked her, I spoke to her, I said, you have been sometimes, you know, there could be different reasons. So I asked her, I don't see you now, last four, five months, you have not been regularly coming. So she says, Prabhuji, I go to the office at 8 am in the morning, come back at 11 pm. She says, 16 hours, 14 hours, it becomes the norm. The whole culture in the organization is like that. The boss stays there, and he expects all his subordinates also to be there in the office. Then she says, even on Saturday, we have to go. And she is really, she says, whole work-life balance, everything has gone for a toss. She says, I am looking for a change. So, Parishrama, Pa, Parishrama. Phena means, you know, high blood pressure, tension, anxiety, completely out of balance. Per-per. Per-per means, per. Bandhan, you are right, bandhan, a person becomes entangled. Bhaya, fear, a person is working hard but there is an insecurity, there is anxiety. And Ma, Ma stands for Mriti. This Per-Per-Bhar-Bharma characterizes our life. We have intense hard work, Parishrama, then we have Phena, we froth, anxiety, stress, hypertension, blood pressure. And then we have Bh, Bandhan, entangled. That very activity which is meant to give us pleasure, satisfy us, entangles us. Bhaya, always fearful. And Ma, we lead that life and that ends in Ma, Mriti. So what is Dharma? Dharma hi apavargasya, that which negates this materialistic life of Pavarga.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### HOW TO BEGIN THE DAY? | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### auspiciousness, spiritual practice, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So it is very important that we start our day with early morning victory."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa welcomes the audience and introduces the topic of how to begin the day in a way that leads to a positive and productive experience.

Hare Krishna, a warm welcome to all of you for today's Geeta Live session. The topic for the day is how to start or begin our day. The way we begin our day, there's a saying, "Well begun is half done." So it is very important that we start our day with early morning victory. We start the day in a right way so that throughout the day it is a good day, good, pleasant, and a positive day. A lot depends on how we go about starting our day. So before we get into the discussion, let us all chant few prayers together. Let's seek the blessings of Srila Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Shri Krishna to bless us so that we can benefit from the insights which they have so wonderfully shared with us in their writings, in their teachings, on the basis of which we will be covering today's topic.

Nama Om Vishnupadaya, Krishna preshtaya bhutale, Srimate Bhaktivedanta, Svamin itinamine, Namaste Saraswate Deve, Gauravani Pracharine, Nirvishesha Shunyavadi, Pasyatya Deshatarine, Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya, Prabhu Nityananda, Priy Advaita Gadadhara, Shiva Sadhi Gaurabhakta Vrinda, Namo Mahavadanya, Krishna Prema Pradayate, Krishna Ya Krishna Chaitanya, Namne Gauratushe Nama, Namo Brahmne Devaya, Go Bramhne Hitaya Cha, Jagad Hitaaya Krishnaya, Govindaya, Namo Namah. Hey Krishna, Karuna, Sindhu, Dina Bandhu, Jagatpate, Gopesha, Gopika, Kanta, Radha, Kanta, Namostute, Tapta Kanchan, Gaurangi, Radhe, Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi, Pranamami Hari Priye Vanchya Kalpat Rubhyascha, Kripa Sindhu Bhya Evacha Patitanam Pavnebhyo, Vaishnavebhyo, Namo Namaha Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare

---
#### auspiciousness, Krishna consciousness, spiritual activities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=239
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But according to scriptures, the most auspicious is that activity which brings us closer to Krishna or to God."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to outline three ways to start the day well, emphasizing that true auspiciousness lies in activities that bring one closer to Krishna, contrasting this with the common focus on mundane auspiciousness.

So there are many ways, there are many things which we can do to start the day well. But we will try to cover three of those many important ways. So in other words, there are many other things which can be done, which can be incorporated in our morning schedule. But we will try to cover three of them today, need not be the most important. The first aspect which is very very important for us to keep in mind, to start the day on a good note. Many of us are very conscious of Shubha. Shubha is a very important thing. Shubha and Ashubha, auspiciousness and inauspiciousness. When people go for a walk, suddenly they find the cat passing the way, they stop. It is considered as inauspicious. Recently there was Dhanteras and I was told that in Jaipur there is one temple where lines and lies of people lined up with new cars, wanting to get the prayers done so that they can have auspicious beginning with the new car. So everyone is concerned about Shubha and Ashubha. Our scriptures give us a very nice perspective of what is auspicious and what is inauspicious. In one sense, the so-called inauspiciousness, there is a lot of auspiciousness in this world and so-called inauspiciousness. Shubha and Ashubha, our scriptures say, it is in one sense Manodharma. It is a mental concoction. We brandish something as Shubha and we label something as Ashubha. But according to scriptures, Shubha, the most auspicious is that activity which brings us closer to Krishna or to God, that activity which makes us remember God. And what is Ashubha? What is inauspiciousness? That activity which takes us away from God, which makes us forget God. Even the so-called pious activity, if it makes us forget God or takes us away from God, it's inauspicious, but it is Ashubha. So with this definition in mind, with this understanding in mind, with this perspective, the best way to start our day is engaging in spiritual activities, which takes us closer to Krishna.

#### prayer, Mangal Aarti, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=435
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The first thing which we should do, no doubt when we get up, we can always, morning ablutions, washing our face, brushing our teeth, immediately after that, we should offer prayers."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the first way to start the day well: offering prayers. He suggests offering prayers immediately after morning ablutions and describes the Mangal Aarti performed in ISKCON temples, providing links to download versions of the prayers.

So one very, very important thing. In fact, the first thing which we should do, no doubt when we get up, we can always, morning ablutions, washing our face, brushing our teeth, immediately after that, we should offer prayers. In the morning, after taking bath, in all ISKCON temples, we have something called as the morning prayers, Mangal Aarti. Generally it happens at 4.15 am. In the description of this video today, I have shared with you a link where you can download three versions of this Mangal Aarti prayers. Each one of you can also do this Mangal Aarti prayers at your place. Just play the audio. It is very nicely done. We have the intention of keeping this purpose in mind. We have many folk hostels all over the city, in different cities in India and even outside where young boys stay and we use this audio. The audio is played. It is a lead and follow method. It also has devotees who are playing Mridanga, Kartal. So the Mangala Aarti. These prayers are glorifying the spiritual master Srila Prabhupada. That's the best way to start the day where we offer our prayers to the spiritual master. If possible you can also do a small Aarti. It could be as simple as offering some incense or offering a small lamp or offering some flowers. So there are three versions. One version is a full version which is for about 37 minutes depending on how much time you have in the morning because everyone is differently placed with different schedules, with different commitments. So we have a full version which is 37 minutes which has the Mangala Aarti, which has Guru Puja, which has Tulsi Aarti, which has Narasimha Puja and ends with Kirtan.

#### prayer, chanting, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=575
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So we chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, we offer these prayers and to begin the day offering prayers, chanting the holy names of the Lord is the best way to start the day."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing the different versions of the Mangal Aarti audio and emphasizes the importance of chanting the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra as a prayer to engage in the service of Krishna.

There is a shorter version which has a full version which is 37 minutes which has the Mangala Aarti, which is for 19 minutes in which the Guru Puja is not there, the Tulsi Puja is there, Narasimha Aarti is there and the Mangala Aarti is there. And there is another version which is the Guru Puja and the Kirtan which is for 12 minutes. Guru Puja is offering prayers to the lotus feet of the spiritual master. So you can download these prayers, the audios, it's in mp3 format and you can use them to start the day by offering the Mangala Aarti. I have also shared with you in the description below a link of the prayers which are there in the Bhagavatam of exalted devotees like Prahlad Maharaj, like Dhruva Maharaj, like Kunti Maharani. They are being nicely rendered and chanted by the devotees with some nice melodious background music, very soothing. You can also play these audios when you get up in the morning while you are getting ready for the work. You can play these audios, Purush Sukta prayers. You can have these prayers. It will create all auspiciousness in the home. It will create a very nice spiritual environment. And for sure, yes, after we do these prayers, we can do the most important prayer which is chanting the holy names of the lord. Chanting is also a prayer and the prayer is, O Lord Shri Krishna, O energy of Krishna, please engage me in your service. Right now, we are engaged in service of Maya, service of our own mind, service of our own senses. We engage in the service of false masters. We cannot avoid service. And this Hare Krishna Maha Mantra is prayer to Lord Sri Krishna and His energy to engage us in His Seva, in His service. So we chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, we offer these prayers and to begin the day offering prayers, chanting the holy names of the Lord is the best way to start the day.

#### prayer, gratitude, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=727
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The best is that we simply offer prayers glorifying the Lord, following the footsteps of the prayers which are offered by great devotees of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of offering prayers that glorify the Lord, following the example of great devotees like Prahlad Maharaj and Kunti Maharani, rather than asking for personal favors.

In fact the first thing we should try to do before we get caught up with our other duties, other responsibilities, we should do this most important duty. They are saying the most important thing is to offer prayers. Apologies for the technical hitch. So as I mentioned, the best way to start the day is to offer these prayers. And these prayers I have shared the link with you, all of you can download and benefit from these prayers. I also mentioned that many times we offer prayers which is more of asking favours from the Lord. We all have some challenges which we are encountering in life, some difficulties, some rough phase and the prayer is all about seeking the blessings of the Lord so that we can tide through those difficult times. The best is that we simply offer prayers glorifying the Lord, following the footsteps of the prayers which are offered by great devotees of the Lord. That's why it is best that we can take example, we can learn from the prayers which are mentioned in the Srimad Bhagavatam. Prahlad Maharaj is offering prayers, Bhishma is offering prayers, Dhruva Maharaj is offering prayers, Ambarish Maharaj is offering prayers, Gajendra is offering prayers, Kunti Maharani is offering prayers and we can learn from such prayers as to what are the prayers which we should offer. What are the prayers which will please the Lord? What are the prayers which will bring us closer to the Lord? And when the Lord is pleased, all our material needs also are automatically taken care of.

Lord is all-knowing, He is there in our heart. He is a well-wisher of the devotees, in fact for everyone. Once there was a king who was going around in his kingdom in a procession. other people who were there with an umbrella on top of his head in his entourage they stopped and one of them removed the shoe and saw that the sole had fallen off and now that's this is they're quite at a far distance from the palace they thought that it is best if you could somehow do some repairs so, they tried looking for a cobbler and luckily they found a cobbler nearby they rushed to him and gave him the shoe in the meantime they requested the king to be seated and the cobbler quickly repaired the shoe and it was ready so the minister asked him the king was seated very close to that place opportunity it's okay but the king insisted no no please take something so the cobbler felt very embarrassed he said the king himself has come it's a small opportunity just two paisa worth job he has done he has just stitched something how can he ask something from the king so seeing that the king was insisting that he should take something he told the king whatever you want you can give job so the king looked at the minister and said give him two villages the cobbler could have asked the king give me two annas which according to him is a very big thing and then the king said the king would have given the fox whatever he had the king replied he would have given the fox but he said whatever you fish whatever you want you can give and the king gave him two villages much much much much more than what he could have even dreamt of so even the Lord who is omnipotent who is a well-wisher he actually wants to give us some benediction which is much much greater than which we raise us up for we glad that is what we have done he said so that he could have everybody return and this was it so it means that the king gave him two villages so it means that he could have everyone who grieve for #### prayer, humility, divine will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1084
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We seek out of ignorance because we don't know what is our real self-interest, what is our real good and at times out of ignorance we ask for some petty things."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that we often ask for small things out of ignorance, not knowing what is truly good for us, and it is better to offer general prayers of glorification and gratitude.

We seek out of ignorance because we don't know what is our real self-interest, what is our real good and at times out of ignorance we ask for some petty things. So best is when we offer prayers, we keep it general. It should be more of glorifying the Lord, thanking the Lord, expressing our gratitude and not be very specific.

---
#### prayer, surrender, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1120
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "O Lord, if you so desire, let me pass this exam."
**Summary:** The speaker suggests that if we are tempted to ask for specific benedictions, we should add the caveat, "If you so desire," acknowledging the Lord's higher plans and well-wishing nature.

In fact, I have shared this in one of our earlier sessions. In case we get tempted to ask specific prayers, let's say throughout the year you have been preparing for an exam, a critical, important exam. And today is the day, the most important day where it's a make or break day. If you clear the exam, it takes you to a next level and it's very important for you to clear the exam and you approach the Lord and pray to the Lord. If you are specific in offering prayers, a saving grace is we can ask the Lord after we have asked specific benediction, if you so desire. O Lord, if you so desire, let me pass this exam, because the Lord is our well-wisher. Many times what we think is the best for us may not be the best for us. Maybe the Lord has higher plans, better plans, something better in store for us. So we don't be specific in our prayers. We follow in the footsteps of great devotees. We take, we learn from the prayers what they have offered. I have also shared with you all a link in the description of this video, some authorized prayers. In fact, there are three links, some authorized prayers. One link, one PDF is a compilation of 21 authorized prayers from different authorized scriptures like Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, prayers to spiritual master, prayers to the Lord. So there are 21 prayers. So you can keep a printout with you and you can recite those prayers looking at the printout. And in due course of time, few days, you will be able to memorize those prayers. And then you don't have to have a printout. You can fold your hands and you can offer the prayers. You can offer the Sanskrit part of it, the shlokas, or you can even offer the translation, the meaning of that prayers. Best is if you can do both the translation and the original prayer, the verse. And if you find it difficult to pronounce the Sanskrit shlokas, you can just offer the translation part of it. There is a shorter version also, another PDF which has about 8 to 10 prayers, simple prayers, two line, three line prayers. You can also take a printout and use those as prayers which we can offer to the Lord.

**Summary:** The speaker summarizes the first point: starting the day with prayer, including listening to prayers, offering a small aarti, reciting authorized prayers, and chanting the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra, emphasizing the benefits of remembering, thanking, and glorifying the Lord.

To sum up, the first point, the first aspect, start the day with prayer. We can, while we are getting up, while we are getting ready, we can always put the prayers which are playing as like Dhruva Maharaj prayers, Kunti Maharaj. I have shared with you the audio links. And once we are ready, we can fold our hands, go in front of the Lord and do a small aarti. We can have the Mangal Aarti audio track. We can offer that aarti to the Lord. We can offer some prayers. I have shared authorized prayers, 21 prayers or the shorter version of that. And when we offer these prayers, we will get an idea of what kind of prayers we should offer. And after offering these prayers, the Mangal Aarti, we can always offer the most important prayer, which is to chant the holy names of the Lord, to chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra. #### attitude, devotion, spiritual life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1385
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "While we are becoming devotees, it is very important that we have the right attitude."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second aspect of starting the day well: cultivating the right attitude. He uses the analogy of honey bees and houseflies in a garden to illustrate the importance of focusing on the positive aspects of life and spiritual practice.

The second aspect which I wanted to cover, which is again very very important, is that in life, we will have good times, we will have bad times. We will come across good people, we will come across bad people, cruel people. Prabhupada gives an example, if you walk in a garden, typically you will find two kinds of plies, or insects. One is the honey bee. It moves around in the garden, hovers around the flowers, and collects the nectar from different flowers, goes flower to flower and connects the nectar. So that's the honey bee. In the same garden, there is another kind of fly, the house fly, which looks for filth and garbage in the garden and goes and hovers around it. Where is stool, where is filth, where is garbage, and you will find that house fly hovering around it. Now, in every garden, even if it is well maintained, there is some crap, there is some garbage, maybe in one corner, piled together, there is also some nice, beautiful, aromatic flowers blossoming. And there are two kinds of flies. The honey bee, which focuses on the aroma, the flowers which are blossoming, and there is another kind of fly which is this. So, while we are becoming devotees, it is very important that we have the right attitude.
