#### attitude, negativity, spiritual growth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1490
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We can be overly focused on the problems and challenges which I have to encounter in my spiritual life."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of exemplary behavior and the tendency to have a negative outlook on life, focusing on problems and challenges rather than blessings.

Our behaviour is exemplary. Prabhupada said, people will judge me, people will judge your spiritual master, seeing your behaviour. So, many times, by default, because of our upbringing or because of conditioning, we can have a very negative outlook to life. We can be overly focused on what is not okay. We can be overly focused on the problems and challenges which I have to encounter in my spiritual life. And as a result, we can be always complaining, blaming, playing the victim card. So, in the morning, I had also shared in the previous session that on an average day, an average person thinks 60,000 thoughts. And most of the thoughts are random and unnecessary. And they have also figured out that around 77%, on an average, 77% of the thoughts are negative and counterproductive. So, in other words, we invariably, unless we proactively work on ourselves, we invariably have a tendency to get focused on what is not okay, what is, some things which are negative, some things, the challenges which we are going through. We get focused on that. And all the blessings, all the good things in life, we take it for granted.

---
#### gratitude, attitude, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1621
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you develop an attitude of gratitude, you will always find there is so much more to be grateful for."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second principle: cultivating an attitude of gratitude. He shares his personal practice of listing the wonderful things the Lord has blessed him with, emphasizing that gratitude leads to recognizing more blessings.

So, the second principle which is very, very important, which we should definitely try to cultivate, and the principle is attitude of gratitude. I repeat. Attitude of gratitude. I'll tell you about my own life in the morning, my own schedule. In the morning when I get up, even before taking bath, I sit near the window, fresh air, and I have made list of wonderful things which Lord has blessed me in my life. And that list keeps expanding. For example, in my life, I am blessed to have Shila Prabhupada as my spiritual master. I am blessed to have opportunity to chant the holy names of Krishna daily. I am blessed to have the knowledge of Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam. I am blessed to have nice prasadam to eat. There are lot of people dying of starvation. They may have food to eat, but here I have opportunity to honour daily sanctified prasadam, which is offered to the Lord, the remnants of that food, which is spiritually uplifting. I am blessed to have that prasadam. I am blessed to have nice parents. I am blessed to live in association of devotees. I am blessed to have nice opportunity to grow in spiritual life. So I have listed down lot of such points, because suddenly if you start thinking, you know, you may forget something. So this list keeps expanding. If you develop an attitude of gratitude, you will always find there is so much more to be grateful for. The reverse is also true. If we get into a negative cycle, if we start complaining, blaming, playing the victim game, then we will have more opportunities to play that game.

---
#### gratitude, pranayama, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1776
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Even if there are some negative things happening in your life, the good things in your life which you are counting on a daily basis, count your blessings, it overrides."
**Summary:** The speaker describes his morning routine of combining deep breathing (Pranayama) with contemplating his blessings, explaining how this practice oxygenates the blood, freshens the mind, and cultivates an attitude of gratitude that overrides negativity.

So what I do is I sit near the window with fresh air in the morning. No disturbance. I take a deep breath. And while I hold the breath for about 30 seconds, I mean, I don't keep a track of how long it holds, as much as I can. I just make note of, I just read one point. It's written in a sheet of paper. I am blessed to have Srila Prabhupada as my spiritual master. So I hold the breath. And I feel good about Srila Prabhupada as my spiritual master. I really feel that I am so blessed. I am so happy that I have Prabhupada as my spiritual master. So then I really... So in other words, two things are intertwined here. One is the Pranayama. This is deep breathing because it also oxygenates the blood. You feel completely charged because of that oxygen inflow. Your mind becomes so fresh. And simultaneously, you are counting your blessings. You are developing the attitude of gratitude. If you do this for 10 minutes, you do day after day, day after day, you will feel so blessed. Even if there are some negative things happening in your life, the good things in your life which you are counting on a daily basis, count your blessings, it overrides. Because your attitude has overall taken a transformation. It has become an attitude of gratitude. You always start looking at the good things. You always start, instead of blaming the Lord, you always start thanking the Lord. So I do this and this will gradually help build the attitude of gratitude.

---
#### attitude, fault-finding, introspection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=1906
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When faults in others misguide and delude you, have patience, introspect and find fault in yourself."
**Summary:** The speaker shares quotes from Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakura and Srila Prabhupada, emphasizing the importance of introspection, amending oneself, and avoiding fault-finding, which is described as another fault.

I will read out few quotes of Srila Prabhupada. When faults in other misguide you. This is from Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakura, who is the Guru of Srila Prabhupada. When faults in other misguides you, we see faults in others. There are faults in people, faults in the environment around us, faults in the circumstances we are in. When faults in others misguide and delude you, have patience, introspect and find fault in yourself. Know that others cannot harm you unless you harm yourself. The biggest victory which we can aim for, which we can achieve in life, is victory over our own selves. Victory over our minds, victory over our senses. That's why probably Jesus made this statement, What profit the man if he wins the whole world, but loses his own soul? So, the morning time is predominantly meant for winning over our own self, nourishing the soul, nourishing our mind, nourishing our intellect with spiritual insights, spiritual instructions, spiritual thoughts, reading Bhagavad Gita, reading Srimad Bhagavatam. Then Prabhupada says, Look within, amend yourself, rather than looking into the weaknesses of others. Now, as I told you, looking into weaknesses of others is our default mode. We have got conditioned, we have got habituated. It's difficult to change the conditioning. So, that's why I am saying, cultivate the attitude of gratitude. Start seeing good in others. Then Prabhupada says, Fault finding which is another fault. The very act of fault finding itself is a fault. Just like the flies, they are finding out the soar, and the bees, they are finding out where there is honey. The two animals, they have got two different businesses. Fault finding and collecting the good things. There are two classes. Similarly, there are many people who are simply fault finding. So, how do we change that mode, default mode?

---
#### gratitude, attitude, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oef0Qd292OM?t=2070
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We change that mode by counting our blessings."
**Summary:** The speaker suggests changing the default mode of fault-finding by counting blessings, and he mentions the importance of ending the day with gratitude, recollecting three good things that happened and thanking the Lord.

We all find faults, we all complain, we all blame. How do we change that mode? We change that mode by counting our blessings. I also saw in one of the videos, it is not just how we start our day well, how we begin, it's also how we end our day. How we end our day also has a bearing on how we start our day. Sometimes we are so tired, we just crash into our bed, don't end the day, don't offer the prayers, and just crash into the bed. Quite possible we will also start the day defeated, or in a wrong way. So, there is another video which I saw, where a person is recommending that it is always good in the night before we take rest, we offer prayers, we thank the Lord, and we also count, we also try to recollect. What are three things which I should be grateful for? What I should thank Lord? What has happened today? You know, this arrangement happened, it was so nice. Although there could be many wrong things in the day, many things which were not very pleasant, but try to remember, recollect, or think of three things, three good things which happened to you. And remember the Lord. One thing is we can just thank, be gratitude, but gratitude keeping the Lord in picture. It's because of Him. Today because of Him, we got such nice Prasadam feast. There was a festival, we got opportunity to do nice Kirtan, and we had nice Prasadam. Prasadam feast. The Lord helped me with this wonderful article, which I read, which was very enlightening, gave me very important insights about how to offer prayers. For example, if today you gather something from this video, from this talk, maybe you can end the day thanking the Lord, I got an opportunity to hear this nice talk, in which I got wonderful insights how to start the day. So when we start counting our blessings, we gradually change our attitude to an attitude of gratitude.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### HUME GUSSA KYUN AATA HAI? | HG AMITASANA DAS | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### anger, root cause analysis, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The root cause of anger is anger."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of anger, its root cause, and the plan to explore relevant verses from the Bhagavad Gita, emphasizing the universality of anger and seeking blessings for a meaningful discussion.

Hare Krishna. A warm welcome to all of you for today's Geeta Live session. We have chosen a very, very important topic, a very practical subject which entails practical application of some of the teachings of Bhagavad Geeta. Krishna in the Bhagavad Geeta, in many, many places, talks about anger. So in today's discussion we will touch upon these verses to understand why we get angry in the first place. What's the root cause of anger? In today's world, many times when people have to solve a problem, the first step is root cause analysis. So we will try to understand in today's session what's the root cause of anger. For that matter, we will relate to whatever leads to anger. All are victims of anger. So the root cause of anger is anger. All humans are victims of anger. Every one of us get angry to different degrees in different situations in different circumstances. But the root cause remains the same. What's that? We will understand before we get into the discussion. Let us start with prayers to Srila Prabhupad, to Lord Chaitanya, Lord Shri Krishna and seek Their blessings so that we can have a meaningful discussion and have some very nice takeaways from today's session. So we'll start with the prayers.

---
#### prayers, Vaishnava tradition, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=116
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna Hare Hare, Hare Rama Hare Rama, Rama Rama Hare Hare."
**Summary:** The speaker leads the audience in chanting prayers to Srila Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya, and Lord Krishna, reciting various Vaishnava mantras and seeking their blessings.

So we'll start with the prayers, request all of you to kindly fold your hands if possible and also repeat the prayers as I chant them. Namaste Saraswate Deve, Gauravani Pracharine, Nirvishesha Shunyavadi, Paschatya Deshatarine, Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya, Prabhu Nityananda, Shri Advaita Gadhadhara, Shri Advaitha Gadhadhara, Shri Advaitha Gadhadhara, Shri Advaitha Gadhadhara, Shiva Sadhi Gaurabhakta Vrinda, Namo Mahavadanyaya, Krishna Prema Pradayate, Krishna Ya Krishna Chaitanya, Namne Gauratushenamaha, Namo Brahmane Devaya, Go Brahmane Hitayaja, Jagadhitaya Krishnaya, Govindaya Namo Namaham, He Krishna Karuna Sindhu, Dina Bandhu Jagatpate, Gopesha Gopika Kanta, Radha Kanta Namostute, Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi, Radhe Vrinda Vaneshwari, Vrishabhanu Sute Devi, Tranamami Hari Priye, Vanchakalpat Rubhyasya, Kripa Sindhu Bhyaivacha, Patitanam Pavnebhyo, Vaishnavebhyo, Namo Namaham, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna Hare Hare, Hare Rama Hare Rama, Rama Rama Hare Hare.

#### anger, root cause analysis, legitimate anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=278
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we do root cause analysis, the cause is one."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the four sections of the talk, focusing on the root cause of anger, circumstances where anger is legitimate, quotes from the Bhagavad Gita on anger, and how to control anger.

So today, we will broadly divide the talk into four sections. First, we will talk about the root cause of anger, gussa kyun aata hai. As I mentioned, we all get angry in different circumstances, in different situations. If we do root cause analysis, the cause is one, so we will talk about that in section one. Section two. Section three. Section four. Section five. Section six. Section seven. Section eight. Section nine. Section ten. Section eleven. Section twelve. Section thirteen. Section fourteen. Section fifteen. Section sixteen. Section seventeen. Section eighteen. Section nineteen. Section twenty. circumstances, we should get angry.

But there is something called as legitimate anger. There are situations, there are circumstances in which when we become angry, it's legitimate, it's bona fide, it is required. So that way we can get a sense of relief that many times your anger could be justified. We don't have to feel guilty every time we get angry. Then we'll talk about some more quotes from the Bhagavad Gita. Krishna many, many places in the Gita talks about anger. So we'll talk about what are those verses? What are the Krishna, what is Lord's instructions on this subject? And towards the end, Lord Krishna says, we'll briefly touch how to control our anger. In fact, I plan to have a separate session on some do's and don'ts, how we can manage or how we can control our emotions, how we can act in situations when we helplessly, you know, get irritated, get angry. #### anger, desire, sense objects
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=434
**Verse References:** BG 2.62
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When contemplating the objects of senses... one develops an attachment."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to explain the root cause of anger according to the Bhagavad Gita, specifically verse 2.62, highlighting the process of contemplating sense objects, developing attachment, and the arising of desire.

So, first we'll talk about why people get angry? What's the root cause of anger? So, we will talk from the Bhagavad Gita. This is the 62nd verse from the 7th, from the 2nd chapter of Bhagavad Gita, where Krishna says, dhyayato visheya pumsa sanghaste shupajayate sanghat sanjayate kama kama krodhot abhijayate. Where Krishna is saying, when contemplating the objects of senses, dhyayato visheyan pumsa, visheyan means sense objects. We have 5 senses, the corresponding sense objects. Here there are five senses, Dhyayato visheyan pumsa. When we contemplate objects of the senses for example, eyes aspire to look for beautiful forms, ears look for hearing some melodious sounds and we contemplate on them, desire such contact.

#### lust, desire, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=511
**Verse References:** BG 2.62
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When that kama, when that desires manifest, it is desire."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining the chain of events leading to anger, from attachment to lustful desires, and how anger arises when these desires are not fulfilled or are obstructed.

dhyayato visheyan pumsa… sanga steshupajayate. One develops an attachment. Especially in communicatesnutim ऄद्री धिश्म्मेशे जettes. and develops attachment. Desires develop. And from that attachment, from that contemplation, lust develops. What is lust? We briefly covered. It is desire to enjoy materially, with our material mind and with our senses. Sankhash deshu pajayate attachment develops and from that attachment desire. Sankhat sanjayate kama lustful desires, lusty tendencies manifest. Sankhat sanjayate kama kamat krodhat abhijayate and when that kama, when that desires manifest, it is desire. Sankhat sanjayate kama lustful desires, lusty tendencies manifest. Sankhat sanjayate kama, lusty tendencies manifest. Do not get fulfilled or there is some obstacles in fulfilment of that desire. For example, I have a desire for peace of mind. Now right now I am not touching whether it is right desire or wrong desire, whether it is spiritual desire or material desire. Assuming that I have a desire to enjoy peace of mind, it's Kama and something obstructs that. Somebody makes a sound which irritates me, then anger develops. In other words, we have the sense objects, we develop attachment to them by contemplating on them and from that attachment develops desires, karma and when karma is not fulfilled, when fulfillment of karma, our desires don't happen the way we want to happen, then anger develops. We want things to happen in a particular way, that's our desire. It doesn't happen that way, we feel irritated, we get angry. We want certain people to behave in particular way, if it doesn't happen that way, we get angry. We want situation circumstances to transpire according to my wish and will, it doesn't happen, I get angry.

#### anger, delusion, intelligence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=661
**Verse References:** BG 2.62
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "With that delusion, our memory gets bewildered."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to elaborate on the consequences of anger, explaining how it leads to delusion, loss of memory, and ultimately, loss of intelligence and discrimination.

So this is a very nice analysis which the Lord has done. It's in the second chapter, text number 62. Then what happens? First is attachment, from attachment comes lust, unfulfillment or when the lust is not fulfilled it leads to krodha. Then further leads to, when we become angry, krodha bhawati, some more aha kwoodna, from that anger the delusion arises. Sammoha and Sammohat Smriti Vibhrama. With that delusion, our memory gets bewildered. You know, we have good sense, we know that I should not shout, we know that I should not get angry, we know that I should control my anger, we know that we should listen more, we know that we should control our emotions, but all that good sense is lost. Trodad bhavati sammoha, sammoha Smriti Vibhrama and then Smriti Vibhramsa Buddhinasho, when memory is bewildered, then intelligence is lost. All that fine sense of discrimination, what I should speak, what I should not speak, what I should do and what I should not do. We have many, many examples of people in fits of anger speak certain words, do certain things which rest of the life they regret. We all have some experience of this in that sport of moment when we are emotionally hijacked, we act in certain ways, later on we keep regretting, I shouldn't have done it. Not only in a short while moment that anger comes down, we regret, I shouldn't have done that, why did I do that? At that time we are emotionally hijacked, prodha bhavati sammoha sammoha smriti vibhrama, our memory is lost, from that memory the intelligence, that fine intelligence what we have by which we discriminate, that's gone, we lose that intelligence momentarily and one falls down again into material pool, material consciousness.

If we apply this Kama it means anger, how is this Croda and Kama bond rajo guna samudbhava by mode of fashion so we have three modes we have heard it many times togona rajo guna and t evenly when we are Dom觹hara krodha and Kama born we are very passionate about our food Itis why it is that we became quite in passion, overpowered by mode of passion, Rajoguna, then Kama is born. Lusty desires, that's the breeding ground. When we are overpowered by passion, we will have lot of material desires, intense hankering and Kamaesha, Krodesha and then when this Kama is not being fulfilled, desires, because everything what we desire will not get fulfilled. Sky is the limit. We keep desiring so many things, so many material desires. Kamaesha, Krodesha, this anger which arises non-fulfillment of my material desires. Rajoguna, which is born out of passion, Samudbhava, Mahashanoh, Mahapapa, it's the greatest enemy of the living entity.

#### anger, desire, enemy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HmMIJ9r2mwk?t=916
**Verse References:** BG 3.37
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We have a much bigger enemy, which many of us are not aware of, and that is Kama, Krodha, which is born out of passion, unbridled passion, unbridled material desires."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the greatest enemy is not external but internal: lust and anger born from unbridled passion, which cause suffering when material desires are not fulfilled.

So, we all have a sense of, he is my friend, he is my enemy. We all are, you know, these days, the standoff between India and China. So, we all have a sense of, oh, so and so is our enemy, they have intruded, let's boycott, let's not buy any more Chinese goods. So, we have a sense of friend and enemy. But Krishna is saying, it's the greatest enemy of the living entity. What is that? Kamaesha, Krodesha, Rajoguna, Samudbhava, which is born out of Rajoguna, Mahashanoh, Mahapapa, it is the greatest enemy of the living entity. We are suffering because of this. We are not suffering, not necessarily because Pakistan is intruding or China is intruding. No doubt, we have to protect our sovereignty, our, you know, our territory. We have territorial rights. But each one of us has a sense of, oh, so and so is our enemy. So, we all have a sense of, oh, so and so is our enemy. We have a much bigger enemy, which many of us are not aware of, and that is Kama, Krodha, which is born out of passion, unbridled passion, unbridled material desires. And those desires, when it does not get fulfilled, things don't happen the way I want to happen. When my desire to be peaceful, my desire to be happy is somehow obstructed, things don't happen the way I want to happen, I get angry. It could be with our near and dear ones, it could be with our neighbours, it could be with our friends and relatives. The root cause is Kama, desires, material desires.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that material desires arise from attachment to sense objects and the mode of passion, and that true victory lies in overcoming base qualities like lust, anger, greed, pride, envy, and jealousy.

How these desires are born? Out of contemplation, out of attachment to sense objects. And why this contemplation happens? Why these desires arise? because of Rajoguna, because of mode of passion. So, spirituality is all about, these are all base qualities which we all get, so to say, overpowered with – Kama, Krodha, Lobha, Lust, Anger, Greed, Pride, Envy, Jealousy and all of us have different goals, aspirations in life, we all want to achieve things, we want to lead successful life. Actually, real victory is victory over these base qualities. Real victory is not how much wealth I have amassed. Real victory is not how much name and fame I have amassed. They have people like recent past, Sushant Singh Rajput, famous, you know, he had accumulated lot of wealth, lot of success, a very short age, 34 years. But what really matters is not the money, what we have accumulated. No doubt, I am not saying money is not important, it is important. But real victory, real achievement, real thing which we have to aspire for is victory over these base qualities, over Lust, over Greed, over Pride, over Envy, Jealousy and all of us have accumulated.

envy over anger or jealousy over envy and then and then only we can experience genuine happiness, genuine peace. there are circumstances, there are situations and to the right degree we can become angry. Anger has a place. Every time in a mother chastises his son, it is not necessarily wrong. Every time a teacher chastises a pupil or a child or student for not doing his homework, it's not necessarily wrong. There was earlier a time, I remember when I was young, I remember when I was young, although these days it's a strict no-no, but I remember, you know, in our school there used to be a punishment called caning, especially if a person does something grossly wrong, terribly wrong, he was taken to principal's office and the principal would ask us to put the hands like this and there was a thin cane used to be very painful and would do that, although in present times it is strict no-no, you know, we have a principle, the example of students even filing a case against the parents, against the teachers, against the school. There could be reasons why it is not allowed, but I remember that was a very big deterrent for us to act in wrong ways. Punishment does have a role in our lives to correct ourselves, to put us back on the right path. So Aristotle said to get angry for the right reason, in the right circumstances, in the right circumstances or in the right anyways, this is the point where there is a need for an act of correctness, so here's a possibility to the right degree, it is not easy, it requires fine discrimination, it requires fine intelligence.

anger can be controlled, we cannot stop becoming angry all together but if we simply become angry with those who are angry with us, we cannot stop becoming angry all together but if we simply become angry with those who blaspheme the Lord or his devotees, we control our anger in Krishna consciousness. #### knowledge, understanding, reason

Muslims medru through namaz, madru through some rosas or Ramzan or whatever the festival, it's the same thing, trying to understand the supreme, establish a relationship by prayers, by fasting, by some penances, by some austerities, by offering some, you know, namaz, whatever. #### spiritual life, struggle, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/VGk2COv_MC0?t=987
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Actually genuine spiritual life is a very very difficult path it's like declaring a war on illusory energy"
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that genuine spiritual life is not easy but a difficult path, akin to declaring war on the illusory energy of maya.

Sometime people think spiritual life is easy. They don't have any work, they don't have any family responsibilities, they have a life of rest, come on Sunday and give a speech to the common people, they will give donation and they will live. Actually genuine spiritual life is a very very difficult path. It's like declaring a war on illusory energy. Maya, which is illusory energy of the Lord, is trying to pull us down back in illusion, back into sense gratification and a yogi is constantly trying to battle with those allurements, trying to elevate his consciousness to avoid all the traps which are laid in front of him, gross and subtle.

#### conscious incompetence, real growth, self-control

**Verse References:** —

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### How Do We Choose the Right Bhagavad-gita Commentary Among the Many Available? | Sri Amitasana Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### realization, spiritual authority, gradation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PXu5Bz7NUtA?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To what extent a person is blessed, to what extent a person is realized, to what extent he has understood the absolute truth, varies."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while many people may read the same scriptures or claim to be spiritualists, their understanding and realization of the truth can vary greatly, like different carats of gold.

Many times, multiple persons are reading the same books, same scriptures, but even among them also, they come up with different opinions and conclusions. Why is it so? I think that I am whatever I am doing from this book, I am trying. Very good point. He is saying that Bhagavad Gita also written by different so called scholars or so called spiritualists or so called spiritual masters, they may also differ. So what is the guarantee that what Srila Prabhupada is writing or somebody else is writing, and there is a difference? There is an apparent contradiction. So how do I put faith? So even in spiritual masters, we mentioned about 4 lakh species. Yes or no? Out of 84 lakh. Everyone is different. Everyone is realized to different degree. All are realized. All are self-realized. There are many rich men in Powai, but richness may vary. You can call this person is also rich, that person is also rich, but richness may vary. Mukesh Ambani is also rich. Even Bill Gates is rich. Richness is different. 22 carat gold also is gold. 24 carat gold is gold. 5 carat gold also is gold. Ornaments made from both of them look like gold only. So yes, for a common man, even for people like us, it becomes very difficult to identify what is pure gold, what is 24 carat gold, what is 22 carat gold. Because all are spiritualists, all are talking about God only. But it is a fact that there are gradations. There are gradations. Now here it is saying, because you are gentle, because you are submissive, you have been blessed with all the realizations. Not everyone is blessed with the ultimate realization. They are blessed with some realization. Even I have some realization, but I am not blessed with all the realizations. Even you have been practicing Krishna, you have been blessed with some realization. But there may be difference. To what extent a person is blessed, to what extent a person is realized, to what extent he has understood the absolute truth, varies. And everyone is trying to explain to the best of their ability. What he is seeing, he is describing. He may not be even doing disservice. Sorry, he may not be doing it intentionally. That's what he is able to see. He is describing. Somebody is able to see more, somebody is able to see less. Does that answer your question? So even among spiritualists, even among people who are talking about this thing, there is a difference in terms of depth of their understanding, in terms of their realizations. Now, it is beyond the scope of today's discussion as to how to understand who has more depth.

#### association, influence, learning from negative examples
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Aq8FbtXc0p0?t=24
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In other words, we can learn even from the negatives, how not to be like them."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the story of two brothers, one who became a drunkard and the other an IAS officer, to illustrate how one can learn from negative examples and avoid demoniac tendencies.

Now, a question may come in our mind, why do we need to discuss or understand how do demoniac think? We can as well be thinking about how the saintly people think, what their qualities are. So, once there was a person who was a drunkard, alcoholic. All kinds of bad qualities, you could call, were there in his character. And no doubt the whole family life was in complete chaos. He used to beat his wife. Financially, they were in a very bad state. So, he had two sons. So, they were brought up in these circumstances. So, one of them also became a drunkard. And the second one, he studied quite well and eventually became an IAS officer. So, both these brothers were interviewed. The first one who became a drunkard, he was asked, how did you become a drunkard? Why did you become a drunkard? So, he told that my father was a drunkard. What else do you expect from me? I have all along seen my father drinking, beating my mother, doing all kinds of these things. So, what else do you expect from me? Sangat, Sanjayate, Kama. Based on the association we keep, the kind of people we associate with, that kind of desires, that kind of character we develop. So, what else do you expect from me? And quite in contrast to this brother, the other one became an IAS officer, very well educated. So, he was asked that your brother is a drunkard, your father is a drunkard. How did you, you know, became so successful in life? So, he said, my father was a drunkard. All along, I have seen him drinking. What else do you expect me to become? In other words, we can learn even from the negatives. How not to be like them. So, emphasis is we are going to discuss about how the demoniac think. So, we should not think like them. How do they act? We should not act like them.

So much if we have to organize it well. Now, we see this world with so much of orderliness. Different planets moving in their respective orbits. Has it come by chance? There is no creator. If someone were to tell you that, you know, the waves were tossing the beach and due to the combination of sand particles, over a period of time, over many centuries, they come together and align themselves and by chance, this watch came into being. #### materialism, illusion, meaninglessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Aq8FbtXc0p0?t=413
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So world is empty and meaningless. Whatever you see is the meaning you have given."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts his experience at a Landmark Forum seminar, where the conclusion was that the world is empty and meaningless, contrasting this with the Vaishnava understanding of reality.

It's just come into being. Everything is matter only and the whole cosmos is supposed to be a mass of ignorance. Few years back, I attended a seminar called Landmark Forum. Have you heard of this? It's a very so-called popular, internationally acclaimed workshop called Landmark Forum. Anyone attended Landmark Forum? So it's a three-day workshop, very intense. You go in the morning at 8.30 and till 11 in the night or at times 10 in the night, you are discussing. So at the end of this three days Landmark Forum, finally, the conclusion which is brought about with all the discussions is the world is empty and meaningless. And we are made to emmerite. And all of us in unison together rise up and says this world is empty and meaningless. All big, big educated people, intelligent people, you know, three days, very intense. And you pay 14,000 rupees for those three days. And the rates are increasing year after year. The time I came, you know, somehow somebody convinced me that it's very this thing. And just wanted to understand what is being discussed. So world is empty and meaningless. Whatever you see is the meaning you have given. You have given meaning to the world. This world is empty and meaningless. You have defined this is good, this is bad. It's all your definition. It's all your meaning. Otherwise, the world is empty and meaningless. That's the conclusion. Now, they say that it's just a dream. And we have created just like in a dream. We create so many things. We dream of a palace. We are a king. So this world is also a dream and dream will come to an end. It's not real. This world is not real.

#### material entanglement, atheism, divine direction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Aq8FbtXc0p0?t=531
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Under My direction, this nature is working and creating both moving and non-moving beings."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the idea of the world as a dream with the reality of entanglement and suffering within it. He then discusses the demoniac belief that there is no soul or life after death, contrasting this with Krishna's statement in the Gita that nature works under His direction.

But still, even if they call it a dream, but they are quite busy enjoying the dream. And because of bereft of the knowledge, we are getting more and more implicated in the dream world. More and more entangled. More and more suffering in the so-called dream world. That's a reality. They say that a child is a combination of matter, emulsification of two liquids. And there is no soul. There is no life after death. This is the thinking of demonic people. And unfortunately, many, many people subscribe to these ideas, although they may be apparently religious. They may be going to church. They may go to some temple for worship. But then the right understanding, scientific understanding of God, soul, life after death is not there. It's very ritualistic. They do not believe in the world. And this is what Krishna has revealed. He has said this in the Gita, where Krishna is saying, Mayadyakshena prakriti suyate. Under My direction. Krishna is saying, under My direction, prakriti suyate saa-characharam. Under My direction, this nature is working and creating both moving and non-moving beings.

#### epistemology, human imperfection, sources of knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Aq8FbtXc0p0?t=1168
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We all have intrinsically four defects...imperfect senses, subject to illusion, a tendency to commit mistakes, and a tendency to cheat."
**Summary:** The speaker argues against relying solely on so-called scholars due to the four intrinsic defects of conditioned souls: imperfect senses, proneness to illusion, tendency to make mistakes, and propensity to cheat.

Now, this is true. You will all agree with this. We all have intrinsically four defects. What are the four defects we have? Be it the most... Most knowledgeable person in this world, most erudite, most brilliant person, a noble laureate, he will also have these four intrinsic defects. These are called defects of a conditioned soul. And the four defects are, the four imperfections are imperfect senses. Our senses are imperfect. And we have knowledge acquiring senses. We are acquiring knowledge from... We are acquiring knowledge from the senses. And that's how we have built a world view. But the senses themselves are imperfect. To give you an example, a merchant who is trying to be honest, ethical, and not cheat his customers, but what if the weights what he is using have some defect, are imperfect. Then even though his intention is to be... Not cheat the customer, he ends up cheating the customer because the weights are imperfect. The many people are involved in perceiving the world through the senses and getting a world view, they are not necessarily... They are trying their best, but intrinsically our senses are imperfect. Now, I will give you examples. Pramodha, we are subject to illusion. This is the second defect. All of us are subject to illusion. This is the second defect. All of us are subject to illusion. Third, we have a tendency to commit mistakes. 3. keeps suffering. Third, we have a tendency to commit mistakes. Third, we have a tendency to commit mistakes. That's why it says to, earth is humans. And fourth, we have a tendency to cheat. Any conditioned soul, the degree may vary. Somebody may have huge, big... He may be a big cheater. Somebody may be so called ethical. I don't bribe. I don't cheat. I don't lie. I'm very honest. I'm truthful. I'm Mythical. I follow all the laws degrees may vary but all these four defects are there in a conditioned soul

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### How to Live Harmoniously with Devotees | Sri Amitasana Dasa | SB 6.10.9 | 24.04.2025
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### compassion, religious principles, non-violence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "One should be happy to see others happy and one should be unhappy to see others unhappy."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the core principle of Vaishnavism: being happy in others' happiness and unhappy in their distress, contrasting it with hypocrisy and advocating compassion for all living beings.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto No. 6, Chapter No. 10, Text No. 9: Etavana vyayodharmaha kunya shloke rupashitaha yo bhuta shoka har shabyam atma shochati hrishyati. Translation: If one is unhappy to see the distress of other living beings and happy to see their happiness, his religious principles are appreciated as imperishable by exalted persons who are considered pious and benevolent. One generally follows different types of religious principles or performs various occupational duties according to the body given to him by the modes of material nature. In these words, however, real religious principles are explained. Everyone should be unhappy to see others in distress and happy to see others happy. Atmavatsarva bhuteshu. One should feel the happiness and distress of others as his own. It is on this basis that the Buddhist religious principles of non-violence, ahimsa bhavata, and paramo dharma is established. We feel pain when someone disturbs us and therefore we should not inflict pain upon other living beings. Lord Buddha's mission was to stop unnecessary animal killing and therefore he preached that the great religious principle is non-violence. One cannot continue killing animals at the same time be a religious man. That is the greatest hypocrisy. Jesus Christ said, 'Do not kill,' but hypocrites nevertheless kill. Hypocrites nevertheless maintain thousands of slaughterhouses while posing as Christians. Such hypocrisy is condemned in this verse. One should be happy to see others happy and one should be unhappy to see others unhappy. This is the principle to be followed.

---
#### compassion, equality, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=159
**Verse References:** BG 14.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Vaishnava sees all living entities as part and parcel of God."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes compassion for all living beings as children of God, contrasting it with selective humanitarianism, and highlighting the Vaishnava's role in preaching Krishna consciousness.

Unfortunately, at the present moment, so-called philanthropists and humanitarians advocate the happiness of humanity at the cost of the lives of poor animals. That is not recommended herein. This verse clearly says that one should be compassionate to all living beings, all living entities. Regardless of whether human, animal, tree or plant, all living entities are the son of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Lord Shri Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita 14.4: sarva yoni shukontaya murtaya sambhavantiya tasam brahma mahad yonir aham bija pradapitah. It should be understood that all species of life, O son of Kunti, are made possible by birth in this material nature and that I am the seed-giving father. The different forms of these living entities are only their external dresses. Every living being is actually a spirit soul, a part and parcel of God. Therefore, one should not favor only one kind of living being. A Vaishnava sees all living entities as part and parcel of God. As the Lord says in the Bhagavad Gita, all living beings are meant to be a part and parcel of God. He is not a separate entity of the divine. He is the integral unit of the divine. He is the one who describes everything with the divine. So this way, one who is transcendentally situated at one realizes the Supreme Brahman and becomes fully joyful. He never laments nor desires to have anything. He is equally disposed to every living entity. In that state, he attains pure devotional service unto me.

---
#### Vaishnava traits, community, spiritual advancement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=279
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Vaishnava, therefore, is truly a perfect person because he laments to see others unhappy and feels joy at seeing others happy."
**Summary:** This segment defines a Vaishnava as someone who feels others' pain and happiness, contrasting this with non-devotees, and emphasizing the importance of community for manifesting these traits.

A Vaishnava, therefore, is truly a perfect person because he laments to see others unhappy and feels joy at seeing others happy. A Vaishnava is para dukha dukhi. He is always unhappy to see the conditioned soul in an unhappy state of materialism. Therefore, a Vaishnava is always busy preaching Krishna consciousness throughout the world. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Bhava. We were on a long drive and generally we see lorries, big big trucks on highways. And invariably on those lorries, trucks, something is written. Some catchy sentences. Buri najar wale tera mu kaala. I remember having seen one very nice sentence. It struck me. And this is in line with what this verse is saying. It was written in Hindi. Insaan ko. Everyone hear carefully. Insaan ko apne dukh se itna dukh nahi hota jitna ki dusno ke sukh se. A Vaishnava is para dukha dukhi. And a non-devotee is para dukha sukhi. And we all are trying to become Vaishnavas. The traits of a Vaishnava are still there. And what are the traits? Para dukha sukhi. And these traits become manifest when we are living in a community, in our daily interactions, in our dealings.

---
#### spiritual advancement, Vaishnava community, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=426
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every moment is an opportunity to please them or displease them."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of the stock market, this segment explains that living in a Vaishnava community offers great opportunities for spiritual advancement through serving and pleasing Vaishnavas, but also carries the risk of displeasing them, emphasizing the need for caution and humility in all interactions.

In the stock market, some of you probably may be aware, there are different kinds of investment options. There are some investment options. The risk is almost nil. You'll never lose money. But the returns are almost what the banks give, saving account. But there are also stock options where your money can double. You can double in five days. But you can lose the entire money in one day. That is the kind of risk. More the risk, more the gain. So in Vaishnava community, when we are living, we have a great advantage. We can advance in leaps and bounds. Why? Because we have great opportunity to serve Vaishnavas. Please Vaishnavas. Get the blessings of Vaishnava. Yesya Prasad Aad Bhagwat Prasado. We sing this for Prabhupad. But it's not just Prabhupad. All devotees also. We are also devotees. We are Vaishnavas. So when we please other Vaishnavas. That's why in line with this in Vedic culture, Vaishnava bhoj. Why? Because you please a Vaishnava. Please a Brahmana. God eats through the mouth of a Brahmana. So we have great opportunity every day at every moment to please the Vaishnavas. And we can advance in leaps and bounds by getting their blessings, by getting their mercy, by pleasing them. But it can also be risky if we are not careful because we can displease them also, which is also called Vaishnava Prad. So like a stock market example, you can multiply many fold but risk also is there. So we have to be cautious, careful. For Durvasamuni it was one devotee, Ambarish Maharaj. But for us, so many Ambarish Maharaj. We should not think we are Ambarish Maharaj but so many devotees around us. Every day, every moment we have dealings with them. Every moment is an opportunity to please them or displease them. Every interaction if you see is an opportunity. Now we get out, we go for Prasadam, Prabhu can I serve you? It's an opportunity to please. It's also an opportunity to displease. Somebody ask something and for some reason we are not in a good mood and we speak harshly. We deal harshly or somebody dealt with us harshly and we feel offended and our relationships become sour and for days together, for months together we don't talk to each other. So, this requires every dealing if we are humble, if you are cultivating humility, cultivating the perception, how I can please the Vaishnava. Prabhupada says when you call Arsath Prabhu, that is not just for the heck of calling. Prabhu means he is master, I am your servant. That should be the background consciousness.

#### humility, servant leadership, Prabhupada's example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=686
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I am not the spiritual master, I am the spiritual servant."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of humility and servant leadership, illustrating it with an anecdote about Srila Prabhupada who, despite his position, identified himself as a spiritual servant, and highlighting the need to maintain this consciousness even within the hierarchical structure of a community.

In a community at a Vivaharik level, we will have designations somebody is senior devotee, somebody is junior devotee, somebody is vice president, but at heart, at a conscious level we should know I am the servant. Even if I am a leader, I am servant. Servant leader. Srila Prabhupada once was in Los Angeles and Shamsundar Prabhu one of the early disciples of Srila Prabhupada he used to bring many eminent people to meet Prabhupada. So, one time he invited some priests so they came and they were sitting with Srila Prabhupada and some nuns also came. So, the priests who had come early they introduced Srila Prabhupada to the nuns and said he is the spiritual master of all these devotees. So, Prabhupada immediately said no I am not the spiritual master I am the spiritual servant. Although Prabhupada yes, you know guru puja happened and but in his heart, in his consciousness even though he may be using harsh words, he may be chastising his disciple, it was in the mood of serving the devotees. Sometimes superficially we can get mistaken. Prabhupada using harsh words sometimes people can feel he is arrogant, he is proud. But what's going on in the heart of Srila Prabhupada, you see in Jaladutta diary, Prabhupada never thought that this Jaladutta diary one day will be published world over people will be reading the content. No, people never thought. It was written, it was just a personal diary. And what is Prabhupada's writing? The innermost consciousness, his thinking, his feeling is manifesting in those words. Oh Lord, I have no power to preach. I have no knowledge. I am a puppet in your hand. Make me dance the way you want me to dance. And then he signs off the most insignificant beggar. Beggar is already insignificant. Insignificant beggar. Among beggars also there can be a proud beggar. Insignificant beggar. That was his mood.

#### offenses, humility, managing emotions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=862
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One of the offences is to follow the rules and regulations for the heck of following."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the dangers of getting lost in rituals and following rules without understanding their purpose, using an anecdote about Prabhupada's reaction to poorly arranged initiation ceremony to illustrate the importance of genuine understanding and humility, and highlighting the need to manage emotions and avoid offenses in interactions with devotees.

In a community when we are living we can get lost at times in the rituals. One of the offences is to follow the rules and regulations for the heck of following. Shri Prabhupada was very humble. In the early days when the first initiation ceremony was happening so he had instructed you know all of them were very new to Krishna Consciousness just one year, two year. Which year the first initiation happened? Sixties. So they may be hardly just six months, six months, six months, six months. So they were all foreigners. No idea of Indian culture. Have never seen a Yajna. You know we all as Indians it's all part of our even if Yajna in ISKCON may be new to us but it's very common. Fire sacrifice. We have seen it many times. So for them it was completely a new thing. First time they are going to participate and they were also interested in the task of making arrangements. So Prabhupada explained there will be a fire sacrifice. Kund has to be made like this. We can get the bricks together. He explained the details. And some of them to the best of their understanding because they have never seen a Yajna so they tried arranging things. On the day of initiation when Prabhupada came to the temple hall or to the room when the initiation was happening Prabhupada was visibly upset. No arrangements properly made. They have not understood what is a Yajna. What has to be done. And Prabhupada was upset. To the extent he was little angry at them. So then Prabhupada said Yajna is there. Take some offerings. Ghee, clarified butter, fruits, arrangements, flowers. Nothing was there. He said where are the fruits? Yes Prabhupada. And one devotee went to the kitchen and after three minutes came back with a plate cut fruits. Prabhupada fruits. Prabhupada became even more angry. And then one visitor who was a foreigner hippy he was there and whenever he would come for Sunday love feast so whenever devotees he would observe he would say anybody who is disturbed anybody who is upset anybody who is angry they would chant Hare Krishna. So that hippy came to Prabhupada seeing that Prabhupada is upset and told Prabhupada chant Hare Krishna. Prabhupada yes took his hand in the beads took out the bin. Prabhupada could have got offended. Who are you? Who are you? Sometimes this thought comes in our mind. Who are you to tell me this. Prabhupada an exalted devotee and a you know hippy is coming and saying chant Hare Krishna. So many instances will come in our lives we can get offended and tit for tat. We can be in a mood of I will give back. A yantra from the innermost core I will give back. Revenge. Sometimes revenge we cannot take externally because some do's and don'ts are there but deep within there is real feelings hatred brewing and how does it manifest? Coldness in relationship I will not talk to that person What does he think of himself? And many times if we are not disciplined in our thinking in managing our feelings in managing our thinking feeling and willing we can commit offences we can even use harsh words.

#### knowing-doing gap, self-improvement, conditioning
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=1174
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Many times there is a gap between knowing and doing."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of the 'knowing-doing gap,' explaining that despite knowing what is right, we often fail to act accordingly due to various factors like samskaras, upbringing, and present circumstances, and emphasizing the need for continuous effort and self-awareness to bridge this gap.

And as I mentioned we have opportunity to please Vaishnavas we have opportunity even to displease Vaishnavas I came across a very nice article which talked about which I have shared in my previous sessions also it is very interesting some of you probably would have heard about it is called the knowing doing gap. Many times there is a gap between knowing and doing for example we know that we have to chant attentively but there is a gap between knowing and doing we know that we have to please Vaishnavas we have to be tolerant but there is a knowing doing gap. We know that we should not over eat we know that we should get up early in the morning we know that we even decide we even set alarm but morning when the alarm rings knowing doing gap gets triggered. So in every area if you see there is a knowing doing gap and the reason for there can be many reasons why this gap is there it can be our samskaras it can be our we know that I should not get angry we know that I should not get upset I should be tolerant but there can be knowing doing gap and there can be many reasons it can be our upbringing. First seven years of our upbringing is very crucial our subconscious mind gets programmed you know everyone has programs that's how we all behave differently same circumstances same situation same triggers we act differently so the programs the conditioning the upbringing the samskaras the vasanas present circumstances somebody is angry smriti vibhramas buddhi nasho already somebody is upset or somebody is very tired circumstantially on that particular day or the modes that particular day we are in tamo guna slept for nine hours very tamasic state of consciousness so there can be we can act out in certain ways which later on we regret I shouldn't be doing like this I shouldn't have done like that I shouldn't have spoken like this I shouldn't have dealt like that in that moment different different forces were in play.

Even lord understands Prabhupada understands everything is not in our control genetics. So when we realize that I have made mistake which can happen to others human we can lose temper we can use harsh words we can be intolerant we can be impatient we can treat someone harshly we can end up using foul words. When we realize that we have done a mistake in that particular moment may be few minutes later or few hours later what we do then is very important. Today if you decide I will not speak harshly to devotees you cannot there will be knowing doing gap it takes time for the nature to change if by nature you are short tempered it will get triggered anger will get triggered that's why we have to practice Krishna consciousness enthusiastically with patience and determination. That's why sometimes people wonder why after so many years he is not changing again where he started from that's important everyone's starting point is different everyone's vasana is different programming is different DNA is different circumstances are different. So when we realize that's why they say it's always good we beg forgiveness. If we realize that I have done a mistake please someone I shouldn't have spoken like this always go and humbly then ego should not come why I should last time also I only this time he should he should bend no we should be always willing to beg forgiveness.

Actually if you analyze in this world at a level the foundation itself is very unfair karmic level there is perfect justice everyone is getting what is due to him karmic level there is perfect justice but at a vivaric level everyone somebody is born brilliant intelligent somebody is born buddhu cannot understand cannot pass fifth class somebody is handsome somebody is ugly where is fairness where is fairness only when we understand the soul atma many lives karmic reaction we can reconcile everything for 99.9% who don't understand all this philosophical this thing where is fairness. So we also should know there is perfect justice everyone is getting what is due to us so we should not fall in the trap of material fairness sometimes devotees can fall in that trap material fairness why why it happened to him why not me even I worked hard and we will not fall in the trap if at a very consciousness level we are humble even if there is an apparent unfairness.

#### karma, detachment, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=1830
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What Krishna is sanctioning is for my good."
**Summary:** This segment continues the discussion on dealing with unfairness, explaining that even if our minds cannot understand why we are experiencing certain things, we should trust that Krishna is sanctioning it for our good, and encouraging devotees to adopt a humble and detached attitude, focusing on serving Vaishnavas and remaining in the background.

My mind may not be able to understand why I am getting what I am getting but at a karmic level what Krishna is sanctioning is for my good. I deserve it. In fact I deserve much more. If we are having this consciousness, then so-called, you know, injustice, so-called unpleasant experiences, so-called, you know, somebody did something bad to me, somebody spoke harsh words, somebody gave a sarcastic comment, somebody criticized me, all that can be easily taken in stride. Otherwise, sometimes it can become, lot of unpleasantness can accumulate, an avalanche effect can happen. So, if we have all these things taken in stride, whatever we are reading, whatever we are hearing, all these leelas of great devotees, we take it in stride, keep it in our consciousness, we can sail through very smoothly. Susukum kartum avalanche. This path can be very joyfully, this process can be very joyfully performed. All of us have heard about Jainanda Prabhu. Prabhupada liked him a lot because of his humility. He was always there, you know, serving devotees, pleasing devotees, executing the orders of Prabhupada. So, one time when, when Sri Mishra Prabhupada came, there was a Rathayatra and all the devotees were waiting when Prabhupada would come and there was a small room where Prabhupada was supposed to come and Guru Puja was supposed to happen, jam-packed. All the devotees had gathered, waited for Prabhupada to come in so that Prabhupada, in an arrival address, he could speak to the devotees. So, everyone was in that room waiting and then when Prabhupada came, loud kirtan happened and then, they sat down and, you know, eagerly waiting for Prabhupada to speak. So, Prabhupada came, he was very pleased, the devotees, kirtan was happening and then he sat down, was about to speak, he saw all around, all the devotees, very enthusiastic, very joyful, eagerly waiting for Prabhupada and then Prabhupada looked around and then he said, Where is Jainanda? So, Prabhupada said, call him. So, one devotee went out after five minutes, came back and Jayananda Prabhu also came. And his hand was full of grease. You know, he was in the cart, that Ratha. And all his, he was little shy also because all his clothes were also dirty, grease and all that thing. So, Prabhupada waited till Jayananda Prabhu came. And then he spoke. And then even appreciated Jayananda Prabhu. So, we as devotees, if we are in a mood of serving Vaishnavas, being in the background, pray to the Lord, if required, I don't want to come in limelight. Let me be a humble, simple devotee. Be in the background. Let me serve other Vaishnavas. That is pleasing to Prabhupada.

#### pleasing Prabhupada, self-assessment, spiritual progress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=2031
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I will be very happy if you all cooperate among yourself."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that pleasing Prabhupada involves following his instructions, cooperating with other Vaishnavas, and focusing on personal spiritual progress rather than comparing oneself to others, urging devotees to regularly assess their own humility, tolerance, and compassion.

That is pleasing to Krishna. Prabhupada also mentioned that, you know, many times devotees, they were always around Prabhupada. In fact, some of the devotees who were, they were always travelling with Prabhupada. Wherever Prabhupada would go, they would also travel with him for devotees association, Prabhupada association. Proper understanding of philosophy is, if we want to please Prabhupada, we want to please by following his instructions. We have heard quite often, Bapu Seva and Vani Seva. Prabhupada has a mission. He has given his instructions. He has given what pleases him. I will be very happy if you all cooperate among yourself. If we are serving, if we are pleasing other Vaishnavas, if we are cooperating with each other, if we have loving relationship among Vaishnavas, it will be very pleasing to Prabhupada. If we are doing our assigned services very nicely, we are humble, we are in the background, we are not seeking limelight, we are not seeking power, position. It will be very pleasing to Prabhupada. We have come here to advance in Krishna consciousness. Sometimes we end up comparing with other devotees. He got this facility, he got that facility. In fact, we should just compare, not compare with others, we should compare, am I progressing? What was I two years back? What was I five years back? Am I becoming better? Am I becoming humbler? Am I becoming more meek? Am I becoming more tolerant? Am I becoming more forgiving? Am I becoming more kind, more compassionate? Am I becoming more para dukha dukhi? Do I feel hurt when others are hurt? Do I feel unhappy when others are unhappy? Or I continue to remain para dukha sukhi? So this introspection, this some kind of assessment, some kind of evaluation has to go on, on an ongoing basis. Some of you would have heard about, you know, management, there is a cycle called Deming cycle, Deming. It's a process of continual improvement, plan, do, check, act, act and check. And it should go in cycle. Plan, do, act and check. We may not follow that principle but then we are acting, we are planning a morning sadhana, how we can remember Krishna, how we can please Vaishnavas, how we can always remain humble, how we can be tolerant, how we can be patient.

---
#### self-improvement, learning from mistakes, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/8PrjspdH0Sw?t=2224
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Mistake is not a mistake if two things happen, first, we are willing to admit it's a mistake, and second, learn from it."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of learning from mistakes, emphasizing that a mistake is only truly a mistake if we fail to admit it and learn from it, and highlighting the need for humility and a willingness to incorporate lessons learned into future planning.

Do, we should check. At times we may fail. There may be knowing-doing gap, which is understandable. Everyone's nature is different. What do we do after that? Do we incorporate that in our next planning, same instance, the same circumstance, same situation, same triggers? Do I act differently? Have I learnt my lessons? Have I learnt from my mistakes? Am I becoming better? Otherwise, how do I act? Otherwise, the same thing can repeat. Same error, same mistakes. Mistake is not a mistake. They say, there's a very nice saying, mistake is not a mistake. Error is not an error. If two things happen, first, we are willing to admit it's a mistake. I messed up. I made a mistake. It requires humility. And second, learn from it. If both the components are missing, then it's genuinely a mistake. Otherwise, yes, beg forgiveness. I made a mistake. I am sorry. Admit. Be humble enough to admit and learn from it. If we learn, then we can become better. In English, there's a saying, feedback is a breakfast of champions. We can become better. Many times, we are reluctant to hear any criticism against us. Even though that criticism may be right, even though it may be true, there's a natural repulsion. You know, it's said that Hitler was so arrogant, in fact, one of the reason why he lost, he was never very arrogant. He was never willing to accept his mistake. He cannot be wrong. Even his close, trusted lieutenants would come and try to say, here you're going wrong. You're not able to see, it's your blind spot. But he would get them executed. Well-wisher means he should praise me.

See, all what we are practicing here, chanting, reading, hearing bhagavatam, darshana aarti, mangal arty, actually if you see technically it's possible to do from home. You can here these days, mangal arty. You can participate from your home, online. In fact, I did a zoom in-zoom out. You get much bigger views, much better views. There's a person. Everything is zoom out and there's a person. Every day without fail in Bombay, every day without fail he attends a Mangal Aarti here. Including that reading of that Prabhupada quote, Tulsi Puja, everything, the entire morning program he attends online. Bhagavatam class, live. Hope it is live. It is live. Everything can be done from home. Deity worship, offering Prasadam, honoring Prasadam. One thing cannot happen from home. Can anyone tell what cannot happen from home? One thing cannot happen from home. Is someone pointing out your mistake? And if you are not willing to admit and not reluctant to take any feedback and we get offended, who are you to tell me? Then the great opportunity of improving, in fact sometimes it so happens when we see some devotees very sensitive and we know that he will get disturbed, he will get agitated, we are reluctant sometimes to give feedback. Some devotees were nicely situated, very on his face you can tell, Prabhu this is wrong. Yes Prabhu.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### How to Meditate on God? | Bhagavad Gita Chapter 10 Verses 17 - 21 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### meditation, Arjuna's questions, Krishna's opulence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "How should I meditate on you? In what various forms are you to be contemplated, O blessed Lord?"
**Summary:** Arjuna asks Krishna how to meditate on Him and in what forms He can be contemplated.

So, today we will discuss from the 10th chapter of the Gita. The topic is how to meditate on God, based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada. Let's chant this verse together, this is the 17th verse: *Katham vidyam aham yogims, tuam sada pari chintayan, keshu keshu cha bhaveshu, chintyo si bhagvan mayan.* How should I meditate on you? In what various forms are you to be contemplated, O blessed Lord? So these are the words spoken by Arjuna to Krishna.

---
#### devotion, revelation, yoga maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=65
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He is asking this question so that people like us, who are not able to think of Lord, who are not able to see the Lord, so that people can get some clue how we can contemplate on the Lord."
**Summary:** Arjuna asks the question for the benefit of those who cannot perceive the Lord, and Krishna explains that He is not visible to everyone because He is covered by His yoga maya potency.

He is saying, how do I meditate on you? What are the various forms in which we can think of you, we can contemplate on you? Now why is Arjuna asking this question? As far as he being the devotee is concerned, for him to remember the Lord, to think of the Lord is not difficult. In fact, he has the Lord in his company. He has the association of the Lord, Lord is right there with him. He is asking this question so that people like us, who are not able to think of Lord, who are not able to see the Lord, why? So that people can get some clue how we can contemplate on the Lord. Now Krishna in the Gita says, *naham prakasha sarvasya*, *naham* means I do not, *prakasha* means reveal, become evident, become visible, *prakasha sarvasya*, *sarvasya* means to everyone, I do not reveal myself, I am not evident to, I am not visible to, *sarvasya*, *sarvasya* means to everyone. *Yogo maya samanvrta*, I am covered by my yoga maya potency.

---
#### devotion, perception, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=146
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only through love, only through devotion, *bhaktia mam abhijanati*, can I be known."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that only through devotion can one know the Lord, and that by culturing devotion and surrendering to the Lord, we can perceive Him.

Although the Lord is there, Prahlad Maharaj was asked, is the Lord there in the pillar? He very confidently said, yes he is there. But Hrindakashuku could not see that Lord. So those who are rebellious, who are, that's why the word used is saintly kings understood it in that way. Unless we become saintly, we are not able to perceive the Lord. *Bhaktia mam abhijanati yavan yas chasmi tatvata*. Only through love, only through devotion, *bhaktia mam abhijanati*, can I be known. Although the Lord is omnipresent, he is present everywhere, but we are not able to see it. The example is given just like the butter is there in the milk, but it is not visible. But by following a process, the butter manifests. So by culturing devotion, by becoming a devotee of the Lord, by surrendering to the Lord, by following the process, we can also perceive the Lord, the Lord becomes manifest.

#### faith, doubt, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We are not able to see the Lord. Our faith is very Komala. We are not very sure Lord exists, not exists. Many incidences we see in this world around us which bewilders us."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the challenges faced by those who cannot see the Lord, whose faith is weak, and who are bewildered by the world around them.

Now for devotees who don't have yoga, maya, samanvrta, who can see the Lord always, they don't require any forms to contemplate on. The Lord is evident. They can perceive the presence of Lord every moment. They can experience the reciprocation of the Lord. But what about people like us? We are not able to see the Lord. Our faith is very Komala. We are not very sure Lord exists, or not exists. Many incidences we see in this world around us which bewilders us.

---
#### yoga maya, maha maya, darkness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=264
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When we are not able to perceive the Lord, when we see things independent of Lord, when Lord is not in the picture?"
**Summary:** The speaker explains the two forms of maya: yoga maya, which illuminates and allows us to see the Lord in everything, and maha maya, which is darkness and prevents us from perceiving the Lord.

So, maya has two forms. All of us have heard about this word maya? Yes, no? Yes. At least you must have heard that maya has two forms. When the light falls on a mirror, it gets reflected. So, one side of the mirror where the light falls and it gets reflected, it is illuminated. It has light. And that place is called Abhasa. Abhasa means that potency of the Lord by which everything can be seen in relation with the Lord, can be perceived. We can perceive the presence of Lord in everything and in everyone. That potency is called yoga-maiya. There is another potency called Mahamaya which is Tamaha. Tamaha means Darkness. The below bottom side of the mirror in which the light falls and gets reflected is Dark. And what is the darkness when we are not able to perceive the Lord, when we see things independent of Lord, when Lord is not in the picture? So devotees of the Lord are under the influence of Yogamaya. Yashoda Mai could not perceive that Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. She was covered by the Yogamaya potency of the Lord. We who have forgotten the Lord, rebelled against the Lord, are under the influence of Mahamaya, which is darkness.

#### Krishna, sun, maya, darkness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=390
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Wherever there is sun, there cannot be any darkness. So likewise, when we bring the Lord into our life, the remembrance of the Lord in our life, when we are able to see things in relationship with the Supreme Lord, Maya has no grip on us."
**Summary:** The speaker compares Krishna to the sun and maya to darkness, explaining that when we bring the Lord into our lives and see things in relationship with Him, maya has no power over us.

*Krishna Surya Sama Maya Haya Amaya*. The Lord Krishna is like Surya. Surya means sun. *Maya Haya Andhakaar*. Maya is like darkness. Wherever there is sun, there cannot be any darkness. So likewise, when we bring the Lord into our life, the remembrance of the Lord in our life, when we are able to see things in relationship with the Supreme Lord, Maya has no grip on us. Why we say we are trapped in the web of Maya, because we are doing activities completely oblivious of the Lord and His pleasure, His satisfaction. The goal of life has become my pleasure, my satisfaction. Could be extended pleasure, my family's pleasure, my society's pleasure, my country's pleasure. Lord is out of picture. So that is Tamaha, that is darkness. And that is why it says *Tamaseema Jyotirgamaya*. Come to light. It is said *Brahma Satyam Jagat Mithya*.
