#### illusion, reality, perception
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_E1Fg1TnOs?t=470
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we perceive a rope as a snake, that is illusion."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies the statement that the world is false, explaining that it is not the world itself that is false, but our perception of it independent of the Lord.

This is Shankaracharya who said this: Brahman is Satya and this Jagat is Mithya. This world which we are living in is false. A question to all of you, this world is false, yes or no? All what we see around us, FIFA World Cup happening, it is all illusion or it is a reality? How many of you say it is a reality? FIFA World Cup is actually happening. Brazil has lost. But here it is saying it is Mithya, it is illusion. Okay. Now how many of us say that this is a snake? How many of us say it is not a snake, it is a rope? How many of us say it is both? If we perceive a rope as a snake, that is illusion. If you see a rope and it looks like a snake because of something, that is illusion. Rope is not Mithya, rope is not unreal. But perception of rope as snake is Mithya. So when we perceive Jagat, independent of the Lord, with no relation with the Lord, that is Mithya.

One time, this is a twist to the story which some of you would have heard about, Honest Thieves. Please very carefully hear this story. You will be able to relate with what is happening in this world. One time there were 20, a group of 20 people, out of those 24 were ladies, women and 2 children. They all got together and they decided to rob a bank. Each one had some very specific role to play, including the children, in robbing the bank. So they all schemed together, planned together and they very meticulously executed the plan. And they finally succeeded in robbing the bank. And they fled with the booty, with all the cash in the bags. And finally reached a place, a hideout which they had initially planned. And then they all got together and said, now let's distribute the wealth, which we have jointly, so to say, robbed.

So while this fight was on, they almost fought like cats and dogs. So one of them was relatively elder in the group. He said, let's not fight like cats and dogs. I don't think we'll come to consensus like this. And all of us are having heated arguments. I don't think today we'll be able to resolve. Let's convene a meeting tomorrow morning. And with fresh mind, let's think how to divide this wealth. So next day morning they assembled. And then one of them said, I think if we just argue like that, we are not going to reach any consensus. Let's do one thing. Let's use a democratic process to divide this wealth. Let's elect a person. All of us will vote. And that person will be empowered to decide how the wealth will be divided. The democracy. So while the election process was on, so now the question came, should the women vote or not? Should these children be given voting rights or not? It became a women rights issue. The children should be voted on. Again they started fighting. Then finally one of them suggested, again looks like we are not going to reach consensus. Not going to reach anything. Better, let's do one thing. Let's have a four man committee. And let this committee come up with a constitution, how to divide the wealth. So again they started jointly discussing and all those things. Mind you they are talking about big big words of democratic way. Nice way of dividing so that it is fair. It is just. But what are they talking about? What wealth they are talking about? Ill gotten wealth. In the meantime the police came and they caught hold of them and they were taken to police custody. And they were brought to the trial court. In the trial court again the advocate who was representing this case he said, My lord although they have robbed the bank but please understand the spirit. They were trying to distribute the wealth very justfully, very rightfully. You know they were having a consensus. They were democratic way of this thing. Please consider that. The judge says no way. After all it is a robbery.

#### fundamental truth, ownership, honest thieves

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker draws a parallel between the thieves and society, emphasizing that without recognizing that everything belongs to the Lord, even just actions are like those of honest thieves.

Now in this world we have democratic way of electing government. Appears to be very fair. We have a constitution which we abide by. We have well defined laws. Yes or no? We have women rights. We have child rights. We try to assure that everyone there is justice. There is equality. But if we don't understand a very fundamental truth. What is that? What is that? That this property what we have is not a lord. Is not a lord. Is not a lord. *Isha vasya idam sarvam*. Then Prabhupada used this word sarcastically. If we don't recognize that fundamental thing. All the abilities what I have. The strength I have. The wealth I have. It all is coming from the lord. Today I may be having this house. I may have a document signed that this house belongs to me. Have I created this bricks? The minerals whatever that has gone in building the house. Before I came in it was existing there. And after I depart I will still exist. So all these terms just like this honest thieves. They were very honestly trying to very justifiably distribute the wealth. But unless we recognize that this wealth is not mine. It is not. But we are at best called as honest thieves. Because the materialists who are not able to see the lord. Cannot see the hand of the lord. Cannot able to see the relationship with the lord. Arjuna is asking. What are the physical things? What are those gross objects? On which we can meditate on.

#### spiritual struggle, threshold, unadulterated bliss

**Summary:** The speaker explains that spiritual life involves a struggle until one crosses a threshold, after which it becomes a state of unadulterated bliss, and that scriptures guide us towards this goal.

These are all stages. Till that Anarthanivritti there is a battle, there is a struggle, there is a dilemma. Once we cross that threshold level, just like the rockets when they lift off. There is a threshold velocity. Initially the amount of fuel which it consumes is phenomenal. Once it gets into the orbit it seamlessly moves. So after we cross that threshold this thing it is Kevala Ananda Khanda which means it is bliss and bliss only. Actually we all want that. We want that. We are aspiring for that. We are hoping against hope that this will happen, we will get this, I will be happy. But what we are actually striving for is Kevala Ananda Khanda, unadulterated bliss and the path for that is what our scriptures is talking about. In fact scriptures are not aiming gunning for something which, you know, which we don't want. They are asking us or rather showing us a path to achieve the same thing. We are just waiting for that to come, what we are wanting to achieve, just that we have chosen a different path, a wrong path, a path which appears lucrative but will lead in a wrong direction.

**Summary:** Krishna agrees to explain His splendorous manifestations to Arjuna, but only the prominent ones, as His opulence is limitless.

Now in reply to the questions asked by Arjuna, Krishna says, *Shri Bhagwan Uvacha*, the Blessed Lord said, yes I will tell you of my splendorous manifestations but only of those which are prominent. Oh Arjuna, for my opulence is limitless. Now Krishna is implying that yes you have asked me what manifestation we can contemplate on you. He says, yes I will explain my glories, my splendorous manifestations but only those which are prominent. For my opulences are limitless, *kya kya main bolu, kya kya*, what all, everything is emanating from me, *sarvasya prabhavo matta sarvam pravartate*, everything is emanating from me. It's not possible to comprehend the greatness of Krishna and all his opulences. It's not possible. Therefore Krishna is saying, now I'm going to share with you some principal details. Just like for example when we have to prepare for exams, we have limited time assuming two hours, then what we do, we bullet point some important highlights in front of the chapters there, some this thing, depending, time is less. So likewise, now Arjuna is saying, explain to me your glorious manifestation. Krishna says, it's limitless, I will explain to you some principal important manifestation.

Why build this temple? So many donors, so many problems, so many escalations. There can be this mentality. There could be somebody who is interested in his own deliverance, personal deliverance. A person with that mentality, why should I break my head? Why should I be involved in this thing? Unnecessary tension, stress, anxiety. #### Kali Yuga, Cultural decline, Injustice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The first victim of brahmana injustice, brahmanical injustice, was Maharaj Pariksit."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the context of the verse, explaining how Pariksit Maharaj's actions and the influence of Kali Yuga led to a decline in cultural life and the beginning of brahminical injustice.

tasya putrati tejasvi, niharan balako arba kahe, rajnagam prapitam tatam, sutva tatredam abhravesh. Translation and purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. Translation: The sage had a son who was very powerful, being a brahmana's son. While he was playing with the inexperienced boys, he heard of his father's distress, which was occasioned by the king. Then and there the boy spoke as follows. Due to Maharaj Pariksit's good government, even a boy of tender age who was playing with his other inexperienced boys could become as powerful as a qualified brahmana. The boy was known as Shringi, and he achieved good training in brahmacharya by his father, so that he could be as powerful as a brahmana, even at that age. But because the age of Kali was seeking an opportunity to spoil the cultural heritage of the four orders of life, the inexperienced boys gave a chance for the age of Kali to enter into the field of Vedic culture. Hatred of the lower orders of life began from the brahmana boy under the influence of Kali, and thus cultural life began to dwindle day after day. The first victim of brahmana injustice, brahmanical injustice, was Maharaj Pariksit, and thus the protection given by the king against the onslaught of Kali was slackened. Thus ends the bhaktivedanta purva.

---
#### Divine plan, Tolerance, Insult
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=106
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So overall, it's a divine plan of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment provides the context of Pariksit Maharaj's encounter with the sage, his anger, and Shringi's curse, framing it as part of the Lord's divine plan and the inevitable onset of Kali Yuga.

So just to give you a context of what's being mentioned in this verse. In the previous verse, Pariksit Maharaj, while he was in the forest, he saw the sage, reached his hermitage. And since the sage was busy in his meditation, he didn't open his eyes. He didn't properly receive the king as per the etiquette, so he was very angry. Many leelas which are mentioned in the Bhagavatam, if we understand in the context of the divine plan of the Lord, we'll be able to understand them correctly. One may question, if Pariksit was such a great devotee of the Lord, why did he become angry? Why did he feel insulted just because he was not received respectfully? In fact, a devotee of the Lord is very tolerant. Like Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says, he is more tolerant than a tree. He is humbler than a blade of grass. So overall, it's a divine plan of the Lord. As we all know, there are four cycles, four yugas: Kali Yuga, Treta Yuga, Satya Yuga, Dwapara Yuga, and Kali Yuga had to begin. So Shringi, who is the Brahmana's son, so what did Pariksit do? When he felt insulted, he took a dead snake which was lying nearby and put it around the neck of the Brahmana. So Shringi, who is the son of this Brahmana, he saw his father being insulted. He felt very offended and he cursed Pariksit Maharaj.

---
#### Kali Yuga, Good governance, Acceptance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=241
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the world lost a great king like Pariksit Maharaj and once the king is not very strong then things start deteriorating."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the influence of Kali Yuga, the importance of good governance, and Pariksit Maharaj's acceptance of the curse, leading to his death and the subsequent deterioration of the kingdom.

And in this verse, Prabhupada is mentioning that the influence of Kali, when there is a strong king like Pariksit Maharaj, good governance means to protect the citizens, kshatriyas and also protect the kingdom from the influence of the sinful habits, sinful tendencies like meat eating, intoxication, gambling, illicit sex, which is very rampant in present times because we are in the midst of Kali Yuga. And thus cultural life began to dwindle day after day. So since Pariksit Maharaj was cursed and then he was cursed to die in next seven days, so the world lost a great king like Pariksit Maharaj and once the king is not very strong then things start deteriorating. The first victim of Brahminical injustice, now Sringi was a Brahmin boy, Brahminical injustice against the lower orders like Kshatriyas, Brahminas, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, Shudras. So it says the injustice of the Brahminical injustice began with Pariksit Maharaj. So, to curse Pariksit Maharaj to die in seven days was not appropriate even if we were to evaluate what Pariksit Maharaj did. But Pariksit Maharaj instead of counteracting or counter cursing Sringi, he accepted it. He went to the bank of Ganges, heard the Srimad Bhagavatam and died remembering the Lord. So,

#### Kali Yuga, Degradation, Self-protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=372
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "First we have to protect ourselves."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the degraded nature of Kali Yuga, its visible symptoms, and the importance of self-protection and insulation from its ill effects for devotees.

as I mentioned earlier, we are in Kali Yuga and Kali Yuga is of all the four ages is the most degraded. And, we can see the symptoms. What do you mean by that? Yes, they are degrading. Yeah. It's coming to you. He's seem consistently degrading. It has not only sounded degradatory but. But I'm not saying he is dead. In fact, the symptoms are mentioned in the Srimad Bhagavatam and which are very much visible to us in present times. There's a lot of fights happening, lots of wars happening, a lot of confusion, a lot of quarrels happening. Meat-eating is rampant, intoxication, gambling, illicit sex, extramarital affairs, live-in relationships. What was not so common, what was unheard of maybe 20 years back, has become very common. So as devotees of the Lord, yes, we have a duty to see how we can reach this message, distribute the holy name to people. It's our duty, it's Prabhupada's mission. We are part of that mission. But Chetanaya Mahaprabhu says, first we have to protect ourselves. First we have to realize that, yes, Kali Yuga is at its full force and things are degrading. We have to protect ourselves. We have to insulate ourselves. Like in the flight also when you travel, they say that the oxygen thing will, the mask will fall down from the top. Before helping others, kindly put the mask. So we have to help ourselves. We have to protect ourselves, insulate ourselves from the ill effects of Kali Yuga.

#### Compassion, Detachment, Family life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=512
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "I can give you enough money, so much money that for the rest of the life you can live happily."
**Summary:** This segment begins a story about a poverty-stricken man who faces a challenge to live in isolation for ten years in exchange for enough money to solve his financial problems and marry off his daughter.

And try our best, whatever we can, to help others. Janma Sarthak Kari Koro Pakaar. Many years back, I came across this story. In fact, it's not a story, I saw a television, this episode. This was more than 20 years back. And those days, I think, it would have been 20 years ago. It was black and white television. And I, even till date, remember this. But those days, it was not like you like some episode, you save it, you can download it. It's on YouTube. I vaguely remember. There was a man. He was very poverty stricken and practically had no money with him. He had a young daughter and he was barely able to manage the day-to-day needs of the family, including groceries and clothes. So the daughter was getting, you know, the marriageable age and he was in anxiety, how to marry the daughter. And even in Vedic times, there was, you know, marriage. It was done in a way. Some dowry was also given, not in the sense, what we see in present times, it is demanded. It was out of love. So, this person was a lot of, had a lot of anxiety, lot of worry, how to manage his household expenses, how to marry the daughter. And he was trying various means to earn money but things were not happening. So one day a friend of his gave him a challenge. He said you need money. I can give you enough money, so much money that for the rest of the life you can live happily. You don't have to worry about your finances. Not only that, you can very easily get your daughter married.

#### Sacrifice, Materialism, Relationships
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=670
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Man is a social animal. We thrive on relationships, social beings. Relationships are very important."
**Summary:** The story continues, detailing the challenge's conditions: ten years of complete isolation, no interaction with anyone, and the man's acceptance of the challenge despite the anticipated struggle.

So he couldn't believe such a thing as possible, but there is a challenge. Now he was so desperate for money, he said I am ready to accept any challenge. He said the challenge is that you have to live in a room all by yourself, all alone for 10 years. Three times the food will be given in that cell, in that room. But you will not be allowed to interact with people, with anyone, including your family, including your near and dear ones. Even in prisons they have, you know, in a month you can for some five minutes or something you can meet. There's some protocols. But here the condition was that for ten years you cannot talk to anyone, you cannot meet anyone, you cannot hear anything. There were no smart phones, there was no mp3 players. You can hear the songs, FM radio, nothing. And you all know that man is a social animal. We thrive on relationships, social beings. Relationships are very important. So this man accepted the challenge and he thought instead of struggling the whole life, it's a struggle for 10 years. I'll somehow manage. But he never anticipated what that struggle would be, how intense that struggle would be. So this whole documentary or film what was made is basically to showcase his emotions.

#### Isolation, Mental health, Suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=800
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He started becoming uncomfortable, little jittery. Felt like talking to someone but there's nobody."
**Summary:** This segment describes the man's initial experiences in isolation, his growing unease, discomfort, and agitation as the days turn into months, leading to mental distress and a struggle to cope with the loneliness.

What this person went through, what struggles and this whole camera was focusing on his facial expressions, on what he was doing during those 10 days. These days you have some reality shows where somebody is put in some house. I haven't seen anything, just maybe some idea and then people watch what they are doing. So for 10 years he was in this room, locked. Three times food was served to him, and then first initial 1 week 10 days. He was... He started missing something, something unusual. See one day we can manage food, second day, something, you know, we feel very uncomfortable. So likewise if we are in a room and all by itself... One day, two day, we can somehow manage. So after one week, ten days, he started feeling very uneasy and that started showing up on his face, on his expression. He started becoming uncomfortable, little jittery. Felt like talking to someone but there's nobody. Even the camera captured whatever he was speaking, whatever he was expressing, whatever words he was speaking also was being captured, was being shown. So he even started thinking that is it really worthwhile taking this challenge and stay for ten years? Because seven days itself was so difficult. And he started even thinking that maybe let me walk out. But then what else will he do? How will he get the money? So he continued. One month passed, two months passed, three months passed. And then after three months, he became so agitated because of loneliness. No companionship, nobody to talk to. Sometimes he would be shouting, screaming, hitting his head on the walls. Sometimes he started becoming a little bit mad.

#### Mental deterioration, Hope, Endurance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=952
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And now he almost became like mad."
**Summary:** The story progresses, depicting the man's further mental deterioration over the years, his descent into madness, and the eventual glimmer of hope as he anticipates the end of his confinement.

Anyway, he kept on going. One year passed, two years passed, three years passed. And now he almost became like mad. He had big beard, you know, hairs not combed. Maybe not bathing daily. And at times screaming, at times howling. The camera would capture. He would be lying for hours together on the floor. No proper time for sleeping. You know, we all have a schedule. Morning we get up, we have to go for work. Somebody comes, meet. And then night time comes, we sleep. But here you have no one. You practically don't know whether it's day or night. It's a dark, dungeon room all alone. So three years passed. Four years pass. And it was quite evident from his face that he's suffering. He's suffering. He's miserable. He's not able to take it. But somewhere, whatever sanity he had, he was trying to reconcile. Another five years. Another four years. And then a couple of more years passed. Seven years. Eight years. And by now, he had practically become mad. For you would see the camera would capture. He would start screaming. Whatever he's speaking is incoherent. Whatever is not making any sense. He's shouting. He's screaming. All by itself. Just like you go to a Nimhans or some mad people, you can see. They'll be laughing. No reason. Talking. No reason. What they're talking is all gibberish. So, the camera was capturing everything. You could hear what he was speaking. You could hear what he was shouting, screaming. Everything. Now, nine years passed. This man, although a little bit, whatever little sanity is waiting, when that one year will pass and he can walk out. Sometimes, some very interesting thing which has to happen. Or some very important festival or something. So, we wait. Countdown. Countdown starts. 30 days left. 35. 28 days left. So, in his mind, probably the countdown had begun.

#### Transformation, Spiritual awakening, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=1112
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Little by little, he started getting interested in what is being written, what is written in the Gita."
**Summary:** This segment marks a turning point in the story as the man receives a copy of the Bhagavad Gita and gradually becomes engrossed in its teachings, leading to a transformation in his mental state.

So, then, I clearly remember, somehow, he got a copy of Bhagavad Gita. Just for your information, in ISKCON also, many devotees go and preach in prisons. And just for your information, not everyone who is behind bars or who is in prison is quite possible there could be innocent people also. So, not everyone. Everyone is a hardcore criminal. So, devotees go and many times the Gita's also are distributed. So, somehow, this person got the Gita and, so the camera is not, what is he doing with the Gita? He is all alone, all the time in the world, nothing else to do, started reading Gita. So, initially when he was reading Gita, it was quite evident from his face, facial expression, it is not making any sense to him. Probably he is not able to understand what is written. Like it happens when you first try to read Gita, it may look like it is very philosophical and maybe for some it may be difficult to understand. So, he started reading and then it was quite evident that it is not interesting. It is not making any sense. So, he would, you know, flip few pages and then he would close it and keep it. Again, he would pick up after some time or maybe after few days. And then, the camera captured very interesting thing. Little by little, he started getting interested in what is being written, what is written in the Gita. So, he started with some bit of sobriety reading what is there in the Gita. He started becoming little sober. It appeared, this whole episode was, the camera is capturing the action. So, whole expression was that what he is reading is now started making sense to him.

And then, nine years, six months passed. Now, daily, three hours, four hours, he is reading Gita. And then, seven months passed. Nine years, seven months passed. By now, he had calmed down. All that agitation. All that anxiety. All that screaming, shouting, howling. All that had calmed down. And then, six hours, seven hours, he is reading Gita. Nine years, nine months passed. And now, he was completely calm. He was very sober. Very intensely, very sincerely reading Gita daily. Nine years, eleven months passed. Thirty days to go. And you could see from his face, there is some bit of divinity reflecting. You know, some sages, very austere, you can see from their face. So, the camera captured that. Very calm, very sober, very serene, very contentment reflecting on his face. Ten days left. Nine years, eleven months, twenty days over. He started experiencing so much of joy, so much of bliss, so much of happiness, reading Gita.

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The man realizes that he has achieved everything he needs through spiritual understanding, renounces the material reward, and leaves the room before the ten years are up, inspired by the pastime of Dhruva Maharaj.

That started feeling that whatever I had to gain in life, whatever I want, I have already achieved. After all, why we need money? Why we need, ultimately, the common denominator in everyone's life. Whether somebody needs money, somebody needs adventure, somebody is fond of travelling, somebody is fond of renunciation, somebody is fond of different hobbies. Ultimately, it's for happiness, feeling good. So, the camera was capturing, which is in line with the pastime of Dhruva Maharaj, who went to the forest, did austerities, and then when the Lord came, gave darshan and said, What do you want? He said, I don't want anything. Now that I have got it. I was looking for broken pieces of glass. Now that I have got you, which is like valuable jewel, what I was looking for was nothing. I don't want. I don't want any varam. I don't want any benediction. I just want you. Because now that I have you, I have everything. So, maybe the inspiration from this particular pastime, so this person, nine years, eleven months, one day left, for him to walk out of that room, with that huge sum of money, he felt that money is not required. Now that he has everything, he has understood the purpose of life, he has understood the message of Krishna, and then this person is shown that one day before his tenth year getting completed, he walks out of that room and he says, I don't want your money. Maybe whatever that episode was made, or that film was made, inspiration must have been taken from Dhruva Maharaj's pastime. Wanted to maybe convey in this thing.

#### Kali Yuga, Distraction, Social media

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Many of us may not be even aware that we may already be addicted to smartphones."
**Summary:** This segment shifts to a discussion of the allurements and distractions of Kali Yuga, particularly the addictive nature of smartphones and social media, and how they capture our attention.

The point here is, in Kali Yuga, we will be tempted. There are lot of distractions. There are lot of allurements. And as time is passing by, it's on the rise. Many of us may not be even aware that we may already be addicted to smartphones. These algorithms have been designed by the best of brains. MIT, Harvard, Stanford, all these elite students, they work in Facebook, Google, all that. And they are, they are, in one sense, the common people are guinea pigs. Our, so to say, our innocence is being played with. We are victims. No doubt, I am not against social media or smartphones. But, many of us may not realize, or already would have realized, that how it is addictive. 15 minutes, 20 minutes, you want to watch something, or do something, but then, hours pass by. Because that's how it is designed. The algorithms are designed. You see something, the next related thing will be served. We are having a symposium going on, which says that whole principle is, to capture your attention.

#### Attention economy, Addiction, Self-control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=1672
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The more we say, no, no, no, no, no, it again hits us back with more intense force."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how content creators and advertisers exploit our attention through algorithms, and how simply resisting temptations can backfire, leading to a stronger pull towards them.

The content you produce, if you are able to capture the attention, they will promote your content. And the content creators, brainy fellows, in different fields, producing content. And it's flooded with content. And algorithms are serving that content, and capturing our attention. And that captured attention is being sold to the advertisers. Okay, you have a product. Whom do you want to show? Which demographic? Which country? Which age group? Which gender? Which city? Which town? Which locality? I will serve. Give me this much money. They may not say, but this is how the whole industry is. So we as devotees, janma sarthakari, we have got Prabhupada's message. We have got, khanda brahmite kono bhagyavan jeev, guru krishna prasade paaye bhakti lata abhij. We have got the bija. But, we should protect the creeper of bhakti. And as I said, with the influence of Kali Yuga, there are lot of allurements, lot of temptations, lot of distractions. And if we don't safeguard ourselves, we will fall for it. Krishna also in the Gita says, just by artificially restraining ourselves, restricting ourselves, we cannot protect. We cannot say, I will not see, I will not do, I will not do this, I will not say this. Sometimes, devotees can also fall in a trap of, that what we try to resist, it persists. The more we say, no, no, no, no, no, it again hits us back with more intense force.

#### Hunger, Necessity, Survival
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=1811
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Necessity is the mother of invention."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal story about being stuck in a hostel during a curfew with no food, illustrating how extreme circumstances can force one to accept even the most unappealing options.

I remember when there was some curfew, I was studying, when I was doing my engineering, we were in the third year, sixth semester, and somehow there was curfew, and we were stuck in the hostel. It was a long curfew. And even the mess, the canteen, where the food was cooked, that was also shut down, and people had left. So few of us were there in the hostel, we cannot now take a bus and go back home. We were in the hostel. The mess is closed. And I clearly remember that day, not because we were fasting, sometimes we don't eat, not because food is not available, we choose not to eat. But that was a day, when morning to evening, we had no option to eat. There is nothing around, which you can eat. So it was four o'clock in the evening, although there was a curfew, we, friends, walked out of our room, went to nearby kiosk, where they used to make some tea, coffee and all that. And there was one small shop, it was closed, we somehow requested it, and got some rice. I can relate with what is hunger. It was four in the evening, not eaten since morning, maybe previous night also not eaten. Got some rice, there was nothing, no vegetable, no dal, no masala, this thing and all that. Came back to the room, and in hostel room, some of you may be able to relate with what I am saying, maybe times have changed. So we used to have those electric heaters, raw coils, and then some pan to boil water, or to make tea sometime.

---
#### Acceptance, Higher taste, Analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/FaqitQt5cGc?t=1933
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you have no option, you will eat it."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, describing how they cooked the rice in a dirty pan and ate it despite its unappetizing appearance, drawing a parallel to how a lack of higher taste leads one to settle for lower pleasures.

And in hostel typically, that pan is full black, because you don't wash it so often, or maybe you don't know how to wash it. So came back to the room, had never cooked rice, no idea how the rice is cooked, but circumstances of such you have to, necessity is the mother of invention. So put the rice, put the water, and put it on that heater. And slowly that water started boiling, and no idea how much it has to boil, how long it has to boil. And first time in my life, I saw black rice. We have heard of brown rice, red rice, I saw cooked black rice. And, couple of my friends, we had option either to throw it, visual thing was to throw it, but stomach was saying, no, eat it. And we ate it. I guarantee, if anybody were to see that rice, you will spit at the thought of eating it. But, if you have no option, you will eat it.

#### Sadhana, Addiction, Higher taste

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

Real pleasure is not there and we are hankering for that. So, if we have to, so it is, Prabhupada has given us the morning sadhana, raso varjam raso piyasya param drishtva nivartate. There was an article called six human needs. There are needs, just like food, clothing, shelter, it's a need. Likewise, there are other needs. Needs for variety, needs for stability, needs for recreation. And if we don't get in a right way, the right quality of rasa, then we will fall for lower rasas. And that is what Kali Yuga is. The higher taste is missing in people. And that's why people are just helplessly falling for the lower tastes.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Importance of Developing Higher Taste| Sri Amitasana Dasa | SB 1.18.32 | 28.07.2024
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Kali Yuga, detachment, challenges
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XLaAtB67aPQ?t=1293
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "First we have to protect ourselves, we have to insulate ourselves."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the degraded state of Kali Yuga and emphasizes the need for self-protection, illustrating this with a story about a man who accepts a challenge to live in isolation for ten years in exchange for money.

As I mentioned earlier, we are in Kali Yuga, and of all the four ages, Kali Yuga is the most degraded. We can see the symptoms; in fact, the symptoms are mentioned in the Srimad Bhagavatam and are very much visible to us in present times. There's a lot of fights happening, lots of wars happening, a lot of confusion, a lot of quarrels happening. Meat eating is rampant, intoxication, gambling, illicit sex, extra marital affairs, live-in relationships. What was not so common, what was unheard of maybe 20 years back, has become very common. So, as devotees of the Lord, yes, we have a duty to see how we can reach this message, distribute the holy name to people. It's our duty, it's Prabhupada's mission, we are part of that mission. But Chaitanya Mahaprabhu says, janma sarthaka kari karopakaar. First we have to protect ourselves. First we have to realize that yes, Kali Yuga is at its full force and things are degrading. We have to protect ourselves, we have to insulate ourselves. Like in the flight also, when you travel, they say that the oxygen mask will fall down from the top. Before helping others, kindly put the mask. So we have to help ourselves, we have to protect ourselves, insulate ourselves from the ill effects of Kali Yuga and try our best, whatever we can, to help others. Janma Sarthaka Kari Karopakaar. I came across this story, in fact it's not a story, I saw a television episode more than 20 years back, and those days I think it was black and white television and I even till date remember this. But those days it was not like you like some episode you save it, you can download it, it's on YouTube. I vaguely remember there was a man, he was very, very poverty-stricken and practically had no money with him. He had a young daughter and he was barely able to manage the day-to-day needs of the family, including groceries and clothes. So the daughter was getting to marriageable age and he was in anxiety how to marry the daughter. Even in Vedic times there was, you know, marriage, it was done in a way some dowry was also given, not in the sense what we see in present times it is demanded, it was out of love. So this person was a lot of a lot of anxiety, a lot of worried how to manage his household expenses, how to marry the daughter, and he was trying various means to earn money, but things were not happening. So one day a friend of his gave him a challenge. He said, you need money? I can give you enough money, so much money that for the rest of the life you can live happily. You don't have to worry about your finances. Not only that, you can very easily get your daughter married. So he couldn't believe such a thing as possible. He said, but there is a challenge. Now he was so desperate for money, he said, I am ready to accept any challenge. He said, the challenge is that you have to live in a room all by yourself, all alone, for ten years. Three times the food will be given in that cell, in that room. But you will not be allowed to interact with anyone, including your family, including your near and dear ones. Even in prisons they have, you know, in a month you can, for some five minutes or something you can meet. There are some protocols. But here the condition was that for ten years you cannot talk to anyone, you cannot meet anyone, you cannot hear anything. There were no smartphones, there was no mp3 players. You can hear the songs, FM radio, nothing. And you all know that man is a social animal. We thrive on relationships, social beings. Relationships are very important. So this man accepted the challenge and he thought, instead of struggling the whole life, it's a struggle for ten years. I'll somehow manage. But he never anticipated what that struggle would be, how intense that struggle would be. He thought. So this whole documentary or film what was made is basically to showcase his emotions, what this person went through, what struggles. And this whole camera was focusing on his facial expressions, on what he was doing during those ten days. These days you have some reality shows. Very. Somebody is put in some house. I haven't seen anything, just maybe some idea. And then people watch what they are doing. So for ten years he was in this room, locked. Three times food was served to him. And then first initial one week, ten days, he was, he started missing something, something unusual. See, one day we can manage with food, second day, you know, something, we feel very uncomfortable. So likewise, if we are in a room all by itself, one day, two day, we can somehow manage. So after one week, ten days, he started feeling very uneasy and that started showing up on his face, on his expression. He started becoming uncomfortable, little jittery, felt like talking to someone. There's nobody. Even the camera captured whatever he was speaking, whatever he was expressing, whatever words he was speaking also was being captured, was being shown. So he even started thinking that, is it really worthwhile taking this challenge and stay for ten years? Because seven days itself was so difficult. And he started even thinking, maybe, let me walk out. But then, what else will he do? How will he get the money? So he continued. One month passed, two months passed, three months passed. And then, after three months, he became so agitated because of loneliness. No companionship, nobody to talk to. Sometimes he would be shouting, screaming, hitting his head on the walls. He started becoming a little bit mad.

---
#### transformation, Bhagavad Gita, higher taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XLaAtB67aPQ?t=1851
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Little by little he started getting interested in what is being written, what is written in the Gita."
**Summary:** Continuing the story, the speaker describes the man's descent into near madness due to isolation, until he receives a Bhagavad Gita and gradually finds solace and transformation through reading it.

Anyway, he kept on going one year passed, two years passed, three years passed. And now he almost became like mad. He had big beard. You know, hairs not combed, maybe not bathing daily, and at times screaming, at times howling. The camera would capture, he would be lying for hours together on the floor, no proper time for sleeping. You know, we all have a schedule, morning we get up, we have to go for work, somebody comes, meet, and then night time comes, we sleep. But here you have no one, you practically don't know whether it's day or night, it's a dark dungeon room all alone. So three years passed, four years passed, and it was quite evident from his face that he's suffering, he's suffering, he's miserable, he's not able to take it. But somewhere, whatever sanity he had, he was trying to reconcile, another five years, another five years. Four years. And then a couple of more years passed, seven years, eight years. By now he had practically become mad. You would see the camera would capture, he would start screaming, whatever he's speaking is incoherent, whatever is not making any sense. He's shouting, he's screaming, all by himself. Just like you go to a Nimhans or some temple. Mad people, you can see, they'll be laughing, no reason, talking, no reason. What they're talking is all gibberish. So the camera was capturing everything. You could hear what he was speaking, you could hear what he was shouting, screaming, everything. Now nine years passed. This man, although a little bit, whatever little sanity is waiting, when that one year will pass, and he can walk out. Sometimes some very interesting thing which has to happen or some very important festival or something. So we wait, countdown, countdown starts. 30 days left, 35, 28 days left. So in his mind, probably the countdown had begun. So then. What? What? What? What? I clearly remember, somehow he got a copy of Bhagavad Gita. Just for your information, in ISKCON also, many devotees go and preach in prisons. And just for your information. Everyone who is behind bars or who is in prison is, quite possible, they could be innocent people also. Not everyone is hardcore criminal. So devotees go and many times the Gitas also are distributed. So somehow this person got the Gita. And so the camera is not, what is he doing with the Gita? He is all alone, all the time in the world. Nothing else to do. Started reading Gita. So initially when he was reading Gita, it was quite evident from his face, facial expression, it is not making any sense to him. Probably he is not able to understand what is written. Like it happens when you, you know, first try to read Gita. It may look like it's very philosophical and maybe for some it may be difficult to understand. So he started reading and then it was quite evident that it is not interesting. It is not making any sense. So he would, you know, flip few pages and then he would close it and keep it. Again he would pick up after some time or maybe after few days. And then the camera captured very interesting thing, little by little he started getting interested in what is being written, what is written in the Gita. So he started with some bit of sobriety reading what's there in the Gita. He started becoming little sober. It appeared. The whole episode was the camera is capturing the action so whole expression was that what he's reading is now started making sense to him and then nine years six months passed and now daily three hours four hours he's reading Gita and then seven months passed nine years seven months pass by now he had calmed down all that agitation all that anxiety all that screaming shouting howling all that had calmed down and then six hours seven hours he's reading Gita.

**Summary:** The story concludes with the man's complete transformation through the Gita, leading him to renounce the promised money and understand the true purpose of life, connecting this to the challenges and allurements of Kali Yuga.

Nine years nine months passed and now he was completely calm he was very sober very intensely very sincerely reading Gita daily nine years 11 months passed 30 days to go and you could see from his face there is some bit of divinity reflecting know some sages very austere you can see from their face so I understanding the templ argues that there are some bigÊ over the camera captured that very calm very so poor very serene very content contentment reflecting on his face ten days left nine years 11 months 20 days over he started experiencing so much of joy so much of bliss so much of happening so many what you were completely focused and reading Gita, that started feeling that whatever I had to gain in life, whatever I want, I have already achieved. After all why we need money? Why we need ultimately the common denominator in everyone's life, whether somebody needs money, somebody needs adventure, somebody is fond of traveling, somebody is fond of renunciation, somebody is fond of different hobbies, ultimately it's for happiness, feeling good. So the camera was capturing, which is in line with the pastime of Dhruva Maharaj, who went to the forest, did austerities and then when the Lord came, gave darshan, and said, what do you want? He said, I don't want anything. Now that I have got it, I was looking for broken pieces of glass. Now that I have got you, which is like valuable jewel, what I was looking for was nothing. I don't want. I don't want any I don't want any benediction, I just want you. Because now that I have you, I have everything. So maybe the inspiration from this particular pastime, so this person, 9 years, 11 months, one day left for him to walk out of that room with that huge sum of money. He felt that money is not required. Now that he has everything, he has understood the purpose of life, he has understood the message of Krishna. Then this person is shown that one day before his death, 10th year getting completed he walks out of that room and he says I don't want your money. Maybe whatever that episode was made or that film was made, inspiration must have been taken from Dhruva Maharaj's past time. Wanted to maybe convey in this thing. The point here is in Kali Yuga, we will be tempted. There are lot of distractions, there are lot of allurements and as time is passing by, it's on the rise.

#### addiction, social media, higher taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XLaAtB67aPQ?t=2478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Many of us may not realize or already would have realized that how it is addictive."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the addictive nature of smartphones and social media, highlighting how algorithms are designed to capture attention, and emphasizes the importance of protecting oneself from these distractions in Kali Yuga.

Many of us may not be even aware that we may already be addicted to smart phones. These algorithms have been designed by the best of brains, MIT, Harvard, Stanford, all these elite students, they work in Facebook, Google, all that and they are, actually in one sense, the common people are guinea pigs. Our, so to say, our innocence is very much being played with. We are victims. No doubt I am not against social media or smart phones but many of us may not realize or already would have realized that how it is addictive. 15 minutes, 20 minutes, you want to watch something or do something but then our innocence has passed by. Because that's how it is designed. The algorithms are designed. You see something, the next related thing will be served. We are having a symposium going on which says that whole principle is to capture your attention. Whatever content you produce, if you are able to capture the attention, they will promote your content. And the content creators, brainy fellows in different fields producing content. And it's flooded with content. And algorithms are serving that content and capturing our attention. And that captured attention is being sold to the advertisers. Okay, you have a product, whom do you want to show? Which demography, which country, which age group, which gender, which city, which town, which locality? I will serve. Give me this much money. They may not say, but this is something. This is how the whole industry is. So we as devotees, janma sarthakari, we have got Prabhupada's message. We have got brahmanda brahmite kono bhagyavan jeev, guru krishna prasade paaye bhakti lata abhij. We've got the bija. But we should protect the creeper of bhakti. And as I said with the influence of Kali Yoga, there are a lot of allurements, lot of temptations, lot of distractions. And if we don't safeguard ourselves, we will fall for it. Krishna also in the Gita says just by artificially restraining ourselves, restricting ourselves, we cannot protect. We cannot say, I will not see, I will not do, I will not do this, I will not say this. Sometimes devotees can also fall in a гита and become a kojna, and by that you can say, I will not do this, I will not do this, I will not do this. Sometimes, devotees can also fall in a gita and become a kojna. You say, no, I will not do this, I will not do this, I will not do this. You see, we move from place to place and they think, in a trap of that what we try to resist, it persists. The more we say no, no, no, no, no, it again hits us back with more intense force. So that's why Krishna in the Gita says, raso varjam rasopi asya param drishtva nivartate, by experiencing a higher taste.

#### higher taste, detachment, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XLaAtB67aPQ?t=2703
**Verse References:** BG 2.59
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you have no higher taste, if you are not relishing higher taste, you will fall for lower taste."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that experiencing a higher taste is essential for avoiding lower temptations, using an analogy of eating unpalatable food out of necessity and referencing Krishna's teaching on detachment through higher engagement.

And how will we experience a higher taste? All the emanations, all the tools, everything Prabhupada has given. If we don't use that, if we don't experience a higher taste, we will fall for lower taste. I remember when there was some curfew, I was studying, when I was doing my engineering, we were in the third year, sixth semester, and somehow there was curfew and we were stuck in the hostel. It was a long curfew. And even the mess, the canteen where the food was cooked, that was also shut down and people had left. So few of us were there in the hostel, we cannot now take a bus and go back home. We were in the hostel. The messes closed. And I clearly remember that day, not because we were fasting sometimes we don't eat, not because food is not available. We chose not to eat. But that was a day when morning to evening we had no option to eat. There is nothing around which you can eat. So it was 4 o'clock in the evening. Although there was a curfew, we were friends and walked out of our room. We went to nearby kiosk where they used to make some tea coffee and all that and there was one small shop it was closed, we somehow requested it and got some rice. I can relate with what is hunger it was 4 in the evening, not eaten since morning, maybe previous night also not eaten, got some rice there was nothing, no vegetable no dal, no masala this thing and all that, came back to the room and in hostel room, some of you may be able to relate with what I am saying maybe times have changed so we used to have those electric heaters, raw coils and then some pan to boil water or to make tea sometime and then we used to have and in hostel typically that pan is full black because you don't wash it so often or maybe you don't know how to wash it so came back to the room had never cooked rice no idea how the rice is cooked but circumstances of such you have to necessity is the mother of invention so put the rice, put the water and put it on that heater and slowly that water started boiling and no idea how much it has to boil how long it has to boil and first time in my life I saw black rice you have heard of brown rice, red rice I saw cooked black rice and couple of my friends we had option either throw it visual thing was to throw it but stomach was saying no eat it and we ate it I guarantee if anybody where to see that rice you will spit at the thought of eating it But if you have no option, you will eat it. So same is the case with us. If you have no higher taste, if you are not relishing higher taste, if you are not chanting daily, regularly, Krishna also talks about how you can be like a lotus in a dirty pond, untouched by the dirt of the pond.

So, Prabhupada has given us everything. The higher taste is there. Nice, wonderful kirtans, deities. We can come. We can spiritually rejuvenate, recharge. The temples are like oasis. It's like a desert outside. Desert in the sense, although it may be raining, we may have green parks or something. A desert of material existence. We are parched, we are thirsty. Real pleasure is not there. And we are hankering for that. So, if we have to, so it is, Prabhupada has given us the morning sadhana. So, if we have that higher taste, if we are on a daily basis taking that medicine, chanting, reading, hearing, then we will not fall for the lower things. These days, lot of people are struggling with different kinds of addiction. Alcohol, smoking, drinking, social media, smartphones, wrong sites. And people are also trying different things, how to come out of those addictions. There was an article called six human needs. They are needs. Just like food, clothing, shelter. It's a need. Likewise, there are other needs. Needs for variety, needs for stability, needs for recreation. And if we don't get in a right way, the right quality of rasa, then we will fall for lower rasas. And that is what Kali Yuga is. The higher taste is missing in people. That's why people are just helplessly falling for the lower tastes. So, we can always blame. People hope against hope. The new government will come. Ache din aayenge. Ache din aane wale hain. Yesterday in the symposium, somebody was saying how the campaign is made. Political parties, they strategize. People believe. Ache din aane wale hain. And they vote. Unless our consciousness changes. Unless our... One thing is very clear. You all can relate with this. We always think that if I achieve this, I will be happy. A small story. There was a person, a king, living in a palace. But somehow he was not happy. Always, you know, sad, morose. Even though he was living in a palace. Had everything. All the opulence, all the luxuries. Everything at his beck and call. But not happy. And whenever he would come on his balcony, he would see... He could see at a distance, there was one small hut made of thatched roof. And the person staying, that farmer who was staying in that hut, whenever he would walk, he would just be fully jubilant, smiling, happy, singing, dancing.

#### happiness, contentment, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XLaAtB67aPQ?t=3234
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Because real happiness is a different commodity. Money cannot buy that."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about a king envious of a happy farmer, illustrating that material possessions do not guarantee happiness, and introduces the concept of the '99 club' to explain the dissatisfaction that arises from focusing on what is lacking rather than appreciating what one has.

So the king became jealous. Became envious. What is this? I have everything. And I am not happy. This person is smiling. This happened with me also. When I was... My father was in public sector. He used to live in a big bungalow. And, you know, everything was there. All facilities was there. Somehow, exam time, lot of stress, tension, anxiety. And there was one servant. Servant quarter. He would be coming and then he would have a transistor and he would put some film song and he would, you know, smile. I used to also feel, you know. He is smiling. He is happy. Living in a servant quarter. Living in a big bungalow. And same is the case with most of the people. Don't think Mukesh Ambani is fully happy. Although he may show. Same is the case with everyone. Because real happiness is a different commodity. Money cannot buy that. Money is important for... Money can buy us lot of goods, lot of materials, lot of things. But not necessarily person who has money is happy. So, this person would walk out of his house and he was very happy. Always singing, smiling. So, the king became envious. So, he asked his minister, How is it that I am not happy? I have everything. I am living in a palace. And this person has nothing. But he seems to be smiling. So, the minister immediately looked at the king. Looks like he has not joined the 99 club. So, the king had heard of Rotary club, Lions club. What is this 99 club? So, he said, I will show you what is 99 club. And what it means to join 99 club. So, then this minister took a bag of 99 gold coins. And one night, quietly went and placed this bag outside the door of this hut. So, next morning, this farmer, he came out of the house. He saw something very unusual just outside the door. He saw a bag. He became curious. He picked up the bag. He opened the bag. And he was delighted. 99... Oh, sorry. Gold coins. He didn't know how many. So, when you get something, some lotteries of curiosity is that, okay, how much? How much? So, he started opening the bag. Started counting the coins. One, two, three, four, five. And the last coin, it was 99. So, he said, something is wrong. One coin is missing. Generally, if you get a bundle of notes also, you know, it could be either 100. Many times, you don't even count. Somebody says this is a bundle of 100. 100 notes. So, he counted again. One, two, three, four. And the last coin he counted, 99. So, he should be happy or sad? He became sad. Where is the one hundredth gold coin? Who took it? Where did it go? So, then he said, doesn't matter. maybe Lord wants me to work hard to get it so till then he had left lived a very carefree life happy-go-lucky merry life and then from that day onwards he started working hard to earn enough money so that he can collect enough money buy a gold coin and have the satisfaction and happiness that he has 97 hundred gold coins early he used to very leisurely come out of his house whatever time he would feel he would work and then come back relax and then now he would go out of his house early in the morning 8 o'clock work very hard come back late stressed anxiety and the King noticed that from his balcony certainly what has happened to him there's no longer smile on his face he's always there's no smile on his face he's always stressed out coming back late to his horse so minister now he has joined the 99 Club.

#### gratitude, contentment, aspirations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XLaAtB67aPQ?t=3532
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "99 Club means you have 99 things to be happy 99 things in your life which are good to be grateful for to be happy to be delighted to be thankful to thank Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the '99 club' concept, urging gratitude for existing blessings rather than focusing on what is lacking, and emphasizes that while aspirations are acceptable, they should not be the sole source of happiness.

So all of us can laugh but we are also a part of this 99 Club what is 99 Club 99 Club means you have 99 things to be happy 99 things in your life which are good to be grateful for to be happy to be delighted to be thankful to thank Lord Krishna Krishna you have given me so much but we are crying waiting for one thing to happen when we will be happy when we will go back to Krishna and say thank you Krishna you have given me so much if that is our state will never be happened and most of us are not grateful at times even as devotees sulking finding faults criticizing blaming blasphemy How will we experience higher taste? Higher taste is not just, okay, chanting is very important, reading is very important, but what do we do the rest of our day? Are we always sulking, complaining, blaming, finding faults, cursing people, cursing others, cursing the environment, cursing the traffic? Or counting our blessings, Krishna you are so kind, Krishna you are so merciful, Prabhupada you have given me so much, you have given me the holy name, I am so rich, I am so fortunate, I have everything in this world. So it is very important that we count our blessings, we be grateful to Krishna for giving us so much. Yes, all of us may be lacking something, everyone will be lacking something, something may be missing. Yes, we may also have some goals, I want to achieve this, be this, do this, have this, that's okay, that's fine, we are not saying that life should be completely bland, yes, we should be contented with what we have and for the sake of doing good to others, we should have some goals, Prabhupada also was very, had aspirations, there is nothing wrong in having aspirations, I have to distribute more books, reach out to more people, open more temples. But goals not because I am lacking something and with the hope, with the desire that one day when I have that, I will be happy. Then it's a rat race and there is no end to it. I remember when I was in tenth, my father used to tell me, if you get good marks in tenth, your life is done, then you know, everyone is laughing because you have also heard from your parents. Then same thing repeated in twelfth, but twelfth it was with more force beta, this is the time when you get in good engineering college, then whether four years, five years, at least you will be an engineer. But after you get into engineering college, it's a rat race, always, there is a book written by a professor in MIT, who did his studies in MIT and lived there. He said, I want to be an engineer. He said, I want to be an engineer. He said, I want to be an engineer. Later on, he became a professor and he has written a book on happiness.

---
#### repentance, divine plan, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxWcBMs-zpY?t=33
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Repentance comes in the mind of a good soul as soon as he commits something wrong."
**Summary:** The speaker reads the translation and purport by Srila Prabhupada, emphasizing the importance of repentance and the Lord's plan in the events surrounding King Parikshit.

Translation and purport by His Divine Grace, Sri Bhaktivedanta Swamishila Prabhupāda. Translation: Upon returning, he began to contemplate and argue within himself whether the sage had actually been in meditation with the sense concentrated and eyes closed, or whether he had just been feigning trance just to avoid receiving a lower kshatriya. Purport: A king being a devotee of the Lord did not approve of his own action and thus he began to wonder whether the sage was really in a trance or was just pretending in order to avoid receiving the king. He was a kshatriya and therefore lower in rank. Repentance comes in the mind of a good soul as soon as he commits something wrong. Srila Vishwanatha Chakravarti Thakura and Srila Jiva Goswami do not believe that the king's action was due to his past misdeeds. The arrangements were so made by the Lord just to call the king back to home, back to Godhead. According to Srila Vishwanatha Chakravarti, the plan was made by the will of the Lord and by the will of the Lord the situation of frustration was created. The plan was that for his so-called misdeed the king could be cursed by an inexperienced Brahmana boy infected by the influence of Kali and thus the king would leave his hearth and womb for good. His connection with Srila Shukadeva Goswami would enable the presentation of great Srimad Bhagavatam which is considered to be a book incarnation of the Lord. This book incarnation of the Lord gives much fascinating information of the transcendental pastime of the Lord like his Rasalila with the spiritual cowherd damsels of Rajabhumi. This specific pastime of the Lord has special significance because anyone who properly learns about this particular pastime of the Lord will certainly be desuited from mundane sex desire and be placed on the path of sublime devotional service to the Lord. The pure devotee's mundane frustration is meant to elevate the devotee to a higher transcendental position. By placing Arjuna and Pandavas in frustration due to the interview of their cousin brothers the prelude of battle of Urukshetra was created by the Lord. This was so incarnated the sound representation of the Lord Bhagavad Gita. So by placing Arjuna and Pandavas and by placing King Pariksit in an acquired position the incarnation of Srimad Bhagavatam was created by the will of the Lord. Being distressed by the hunger and thirst was only a show because King endured much even in the womb of his mother. He was never disturbed by the glaring heat of Brahmastra released by Ashwatthama. The King's distressed condition was certainly unprecedented. The devotee like Maharaj Pariksit are powerful enough to forbear such distress. Such distress by the will of the Lord and they are never disturbed. This situation in this case was therefore all planned by the Lord. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Karupada.

---
#### Krishna consciousness, etiquette, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxWcBMs-zpY?t=228
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As soon as the Pariksit Maharaj went, there is no response and Sage was sitting silent in a meditation."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of the verse for Krishna consciousness practitioners and recounts the events leading up to the verse, including Parikshit Maharaj's anger and actions.

Hare Krishna. Today's verse is a very, very important verse especially for those who are practicing Krishna Consciousness. Why and how we will see in this verse? Before that, before this verse, we know the previous verses, how and what position Pariksit Maharaj came and how he came in. Once Pariksit Maharaj went to hunt and by the will of the Lord he became fully fatty and thirsty and he entered the Hermitage of King Shamikarushi. And usual general etiquette or anyone guest comes to our house what we will do? We will give at least, at least we will smile at them. Smile at them or give a glass of water or something like that. And also who is going to where? Is a King, Emperor of the world is going to Hermitage of a Shamikarushi and Rishi, Sage. But what happened is opposite happened there. Because he is unprecedented. That's why in the previous verse also if you see Prabhupada writes is unprecedented events. And as soon as the Pariksit Maharaj went there is no response and Sage was sitting silent in a meditation. And even Pariksit Maharaj is a great Emperor and he was a King. And more than that he was a devotee of the Lord. But situation was totally opposite. Then as in the previous verses mentioned the will of the Lord the King Pariksit Maharaj become very angry. Very angry. Then what happened? He took a dead snake and put around his shoulder and he came out from the Hermitage. And while coming to his kingdom this happened in this verse is explained. What has happened is explained here.

#### contemplation, devotee, Srimad Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxWcBMs-zpY?t=371
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is a ripened fruit of all Vedic literature."
**Summary:** The speaker reads the translation of the verse and discusses Parikshit Maharaj's contemplation and the importance of Srimad Bhagavatam as the essence of all Vedic literature.

Upon returning he began to contemplate. He began to contemplate. That's why he said Prabhupada writes in the purport. The King that being a devotee of the Lord did not approve his own actions. Thus began to wonder whether the Sage was really trans or pretending to order to avoid receiving the King. The translation is saying upon returning he began to contemplate and argue within himself. He is arguing within himself. Whether the Sage had actually been a meditation with the sense concentrated and closed eyes or whether he had just been feigning pretending trans just to avoid receiving a lower Kshatriya. See there is a order lower Kshatriya. He is not saying I am the King. He is feeling a lower Kshatriya. Means Brahmanas are always highly respected. Brahmanas are always highly respected. That is our culture. But here there is a different situation happened. Let us hear. Srimad Bhagavatam is not ordinary literature. Srimad Bhagavatam is a ripened fruit of all Vedic literature. Essence Saram Saram all the Saras are explained in Srimad Bhagavatam. And everything is there in the Srimad Bhagavatam. Everything. Political, economical, social, moral, religious. Everything is there in this Bhagavatam. That is why Bhagavatam is a complete whole.

**Summary:** The speaker presents the answer to the question of where religious principles went, explaining that Srimad Bhagavatam is the brilliant sun that arose after Krishna's departure, providing knowledge and guidance in Kali Yuga.

And answer this question Shukadeva, Suta Goswami answers this question in a first canto 3rd chapter 43rd verse. He says bruhi yegeshvare krishne brahmani dharma varnami swamkshata adunopeti dharmakam sharanam gatah. It is mentioned since Shri Krishna, the absolute truth master of the mystic master has departed from this own abode, please tell us who is the religious principle has taken. Answer to this question krishne svadhamno bhagate dharmo jnanadivihi saha kalo nasta dharmashe purna krodhunavati vidhi. The translation: This Bhagavatam Purana is brilliant as a sun and it arises just after departed of Lord Krishna to his own abode. It is a brilliant sun. Brilliant sun means there is no darkness. Brilliant sun. Accompanied by religious not only religious Prabhupada translates knowledge, not only knowledge etc. etc. means morality. Prabhupada explains another place political. Person who has lost their vision due to the dense dark, dense darkness of ignorance in the age of kali shall get light from this Purana. That's why Srimad Bhagavatam is various verse actually. That's why dharmo nirmastranam satham Bhagavatam begins verse like that. Because dharma, what is actual dharma explained in Srimad Bhagavatam. Here if you see in the verse what we are hearing, the sages came but he didn't respond. King went to hermitage of sage, he didn't respond. It is effect of kali yuga. Beginning of kali yuga. But in this kali yuga, the brilliant sun like Srimad Bhagavatam gives a knowledge and all the required facility one who is serious about spiritual life. That's why Srimad Bhagavatam is very very important. And especially in this verse, it is very very important for all of us to understand especially those who are practicing devotional service.

This is one of the most important examples because Srimad Bhagavatam begins begins with Parikshit Maharaj's past time. As a practitioner of Krishna consciousness we have to watch full of our thoughts. Not only thoughts, our actions also. Not like that, we are practicing Krishna consciousness, we can do whatever we like to do. No. We are above the modes of all the material natures. No. Krishna says No. What is the last verse of 14th chapter? To get out from the three modes of material nature we have to do means pure unalloyed devotional service we have to do. Then only we can come out from the three modes of material nature. Otherwise, three modes of material nature is very difficult to overcome. That's why Krishna is saying, Daivyesha gunamai mamamaya duratya. It's a my maya. It is not ordinary person's maya. Mamamaya duratya. Maameva prapadyante maya metam tarantite. One who surrendered to Krishna he can be relieved from this three modes of material nature. But, the pure devotee of the Lord is not under the material nature. That is the meaning of pure devotee of the Lord. He is above the three modes of material nature. He will be doing a pure unalloyed devotional service of the Lord.

Janma and karma activities of Krishna and his devotees is not ordinary. It is a transcendental. It is a transcendental. In reality we have to understand. Reality we have to understand. And how reality we have to understand Krishna again explains in Srimad Bhagavad Gita 9th chapter. It is not like that simply by feeling, okay when we go in front of the devotees we will cry after that. Not like that. You have to understand Buddha and Bhava. We should have a feelings and we should have a should have a truth. In a truth we have to understand Krishna. Perfect questions and perfect answers. There is no some different questions and different. No. Perfect questions and perfect answers. But he is thinking and he is contemplating, contemplating. Thus he began to wonder whether this sage is really meditation or he is to avoid this lower Kshatriya. Maybe he is in meditation. He is doing meditation. I disturb him. Or he is want to avoid this lower Kshatriya association. See this is a mood of a king because the king is a devotee of the Lord. Devotees never blame others. Devotees are very humble in nature. They will not expect. Always give respect. And have to think whether it is really mistake happened and what I can do. They will not blame. Even though sometimes past misdeeds also that's why Brahma says, tattenukampam supameekshamanam munjana evatmam kurtam vipakam Even the highest of the highest great calamities happens also will think it is my past misdeeds. It is not because of somebody else. He will not blame anyone else. He will always take responsibility. Because of my past misdeed it happened like this. This is one of the lesson we can learn from this verse.
