#### leadership, adaptation, deity worship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MmZ_mteihGg?t=1800
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhupada did ask me one time, this was in 1973 or four. He said, well, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know. He said, how are you all worshipping the deity?"
**Summary:** The speaker describes Prabhupada's handling of a khajur rasa incident, his views on Lord Chaitanya wearing a peacock feather, and his inquiry into deity worship practices, illustrating Prabhupada's leadership, adaptability, and emphasis on proper worship.

And Bhavananda was the president. Bhavananda Prabhu was the president. And so on the arrival of Shirdi Prabhupada, Bhavananda said, Shirdi Prabhupada, the devotees, they're taking this khajur rasa. This is the the rust, the sap from the palm, the palm tree. They have date palm trees. And they cut the palm and then the juice comes out. Then they boil it down and it turns into molasses, gaur. But you can also drink it as juice. They make it hot and they drink it as juice. But if you, if it's left too long, especially after sunrise, it starts to ferment and becomes alcoholic. So, Bhavananda was complaining to Prabhupada. They're taking this khajur rasa, which is very strong Shirdi Prabhupada, just like whiskey. And the devotees are drinking it. Prabhupada said, oh, oh, really? They didn't say anything more at the time. So then, next, the next morning, we were in, with Darshan, with Prabhupada. And one devotee named Dvija Hari does. He was a marine, kind of a tough guy. And he said, Srila Prabhupada, he said, me, sometimes me and the boys, we take a japa walk down the road and we go where they're making this khajur rasa and we take a few. It was really funny. The way he said, he was like, you know, me and the boys, we go down the bar and we knock a few back. I was laughing. The way he was spelling, explaining to Prabhupada. And Prabhupada was hearing, and he's going, oh, really, really? And he asked Prabhupada, it's really, really good. So now, Prabhupada had to make a decision. He heard the management side of it, how he was trying to stop. And then he had the devotees, some of the devotees were liking it. So, Prabhupada had to make a decision. So the next morning, we went in there, for Darshan with Prabhupada. And then, Shruti Kirti Prabhu came in with a glass of khajuras. And he said, Srila Prabhupada, would you like some khajuras? Prabhupada, he looked at it and, Prabhupada knew what it was. He's a Bengali. Everyone knows, Bengali, khajuras. Hmm, it's quite nice. Hmm, nice. Bring me another glass, empty glass. Shruti Kirti put him another glass. He poured, he said, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. It was his glass. He poured it into the other glass. Gave it to Shruti. He said, pass it around to all the devotees so they can drink. So all the devotees were sitting around, drinking khajuras. Bhavananda was sitting there, he was fuming. Couldn't get his, couldn't get his, couldn't get his, get his way down. He was fuming. But all the devotees. So we see how Prabhupada would adjust things according to time and circumstances. It was 1973 and we had just completed the Lotus building in Mahipur and I went to Prabhupada's room and asked him a question. I said, can Lord Chaitanya wear a peacock feather and he said, why not, he is Krishna. I said, only some of the Gaudiya Math people, they are complaining, they are saying Lord Chaitanya shouldn't wear a peacock feather and Srila Prabhupada became very disappointed and he kind of just looked down, he didn't say anything else. I took it to mean that my Gaud brothers, they always criticise me, in fact he wrote one letter, how can they help us and they can only hinder us, it was an open letter. So that was my understanding that Prabhupada was saying, why are they picking on little points like this, why don't they see what he has done, he has picked us out of the gutter of material existence. We have shaved heads, we have tilak, we have tautis and we are chanting Krishna's name, look at what he has done, rather than trying to find a little detailed fault here and there. That's the way I took Prabhupada's mood. So anyway, since that time I have found many evidences that Lord Chaitanya, where he describes Lord Chaitanya with a peacock feather, in the Gauranga Mahimara for the way to chara, the description of Mahaprabhu and meditation, he says he has his shikipucha, the peacock feather in his hair, and another one over his ear also, and then there is another, in the Nitya Leela of Mahaprabhu, when he, after breakfast he goes with his friends to Sankirtan and they see all the white cows and the peacocks and everything and he gets into his, he corresponds with Vrindavan Leela, when Krishna goes out with the cows in the morning, so he gets into it. He's in the same mood, so he starts calling out all of his cows, shamalai, dhubalai, kangai, jamunai, come on, and all the devotees, what's happening, oh, he's in the mood of Krishna in Vrindavan, so they all assume the same mood as him, they all pick up sticks and start twirling them around like cowboys doing, hai, hai, hai, let's go up to Gaurana, Mithunanda Prabhupada tends to have a buffalo horn, go around, hai, and then that description also is he, Lord Chaitanya wears a peacock feather. And also Prabhupada asked Malati Mataji one time that, why Lord Chaitanya's not wearing a peacock feather? She said, oh, he should be wearing, he said, yes, and she said, what about Mithunanda Prabhu? He said, sometimes, Prabhupada said, sometimes he can wear, but Lord Chaitanya should always wear. Anyway, so we have Guru Saraswati Shastri, it became a little bit controversial, but Prabhupada did ask me one time, this was in 1973 or four. He said, well, I don't know, how are you all worshipping the deity?

---
#### deity worship, tradition, personal service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MmZ_mteihGg?t=2400
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You can please your guru, maybe just by your dress."
**Summary:** The speaker details Prabhupada's instructions on deity worship, his disapproval of Govardhan Shila worship at the time, and his allowance for prayers in one's own language, emphasizing the importance of following instructions and the significance of personal service.

So, I gave him a step-by-step explanation. And we were undressing the deities and we bathed the deities with tea lark, some lemon juice. And we polished them. Then we cleaned them, we bathed them with water. And he said, what mantras are you using? I said, well, we chant Chintamani prayers, Prabhupada. I said, also, we chant Brahma Gayatri. The first thing we would do, we just chant the Brahma Gayatri voice. Oh, Prabhupada said, oh, Brahma Gayatri. Vedic. Aum is there. Very good. Vedic mantra Aum is there. Very good. So that was it. We were just chanting the Brahma Gayatri and Chintamani prayers and bathing. Anyway, then I said, then we dry the deity. Then Prabhupada said, yes. Then at that time, you should offer Tulsi leaves to Krishna's feet, which you don't find in other archana paratis. The offering of the Tulsi generally comes after the bathing, the dressing, the ornamentation, offering scents and things like that. Then you offer flowers and Tulsi at that time. Prabhupada said, at that time you offer Tulsi leaves. When Krishna just, you just bathe and dry it to Krishna. And I took it to mean, like, no, you just, Krishna's just been bathed and dried and he's like, he's naked. So the first offering, what is the first offering? Like Tulsi, Bhakti Devi. If we offer Tulsi, then Krishna's very satisfied. I took it like that. And it may, may be correct, that understanding. Because Srila Bhaktisiddhanta Swaswati Thakur was, installing some deities in one of his Gaudiya Math. And his disciples were doing all the preparations and they, they bathed the deities and everything, got everything ready, dressed the deities and Tulsis and flowers. And then they called in their Guru Maharaj to come and do the Pramaputrista, which is the official calling of the Lord. Generally the Acharya comes and he calls the Lord of his heart, who is yesterday, but generally of the Sampradaya. He calls the deity from his heart into the deity. So this is, it's called the Pramaputrista, the official installation, calling of the Lord. So he, they say Guru Maharaj, you can come in and do the Pramaputrista. So he come in to perform the Pramaputrista and he looked and he saw the Tulsi leaves on Krishna's feet. And he said, oh, now I have nothing to do. Which indicated, he thought that also that Tulsi was like the prime, prime offering for Krishna. More important than even the Pramaputrista. I guess it has something to do with Bhakti. In 1972 also, then one devotee bought some Govardhan Shilas from Govardhan Hill to Mayapur. So Chittananda said, oh great, I always wanted to worship Govardhan Shilas. So he wrote to Prabhupada about, we have Govardhan Shilas, can we put them on the altar? And Prabhupada wrote back immediately, take them back to Vrindavan. And as far as my research went, there was no Govardhan Shilas being worshipped in any temple during Srila Prabhupada's time. And there was another devotee in America, he wrote to Srila Prabhupada and said, I have Govardhan Shilas, can I put them on the altar? And Prabhupada said, when I come there, I will see. And when he came, he said, no, don't put them on the altar. But now they're everywhere. It's a, but anyway, Prabhupada told us to take it back to Vrindavan. Another time, probably 1973 again, and Srila Prabhupada told me, he said, you can offer prayers in your own language, if you like. I used to chant the prayers in the morning, when we used to chant the Guru Vastakam and what did we else we chant? Chintamani or something like that. Maybe Uddalaiyur or not. And I would recite the translations, because Prachuttananda was there, he had just translated all those books. And I would have to recite them. I don't know if it was anything to do with that or not, but I was kind of the reciter during those few years. And Prabhupada told me, you can chant, you can say your prayers through the deities in English, which according to the Shastra, you should only speak to the deity in Devanagari, in Sanskrit language. So, Prabhupada said, you can speak in your own language. And there is a, in the Babasha Purana, Babasha means in the future, the Purana on the future, and it said that one saffron-clad man will come from India and he will teach the Vedas in the language of the Malachas. So, English is the language of the Malachas. And he will initiate with fire. So, generally, everyone offers it when they get the Gayatri mantras, generally, that's a samskara, it needs a fire sacrifice. Prabhupada used to use the sacrifice of first initiation also, which is not mentioned in the Vedas, it's for the Gayatri mantras. But for the holy name, Prabhupada also, and for giving first name, he gave the Hare Krishna mantra in the first name. He used the fire sacrifice. So, that's something unique, which is spoken of in the Babasha Purana. So, that indicates, it indicates, it's Prabhupada. I was just going to perform the Rajabhogarati, and I just shaved, I had my head shaved, and I had just taken a bath, and put tealocks on, and I had my clean cloth on, and then the devotee comes, Prabhupada wants to see me in his room, so I went upstairs to see Srila Prabhupada. And I came in, paid my obeisances, and I said, did you want to see me, Srila Prabhupada? And he was looking at me, he was looking, sparkling eyes, very happy, and he was looking, he was looking me up and down, and he was very happy, and I thought, you know, he's just looking at someone else. But I think it was just my appearance, you know, I think Prabhupada thought, you know, a few years ago, we were rotting in the Gatha, you know, look, now we're wearing tealocks, and it's clean shaven, you know, and I thought, you know, Prabhupada, you know, Prabhupada, you know, it's just clean shaven. He seemed to be very, very pleased. So you can please your guru, maybe just by your dress.

---
#### Shaligram worship, devotional mood, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MmZ_mteihGg?t=2700
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Forget some say."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of establishing Shaligram worship in Mayapur, Prabhupada's instructions on its worship, and an incident where Prabhupada dismissed speculative opinions about the Shaligram's identity, emphasizing the importance of following Prabhupada's instructions and maintaining a devotional mood.

Prabhupada never directly instructed us to worship Govardhan, but he said you should send back to Govardhan Hill, whereas Shaligram Srila, he specifically says in his books, that where temples have sufficient facility, they should start Shaligram worship. So, Mayapur was the first temple that he instructed to start worshipping the Shaligram Srila. So he said, see if you can get one from some Srila, from some friend. So then Jaipur Thakur Maharaj, he had one Shaligram. So we went to Prabhupada's room, Jaipur Thakur Maharaj, Bhavananda and myself. And I don't know, I don't think Prabhupada knew we were coming even, but we went into his room, and Jaipur Thakur Maharaj said, we have the Shaligram Srila Prabhupada. So Prabhupada, he took the Shaligram, and he touched through his head, and then Srila Prabhupada did a puja for the Shaligram Srila, right where he was, sitting on his desk, with whatever was around him. He didn't even get up from his seat, but he did a little puja for Shaligram. And he got his tealock, he had his tealock there, and his Archman cup was there, and he made some tealock paste, and he had a box of matches, and he took a matchstick, and he painted a little face on Shaligram, little eyes, little nose, little mouth, little M-shaped mouth, E-seagull-shaped mouth, and there's little goby dots. And Jaipur Thakur Maharaj had some silk cloth from Shantipur, a very fine silk that they generated, and he offered the Shaligrams. And Prabhupada folded it like a dodi, and then he turned his tealock container upside down. It was like an egg cup shaped thing. So he put the dodi, and then he put the Shaligrams, a perfect fit. And then he folded the other piece of cloth like a little chada. So he had his throne, and he had his dress, and everything with tealock and little face. And at Prabhupada, he just sat down, and just whatever was there, he did this little puja. And he was talking, and then Prabhupada took his garland off, his own garland, and he put it around the Shaligram. And he was talking, saying different things. He said in the month of Vaisakha, that month has just passed just now, month of Vaisakha in summertime, he said you should do the, what we call the Shaligram, Tulsi Shaligram Jaldhan in the Vaisakha calendar. And they drip water on Shaligram, and also on Tulsi daily, in the hot countries at least. It's a cooling pastime. So he said you can perform this pastime with Shaligram. And he said, he said you can pick the Tulsi leaves in the morning after sunrise. And he said you have a piece of cloth, damp cloth, and you put the Tulsi leaves, and then you put another piece of damp cloth on top. He said like this, the Tulsi leaves will stay fresh whole day. It may be a Vrindavan tip because in Vrindavan it gets so hot there. The Tulsi leaves will shrivel up if you just leave them there. I think it was a Vrindavan tip. You can put it in two pieces of wet cloth. And he says the Brahmanas, they carry the Shaligram around in different, they have a little box or in a bag on their neck or something. And he said they go around to different places and then they open up the box and then do some mantras, and then again they put to sleep, and then again they go to another place and then they open and they give mantra, again sleeping. He said this is Shaligram puja. Mantra sleeping, mantra sleeping. He started to laugh. Mantra sleeping. So then we had the Shaligram on the altar. But nobody knew what Shaligram was. Because there was hardly any Indian devotees coming forward at that time. They were very shy. Of course we were CIA. And all things like that at that time. Saibh Mat, CIA. So the Western devotees didn't know what Shaligram was. Who was that? Is it Lord Jagannath? I said, yeah, I guess so. I guess he's the Lord of the Universe. I mean he's black and he's got these white eyes. Few people know Gopal Krishna Brahmacharya at that time. He was there. And so then the devotees, some Indian devotees said, this is Narayana Srila, Krishna Srila. So I was getting confused. Shaligram, Krishna, Narayana. So I went to ask Prabhupada. And I went to his room and I said, Srila Prabhupada, may I ask you a question? He said, yes. I said, some people say that Shaligram is Krishna and some people say he is Narayana. And Prabhupada said, forget some say. Heavy tone. I kind of bowed my way out because the way he said it. I think he said it, you know, Prabhupada would often say when the devotees were talking, he would say, you know what they say, Prabhupada? And Prabhupada, they say, they say. Who is that? What is that authority? He says, they say. What is that authority? I think he was in that mood. The Prabhupada said, forget some say. So I didn't pursue it anymore. I bowed my way out. But anyway, two days later, he sent me a message. And he said, tell the pujari he should offer the flute to Shaligram. He's indicating we want to worship as Krishna.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### KRISHNA'S LOVE | VALENTINE’S DAY SPECIAL | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, prayer, blessings
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Seek the blessings of Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Shri Krishna so that we can have a very very meaningful discussion and we go back enriched with a lot of wonderful insights."
**Summary:** The speaker welcomes the audience, introduces the topic of Krishna's love in the context of Valentine's Day, and leads a prayer seeking blessings for a meaningful discussion.

A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is Krishna's Love. Today being Valentine's Day, we thought let's understand more about what is love and what is lust. In today's world, we talk a lot about love. There are movies made on love stories, and love is glorified. Selfless love is talked about, and there are many stories of people who have done enormous sacrifice for their loved ones. So let's understand from Bhagavad Gita what is real love. Before we plunge into this discussion, let's pray together. Let's fold our hands and offer these prayers. Seek the blessings of Prabhupada. Seek the blessings of Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Shri Krishna so that we can have a very meaningful discussion and we go back enriched with a lot of wonderful insights. So please join me in offering these prayers.

**Verse References:** —

We will chant these prayers together. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktive Nita Hayadanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Dev Gauravani Pracharine Nir Vishesha Sunyavadi Paaschaatyade Shataarin. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gadadhar Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratushenamaha. Namo Brahmane Devaya Go Brahmane Hitayacha Jagadhitaya Krishnaya Govindaya Namo Namaha. He Krishna Karuna Sindhu Dina Bandhu Jagatpate Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Namostute. Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Gopishtha Gopishtha Gopishtha Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye. Vansha Kalpat Rubhya Shcha Kripa Sindhu Bhya Evachat Patitanam Pavne Bhyo Vaishnave Bhyo Namo Namaha. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

Every session we start with prayers and since these videos are there on YouTube, request all of you to memorize these prayers and before you start reading Shila Prabhupada books, whenever you read Bhagavad Gita, you can offer these prayers. We are first offering prayers to Srila Prabhupada, spiritual master. Then we are offering prayers to Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and then to Lord Sri Krishna. And before we start, we also offer prayers to all the Vaishnavas, all the devotees of the Lord, because of whose mercy we are getting this transcendental knowledge. #### love vs lust, material vs spiritual, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=310
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Evoking our love for God is like the gold."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to differentiate between love and lust, comparing them to gold and iron, and stating that worldly love stories are glorified lust, while real love is love of God, which is the ultimate goal.

And what is the difference between love and lust. There is something common between love and lust. Even in a lusty exchange between two individuals, there are two persons, there is an exchange which is happening, and a person is getting attracted to the other person. So Prabhupada says, just like iron and gold, both are metals. And both of them share some common characteristics. Metals have some characteristics, so they are both having some common characteristics. Still, there is lot of difference between iron and gold. Iron is very cheap and gold is very precious metal. So likewise, the so-called love which is there in this material world, which is celebrated, the so-called love stories, from spiritual perspective, are actually lust—glorified lust, which can be compared to an iron. And real love, love of God, love of Krishna, which our scriptures are saying that it's the ultimate goal. This is what we should be after. Evoking our love for God is like the gold.

---
#### lust, desire, soul, Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=421
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Goal of life is to really be blissful, to enjoy life, but in association with the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker defines lust as the desire to enjoy independent of the Lord, clarifying that enjoyment itself is not wrong, but should be done in association with the Lord, as we are part and parcel of God.

The broad definition of lust, although lust is, we generally associate with lusty affairs between a male and a female, but the general definition of lust is desire to enjoy. Desire can be of many forms, many types—desires to enjoy, independent of Lord. Now somebody may say, is it wrong to enjoy? Do you mean to say that it is sin to enjoy life? It's not a sin. In fact, enjoyment is characteristic of the soul. Anandamayo Bhyasat. Goal of life is to really be blissful, to enjoy life, but in association with the Lord. Now why it is important that we enjoy with keeping Lord in the centre? This brings us to a very fundamental understanding that we are amsa of the Lord. We are part and parcel of the God. Just like this hand is amsa, is a part of this body, and it has to serve the desires of the body, of the whole. Whenever there is some itch, the hand immediately goes to that point and helps relieve that itch. Hand is always available to serve the overall purpose. Likewise, we are designed by the Lord.

---
#### service, body analogy, rebellion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=546
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "And when it plays its role, it gets its quota of nourishment, it gets its quota of enjoyment."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that our relationship with the Lord is not master-slave, but like a part of the body serving the whole, and shares a story of the body's limbs rebelling against the belly to illustrate the consequences of rebelling against the Lord.

Mamaiv amsa jiva loke. Jiva means the living entity, the Atma. We are designed to serve the Lord. Now it is not a master-slave relationship. Just like this hand is not a slave. It is just a part. It plays its role. And when it plays its role, it gets its quota of nourishment, it gets its quota of enjoyment. The famous story which Prabhupada often quoted in his lectures, he said once the limbs of the body, they got together and they had a meeting. The hand said that we all work, toil the whole day, do different activities, and this belly sits in one place, does nothing, puts no effort, does no hard work, and simply enjoys all the hard work which we all are doing, simply sitting in one place and enjoying all the wonderful things we eat. So they all got together and said this is unfair. And they said okay we will have a strike. And next day all of the organs went on strike. The hands won't move, the legs will not go for a walk. In fact the hand when it tried becoming little weak, tried instead of putting the food in the mouth, tried squeezing out the nourishment from the food directly. But eventually all the organs started becoming weak. And then they again got together, understood what was happening, and called off the strike.

---
#### rebellion, desire, material world, free will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=640
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "These all are the characteristics of a person trying to take the position of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that rebellious souls come to the material world with the desire to enjoy independently of the Lord, taking the position of Krishna, and that the Lord allows this to give us free will.

Likewise we rebellious souls, when we rebel against the Lord, Ichcha Dvesha Samutthena. Ichcha means desire to enjoy, like Krishna, take the position of Krishna, being the centre of attraction, to be glorified, everyone should worship me, should glorify me, should get attracted to me, I should become famous. These all are the characteristics of a person trying to take the position of the Lord. In other words, instead of we being servant of the Lord, we are trying to become the masters. When this desire comes, Ichcha and Dvesha, envy, disobedience to the orders of the Lord, envious of the position of the Lord, of being the centre of attraction, Ichcha Dvesha Samutthena Dvandamohena Bharata, we come into this material world with an opportunity which the Lord has given to try it out, because Lord does not want to force us to obey Him. Otherwise there is no difference between us and a robot. He has given us a free will, so this material world is created if we want to be the Lord, if we want to be the master, if we want to enjoy independent of the Lord, an even playing field is created and we have an opportunity to try it out. Just like a fish can try out, we can take it out of the water and see if it can survive and be happy and enjoy, but that is not its goal. That is not its constitutional position. It will not be happy, it will wriggle and that fish, you take it back and put it in water, it will immediately be satisfied. No matter what you give to the fish, you make a bedstead, cover it with cushion, with velvet, nice cozy pillows, the fish will not be happy outside water.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that we are amshas of the Lord and share the qualities of Sat, Chit, and Ananda, and that seeking happiness is not wrong, but material enjoyment entangles us in illusion, which the scriptures advise against.

Prabhupada was asked, what is the goal of your movement? by one reporter in Paris. So Prabhupada said, goal of our movement is to enjoy life. Enjoyment is not a sin, but enjoying materially which will entangle us, which will bind us, which will put us more and more in illusion, that is what scriptures are forbidding us to dabble in.

#### lust, love, Lord, center of attraction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=830

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker defines lust as trying to enjoy independently of the Lord, and explains that even celebrated love stories are a form of lust because the Lord is out of the picture and another person becomes the center of attraction.

So the right kind of enjoyment, lust is when we try to enjoy keeping Lord out of picture, independently. It's like the hand trying to squeeze out all the nourishment from the food without putting it in the mouth. Even so called celebrated love, so called love stories, is actually in one form of lust. Just that we need little bit of introspection, thinking with a cool head, because why it's called lust? Because Lord is out of picture, the other person becomes the center of attraction for you, or the person who is lovers, and the person enjoys somebody saying, I can't live without you. For a moment, for few days, for few months, for few years, I become the center of attraction for that person.

#### Lord, Amsa, contradiction, poverty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=906
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Actually that's the position of Krishna. We cannot live without the Lord, we are His Amsa."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the feeling of someone saying 'I can't live without you' mirrors our true relationship with Krishna, and uses the analogy of a rich man's poverty-stricken son to illustrate the contradiction of being an amsha of the all-powerful Krishna and yet suffering in the material world.

Actually that's the position of Krishna. We cannot live without the Lord, we are His Amsa. Suppose I tell you, there is a very rich man, and he has properties all over the world. He owns houses in Dubai, he has houses in Australia, in US, he has 26 big properties, bungalows in different countries. And he is very rich, owns many many businesses. And I tell you, that this rich man has a son, who is very very poverty stricken, who is homeless, who is without proper roti kapda makhan, struggling for bare necessities of life. You will say, how is this possible? It's a contradiction. On one side we are saying, there is a rich man, who has properties all over the world, and simultaneously I am telling you, he has a son, who is very poverty stricken, who does not have food to eat, doesn't have proper clothes to wear, struggling for bare necessities of life. It's a contradiction.

#### Krishna, Amsa, struggle, material nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=993
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Shad Aishwarya Purnam, he is our rich father, and we are his parts, we are his sons."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna is Shad Aishwarya Purnam, possessing unlimited opulence, and we are His amshas, but we struggle in the material world, creating a contradiction.

Likewise, Krishna is saying, mamaiv amsho, we are his amshas. And Krishna is Shad Aishwarya Purnam, unlimited strength, unlimited knowledge, unlimited fame, unlimited beauty, unlimited renunciation. Shad Aishwarya Purnam, he is our rich father, and we are his parts, we are his sons. Mamaiv amsho jeeva loke, jeeva bhuta sanatana, and we are also sanatana, eternal. Mana shashta indriyani, prakriti sthani karshati, we are waging a battle, we are struggling with this material nature, we are entangled, we are engulfed with different kinds of miseries, Adi-bhoti kleshas, Adhyatmi kleshas, Adi-devi kleshas. It's a contradiction.

#### struggle, material nature, lust, death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1047
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Material nature is pushing us back, it's like a chess game."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that we struggle due to uncontrolled mind and senses, influenced by lusty desires, and that material nature pushes back against our attempts to enjoy independently of Krishna, ultimately leading to death where everything is taken away.

Just like the previous example, I gave a rich man's son, but the son is struggling in the streets. So Krishna is saying, mamaiv amsho jeeva loke, in one place Krishna says, I am the source of everything, aham sarvasya prabhavo, matta sarvam pravartate, everything is coming from me, matta paratram nanyat kinchita sthidanam, there is no one superior to me. And then he is saying, you are my amsha, you are my son, and the son is struggling. Why is he struggling? Mana shashta indriyani, with the mind and the senses, prakriti sthani karshati, because of uncontrolled mind, uncontrolled senses, because of lusty desires, kaam esha, krodha esha, rajo guna, samudbhava, under the influence of lower modes, we are struggling, we are waging a battle. Material nature is pushing us back, it's like a chess game. We make a move to enjoy life, independent of Krishna, there is a pushback, there is a retaliation from material nature. And again we make the next move to circumvent that, to ensure that our enjoyment, happiness continues, again there is a block, again there is an impediment, again there is a pushback from material nature, prakriti sthani karshati, we are waging a battle against material nature, and eventually the battle will be won by nature. Mrityu sarva haras chaham, no matter how popular, how big, how famous, how glorified a person may be, eventually death will come and everything will be taken away. So called intelligence, so called wealth, so called strength, so called beauty, everything will be taken away. So called beautiful hairs for which we have gone to saloons and all kinds of shampoos, all kinds of conditioners, all kinds of beauty treatments we have done, in a moment it will be all burnt to ashes.

---
#### real love, Lord, lust, enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1183
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So real love actually is an exchange with Lord in the center."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that real love is an exchange with the Lord in the center, and that even eating palatable food earned honestly is a form of lust if not offered to the Lord first.

So real love actually is an exchange with Lord in the center. Lord, now for example, when we eat nice palatable foodstuff, even if it is earned by honest means, proper means, to fulfill that desire of enjoying that taste, minus the Lord, without offering it first to the Lord, it is actually one kind of lust. That's why Krishna says in the, if one does not offer the foods to the Lord and then eat as remnants, it is He is eating sin only. No doubt being a vegetarian is better than being a non-vegetarian because amount of sin involved is much lesser. But still, the highest recommendation of the Lord is we offer our food to the Lord and eat. So lust is desire to enjoy. It could be between two individuals. It could be between individual and a thing. It could be between senses and sense object. Desire to enjoy minus the Lord.

#### lust, love, spiritual perspective

**Summary:** The speaker explains that even seemingly selfless love between individuals, like the Taj Mahal, is a form of lust from a spiritual perspective, and that they will discuss real love and how to evoke it.

#### demons, desire, enjoyment, Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1320
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "They are called demons because they had a desire to enjoy the property of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that even powerful, religious personalities like Ravana and Hiranyakashipu are considered demons because they desired to enjoy the property of the Lord.

In fact, if we study the Vedic literatures, you will find there were many strong, powerful personalities like Ravana, like Hiranyakashipu. Actually they were very religious. They were very austere. They did lot of Tapasya. They were also God conscious. In that sense they were very religious. But why we call them as demons? Just like in a movie you have an actor, a hero, and you have a villain. In Ramayana, Ravana is like a villain. He was great devotee of Lord Shiva. He did lot of penances and austerity. He was very religious. He followed the Vedic injunctions of performing Tapasya, of worshipping the Devatas. But still he is called a demon. Sometimes we think demons means people who have, you know, typically in story books you have that picture of people having horns and dark faces with teeth protruding out. Why they are called demons? They are called demons because they had a desire to enjoy the property of the Lord.

#### demons, authority, enjoyment, devastation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1410
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "They are taking the position of the Lord of being the enjoyer, of being the centre of attraction."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on why figures like Ravana and Hiranyakashipu are considered demons, emphasizing their desire to control and enjoy independently of the Lord, and defying His authority.

Ravana wanted to enjoy Sita who is the property of Lord Ramachandra. Hiranyakashipu wanted to be the controller of the whole world. He wanted to be immortal minus the Lord. When he asked Prahlad, what is the source of your knowledge, your strength? Prahlad said it's the same source from you are getting the knowledge, your power. And he became angry. So even the so-called religious people, so-called pious people, so-called people who are engaging in Tapasya, austerity, penances, they are called demons because they are defying the authority of the Lord. They are taking the position of the Lord of being the enjoyer, of being the centre of attraction. People should glorify them. And that's why they are called demons. And eventually as we have been seeing in our scriptures, Ramayana, Mahabharata and all our Vedic scriptures, such demons eventually are devastated.

#### Vedic injunctions, material desires, ultimate goal, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1495
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we don't understand the purpose, even so-called following the Vedic literatures, just for fulfilment of my material desires."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that even diligently following Vedic injunctions without understanding the ultimate goal of loving and serving the Lord is like getting stuck on a particular floor of a building, and that Krishna advises rising above the flowery language of the Vedas.

So even following the Vedic injunctions, it's like going from ground floor to the 100th floor. If we are diligently following the Vedic injunctions without knowing the purpose, the ultimate goal, naate vidu svart gatim hi Vishnu, if we don't know, ultimate goal is to come to the platform of loving the Lord, understanding the Lord, serving the Lord. If we don't understand the purpose, even so-called following the Vedic literatures, just for fulfilment of my material desires. If I want money, I worship Lakshmi. If I want good learning, I worship Saraswati. The recommendation are scriptures. And if you just get stuck following the scriptures just for the heck of fulfilling my material desires, then it's like going from ground floor to 100th floor and getting stuck on a particular floor. Don't move ahead. That's why Krishna in the Gita also says, NISTRAI GUNYA BHAVARJUNA. You rise above the flowery language of the Vedas. Yes, Vedas has recommendations for people who are completely materialistic, just to motivate them, inspire them to worship, to surrender to some higher personalities. They are recommendations. But that's not the ultimate.

---
#### Krishna, brahmanas, rules and regulations, essence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1583
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Although they were sticking to the rules and regulations of performing the yagyas, but they forgot the essence of all the yagyas is to please Vishnu or to please Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story of Krishna and Balram asking for food from brahmanas performing yagyas, who refused because they were too focused on the rules and regulations, forgetting the essence of pleasing Vishnu.

Once Krishna and Balram, they were all having a picnic in the forest of Vrindavan. And they became hungry. It was lunch time. Obviously, Lord also is a person. He also becomes hungry like us. No doubt His hunger is different from our hunger. He has transcendental hunger. His hunger, He doesn't have to fetch for foodstuffs like us to fulfill His hunger. That's why it's called transcendental hunger. Just by seeing the foodstuff, His hunger can get satisfied. So they got hungry and they sent word, some of these friends were sent to the brahmanas who were performing different yagyas, sacrifices. And these cowherd boys went to them and said, we are in the forest with Krishna and Balram and we are feeling very hungry. So they have sent us, Krishna and Balram have sent us to get some foodstuff from you. Can you please give us some fruits, whatever you are offering in the yagya. Obviously, yagyas are meant for pleasing Vishnu, for the satisfaction of Vishnu. So the brahmanas immediately said, no, we can't give it. These yagyas have to be completed. How can we give before the yagyas are completed? How can we break the procedure which is to be followed when we do the sacrifices and rituals? Although they were sticking to the rules and regulations of performing the yagyas, but they forgot the essence of all the yagyas is to please Vishnu or to please Krishna. Even when Vishnu came or Krishna sent word that please give the foodstuff, they were stuck with the rules and regulations without understanding the spirit behind those rules and regulations.

---
#### Vedic literatures, lusty desires, pure love, God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1715
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All the Vedic literatures ultimately is trying to purify our lusty desires, material desires, take us from that platform and raise our consciousness, purify our desires and eventually come to pure desires or pure love of God."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes that the Vedic literatures aim to purify lusty desires, raise consciousness, and lead to pure love of God, starting from forgetfulness of the Lord and gradually purifying desires.

So the essence of what I have shared so far is that all the Vedic literatures ultimately is trying to purify our lusty desires, material desires, take us from that platform and raise our consciousness, purify our desires and eventually come to pure desires or pure love of God. We start from the bottom most where full of lusty desires, completely forgetful of Lord and then slowly we purify, we purify, we purify, follow the rules and regulations, control our senses, control our mind, regulate our desires, purify our desires and eventually come to pure desires pure love and that is love of God, love of Krishna.

#### Bhakti, devotional service, sacrifice, love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1782
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Real love means a lover sacrifices for his beloved and that is what Bhakti is all about where we sacrifice our time, our energy, our resources knowing that it is coming from the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker defines Bhakti as practical devotional service, involving sacrifice of time, energy, and resources, similar to a mother's love for her child.

It is also called Bhakti. It is also called devotional service. Prabhupada did not translate Bhakti as some kind of sentimental exchange between a devotee and the Lord. Bhakti means practical devotional service. Just like if you love a person, it is not you just sit in one place and actually do something for that person. A mother loves the child, mother sacrifices her time, her energy, her sleep for the child. So likewise, real love means a lover sacrifices for his beloved and that is what Bhakti is all about where we sacrifice our time, our energy, our resources knowing that it is coming from the Lord. It belongs to the Lord. We give it back to the Lord.

#### purification, soul, love for God, human life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/79mblDROb6U?t=1839
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The goal of human form of life is actually to revive our original love for the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how to purify ourselves from lust, comparing it to moving up floors in a building, and emphasizes that the purpose of human life is to revive our original love for the Lord.

Now we will talk about how do we control or how do we purify ourselves from the lust. That is the first step. In a number line you have minus 3, you have minus 2, you have minus 1 and then you have 0 and then you have positive numbers. If you have to go from basement of a building to let's say third floor or fourth floor, you have to come to the ground floor, you have to come to the first floor, then the second floor and the third floor. No doubt you can go by a lift by just pressing a button. You don't have to stop necessarily on the ground floor. But nevertheless the lift moves from these floors although it may not physically stop. So likewise, how do we move ourselves, the soul which is entrapped now, overpowered by lower modes, Rajoguna, Tamoguna, how does this soul evolve and come to the point where it has pure desires and it has genuine, pure, real love for God. In fact our scriptures say that is the purpose of human form of life. The purpose of human form of life. The goal of human form of life is actually to revive our original love for the Lord.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Knowledge & Ignorance | Bhagavad Gita 13.05 - 13.12 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Vedic knowledge, Parampara, Scriptural authority
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nHOaDWNg_Is?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If someone sentimentally practices Harirbhakti, without referring to bona fide, authorized Shruti, Smriti, Puranadi, Pancharatri ki Vidhi, it creates disturbance in his life and in others' life."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the importance of receiving Vedic knowledge through parampara (disciplic succession) and adhering to scriptural authority (Shruti, Smriti, Puranas, and Pancharatra) to avoid creating disturbance in one's life and the lives of others.

So, today we will be covering from 5 to 12. So, this is the fifth verse. That knowledge of the field of activities and of the knower of activities is described by various sages in various Vedic writings, especially in the Vedanta Sutra and presented with all reasoning as to cause and effect. There is a verse which says, Shruti means, what is Shruti? Shruti means hearing. Scriptures are also called Shruti Shastras. Can anyone help me with an answer? Why they are called Shruti Shastras? Because originally, this Vedic knowledge was imparted to the first created being, Lord Brahma. He passed on to Narada by what? By what means? By hearing. Brahma. Then Narada heard. Then Narada heard, then Vyasa heard from Narada. So, this knowledge was coming down in a Parampara. Parampara means disciplic succession and that was through oral reception. And therefore, our Shastras are also called Shruti. Only 5000 years back, this Vedic knowledge was not imparted to the first created being. It was imparted to the second created being. So, the Vedic knowledge which was coming down in Parampara was put into writing. Otherwise, this knowledge was not in a written form. And Vedavyas compiled the Vedic literatures, the four Vedas, Rig, Yajur, Atharva and Sama Veda. So, it is said that Shruti, Smriti, Puranadi, there are Puranas. How many Puranas are there? Eighteen Puranas. How many Puranas? Eighteen Puranas. Shruti, Smriti, Puranadi, Pancharatri ki Vidhi, Vina. And there were Pancharatra. There are also some Shastras. Vidhi, they give us direction. Vidhi means Marg. They show us the way. Vina, Vina means without, without reference to. Ekantika Harirbhakti. If someone sentimentally practices Harirbhakti, sentimentally practices any spiritual practices. Mere man mein mujhe aisa lagta hai. Mere khyal se. I think so. In my opinion. Without referring to bona fide, authorized, Shruti, Smriti, Puranadi, Pancharatri ki Vidhi, Vina. Uthpaateva kalpate. It creates disturbance in his life and in others life.

---
#### Understanding God, Interpretation, Standardized knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nHOaDWNg_Is?t=200
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the problem of differing interpretations of God across religions and sects, contrasting it with the standardized understanding in fields like physics and chemistry, and attributes this to the lack of well-defined curriculum and systematic teaching in spirituality.

Actually, unfortunately everyone has different understanding of God in today's world. There are many religions. Within a particular religion also there are many sects and everyone has his own understanding of who is God. Can anyone tell me why this is so? When it comes to understanding of... What is God? What is water? Everyone, whether you go to America, whether you go to Europe, any country, go to Australia, everyone will say H2 plus O is water. Am I right? It's not that in Australia it is N plus O is water. Or in some other country, there is some other constitution of water. Why there is a standard understanding as far as physics, chemistry, maths is concerned? Can anyone tell me? And why not so when it comes to understanding of God? People have made their own interpretation. Why they have made their own interpretation? We cannot see many things which science believe and which we... Electron, have you seen? Okay, yeah. So what happens is, as far as, you see how, why there is a difference? You see, as far as physics, chemistry, maths, all the subjects are concerned, they are well-defined textbooks. And, you know, you have boards, IISC, then you have CBSE, who have, so to say, defined, well-defined curriculum. Well-defined books. And experts in that field of knowledge have sat together and have compiled those books. And then these books are systematically taught. Physics in the second standard, in the third standard, in the fourth standard. And only after you have studied and passed in the exam, then you move to the next class. Now, in case of spirituality, what has happened is, in the second class in the third school we teach, the child...

#### Education, Spirituality, Secularism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nHOaDWNg_Is?t=365
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This world is a school in which there are innumerable classes for lack species levels of consciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker compares the structured education system to the unstructured approach to spirituality, highlighting the lack of a defined curriculum and the impact of secularism in schools, leading to individual speculation about God.

So totally became ten. Then five working senses. One is five knowledge acquiring senses, which is eyes, ears, nose, tongue and touch. These are knowledge acquiring senses. Karmendriya. Sorry, Jnanendriya. They are called Jnanendriya means because through these indriyas we are gathering knowledge. If I were to ask you, is it hot today? How is the weather? #### Kshetra, Transformations, Shadvikara
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nHOaDWNg_Is?t=1936
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This Kshetra is going through a Shadvikara, Shad means six. It comes into being, it grows, it stays for some time, it reproduces, it dwindles and finally it dies."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the six transformations (Shadvikara) that the Kshetra (body) undergoes: birth, growth, sustenance, reproduction, dwindling, and death, emphasizing that these transformations do not affect the soul or Supersoul.

And these interactions are happening under the influence of Kala. Now this Kshetra undergoes six transformations. What are the six transformations? Even if our body, if you look. we have Kshetra Gna and then we have super knower which is the super soul, Paramatma. The Kshetra undergoes six transformations and the sixth transformation is this body comes into being, takes birth, then it grows. Like at one time we were having a child's body, it grows and then it stays for some time, stays for some time means we are a child for some time, we are a young man for some time, then it reproduces, it creates by-products and then in due course of time it dwindles, it declines, body starts becoming weak, eyes start becoming blurred and then finally it dies. This is called Shadvikara. So this Kshetra is going through a Shadvikara, Shad means six. It comes into being, it grows, it stays for some time, it reproduces, it dwindles and finally it dies. And this Shadvikara is not happening in the soul, it's not happening in the super soul.

It is happening to body. Why do we say that? I have become old. One day I will die. Why do we say that? Because of bodily concept of life. Because of false ego. What is false ego? I am the body. So why we are experiencing pain in this world? Why we are experiencing death? Why we have fear of death? Because that Atma, which is Kshetragna, which is different from Kshetra. Kshetra which is different from Kshetra. So the physical body which undergoes this transformation, Shadvikara, this Atma, the root cause if you remember one of the quiz, we ask this question, what is a root cause of all the problems we are facing in this world? What is the root cause? The body. The Atma which is having no transformation, Shadvikara has identified itself with those bodies. Arjuna also, in the Bhagavad Gita in the first chapter, what will happen to my family members? What will happen to my family members? What will happen to me? #### leading by example, acharya, instruction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=651
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Acharya means one who leads by example so it's very important for us also now one thing is acharya in spiritual life even in our day-to-day life we do instruct people as an employee."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of leading by example, defining an Acharya as one who embodies their teachings, and applying this principle in both spiritual and everyday life.

Practicing the principles which he was teaching, when he was telling people to follow the four regulative principles, he was following it. They were not just mere words, they were realizations which he was sharing with people. And although he did not speak bombastic words, whosoever heard Shila Prabhupada, there was a transformation in his heart. And that's how we saw that how in lakhs, in millions, people became Krishna conscious, started practicing the principles of Gita. And from 66 to 1977 when Shila Prabhupada left the planet, there were more than 10,000 people who were practicing Krishna consciousness were his disciples. So that was because of his purity, he was leading by example. That's why Shila Prabhupada was also called an Acharya. Acharya means one who leads by example, so it's very important for us also now. One thing is acharya in spiritual life, even in our day-to-day life, we do instruct people. As an employee, a person might instruct his employees, or as parents, you might instruct our children. It is very important for us to keep in mind that example is better than precept.

---
#### action, example, parenting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=754
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is not the words we speak, it's not the intent we have, children also they observe rather than the words we speak, they observe our actions what we are doing."
**Summary:** The speaker illustrates the principle that actions speak louder than words with a story about a family struggling to get their children to sleep early, highlighting the importance of parents setting a good example.

This brings me to the second point. I request the devotees attending today can help me with the first point which we covered and I move on to the second point. The second point is, which is related to the first point, actions speak louder than words. I repeat, action speaks louder than words. I was taking a Gita class for a family and as part of the discussion, Yukta ahara vihara asya, that verse in the Bhagavad Gita where Krishna says one was regulated in his eating, sleeping, recreation and work. And as part of the discussion, I told them that it is very important that we sleep early and wake up early. Night time is meant for sleeping and Ayurveda also says that the time period between 9 p.m. to 12 p.m. is very important period where we can get good quality rest. Early to bed early to rise makes a man healthy, wealthy and wise. So we were discussing that and this lady asked me, Swami ji, we understand it is very important but aaj ki duniya mein bache maante hi nahi hai, at 12 o'clock in the night the kids say abhi toh rati nahi hui hai and they sleep at 1:30, 2 o'clock. So then the lady asked me why, why is it happening so then I became a little curious and I asked the husband who's also attending the session, I asked them what time you all sleep so the husband said Prabhu ji hum log kabhi late ho jata hai baal saade bara ek bach jata hai. So it is very important, it is not the words we speak, it's not the intent we have, children also they observe rather than the words we speak, they observe our actions what we are doing.

#### honesty, practice, entertainment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=896
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This training program at best if we do not practice what we are learning if we do not apply those principles in our life this training is at best an entertainment it's not training it's an entertainment."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the necessity of practicing what one learns, using the analogy of a training program that becomes mere entertainment if its principles are not implemented in daily life.

If we ourselves are sleeping late, we cannot expect our children to be sleeping on time and practice early to bed early to rise. If a person himself is not honest, he cannot be talking big big things about honesty, even if he talks he can actually, you can cheat the world, you can cheat one person for a while but you cannot cheat everyone for all times. We might speak the words but if the words have to have an impact then it is very important that we also practice. I was attending one training some many years back, training seminar in which a person told something very interesting, it was also in line with the same thing which we are discussing today, that it is very important for us to practice what we are learning. He says if we learn so many things, you all have come, you're attending a training program, you're hearing me speak, you're learning so many things, but if you hear and learn so many things but do not practice, do not implement, do not make an effort to practice those principles, you know what is this training called. So everyone in the audience was, you know, trying to come up with different answers, he says this training program at best, if we do not practice what we are learning, if we do not apply those principles in our life, this training is at best an entertainment, it's not training, it's an entertainment.

#### practice, spiritual discourse, perseverance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1018
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Practice makes a man perfect many times people try one time two time no it's too tough it's too complicated we have to practice."
**Summary:** This segment stresses the importance of consistent practice in spiritual life, using the example of japa and the analogy of a child learning to walk to illustrate the need for perseverance despite initial difficulties.

Many times people also go to spiritual discourses, they attend the bhagavata, they hear, but there is no conscious effort to practice, to implement. For example, now we have the soulful japa videos on the YouTube, very nicely done with lot of realization, lot of practical implication to improve our quality of japa, we can hear them, yes, nothing wrong about it, it's very nice, but the main thing is to practice. If he's talking about concurrent chanting, how to practice it and you have to practice. Many of those things, practice makes a man perfect. Many times people try one time, two time, no, it's too tough, it's too complicated, we have to practice. Many times people say that yes, we understand this but swamiji it's very difficult. Many times we might practice something and we may fail, we should not give up. Just like a child, when a child learns walking, he stumbles, he falls, he falls again and again and every time he gets up and in due course of time he starts walking, starts running, that's part of the learning process, so.

#### conviction, fact, belief
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1120
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As far as I am concerned, I am fully convinced. Therefore, I am pushing this forward. For me, it's a fact. Krishna is Supreme Personality of Godhead, it's a fact, it's not a fiction."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts an incident where Srila Prabhupada emphasized the importance of conviction in Krishna consciousness, distinguishing between belief and fact, and highlighting his own unwavering faith.

Sushila Prabhupada, as I mentioned, being an acharya, he taught by example. If he was teaching about Krishna consciousness, conviction in Krishna being the supreme personality of godhead, bhagavan, if he was inspiring people about the importance of Krishna consciousness, it was backed with strong conviction in Krishna. This was in the year 1972 in Los Angeles, in ISKCON, we have the highest body called the GBC, governing body commission. So 1972 people, the GBC members, they were called Los Angeles and Prabhupada was meeting them. And Srila Prabhupada looked at them, some of the GBC members sitting there, right across in that room. After having talked about Krishna, the importance of Krishna Consciousness, Krishna being the Supreme Personality of Godhead, the importance of reaching out to people with this message, the message of Bhagavad Gita, message of Krishna Consciousness, he asked those GBC members, the senior devotees, are you all convinced about the philosophy of Krishna Consciousness? Are you all convinced? He looked at them and it seems for two minutes, nobody spoke anything. It was pin drop silence. Again Srila Prabhupada asked, are you all convinced? I want to know. And then he said, as far as I am concerned, I am fully convinced. Therefore, I am pushing this forward. For me, it's a fact. Krishna is Supreme Personality of Godhead, it's a fact, it's not a fiction. Then Prabhupada said, lot of people ask me, Swamiji, do you believe? I do not believe. Prabhupada says, I do not believe. It's a fact. There is a difference between belief and fact.

---
#### association, conviction, contagious
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Likewise, if a person interacts with a person with strong conviction, strong conviction, that conviction, that faith also is contagious. It's also infectious."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that conviction is contagious, drawing a parallel to COVID-19 precautions and emphasizing the importance of associating with individuals who have strong faith and avoiding negative influences.

In 1905, man believed that atoms are the smallest particle. But that was a belief. Later on, it was found that it's not a fact. Man believed that Earth is flat. But later on, it was not found to be a fact. Likewise, Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. He is Bhagwan. Krishna has spoken the words of Bhagavad Gita. It's not a matter of belief. It's not a matter of faith. It's a fact. For many people, existence of God, although they may be religious, although they may be pious, although they may be praying to Lord, but it's a matter of belief. So Srila Prabhupada says, as far as I am concerned, I am fully convinced. It's a fact. Therefore, I am pushing this movement. Because of that conviction, just like these days, you know, we have this coronavirus and people talk about COVID appropriate behavior. One of them being sanitization and then social distancing, maintain a distance, two feet, three feet. So, why this distance? Why this COVID appropriate? Because if you go very close to an infected person, you can also get infected. So likewise, if a person interacts with a person with strong conviction, strong conviction, that conviction, that faith also is contagious. It's also infectious. That's why Srila Prabhupada used to say that we have to be very, very mindful, very, very cautious about whom we associate with. In fact, he said that one of the most important behavior of a Vaishnava is asatsanga tyaga. Asatsanga means he should give up wrong association, bad association. Because association is contagious.

---
#### purity, conviction, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1405
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhupada's words were simple. But there was transformation in the heart. Whosoever heard Prabhupada. And that was because of his purity, because of his conviction."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Srila Prabhupada's strong conviction and purity, rather than eloquent speech, were the source of the transformative power in his words.

Whatever that faith the person has, whatever his convictions are, unknowingly, unintentionally, it will also influence us. So, Srila Prabhupada had strong conviction. When he was talking about Krishna consciousness, it was not, for him it was not, he was not in a mood of accumulating followers. He was not in a mood of becoming famous. He was not in terms of, you know, how many followers he got and how many countries he visited, countries he traveled and what all he achieved. He was not concerned about that. He was convinced that Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. The message of Bhagavad Gita is the need of the hour. And based on that conviction, Srila Prabhupada was speaking, although he was not speaking bombastic words. Sometimes public speakers, they learn public speaking skills, the tonality, voice modulation, gestures. So, Prabhupada's words were simple. But there was transformation in the heart, whosoever heard Prabhupada. And that was because of his purity, because of his conviction.

#### compassion, example, dedication
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srila Prabhupada said, let's go."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates an incident in Mexico where Srila Prabhupada, despite exhaustion, agreed to a late-night TV interview, demonstrating his dedication to spreading Krishna consciousness.

Srila Prabhupada in 1970, same year, went to Mexico. And the day, the day one, by that time, you know, as I told you, many people started practicing Krishna consciousness. And Srila Prabhupada would visit a particular cities. All the devotees from nearby cities would gather, would be eagerly waiting for Prabhupada to arrive. And when Prabhupada would arrive, there would be kirtans. And whole day, there will be, you know, discussion. There will be talks. There will be lectures. There will be important appointments. People would come and meet Srila Prabhupada. People would at time, Prabhupada would at time go to some universities and address the students. So, that was it. So, day one was completely jam-packed. Day two was for initiations. So, morning to evening, Prabhupada was speaking to those initiated disciples. And then the whole ceremony happened. Lot of devotees had gathered. And then Prabhupada spoke. There was a lecture. And then Prabhupada came back to his room at 9:30 p.m. in the night. After whole day, second day, having travelled from the US. And then the day one, completely jam-packed. Day two, whole day busy with initiations, meeting devotees, talking to them. And then at 9:30, when Prabhupada came back to his room, that was a time when seemingly Srila Prabhupada would be completely exhausted. Mind you, he was at that time, when he went to America in 1965, he was 70 years old. So, 1972 means, add another 7 years, 77 years old. So, one devotee came and told Srila Prabhupada, Prabhupada, just now we got an information. Just now we got a news. They were trying different, you know, important places where Srila Prabhupada could talk. Just now we got to know that your interview in a very, very reputed TV channel, has been fixed. And Srila Prabhupada, unfortunately, the interview has 1 a.m., midnight. I requested them for change of time, but somehow they were saying that this is the only time slot available. And then Srila Prabhupada asked, what is this TV channel? So, he says, this is the most prime TV channel and millions of people watch this. Srila Prabhupada said, let's go.

#### compassion, transformation, truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1640
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Swamiji, please bless me and my family."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, describing how Srila Prabhupada's compassionate and truthful words during the late-night interview deeply touched the interviewer, leading to a heartfelt request for blessings.

And it was quite a distance. So, they drove and then Prabhupada, they reached that studio, where the interview was supposed to be recorded and then telecasted live. So, Srila Prabhupada was waiting and then the interview started at 1 a.m., midnight, 1 a.m., post midnight. And then, the interviewer, being a Mexican, asked many probing questions about why you have come to this country, what is Krishna Consciousness. And Srila Prabhupada, you know, with love, with compassion, with feelings for the suffering humanity, spoke very nicely. He was quite upfront, did not mince words, in the sense, he was not trying to somehow please people, he was telling the truth. At times, using words which might even be little painful for a person to hear. One cannot be happy in this world without being Krishna Conscious. People in this world are suffering because of want of Krishna Consciousness. So, he was speaking these words. So, the interviewer asked lot of questions and then, about 2 a.m., the interview got over. And post the recording, post the interview, this Mexican, who was apparently a regular Mexican, must be eating beef and must be a meat eater and non-vegetarian, a little well-built person, he came to Srila Prabhupada. It seems his heart had melted during the interview. He came to Srila Prabhupada. He folded his hands. There were tears in his eyes and said, Swamiji, please bless me and my family.

---
#### practice, conviction, transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=1801
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He was practicing what he was preaching."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story by emphasizing that the potency of Srila Prabhupada's words stemmed from his consistent practice of Krishna consciousness and his unwavering belief in Krishna's teachings.

Generally, these, you know, they are quite, since they interview many people and all, they are quite hard-hearted. They don't just, they are not very emotional in that sense. Because they come across so many people, so many situations, so many different kinds of news, so many upheavals, ups and downs. So, they are quite strong in that sense. But it seems this person's heart melted after hearing the words of Srila Prabhupada. Folded his hands and said, Swamiji, please bless me and my family. So, that was the potency in Srila Prabhupada's words and that was because he was practicing what he was preaching. He was asking people to be Krishna Conscious. He was Krishna Conscious. He was Krishna Conscious. If he was asking people to believe in the words of Krishna, he himself had hundred times much stronger belief in the words of Krishna. Otherwise, those words will not have an impact. In fact, people will like, people will even clap, people may even appreciate, people may even comment saying that yes, it was so nice, we enjoyed it so much. But the transformation of heart may not happen. Prabhupada used to say, many times people attend this Bhagavad Saptah and at times even people cry. But once they go back home, the life remains the same. There is no transformation of heart. Krishna Bhakti was implanted in the hearts. And that's how so many people, millions of them all over the world, till date, have become devotees of the Lord and continuing to become devotees of the Lord.

#### example, action, purity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=2670

**Content Type:** recap
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We talked about how purity is a force."
**Summary:** The speaker recaps the first two points: example is better than precept and actions speak louder than words, emphasizing the potency of purity and conviction in Srila Prabhupada's teachings.

So we'll recap the points which we have so far covered. The first point was example is better than precept where we talked about how to practice what we preach is so very important. And we covered the example of Gandhiji where he told that small girl, small boy about not eating sweet after having practiced that himself. The second thing was action speaks louder than words. We can't expect a person to follow unless we ourselves practice it. In fact our words will not have that power, that potency. We talked about how purity is a force. And Shri Prabhupada, because he had that strong conviction, his words were quite potent and whosoever heard those words there was a transformation in the heart. The third principle is judged by your behavior.

I repeat, judged by your behavior. As practitioners of Krishna Consciousness, it is very very important for us to be mindful of our behavior.
