#### behavior, patience, guru
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=2771
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It's all the grace of my Guru who has always taught that the tongue should never lose its sweetness."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about a telephone operator who repeatedly connected a boss to the wrong number, and the boss's patience, attributed to his guru's teachings, ultimately led the operator to become a devotee.

This reminds me of a story. There was a lady who was working in a company and these were good old days when there was a telephone operator and they had a switch board and the calls would come and the telephone operator would connect. So lot of calls on that particular day, many people were requesting for calls, short distance and long distance calls and she was quite busy. So the main boss of that company called this lady, the telephone operator and said, Can you please connect me to this number? I have something very important to discuss. So this lady connected the number but unfortunately, when she connected it to that boss, the main person of that company, he found that it's a wrong number. So he called back this lady and said, looks like you've got the number wrong. Can you please check the number? And then he spoke the number again. Please note down this number and can you kindly connect? So this lady connected but again it was a wrong number. She connected the wrong number. He in turn again called and said, Can you please check? I think by mistake you have got the number wrong. Can you please try to connect it again? So she again connected. Believe it or not, nine times she connected the number wrongly. The ninth time, this person tells this lady, somehow this call is very important. Please try your best. Please connect it again. I want to speak to this gentleman. It is very, very important call. Can you please try again and be very careful and try to get this number right? So this time the lady called, connected the number and fortunately it was correct and he could speak. Towards the end of the day, this whole day, this lady was very, very apologetic, feeling very bad. How could I do it so badly? Connected the number wrongly not one time, not two times, nine times. So she was very, very feeling very, very disgusted with herself. Towards the end of the day, she requested, she called that person, the boss and said, Can I, sir, come and meet you? So he says, Okay, please come. This lady walks into the office and says, I am very, very sorry. The whole day I was very disturbed because what I have, every time I connected wrongly, you were very patient and you requested me again and it so happened that it happened nine times, not one time, not two times, nine times. Never you spoke rudely. Never you got provoked. I couldn't see any anger in your voice. Every time you kept on requesting me, Can you please try again? This time be more careful. How is it that you never got angry? How is it that you were so, you were like a thorough gentleman? How is it that I could see this in your behavior? Why did you not get provoked? So he said, It's all the grace of my Guru who has always taught that the tongue should never lose its sweetness. We should never be rude to anyone. That's what I have learned from my Guru.

---
#### behavior, responsibility, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=3021
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What kind of philosophy is this? A person goes and then his performance dips."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of maintaining one's responsibilities while practicing Krishna consciousness, warning against setting a bad example by neglecting duties or appearing groggy due to early rising.

Guess what the lady asked next. I am sure some of you would have guessed it right. The lady asked that gentleman, Who is your Guru? So this person told the name. And then he also said, Fortunately, we are quite blessed that this week he is in town and he is conducting a big discourse. He is giving a big discourse. Would you like to attend? So this lady was very, very keen to attend. She actually went, attended that program and it seems she also became a devotee. So he said, Yes, I would like to attend. So it's very important that when we try to practice the principles of Krishna Consciousness, we wear a Kanti Mada or we apply a Tilak, people get to know that we are practicing principles of Krishna Consciousness, Bhagavad Gita. It is very important. Sometimes we have seen students, because we talk about higher taste, we talk about that how human life is very precious. Many times people start not attending to their responsibilities sincerely. They lose taste. For many students, studying becomes, it's not important. Because somehow we also say that, Yes, in human form of life, Krishna Consciousness is very important.

---
#### behavior, example, guidance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=3119
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Your spiritual master will be judged by your behaviour."
**Summary:** The speaker cautions against fanatical behavior in the initial stages of Krishna consciousness, emphasizing the need for guidance in implementing principles in daily life and highlighting the impact of one's actions on their spiritual master's reputation.

And imagine a student who has come to Krishna Consciousness, who attends our sessions, and then assuming that his his performance starts dipping. Obviously people who are watching would see that what kind of philosophy is this? A person goes and then his performance dips. Or many times people start getting up in the morning quite early. They've heard Brahmamurta time, the importance. And then in the office, whole day a person is very groggy, sleepy, at times yawning. It sets a very bad example. So two things. One is, we have to be careful. Example speaks, example is better than precept. Action speaks louder than words. We may even want to preach. We may want to share this message with others. We have benefited. We may want others also to benefit. But more than the words, we speak, people are watching our actions. People are watching our behaviour. Be it in our family, with our near and dear ones, they are watching our behaviour. Yes, we are attending the classes, but how that is getting translated into your behaviour. And at times we can't blame because initially when a person, it takes time to get a proper understanding of the principles of Krishna Consciousness. At times our behaviour can be very fanatical. I remember a person started attending our session and he somehow got inspired, Brahma Murta time. And next day, you know, he went and kept an alarm. He got up at 3:30 a.m. And the whole family was quite disturbed. But he was insistent, no, I will get up. And for him to get up, living in a small house, kept an alarm at 3:30, disturbing everyone. We have to be mindful. People will get alarmed if we are not very careful in our dealings. How we deal with people, what words we speak, how we behave, how we act, we respond. Prabhupada said, your spiritual master will be judged by your behaviour.

---
#### guidance, implementation, preaching
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=3270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Even if we are not fully realized, even if we don't have full conviction, even if we are beginners, still, we can help others."
**Summary:** The speaker advises seeking guidance on implementing Krishna consciousness in daily life, emphasizing the value of Srila Prabhupada's letters and conversations, and encouraging devotees to share the message even if they are not fully realized.

Just like this, we talked about this lady. So it's very important that we are mindful that we take guidance, how to implement. It's very important we implement what we learn. Nothing wrong about having decided that yes, I should get up early in the morning. Nothing wrong about, yes, we should practice the principles of Krishna Consciousness. But how do we translate in day to day life? It requires some bit of guidance. For that, we can speak to the devotees whom we are in touch with. We can, one of the very important source of practical guidance is Srila Prabhupada's letters. There's a Veda base online. You could, Prabhupada's conversations. So many devotees, the doubts which we might have is not something new. Many devotees in the past who have started practicing Krishna Consciousness would have gone through those stages. So they are, these questions, these doubts are asked. And Srila Prabhupada has given wonderful answers. So we can read those letters which are available online. And we can get many important practical insights how to practice the principles of Krishna Consciousness. Now, it is important that we preach or we share this message with others. Bharat, Bharat bhumile janma hoile yaar. This is Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's instructions. One who has taken birth in this Bharat Varsha, which is now, we call it India. He has two very, very important duties. Janma saarthak kari karo pakar. He should perfect his life and then, help others perfect their lives. Now, does that mean that unless we practice it cent percent, backed with conviction, backed with full knowledge, backed with realization, and example is better than precept, action speaks louder than words, we should not share this message with others. We should wait for one day when we would have got sufficiently realized when we would have that same conviction which the Prabhupada had and then we can preach. The answer is no. Even if we are not fully realized, even if we don't have full conviction, even if we are beginners, still, we can help others.

---
#### preaching, readiness, influence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/z5V3r_rZIAQ?t=3426
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You can wake up a person, but you cannot wake up a person who is pretending to sleep."
**Summary:** The speaker encourages sharing the message of Krishna consciousness, even without full realization, but cautions against wasting time on those who are unwilling to listen, as negative influences can be contagious.

The example is that a person who is in ninth standard can always teach. He is not a teacher. He has not done B.Ed. He is not a recognized teacher, but he can always help a child in the first standard. And to preach is not very difficult. More so if we meet a person who is ready for it. In fact, I met a person who said that I speak to my friends about importance of being a vegetarian and giving up non-vegetarianism with non-vegetarian diet. But somehow this particular person is not getting convinced. Can you please share with me some arguments whereby I am able to convince him? So then I asked, I inquired what are his arguments? What is he saying? Why he is not getting convinced? So we should be very, it is very important for us to know that we cannot irrespective of our irrespective of our arguments, irrespective of our arguments, we cannot convince everyone. Not everyone is ready for it. You can wake up a person, but you cannot wake up a person who is pretending to sleep. You can take a horse to water, but you cannot force it to drink. So there are people who are not at a stage where they are fortunate enough to understand Bhagavad Gita. They are too deeply engrossed in Tamoguna and Drajoguna, completely entangled in material life, lot of material desires. They may not have the right frame of mind to hear and understand the principles of Gita. We can as a matter of duty do it. We can even speak to such people, but we should not waste our time with someone who is not wanting to listen. Forget about practicing, who doesn't want to listen, who is too deeply, very materialistic

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### LESSONS FROM THIS PANDEMIC | | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, pandemic lessons, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** introduction
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In fact, there were many many lessons which we all can learn and more so from the perspective of Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, we can see many of the philosophical truths and the teachings right in front of our eyes unfolding."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of lessons learned from the pandemic from the perspective of Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam, and announces a prayer session before the discussion.

A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is the lessons we learned from the pandemic. In fact, there were many lessons which we all can learn, and more so from the perspective of Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, we can see many of the philosophical truths and the teachings right in front of our eyes unfolding. So what are those truths? What are the perspectives which we can gain from this pandemic? We are going to discuss this today in our session. Before we get into this discussion, let us all pray together. Let us chant a few prayers together. I will be displaying these prayers on the screen and kindly fold your hands and let us all chant these prayers together and then get into this discussion.

---
#### prayer, chanting, Vaishnava tradition
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=78
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare"
**Summary:** The speaker leads the audience in chanting several Vaishnava prayers, including the Hare Krishna mantra.

Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Sri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamini Thinamine. Namaste Saraswati Dev grey gauravaniute vachaine Nirvisheshaah Shunya vaadveer Pascha Thyade Shatare Jai Shree Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Sri Advaita Gadiri Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrindha. Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratushe Namaha. Namo Brahmane Devaya Go Brahmane Hithaya Cha Jagad Hithaya Krishnaya Go Vindaya Namo Namaha. He Krishna Karuna Sindhu Dina Bandhu Jagatpate Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Namostute. Tapta Kanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye. Vanchaa Kalpatarubhyascha Kripa Sindhubhya Evacha Patitanam Pavanebhyo Vaishnavebhyo Namo Namaha. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

#### uncertainty, life, pandemic lessons
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=218
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The first learning, which was very very obvious, which became clear to everyone, is that life is very uncertain."
**Summary:** The speaker begins the discussion by stating that the first major lesson from the pandemic is the uncertainty of life and the unpredictability of human existence.

As I mentioned, although we have many learnings, many lessons to be learnt, I would broadly categorize all the learnings into three important aspects. The first learning, which was very obvious, which became clear to everyone, is that life is very uncertain. There is a lot of unpredictability in this human form of life. Many times we make plans and take things for granted. It is very difficult to establish what life is going to be like.

#### death, illusion, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=288
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Although everyone says that sab kuch chhod ke jana hai, but we can see that how we are so busy making plans as if we are going to live forever."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the illusion of permanence, noting that while people acknowledge the inevitability of death, their actions often contradict this understanding as they accumulate wealth and property as if they will live forever.

So once again, Bhutani, every moment. Ahani Bhutani. Bhutani means living entities. Gachchanti Yamalayam are going to the abode of Yamaraj. Yamalayam. In other words, meeting death. Shesha Sthavra Ichchanti, all those who are left behind, who are still alive, they think or they live with that mentality, they live with that consciousness, they live with that understanding that I am not going to die. I have heard this many times, people saying, sab kuch chhod ke jana hai. Khaaliyaat haayate, khaaliyaat chale jayenge. But our actions don't meet that understanding. We don't walk the talk. Although everyone says that sab kuch chhod ke jana hai, but we can see that how we are so busy making plans as if we are going to live forever. We run behind properties, accumulate riches, wealth, as if we are going to live and enjoy them for eternity.

#### pandemic impact, uncertainty, loss
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "People had planned for their future, but pandemic shook lot of things."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how the pandemic disrupted people's plans, leading to job losses, salary cuts, and the loss of loved ones, highlighting the obviousness of life's unpredictability.

And in this pandemic, we saw that how, in fact, people saw the plans getting completely rattled with this pandemic. People had planned, I will do this, my son will do like this. They had planned for their future, but the pandemic shook a lot of things. A lot of people had made their career plans, they got shaken. People lost their jobs. There were very uncertain times. There were salary cuts. And above all, many people lost their near and dear ones. So it became very obvious to everyone that life is very unpredictable.

#### Pariksit Maharaj, curse, Yogamaya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=433
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pariksit at least had seven days notice. Actually, none of us, none of us have that notice."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Pariksit Maharaj being cursed to die in seven days, contrasting it with the reality that most people do not have such a warning, emphasizing life's unpredictability.

Otherwise, we tend to think that I am 40 years old, 50 years old, still have another 40, 50 years to go, which is not true. Shila Prabhupada mentions that in the Srimad Bhagavatam, when Pariksit Maharaj was cursed, just to briefly touch that story. Pariksit Maharaj is the grandson of Arjuna. He is the son of Abhimanyu. And he was the emperor of the whole world. He was enthroned after the Pandavas retired from the kingdom. Pariksit became the emperor of the whole world. And then one day while he had gone to the forest for hunting, he was very thirsty and happened to visit a hermitage where he saw a sage meditating. And since he was thirsty and he was an emperor, it was a normal protocol that when an emperor goes somewhere, he is received very warmly with a lot of honor, with a lot of respect. But here that simple sage was completely oblivious of this emperor coming to his hermitage. And hence he was busy with his hermitage. He didn't even open his eyes, he didn't get up, nor he welcomed the king to his hermitage. So seeing this, Pariksit became quite angry. Although he was a great devotee of the Lord under the spell of Yogamaya, many of these Leelas, the pastimes which involves great devotees, there is a divine Shakti, energy of the Lord acting which is called the Yogamaya. There is a difference between Yogamaya and Mahamaya. The energy which acts on people like us, the common people who are in Maya, that's called Mahamaya. And the energy which acts on the devotees, which makes them at times forget the supremacy of the Lord, so that the Leela of the Lord can be enacted. That's called Yogamaya.

---
#### Pariksit Maharaj, curse, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pariksit, although he was the emperor, he could counter curse, he could retaliate, he could kill that Brahmana boy, but he did not do that."
**Summary:** Continuing the story of Pariksit Maharaj, the speaker describes how the sage's son cursed the king, and how Pariksit, being a great devotee, accepted the curse with detachment and prepared for death by hearing Srimad Bhagavatam.

Under the influence of this Yogamaya potency, Yashodama was worried about Krishna. Is he really safe? What if the demons attack him? Although he is the Supreme Lord, but under the influence of Yogamaya, Yashodamai could not understand or could not fathom that her child is not an ordinary child, is none other than the Supreme Lord. So under the influence of this Yogamaya, Pariksit became very wild, very angry, and in that rage of anger he saw a dead snake. He picked up with his arrow and put it across the neck of the sage. And when this whole episode, his whole thing of Pariksit getting angry and putting the dead snake with his arrow around the neck of the sage, the sage was seen by Sringi, the son of that sage, he became very angry and cursed the king that he will die with a snake bite. A snake will bite this king and will die in next seven days. So Pariksit, although he was the emperor, he could counter curse, he could retaliate, he could kill that Brahmana boy, but he did not do that. Because he was a great devotee of the Lord, he was quite detached internally. He renounced his kingdom and went to the bank of river Ganges, fortunately met Shukadev Goswami, who is son of Vedavyas. And for next seven days, non-stop, he heard Srimad Bhagavatam.

#### death, consciousness, unpredictability
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=689
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Pariksit Maharaj had at least notice of seven days. Actually, none of us, none of us have that notice."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that unlike Pariksit Maharaj, who had a seven-day notice of his death, most people do not have such a warning, highlighting the unpredictable nature of life and death.

Now Pariksit Maharaj had at least notice of seven days. He knew that he had seven days and in seven days he should utilize his time very optimally so that he can increase his remembrance, he can bring the Lord in his consciousness. Because as we all know, the ultimate goal of life is to somehow be conscious of the Lord. Anta Narayana Smriti, we could breathe our last breath thinking and remembering the Lord, being conscious of the Lord. That's real success. That's how the sages used to, the kings used to retire the forest, so that towards the last end of their lives, they could, without any deviation, without any distraction, they could focus on the Supreme Lord. So Prabhupada mentions Pariksit at least had seven days notice. Actually, none of us have that notice. Life is very unpredictable.

---
#### life expectancy, uncertainty, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=764
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We take life for granted."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a pencil to illustrate how people often take life for granted and assume they have a long time to live, even though death can come at any moment.

And this became very obvious in the pandemic. We take life for granted. In fact, I've shared this in an earlier session. We used to do this demonstration physically. You know, we would have, let's say a pencil, one end of the pencil representing the beginning of life, the day we were born in this world. And the end of the pencil represents the day we are going to leave this world or breathe our last breath. So I have done this exercise many times in many forums, in many talks. And invariably, when I show and I call one of the participants and say, if this pencil represents our entire lifetime, lifespan, the entire life, this represents the day we were born. This represents the day we leave this world. Where are you today? So I'll see the participants doing a quick calculation. If a person is 40 years, you know, he'll point somewhere in between the pencil. If he's 25 years, somewhere one-fourth of this thing, knowing that average lifespan is 80, 90, 100 years. So somewhere around that, they'll point out.

#### death, tragedy, pandemic
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=847
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When death comes, at times, there is no discrimination that this person is 80 years, this person is 20 years, this person is 40 years."
**Summary:** The speaker points out that death does not discriminate by age, citing news of tragedies where young people also die, and highlighting that this was very obvious during the pandemic.

And I've also seen invariably some of the participants who are little wiser would start laughing, saying, no, no, no, it's not correct. The fact is, none of us can be very sure. If you see today's newspaper headlines, some of the news in the front page, you will see invariably there will be news of some tragedy, some natural calamity, where many young people also died. Like today, just news. I didn't get into details. In Kerala, there was one accident in which three generations of family members expired. I was told six in a family expired. So when death comes, at times, there is no discrimination that this person is 80 years, this person is 20 years, this person is 40 years.

---
#### pandemic, death, uncertainty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=908
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This all shows that life is very unpredictable."
**Summary:** The speaker shares several anecdotes of young people and prominent individuals who died during the pandemic, emphasizing the unpredictability of life and the ever-present possibility of death.

And this was very obvious during the pandemic. Although, yes, post 50 years, there were more casualties, elderly people, but there were many young people also who lost their lives. And I called upon one person who was helping us. He is an architect. And the thing was getting delayed. Our work was getting delayed. So I was a little upset and called that person and was more in a complaining mode. Okay, why this has not happened? Thing is stuck up. We are losing a lot of money. Work has not progressed. So the person apologized and said that I am completely shaken. In fact, it was a lady. And this lady is around 45 years. She has a small child. And she told me that just next door today on that particular day, someone very close to them has expired. And then I was curious. You know, it was peak of COVID. I said, I was curious. And I said, because of, she herself said because of coronavirus, COVID. And she was 22 years old. So we had such occurrences also where young people lost their lives. They got infected. They were rushed to the hospital. And in a span of a few days, they were put on ventilator, had issues with their breathing. There was another person who was a chartered accountant. In fact, very eminent chartered accountant in Mumbai. He was very active. They had some association. In that association, he would take some lead roles. He was an organizer in many forums. And that person was hospitalized. And in four days, left the body. It was a big shock. And then we have many celebrities like, recently, film actor Siddharth. He left the body. Cardiac arrest. So this all shows that life is very unpredictable. Any moment we can come into the clutches of death.

#### human life, rarity, death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1053
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This human form of life we have got means we have obtained, we have received, we have achieved. It's very difficult. It's very rare. It's very rarely obtained."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srimad Bhagavatam, emphasizing the rarity and preciousness of human life, obtained after many lifetimes, and urging listeners to utilize it before falling into the clutches of death again.

And that is what Srimad Bhagavatam is saying. This human form of life we have got means we have obtained, we have received, we have achieved. It's very difficult. It's very rare. It's very rarely obtained. After many lifetimes, we have got this human form of life. After many lifetimes of transmigrating from one species to another. And then, which is very obvious, we all can understand that, yes, human form of life is very rare. And then the Bhagavatam says, before we again fall into the clutches of death.

---
#### pandemic, death, fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "What if a day comes when we see lots of dead bodies in the street?"
**Summary:** The speaker describes witnessing a dead body being taken from a house during the pandemic and recalls the fear and uncertainty of those times, including concerns about overflowing graveyards and crematoriums.

Which we saw right in front of our eyes. One day I was looking down from the apartment where we stay in Mumbai. And then I saw a dead body being brought out from the house. It was in a stretcher with a white cloth draped all over the body. And there were peak of pandemic days and there was a lot of uncertainty in the air. Although towards the end, you know, doctors were able to treat it and there were these vaccinations. I am talking about the days before the vaccine was out. There was a lot of unpredictability. And one day, I was speaking to a lady who is also a devotee. She was actually very worried. She was all alone in her house. She was in a state of panic. A lot of unpredictability. In fact, those days when the municipal corporation had to notice the vegetable shops will be closed and the localities only essential supplies will be given. There was curfew imposed. And then when I was seeing this dead body being brought, I thought, what if? And there were news that how even the graveyards and even the crematoriums are getting over flooded with a lot of bodies piled up and all that. And there was one politician in Thane who was very rich and very opulent. And he died and he was being cremated. Then a thought crossed my mind, what if a day comes when we see lots of dead bodies in the street?

---
#### pandemic, fear, Gita study
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "During that time, we saw many, many people wanting to know more about God, learn more from Gita."
**Summary:** The speaker reflects on the widespread fear and uncertainty during the pandemic, noting that many people turned to Gita study and sought to understand the meaning of life and suffering.

That's what happens in a pandemic. There have been cases where, you know, en masse people have died. The whole village have. Because it was very unclear at that time. So that lady was, she was talking to me over the phone. She said, Prabhuji, I'm very worried. And so I tried telling her that please depend on Krishna, everything will be okay and all that thing. But I could see there was a lot of panic in the air. A lot of uncertainty. They say, in fact, during that time, we saw many people wanting to know more about God, learn more from Gita. We floated many Gita courses and a lot of people who participated. Yes, people also had time, but people also going through a lot of shock, a lot of agony, a lot of panic, a lot of worry. And many people attended Gita courses, tried to understand what this life is all about, why this is happening, why good people are suffering and so on and so forth. So Bhagavatam says, before we again fall into the clutches of death.

---
#### human life, sense gratification, Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1312
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Goal of life has become, how we can maximize our sense gratification."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Bhagavatam, stating that human life has a very important goal to achieve, and should not be wasted pursuing pleasures attainable in other life forms, criticizing the modern focus on maximizing sense gratification.

We have a very important duty. We have a very important goal to achieve. And Bhagavatam also says, He says, we should not waste our time pursuing those pleasures, pursuing those goals, which are attainable even in other forms of life. If you see, many of us have goals and the goal is how we can maximize, how we can increase our sense gratification. There could be intermittent goals, there could be goals that I want to achieve this, I want to be this, I want to... But it's all about, by being that, I'll be able to gratify my senses better. I will be able to live in a bigger house. I'll have a better car. I'll have more luxurious surroundings. People will be in awe and reverence. I'll become famous. I'll become a celebrity. People will talk about me. It's all about gratification of our senses. And if we do a little bit of introspection, the goal of life has become how we can maximize our sense gratification.

---
#### sense gratification, human life, uncertainty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1396
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Human form of life is meant to achieve that form of happiness which is not attainable in other life forms."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that sense gratification is available in all life forms, but human life is meant for a higher purpose: to achieve a form of happiness not attainable in other forms, emphasizing the urgency due to life's uncertainty.

So Bhagavatam is saying, means this sense gratification, in every life form. May not be in a very sophisticated way. A dog may have a sexual intercourse on the street right in front of everyone. May not be in a very opulent surrounding. May not be in a very gorgeous environment. But, the enjoyment is the same. So Bhagavatam is saying, This, this enjoyment which comes from sense objects, when they, when there is a touch with the senses and the sense object, it is available in all life forms. Human form of life is meant to achieve that form of happiness which is not attainable in other life forms. So this is what Bhagavatam is saying and this we saw right in front of our eyes. quickly advanced. Why there is eagerness? Because there is uncertainty. Because, although we have got this human form of life after many lives, there is no surety, there is no guarantee that we are going to live for 100 years.

---
#### devotional service, childhood, Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "From the very childhood, from the very young age, we have to take up this process of devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the need to engage in devotional service from a young age, countering the common misconception that it is something to be pursued only after retirement or later in life, quoting Bhagavatam and Prahlad Maharaj.

Therefore it says, there is a sense of means quickly, quickly, without wasting a moment, without wasting any more time. And unfortunately in present time people live with that understanding that I am too young. This is not the right time. This is not the right age. Post retirement, post 80 years, 90 years, 100 years, when I have grown up, I have grandchildren, they all settled, they all married and I have enough time and I am free of all my family duties. I am completely free. Then I will focus on remembering the Lord. No. From the very childhood, from the very young age, we have to take up this process of devotional service. That is what Bhagavatam is saying.

#### Maya, pandemic, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1523
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In the pandemic we could see for some time the reality of life glaring at our face."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that forgetting the reality of death is due to Maya, and the pandemic provided a glimpse of this reality, grounding many people and making them realize the limitations of science.

That is what Prahlad Maharaj is saying. Unfortunately we forget this reality and that is the reason why we are living in this world. And that is because of an energy of the Lord which is called Maya. In the pandemic we could see for some time the reality of life glaring at our face. And many of us got grounded, became humble, realized that even though science has made so much of advancement, still we are helpless.

#### pandemic, helplessness, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1572
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In spite of having all the money, we are so helpless."
**Summary:** The speaker shares the story of a wealthy businessman who became more devoted after the pandemic, realizing the limitations of money and the helplessness experienced during the oxygen shortage in the second wave.

I have shared this in one of our earlier Gita Live sessions. There was a person, he met me in Jaipur. He is a very big businessman and he often comes to our temple here. So he, after this pandemic, has become very devoted. He is now very spiritually inclined, spends a lot of time, he goes to Vrindavan, comes to our temple. He says, I just come, I don't much talk to anyone. I just sit behind, attend the Aarti and just at times for a couple of hours. So then he was sharing his experience. He said that I have traveled all over the world. He has studied abroad. The whole family, the children have studied abroad. Very rich family. They have many industries. And he says, now, you know, there was a time when there was a rat race. Morning to night, it was all an expansion. What next? What next? What next? Where to invest? How much to invest? It was all about financial calculations. And he said that now, after this pandemic, I have learnt a lot of lessons. So I was little curious. I said, what have you learnt? So he said, one particular instance where we saw that how in spite of having all the money, we are so helpless. He shared with me, this was the time in the month of May, the second wave, when people were dying because of shortage of oxygen.

---
#### pandemic, helplessness, money
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1673
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This incident where in spite of having a lot of money, a person was helpless, stuck him very badly."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of the businessman, detailing his desperate attempts to find a hospital bed for his son during the oxygen crisis, highlighting the realization that money cannot always save a person.

There were no hospital beds. All the hospitals were filled. And we have seen that in our television, news channels. So the person is, his 20 year old son was down with COVID. His condition was deteriorating. Oxygen levels started dipping. It came to almost like 85. He was in a small town about 100 kilometers from Jaipur. He called this person knowing that he knows many hospitals, many doctors. So he called this person and told him that I am rushing to Jaipur. Please arrange a bed for my son. He needs to get admitted in a hospital. So this person told him, wait, wait, let me arrange a bed. Let me first talk to the doctors. And since he was a very eminent businessman, had a lot of contacts, a lot of big network and knew many doctors. He tried calling many doctors and first time in his life, he could see that nobody is responding to his calls. Generally, you know, when a person is big, eminent person, a rich man, people generally do not not take their calls. Or even if there is a missed call, you know, immediately reply. But here he is calling the doctors, they are not replying. He is calling some bureaucrats, they are not replying. He is calling some, you know, the owners of private hospitals, they are not. Because they, they themselves are in trouble. They don't, their innumerable calls are coming. How many calls they can attend? How many people they can help? They are also helpless. And nobody is responding. And this person said, I am carrying with me 20 lakh cash. Do what you want, but I need a hospital bed urgently. And then finally this person says, this person did not get a hospital bed, they could finally manage him to get admitted in hospital and he was in a balcony for a while and eventually he got a hospital bed and, some Lord's blessings, he also recovered. But this incident where in spite of having a lot of money, a person was helpless, stuck him very badly. He realized that eventually, this money is not going to save a person.

---
#### detachment, illusion, reality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2rt55RBlTDk?t=1833
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Although we know that in spite of having everything, we are not completely protected."
**Summary:** The speaker reflects on the obviousness of the fact that one must go empty-handed at death and that material possessions do not guarantee protection, questioning why people forget this reality.

So this is another lesson which we can learn. Now how do we, although it is obvious, although we know that we have to one day go empty handed, although we know that in spite of having everything, we are not completely protected. Bhagavatam says, Asat Sainiya. So how do we forget this? What makes us forget this reality? If you see, in the crematorium, you will invariably see written, Ram Naam Satya Hai. Ram Naam Satya Hai. Which means, this name of God is the reality. All this Moh Maya Ki Duniya. It's an illusion. It's Moh Maya.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### LETTING GOD INTO OUR LIFE | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### relationship with God, forgetfulness, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=28
**Verse References:** BG 9.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Today's session is all about how we can let God into our lives, experience His presence and have a nice wonderful relationship with Him."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the topic of letting God into our lives, emphasizing our inherent relationship with the Lord and the common state of forgetfulness.

A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is, Letting God into our lives. We have a very intimate, strong relationship with the Lord. We are part and parcel of the Supreme Lord. Krishna in the Gita says, just like the limbs of our body, they are a very integral part of the body and in one sense cannot be separated, nor can they have their independent existence. So likewise, being part and parcel of the Lord, having a thick relationship with the Lord, we should not expect to have that relationship live. We should have the Lord as our constant companion. We should feel the Lord being with us, guiding us, directing us, leading us, but that may not be the present state of our consciousness. Many a times we are completely forgetful of the Lord, completely oblivious of His presence in our lives. So today's session is all about how we can let God into our lives, experience His presence and have a nice wonderful relationship with Him.

**Content Type:** practical

**Key Quote:** "Let us all pray together, beg mercy of Srila Prabhupada, our beloved spiritual master, Lord Shaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Shri Krishna and all the great Acharyas to bless us with the right understanding."
**Summary:** This segment consists of prayers offered to Srila Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Krishna, and the Acharyas, seeking blessings for right understanding.

So before we get into the discussion, let us all pray together, beg mercy of Srila Prabhupada, our beloved spiritual master, Lord Shaitanya Mahaprabhu, Lord Shri Krishna and all the great Acharyas to bless us with the right understanding. So please, if possible, you can fold your hands and join me in offering these prayers. Let us chant these prayers together and then get into this discussion. Nama Om Vishnupadaya, Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale, Srimate Bhaktivedanta, Svamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve, Gauravani Prajarine, Nirvishesha Shunyavadi, Shri Krishna and all the great Acharyas. Paaschaatya Desha Tarihne. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya, Prabhu Nithyananda, Shri Advaita Gadadharah, Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakt Vrinda. Namo Mahavadanyaya, Krishna Prema Pradayate, Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya, Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya, Prabhu Nithyananda, Shri Advaita Gadadharah, Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakt Vrinda. Namne Gauratushe Namah.

#### Mahat Seva, Daivi Prakriti, spiritual energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=376
**Verse References:** SB 5.5.2, BG 7.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Mahatmas are those souls who have taken shelter of Daivi Prakriti, the spiritual energy, the spiritual potency of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment defines Mahat Seva and Mahatmas, explaining that Mahatmas are those who have taken shelter of the spiritual energy of the Lord, and are free from material entanglement.

The first point is Mahat Seva. Many of us may not be very clear about what is this Mahat Seva. Mahat Seva Dwaram Vimukte. Shri Madbhagavatam says, when we serve the Mahatmas, who are the Mahatmas? Krishna defines Mahatmas in the Bhagavad Gita. Mahatmanastu Maampartha Daivim Prakriti Ashritah. Mahatmas are those souls who have taken shelter of Daivi Prakriti, the spiritual energy, the spiritual potency of the Lord. Broadly speaking there are two energies, one is the material energy and one is the spiritual energy. We as Atmas can either be under the influence of material energy or under the influence of spiritual energy. So Mahatmas are not, these days you have many Mahatmas, generally people have an understanding if you have a beard, you have a turban, you have matted hair, you have some ash smeared on your face, you have worn a dress which is saffron dress, so you naturally conclude here is a Mahatma, here is a Sadhu. But Krishna defines Mahatmas as those personalities, those Atmas who are under the shelter of Daivi Prakriti, internal energy of the Lord. Spiritual energy of the Lord. They are completely free from material entanglement, material desires, material meshes of life.

---
#### detachment, liberation, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=499
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Mahat Seva Dwaram Vimukte, the doors for liberation, for freedom from entanglement of material existence, opens when we serve or associate with Mahatmas."
**Summary:** This segment clarifies that Mahatmas are not necessarily renunciates, but those who have taken complete shelter of the Lord, and that serving such Mahatmas opens the door to liberation.

The example is given just like the lotus, although it is growing in a pond with dirt all around, with mud all around, with mush all around, but it is pristine, pure, untouched by all what is in the surroundings. So Mahatmas does not necessarily mean a person who is in renounced order of life, who is a sanyasi, who is having a beard, who is having a dress like this, not necessary. A person who may be a grahastha, a householder, can also be a Mahatma. The criteria which Krishna talks about is Daivim Prakriti Ashritaha. He is taken shelter of the lotus feet of the Lord. He is under the divine protection of the Lord. He is under the internal energy or spiritual energy of the Lord. And when we serve such Mahatma, Mahat Seva Dwaram Vimukte, Dwaram means the door, door to liberation, freedom from entanglement of material existence, opens up. Our good fortune begins. Our relationship with the Lord gets revived. Lord starts becoming very active in our lives when Mahat Seva Dwaram Vimukte, when we start serving the Mahatmas. Service can be of various types. I will come to that a little later on. Mahat Seva Dwaram Vimukte, the doors for liberation, for freedom from entanglement of material existence, opens when we serve or associate with Mahatmas.

---
#### shelter, materialism, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=611
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For him, the Lord is everything."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts association with Mahatmas with association with materialists, explaining that associating with those deeply attached to material sense gratification leads to entanglement.

And the definition of Mahatmas is not necessarily a person who is a sanyasi, a person who is taken complete shelter of the Lord. For him, the Lord is everything. He is completely dependent on the Lord. Just like Prahlad Maharaj, although he was a boy, but he is a Mahajana. We can call him a Mahatma. Therefore, we study the teachings of Prahlad Maharaj in the Srimad Bhagavatam. So, Mahatmanastu Maam Partha Daivin Prakriti Ashrita. They have taken shelter of internal energy of the Lord. And when we serve such Mahatmas, then the door to liberation gets opened up. Mahat Seva Dwaram Vimukte, Tamo Dwaram Yoshitam Sanghi Sangam. Tamo Dwaram means door to hell, hellish life, door to entanglement, door to completely caught up in the meshes of material existence gets opened up when Yoshitam Sanghi Sangam when we thickly associate with material sense gratification, which is predominantly women and material happiness, material enjoyment. Yoshitam Sanghi Sangam, those who are very materially attached, hardcore materialists, full of material desires, full of ambitions to enjoy this material existence, full of hope that one day we will solve all material problems and we will be happily live here ever after. People with such hopes, people with such consciousness, hardcore materialists, when we associate with them, then Tamo Dwaram Yoshitam Sanghi Sangam.

#### relationship with God, revival, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=728
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we serve the Mahatmas, when we associate with the Mahatmas, when we hear from such Mahatmas, then our relationship with the Lord gets revived and the Lord starts becoming very active in our lives."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of associating with Mahatmas to revive our eternal relationship with the Lord, even if we are currently forgetful.

So if we have to let Lord into our lives, if we have to crystallize our relationship with the Lord, which is eternal in nature, although we are forgetful of the Lord, just like a child may deny that so and so is not my father, but even after his denial, the relationship still exists. He may not be ready to accept that relationship, but the fact remains that there is a relationship. Everyone who is born has a father and a mother. He may not remember that relationship, he may not accept that relationship, still the relationship exists. So like this, when we serve the Mahatmas, when we associate with the Mahatmas, when we hear from such Mahatmas, then our relationship with the Lord gets revived and the Lord starts becoming very active in our lives.

#### Narada Muni, Mahat Seva, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=791
**Verse References:** SB 7.5.23
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Narada, small boy, was also just hearing. May not be fully able to understand everything what they were discussing, but he was just in their company and hearing submissively."
**Summary:** This segment provides the example of Narada Muni's previous life as the son of a maidservant, highlighting how serving Mahatmas and hearing their discussions about the Lord led to his spiritual advancement.

I will give you two examples from Srimad Bhagavatam. The first example we have of Narada Muni. We all have heard about Narada Muni, Narada Bhakti Sutra. Narada is a great exalted devotee, completely liberated, engaged in glorifying the Lord. So Narada Muni, it is said that in his previous life, he was actually a son of a maidservant. He was not very literate, he was not very pundit, he was not a scholar, he was not born in a Brahmana family. He was son of a maidservant. And during the Chaturmas period, generally the sages, they travel from village to village, town to town, preaching the glories of the Lord. And during the Chaturmas, because it is a rainy season, they tend to camp in a particular place. In earlier times, because it is raining, the water could have been clogged, and the chances of snakes, and so many other things. So they would camp in a particular place during Chaturmas period. One such time, the sages were assembled, it was the Chaturmas period, and Narada's mother, that time it was not Narada, because I am talking about the previous life, was engaged in serving the Mahatmas. And the Mahatmas throughout the day were engaged in conversing about the Lord. Shravanam, Kirtanam, Vishnu Smaranam, they were engaged in devotional service, talking about the Lord, hearing Hari Katha, discussing Hari Katha, chanting the names of the Lord. And Narada, small boy, was also just hearing. May not be fully able to understand everything what they were discussing, but he was just in their company and hearing submissively. And he would also assist the mother in serving the Mahatmas. And when they have eaten, when they have taken Prasadam, the leftovers, Narada would clean, and also partake the remnants. When we offer food to the Lord, it becomes Prasadam. Mahaprasadam. And when that Prasadam is honoured, Mahaprasadam is honoured by Mahatmas, it becomes Mahamahaprasadam. And Narada had the good fortune of serving those Mahatmas, and also partake the remnants of the food left over by such Mahatmas.

---
#### Mahamahaprasadam, spiritual credits, opportunities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=991
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That's why we should try all possible opportunities, to make best use of all possible opportunities to associate with Mahatmas, to serve the Mahatmas, to engage in some kind of Seva to the Mahatmas."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the potency of Mahamahaprasadam and the accumulation of spiritual credits through serving Mahatmas, encouraging listeners to seize every opportunity for such service.

Mahamahaprasadam is very potent. It is Prasadam honoured by Mahatmas and the remnants of such Prasadam. Prasadam is what is remnants of food offered to the Lord and that honoured by the Mahatmas and the leftovers of such Mahaprasadam. So Narada honoured such leftover Prasadam. Served those Mahatmas, engaged in some menial service, assisting the mother. And just by doing that, being a small, insignificant, just like an ordinary boy engaged in the service of Mahatmas, he accumulated so many spiritual credits that in next life, he took birth as Narada. That's why we should try all possible opportunities, to make best use of all possible opportunities to associate with Mahatmas, to serve the Mahatmas, to engage in some kind of Seva to the Mahatmas. And when we engage in such service to Mahatmas, then Dwaram Vimukte, our door for liberation gets opened. Having talked about Seva, Seva does not necessarily mean physical Seva. Now someone may say, okay Narada was fortunate, he had sages, you know, he could serve them personally, but here we are in a locked on state, with COVID pandemic, Mahatmas are not moving around, how do we serve them?

---
#### Vapu Seva, Vani Seva, Srila Prabhupada
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1073
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I am serving his instructions."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concepts of Vapu Seva (physical service) and Vani Seva (service through instructions), using Srila Prabhupada's example of serving his spiritual master through his instructions.

So Mahatmas, there are two kinds of Seva, broadly speaking. One is the Vapu Seva. Vapu Seva means serving the body, Vapu, physical Seva. Just like I gave example of Narada in his previous life, serving the food, taking the remnants, hearing from them again. And the second type of Seva, Vapu Seva, not necessarily at all times, everyone will get an opportunity. But one should not consider himself that he is so unfortunate that he does not have an opportunity to Mahat Seva. One can also serve a Mahatma and that Seva is called Vani Seva. Vani means words, instructions. Srila Prabhupada said, I have met my spiritual master, Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakura, only four times in my entire life. These days, we don't have the luxury where in earlier times, there were Vedic Gurukuls, where a disciple would go, stay with the spiritual master and serve him, engage in annual service, just like Krishna, went to Sandipani Muni Ashram. We don't have that system in present times, where we go to an Ashram, stay with the Guru, serve the Guru, serve the Mahatma. No. So Prabhupada said, I have met my spiritual master only four times in my life, but I am in constant touch with my spiritual master through his instructions. I am serving his instructions. My Guru Maharaj, my Guru Maharaj ordered me that if you get money, print books. If you get an opportunity, preach this message in the Western world. Prabhupada went to the West to fulfill the instruction of his spiritual master to do Vaani Seva. So when we engage in serving the Mahatmas, it could be Vapu Seva, it could be Vaani Seva, hearing such Mahatmas, serving such Mahatmas, following the instructions of such Mahatmas, serving the mission of such Mahatmas, then Dwaram Vimukte, door to our liberation gets opened.

#### ISKCON, Mahat Seva, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1227
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To hear Srila Prabhupada's Vaani, to fulfill the wishes of Srila Prabhupada, it's Mahat Seva."
**Summary:** This segment explains that being part of ISKCON and serving its mission is also Mahat Seva, and that fulfilling Srila Prabhupada's wishes is a form of serving him.

This organization, International Society for Krishna Consciousness, Hare Krishna Movement, it's an institution of Srila Prabhupada. He wanted this institution to spread the glories of the Lord. So being part of this mission, to grow this mission, to serve this mission, is also Mahat Seva. To hear Srila Prabhupada's Vaani, to fulfill the wishes of Srila Prabhupada, it's Mahat Seva. So there is no shortage of opportunities to do Mahat Seva. Where there is a will, there is a way. We can always make excuses, I am in this situation. In present times, irrespective of what situation we may be in, if we want, we have enough opportunity to do Mahat Seva. So Mahat Seva, Dwaram Vimukte, and when we do this Mahat Seva, our good fortune opens up, our relationship with the Lord gets crystallized, the Lord becomes very active, becomes very pleased with us, showers His blessings, showers His mercy on us, and starts playing very actively in our lives.

#### Paramatma, surrender, reciprocity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1308
**Verse References:** BG 4.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate."
**Summary:** This segment explains that the Lord is present as Paramatma but remains dormant until we express a desire for Him, emphasizing the principle of reciprocity in our relationship with God.

Generally speaking, the Lord is there as Paramatma, but He is sitting dormant. We don't want Him, He doesn't want to disturb us. Yey atha maam prapadyante, tam satai va bhajaamiham. As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate. If we indicate with our desires, with our attitude, with our actions, I don't want you. I can manage my own lives. I have made arrangements on my own. Okay, all the best. But if we want Him, if we say, No Lord, I want You. Without You, my life is incomplete. I want to depend on You. I want to be conscious of You. I want You in my life. I want Your protection. Then the Lord is willing to do that. And the beginning point is, we can't directly go to the Lord. The beginning point is, we go through Mahatmas. Mahatmanastu maam partha, daivim prakriti ashritah.

#### Srila Prabhupada, Mahatma, confidential knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1374
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Anybody who came in touch with Sushila Prabhupada, served His mission, heard His vani, served Sushila Prabhupada in one way or other, His good fortune opened up."
**Summary:** This segment asserts that Srila Prabhupada is a great Mahatma, citing the positive impact he had on those who served him and his mission, enabling them to understand confidential knowledge.

Sushila Prabhupada is a great Mahatma. How can we say that? Wherever He went, anybody who came in touch with Sushila Prabhupada, served His mission, heard His vani, served Sushila Prabhupada in one way or other, His good fortune opened up. And He also became qualified to understand the glories of the Lord, which is said to be the most confidential knowledge.

#### miracles, transcendental knowledge, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1405
**Verse References:** BG 4.34
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "He will give you transcendental knowledge. He will reveal your relationship with the Lord. He will show you who the Lord is."
**Summary:** This segment recounts an anecdote where Srila Prabhupada was asked to perform miracles, and he responded by emphasizing the importance of approaching a Mahatma for transcendental knowledge through submissive inquiry and service.

And lakhs and lakhs, millions of people all over the world became devotees of Lord Shri Krishna. Prabhupada was once asked, can you show some miracles? Because generally, you know, you have Mahatmas, Sadhus, showing some miracles. Somebody can walk on water, oh, so great He is. Somebody can, you know, do some kind of a magical jugglery, produce some kind of ash, oh, so great He is. So when some person asked innocently in one assembly, Swamiji, can you show some miracles? So Prabhupada said, why should I show miracles? Krishna in the Gita says, tad viddhi pranipatena pariprashnena sevaya upadekshanti te jnanam. You have to approach a Mahatma, a great soul, and what you should do? You have to approach Him, inquire submissively, and serve Him. Mahat Seva. And what He will do? He will not show miracles. Upadekshanti te jnanam jnaninas tatvadarshina. He will give you transcendental knowledge. He will reveal your relationship with the Lord. He will show you who the Lord is. Upadekshanti te jnanam jnaninas tatvadarshina. Because He Himself has seen the truth. So Prabhupada says, just like you go to schools, colleges, for jnana, for knowledge, you don't go to college for seeing miracles. You don't go to Harvard and top Ivy League business schools and say, can you show me miracles? So long as they are competent to give you the right kind of knowledge, you join that college. So likewise, Mahatmas are those who are tatvadarshi, who have seen the truth, who have that transcendental knowledge and who can impart that transcendental knowledge. And what is the process? We have to serve such Mahatmas. We have to inquire submissively and sevaya. We have to serve them.

#### Harir Naam, Kali Santarana Upanishad, holy name
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1594
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Kalau Naastyeva Naastyeva Naastyeva Gathir Anyatha. Again it is repeated three times. There is no other way, there is no other way, there is no other way, Gathir Anyatha."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the second point, Harir Naam Evahikevalam, emphasizing the importance of the holy name of the Lord, and quoting the Kali Santarana Upanishad to reinforce that there is no other way in Kali Yuga.

We move on to the second point and the second point is, Harir Naam Evahikevalam. I repeat, Harir Naam Evahikevalam. Hari we all understand, the Lord, Supreme Lord Hari. Naama is the holy name, Harir Naama, Evah, Evah means only, kevalam, only the holy name of the Lord. It is mentioned in our scriptures, Kali Santrana Upanishad, Harir Naama Harir Naama Harir Naam Evahikevalam. Harir Naama Harir Naama Harir Naama Evahikevalam. Three times. When we emphasize something, we repeat it, to emphasize it, to reinforce it. Harir Naama Harir Naama Harir Naama Evahikevalam. Kalau Naastyeva Naastyeva Naastyeva Gathir Anyatha. Again it is repeated three times. There is no other way, there is no other way, there is no other way, Gathir Anyatha.

---
#### Kali Yuga, fanaticism, processes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1672
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "There are many processes for letting Lord into our lives."
**Summary:** This segment addresses the potential perception of fanaticism, clarifying that while there are many processes for connecting with the Lord, the holy name is particularly emphasized for Kali Yuga.

Anyatha means other process, other solution, other medicine. Now someone may say, it looks like we are trying to be a little fanatical. This is the only way. You go to Christianity, they say Jesus is the only way. And here you are saying holy name is the only way. Now let's understand why our scripture is saying it is the only way. Naastyeva Naastyeva Naastyeva Gathir Anyatha. There are many processes for letting Lord into our lives. There are many processes. In fact, even in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna talks about many processes, different yoga systems, karma yoga. Through karma yoga also you can have Lord into your lives, you can develop your relationship, yoga, relationship with the Lord. Jnana yoga through transcendental knowledge, through knowledge also you can establish relationship and connection with the Lord. So there are many processes. There is no denying the fact. And different religions also advocate different processes.

---
#### Mahat Seva, impracticality, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1758
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "These all are very impractical in present times."
**Summary:** This segment explains that while other religions also have Mahat Seva, processes like meditation, sacrifices, and elaborate deity worship are impractical for most people in Kali Yuga.

But the underlying principles are the same. I talked about Mahat Seva. It's there in all the religions. If you see the Bibles, Matthew, John, all this, these are all great saints. Their teachings, Mahat Seva, you follow their instructions. So, why it is said Naastyeva Naastyeva Naastyeva Gathir Anyatha. Although there are many processes, meditation, sacrifices, elaborate deity worship, these all are very impractical in present times. More so if we were to talk about for majority of people to follow. If we were to talk about elaborate sacrifices, not many people can afford it. Yagyas. There are no qualified Brahmanas who can do those yagyas. If we talk about elaborate deity worship, yes, some people can afford it. Some people can have the luxury of doing it. Not everyone can do it. People are living in very filthy conditions, slums, shared toilets. Deity worship which requires lot of cleanliness, lot of timeliness. It will not be practical for everyone.

---
#### degraded, sinful, forgetful
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5Z27WXUxcCY?t=1847
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In Kali Yuga, most of us are very, very fallen, very, very sinful, very, very degraded."
**Summary:** This segment highlights the degraded state of people in Kali Yuga, emphasizing their forgetfulness of their relationship with the Lord and entanglement in material existence.

The required qualification to do deity worship, even in our society, even in our institution, unless a person has taken Harinaam Diksha, then for two years, and then take the Brahmin Diksha, the sacred thread, he cannot get into the altar and do deity worship. So, there are many processes, but in Kali Yuga, most of us are very, very fallen, very, very sinful, very, very degraded. Krishna in the Gita says, as you surrender, so shall I reciprocate. We are completely forgetful of our relationship with the Lord. We are completely entangled in material existence. In the Srimad Bhagavatam, in the 12th canto, it is mentioned that as Kali Yuga progresses, Kaler, Dosh, Nidhe, Raj, Jambu, and as Kali Yuga progresses, our academic progress has been lost. Since gratification is so rampant, people have forgotten the goal of human form of life. Enjoying, eat, drink, be merry, has become the goal for most of the people. Enjoyment of senses has become the order of the day. Earning money, enjoying life by hook or crook, progresses, what would be the mentality of people in Kali Yuga, which we can start seeing, which we can see that it's happening.

Who has created this world? When I walk in the night, I see millions of stars. Who has made this arrangement? Mand Aishya Sabya, less intelligent, cannot understand, that question doesn't arise. Sumandh Matayo, Mand Bhagya, very unfortunate. Hi Upradhita, and always disturbed. You see, by and large people are very disturbed, there is lot of anxiety. **Key Quote:** "For him I am the swift deliverer from the ocean of birth and death."
**Summary:** The lecture begins by introducing verses 6-10 of the 12th chapter of Bhagavad Gita, focusing on Krishna's promise of deliverance for those devoted to Him.

This 12th chapter, verse number 6 to 10, is based on the teachings of our beloved spiritual master, His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swamishila Prabhupada. Here, these are the words spoken by Krishna to Arjuna: "For one who worships me, giving up all his activities unto me, and being devoted to me without deviation, engaged in devotional service, and always meditating upon me, who has fixed his mind upon me, O son of Pritha, for him I am the swift deliverer from the ocean of birth and death."

#### human birth, ignorance, happiness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oSdXn7olIeY?t=55
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We all are without fail, without any exception, all of us are striving for happiness in this world."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the rarity and importance of human birth, highlighting the universal pursuit of happiness and questioning the relevance of spiritual teachings to everyday life.

There are roughly 84 lakh species: plants, birds, aquatics, reptiles, animals. It is said after many, many lives, very fortunate souls get this human birth. And in human birth, although we all have various aspirations, various things to do, various things to aspire or to achieve, the most important thing which we have to aspire for is deliverance from the ocean of birth and death. For some of us it's not a problem because, you know, all of us are seeped in ignorance to different degrees. For an animal it doesn't matter. Janma Amrit, all this is of no concern to him. But why is this of a concern? Sometimes it appears that Bhagavad Gita is talking about some philosophy which has nothing to do with our day to day real life existence. How many of us feel so sometimes we discuss here something, we have to surrender to God, we have to do devotional service, we have to engage in the practices of bhakti, but you know, the real world is something else. How many of us feel like that? The requirements, the dynamics there, you know, everyone is having aspirations which appear to be real. These all appear to be something, you know, something good to have, but it's okay.

---
#### happiness, birth and death, material existence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oSdXn7olIeY?t=190
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So long as we are caught in the cycle of birth and death, that what we are striving for will always remain lucid. You will not get it."
**Summary:** The speaker asserts that everyone seeks lasting happiness, but this remains elusive while trapped in the cycle of birth and death.

We all are without fail, without any exception, all of us are striving for happiness in this world. We want to be happy. Whether it's a materialist or a spiritualist, whether it's an engineer or a doctor, Indian American, beggar in the street or president of United States, each one of us, the agenda behind our minds is to achieve happiness, and that happiness which is unadulterated and which is lasting. That's our aspiration. If you also introspect, that's what we are looking for. Even if you have some goals, if you want a particular job, if you want, you are gunning for some money, you are gunning for some name and fame, actually it's a means to that end: unadulterated peace and happiness. Am I right? Yes or no? And so long as we are caught in the cycle of birth and death, that what we are striving for will always remain lucid. You will not get it. Bhagavad Gita is blunt, but what you are striving for, what we are painstakingly trying to achieve, we will not get it so long as we are caught up in the cycle of birth and death.

#### karma, reincarnation, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oSdXn7olIeY?t=279
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna here is giving us a clue how we can come out of this cycle of birth and death."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the cycle of birth and death, driven by karma, and introduces devotional service as a means to escape it, clarifying that giving up activities doesn't mean abandoning one's duties.

What is the cycle of birth and death? We have got this material body. This material existence, we are subjected to much against our wish: old age, diseases, death. And much against our wish, without any choice, like none of us chose which birth we will take. We opened our eyes, fortunately found ourselves in good families, we got educated, we are sitting here, we are able to understand what we are discussing today. Karmana Daiva Netrena. Karmana Daiva Netrena. Karmana Daiva Netrena. Jantur Deha Utpathyate. Based on our karma, the soul helplessly moves on to another body. And this is happening life after life, life after life, life after life. So Krishna here is giving us a clue how we can come out of this cycle of birth and death. And unless we come out of the cycle of birth, that aspiration, that what we have of unadulterated happiness is not going to be achieved. In pure devotional service, when we, Krishna here is giving us hints of what is devotional service. One worships me, giving up all his activities unto me. Giving up all his activities unto me does not mean that we resign from our jobs and become like me. That's not the meaning of giving up activities unto me.

And being devoted to me, without deviation, engaged in devotional service. What is devotional service? Devotional service. Garland making. Garland making. Okay. Giving laws in the centre of their life. They have to do that. There are nine ways in which we can render devotional service. Nine processes. Starts with? Shravanam. Shravanam. Hearing about the Lord is also devotional service. Shravanam. Kirtanam. Chanting the glories of the Lord. Chanting the names of the Lord. Kirtanam. Smaranam. To think of the Lord. To remember Him. Padasmanam. Padasmanam. Padasmanam. To engage in tactical devotional service. Archanam. Worshipping Him. Vandanam. Offering prayers to Him. Like that there are nine processes in which we can engage in service of the Lord. So here it says without deviation, engaged in devotional service and always meditating upon Me, who has fixed His mind upon Me.
