**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the Supreme Lord can only be understood through devotional service, which reveals understanding automatically.

Now Supreme Lord, the only way where we can appreciate Him, can understand Him, can comprehend Him, can comprehend our position, who we are, what is the purpose of this creation, who is the Supreme Creator. On our own, using our intelligence, we cannot fathom, we cannot understand, we cannot figure out. There is a verse which says, atha shri krishna naam aadi na bhaved grahiyam indriye. By a blunt senses, we cannot comprehend the Lord and His glories. Sevan muke hi jiva dao, svayam eva his pratyada. But when we start rendering service to the Lord, starting with sevan mukha, mukha means with our eyes. Jeeva dao means tongue. Jeeva dao means tongue. Swayam eva, svayam eva means automatically that understanding is revealed to us. Krishna also in the Gita says, bhaktia maam abhijanati yavan yas chasmi tatvatah. Only through devotional service, bhaktia maam abhijanati. Janati means to know. Bhaktia maam abhijanati yavan yas chasmi tatvatah. Only through devotional service. Bhaktia maam abhijanati yavan yas chasmi tatvatah. In truth, only through devotional service, only through bhakti, when a person becomes a devotee, can he understand me.

#### service, self-realization, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oSdXn7olIeY?t=775
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we serve the Lord, we become self-realized."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes that we are parts of the Lord, meant to serve, and that serving the Lord leads to self-realization and understanding of the purpose of creation.

So what we have so far covered, we are amsa of the Lord, intrinsic inseparable quality is to serve. We can deny to serve God, but we cannot deny to serve. And if we don't serve God, we serve maya, in the form of false masters, in the form of our own senses. And when we serve the Lord, we become self-realized. We understand God, we understand who we are, we understand the purpose behind this creation. Why we are suffering in this world? What is this mritu samsara vartami? Which Krishna told us. Krishna talks about cycle of birth and death, we understand all that. The devotee does not desire any achievement other than pleasing the Supreme Personality of Godhead. So this is a purified state. Right now, it's all about pleasing my mind, pleasing my senses. But a purified state, a devotee desires, his all actions are guided by the pleasure of, or the satisfaction of, the Supreme Lord. And the process is very simple, it is not complicated. Our acharyas have given a simple process, some do's and don'ts, beginning with hearing about the Lord, chanting His glories, chanting the Hare Krishna Mahamantra, and svayam evahis pruthi adah, automatically it is revealed. We don't have to be big pundits, we don't have to be big scholars, we don't have to have knowledge of swamis. We don't have to have knowledge of Sanskrit. If we are simple, devoted, ready to serve the Lord, with humility, this knowledge is revealed to us.

#### knowledge, understanding, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oSdXn7olIeY?t=891
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We should not chant the shlokas without understanding."
**Summary:** The speaker tells a story of birds who memorized a mantra but failed to understand its meaning, leading to their capture, to illustrate the importance of understanding over mere recitation.

He will without delay deliver a devotee thus engaged from the ocean of material existence. There was once a group of birds. And they happened to go to a sage, and they said that, you know, we all get trapped in the nets, hunters come and they trap us, we get caught, and many of us, you know, we have lost our near and dear ones. So can you please give us some solutions, solution. So this sage gave them a mantra. He says, you remember this mantra always. Do this, and you will be protected. So what was the mantra? Anyone? Hare Krishna. So the mantra was, shikari aayega. Everyone repeat. Shikari aayega. Dana daalega. Repeat. Dana daalega. Jal bichhaega. By the way, all of us are part of this whole thing. Shikari aaya, dana dala, jaal bichhaya. So, they were given this mantra and the birds quickly memorized that. Just like we memorize the shlokas. And they started repeating the mantras. What was the mantra? Shikari aayega. Dana dalaega. Jaal bichhaya. Lope se usme fhasna nahi. So, actually Shikari came. And the birds sang, Shikari aayega. Dana dalaega. Dot, dot, dot. And actually, dana dala. And the birds were chanting, Shikari aayega, dana dalaega, jaal bichhaya. And they were eating the dana. And then Shikari aayega. And then Shikari came and actually spread that jaal also. The net also. And still the birds were chanting, Shikari aayega, dana dalaega, jaal se patna mand. And all that. And finally, they all got caught in the net. And Shikari came, collected the net with all the birds caught. And while the net was being taken away with all the birds caught, all of the Shikari aayega, dana dalaega, jaal bichhaya, lope se usme fhasna nahi.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Lord's Universal Form | Bhagavad Gita Chapter 11 Verses 14- 21 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### Universal Form, Bewilderment, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/znPdB4LJSLI?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Now, his natural loving friendship was overwhelmed with wonder."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of the Universal Form of the Lord and Arjuna's reaction of bewilderment and astonishment upon seeing it, transitioning from a friendly relationship to one of awe and reverence.

So, the title for today's discussion, the topic is The Universal Form. So, today we will be discussing based on the teachings, the purpose of His Divine Grace Srila Prabhupada. Text 14: Then, bewildered and astonished, His hair standing on end, Arjuna began to pray with folded hands, offering obeisances to the Supreme Lord. This is after the Lord has shown the Universal Form to Arjuna. So, this is what Arjuna is going through. He is bewildered, he is astonished, his hair standing on end. And when a person is in extreme ecstasy, you know, sometimes the hair stand on end, began to pray with folded hands, offering obeisances to the Supreme Lord. Now, till now, Arjuna had a relationship of the Lord as his friend, as his chariot driver, as his charioteer. And when he saw that he is not an ordinary person, he is not an ordinary friend, he showed the Universal Form which has all the universes, all the devatas, all... as we move forward, we will see what all he saw. Then, when he saw that, it is that very same Lord who has become the chariot driver, has become like a menial servant. He has taken on the menial job of driving my chariot. He was astonished, he was bewildered. He couldn't fathom what he is seeing. So, he folded his hand. He was in ecstasy, and paid obeisances. Now, his natural loving friendship was overwhelmed with wonder.

What he was seeing, he was unable to believe. He was wonder-struck. Arjuna watched. So, Arjuna said, my dear Lord Krishna, I see assembled together in your body, all the demigods. All the different Devi and Devatas, and various other living entities. I see Brahma sitting on the lotus flower, as well as Lord Shiva, and many great sages and divine serpents. So Universal Form of the Lord is all-encompassing. Everything what we see, higher planets, the lower planets, different living entities, different kinds of Devatas. All were, so to say, there in the Universal Form, and he saw that form. And his vision, his ability to see that Universal Form was the divine grace. The Lord gave him that vision. The Lord gave him the ability to fathom or to see that Universal Form. The causeless mercy of Lord Vishnu is unparalleled, and such mercy is perceived by the devotee only by the grace of the Lord. So even to fathom the Lord, to understand the Lord, we started our discussion today with that, so to say, point of view. To understand the Lord, we have to get the mercy of the Lord. #### Divine Vision, Krishna's Supremacy, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/znPdB4LJSLI?t=238

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything is emanating from me."
**Summary:** This segment addresses questions about who else saw the Universal Form, clarifying that Arjuna's vision was unique due to divine grace, and emphasizes Krishna's supreme position as the source of everything, leading to devotion and surrender.

Anyone has any question, anything you'd like to ask? Yeah, the others were also seeing the form, but specifically, what Arjuna saw, maybe what they saw is different from what Arjuna saw. He was, as we move forward in this discussion, it says, Arjuna saw because of the divine vision given to him by the Lord. So others may not have been given that. They saw something, but what they saw, was it the same form, Universal Form, which Arjuna saw? It's not clearly mentioned. Who was saying it? In the Gita? No, it is not true. 90 minutes was the duration in which the Gita was spoken. It's not true. Others were also hearing. Others were also seeing Krishna. I'll come to that part, as to why Krishna spoke the message of Gita in the battle field. And that too, at a very crucial juncture, where the battle is about to start. Where the battle is about to begin. There is a message for all of us. Why it was spoken there? It could have spoken somewhere else. O Lord of the universe, I see in your universal body many, many forms, bellies, mouth, eyes, expanded without limit. There is no end, there is no beginning and there is no middle to all this. In other words, it's unlimited, infinite. There is no start and end. There is no beginning and an end. Now Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead and he is unlimited. He is the source of matta sarvam pravartate, aham sarvasse prabhavo. Krishna says in the Gita, everything is infinite. Everything is emanating from me. Aham sarvasse prabhavo, matta sarvam pravartate, iti matva, one who has that understanding. Matva means the understanding. Matta means belief. Bhajante maam, worships me, glorifies me. Buddha bhava samanvitaha. Buddha means such an intelligent person, such a wise person. Bhava samanvitaha. With devotion, with bhava, with feelings, not sentimentally, not ritualistically, but having the right understanding, that supreme personality of Godhead, who is the source of everything, everything is emanating from him, the all-powerful, limitless, infinite Lord. When we have the tattvata understanding, the natural consequence of that understanding will be, we will surrender to him.

#### God's Nature, Krishna's Names, Attractiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/znPdB4LJSLI?t=409
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** This segment addresses common questions about God, such as whether God is a person and if God has a name, explaining that Krishna is unlimited and has infinite names based on His dealings with devotees, with Krishna being the principal name meaning 'to attract'.

Now some frequently asked question when it comes to God, is God a person? A lot of people believe in God. You ask, do you believe in God? Yes. What is your understanding of God? So a lot of people say there is some power, some energy, some divine force, some supreme controller. But it is vague. What is that divine? What is divine? So you have the dual Revelation, The person Dis pope, and me, before that, the self is dominated, off the streets, by the divine, divine authority and the will of god, that is order. Because I am alive, i am self, and whether i am able to ever breathe him, that will not be possible in jahman, just that word will What is that supreme controller? And second frequently asked question is, does God have a name? Is it a vague energy, divine force, supreme power? Is he a person? Does he have a well-defined name? Just like you and me, we are a person. We have an identity, we have a name. So is it some specific thing or it's a vague concept? It's a belief. Now, since God is unlimited, we talked about Krishna being unlimited, infinite, his names also must be unlimited. Everything about the Lord, his form is unlimited, his names are unlimited, his energies are unlimited, his potencies are unlimited, his creation is unlimited. Since God is unlimited, his names also must be unlimited. Therefore, we cannot settle on one name. God has many names. Can anyone help me with the names of God? So Prabhupada explains, for instance, Krishna is sometime called Yashodanandan, the son of Mother Yashoda. Any other names of God? Or Devakinandan, the son of Devakinandan. Or Vasudevanandan, the son of Vasudeva. Or Nandanandan, the son of Nandamahara. Sometime he is called Parthasarathy. So there are unlimited names of God. Infinite names of God. And all these names, because of many dealings of God with his devotees, according to those dealings, he is called by different names. His name, Krishna. Krishna, Krishna means to attract. His principal name, Krishna, the name, the word Krishna, Krishna, Krishna means to attract. And Lord, because he is infinite, he has infinite opulences, he is Shad Aishwarya Purnam, he is all attractive.

#### Krishna's Attractiveness, Definition of God, Opulences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/znPdB4LJSLI?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "He is not attractive only to human beings, only to some, this thing, even to other living beings. He is all attractive."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on Krishna's all-attractiveness, extending beyond humans to all living beings, and introduces Parasramuni's precise definition of God as Shad Aishwarya Purnam, complete in six opulences.

Now, when we see many pictures of Krishna, we see that he attracts even the cows, calves, birds, bees, trees, plants, even the water in Vrindavan. He is not attractive only to human beings, only to some, this thing, even to other living beings. He is all attractive. And that was demonstrated when Krishna personally descends in Vrindavan. He is attractive to the Cahod boys, to the Gopis, to Nandamaharas, to Pandavas and to all human society. Now Parasramuni, who is the father of Vedavyasa, he gave a very, very precise definition of God. Now, it is not just a vague understanding of God, very precise definition. Just like in science we study, if it is an electron, there is a definition for that. Or if there is any particular object which we are studying, we try to define it, to make it very specific. It is not left very vague. So, what is the definition of God? He defines God as, a person who is Shad Aishwarya Purnam. Shad means six, Aishwarya means opulences, Purnam means complete. Aishwarya means a person who has unlimited opulence. Who is full in six opulences, who is full in strength, fame, wealth, knowledge, beauty and renunciation.

So, if you find a person who has these opulences, to an unlimited extent, he is God. Now, let's go one by one, each opulence. We see in this world, there are many rich people, am I right? There are many people like rich people, like we have Bill Gates, now we have, who will become a millionaire, himself a millionaire, Michael Jackson, and celebrities whose earnings are in millions and billions. And because they are rich, they are attractive. May not be attractive because of their facial beauty. Because of their opulence. If somebody gets an autograph, I have Bill Gates' autograph. So, he is attractive because of his opulence. Now, visualize a scenario, a person who has unlimited opulence, he can proudly say, or rather he can boldly say, all what we see is my opulence. That's why, I think two classes back I said, only two people can say, everything what you see is my opulence, is mine. What are the two people? One, a madman can say that, and God can say that. Everything is coming from me. Everything belongs to me. I am the proprietor. Matta, Sarvam, everything is emanating from me. So, Bhagawan, the Supreme Personality of Godhead is the proprietor of all riches.

No one can claim. But God claims that. Krishna, now he gave some demonstrations of his richness or opulence. He had 16,108 wives and each wife lived in a separate palace. Palaces were built with first class marble. Pillars of palaces were made of kural. So, there is a description in the Bhagavatam about the palaces in Dwaraka. Krishna expanded himself into 16,108 forms and was personally present with each wife in each palace at the same time. This is the opulence of the Lord. It is not that he married 16,108 wives. Now, strength. We see lot of strong people, you know, for their strength. World heavy weight champions, boxing champions, known for their strength, athletic ability, and they become very famous. Can you give me example? Muhammad Ali, Mike Tyson, and many other so-called, you know, well-built personalities, very strong. Bhagawan, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, has unlimited strength. Now, this strength what we see, in the so-called strong people, is fleeting. It's temporary. Yes or no? With passage of time, even the so-called strong people, you know, become very weak, frail, old, diseased, and finally die. Bhagawan, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, has unlimited strength. His strength was present from the moment of his birth. It's not that he became God. When Pootana came to kill Krishna, she was a very big demoness. She was only three months old. He effortlessly killed the demon Pootana. When Krishna descended 5000 years back, enacted various pastimes, this was like small trailer, to give demonstration. These are all not fictitious stories. This is not fiction. Even when you go to Vrindavan, these places of pastimes are there, where Govardhan Hill is there. He gave that demo just to demonstrate that how he is that same Supreme Person. When Lord Krishna was seven years old, he lifted Govardhan Hill on the little finger on his left hand for seven days effortlessly. God is God from beginning. Krishna does not become God by some meditation or by some mystic power.

Beauty. We see a lot of beautiful people, but again, beauty is fleeting. And because of their beauty, they are attractive. Bhagavan, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, possesses unlimited beauty. All gigantic, beautiful creations, what you see, is just a small spark of His splendour. Then we talk about knowledge. #### Misconceptions about Gita, Holy Book, Meaningful Life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/znPdB4LJSLI?t=1333
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** This segment addresses common misconceptions about the Gita, such as it causing fights if kept at home or being exclusively for Hindus, clarifying that it is a holy book bringing auspiciousness and is meant for all, not just a post-retirement study.

Possible misconceptions, yes. You are right. So some people believe it, we should not keep Gita in home. Just like in the Gita, it's Mahabharata, the war is happening. If you keep Gita in the home, war will happen, fight will happen. Which is again, as you rightly pointed out, it's a misconception. In fact, it is a holy book. It brings all auspiciousness into the house if you keep Gita. In fact, Gita should be worshipped. Yes. It is a Hindu scripture. It is meant for Hindus. It's a Hindu scripture. Okay. Yes. It is a fiction created by a wise man or the whole thing is a fiction, including the wise man who spoke. Everything is fiction, it is mythology. Indian mythology. Any other misconceptions which we generally hear of when it comes to Gita? Okay, I'll come to couple of them which I heard many times. First thing that many people say, it's post-retirement study material. Abhi toh time hai, zindagi jeene ka, enjoy karne ka, career banane ka, har cheez ka time hota hai. Jab retire ho jaoge, aur kuch karne ko nahi hai, nothing better is left in your life, then read Gita peacefully. Actually, Gita is all about how to lead a meaningful and successful life, a dynamic life, in consciousness of God.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### MAKING THE RIGHT CHOICES | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### choices, destiny, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=60
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Every day, every moment, life throws up many choices on us, and the choices we make have an impact on our future, our destiny, our life."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of making the right choices based on the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita and leads a prayer with Sanskrit verses.

Hare Krishna, a warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita live session. It's a very interesting topic today. It's about making right choices. Every day, every moment, life throws up many choices on us, and the choices we make have an impact on our future, our destiny, our life. How to go about making the right choices? This is the topic which we are going to discuss in detail based on the teachings of Lord Shri Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita. So before we get into the discussion, get into the nitty-gritty of what constitutes right choices, let's seek the blessings of the Lord. Let's pray together. Please, if possible at your end, you can also fold your hands and along with me, you can chant these prayers, and after that, we'll get into this discussion. Nama om vishnu padaya krishna prishtaya bhutale, srimate bhaktivedanta swamin nitinamine. Namaste saraswate deve gauravani pracharine, nirvishesha shunyavadi pashchatyade shatarine. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda, Shri Advaita Gadhadhara Shiva Sadhi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate, Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratuse Namaha. Namo Brahmane Devaaya Go Brahmane Hitaya Cha Jagadhitaya Krishnaya Go Vindaya Namo Namaha. Hey Krishna Karuna Sindhu Deena Bandhu Jagadpate, Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Namostute. Taptakanchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindavaneshwari, Vrishabhanusute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye. Vanchakalpatarubhyasya Kripasindhubhyaevachya, Patitanampavanebhyo Vaishnavebhyo Namo Namaha. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare, Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

---
#### karma, equality, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=279
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are where we are in our lives, whatever good or bad we are encountering, it's the consequence of the choices we have made in the past, in this life or our previous lives."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the impact of choices on our lives, emphasizing that the disparity we see in the world is a result of our past actions and choices.

So we are talking about choices. Choices have a lot of impact on our lives. The choices we make moment to moment. The first aspect which we will cover today is the consequences of choices. We should know the consequences. How important it is to make the right choices. Although yes, we will falter at times. We will, in spite of all good intentions, end up making wrong choices. That's part of life. So but let's first understand what are the consequences of making the choices, right choices or the wrong choices. First thing we have to understand is that the Lord is equal to everyone. Although there is a lot of disparity in this world. We see people who are fabulously rich, and some people living in abject poverty. Some people very healthy, and some people are suffering from multiple diseases. Some people are very, very handsome, good looking, and some people are not so good looking. There is a lot of disparity in this world. Although we see a lot of disparity, Krishna in the Gita says that I am equal to everyone. He is our father. He is all powerful. He has created us. He has created these bodies. And there is a lot of disparity. Although there is disparity, he is saying I am equal to everyone. So why? The answer is we are where we are in our lives, whatever good or bad we are encountering, it's the consequence of the choices we have made in the past, in this life or our previous lives.

---
#### suffering, consequences, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=411
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "He could not take what he saw there, so much of poverty, so much of starvation, so many people die."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of a wildlife photographer who won an international award for his photographs of a starving girl in Sudan, but later committed suicide due to the suffering he witnessed.

Once there was an article which was published in National Geography. There was a wildlife photographer who had heard about the death triangle. Sudan, I am sure all of us have heard about. It's in South Africa. The three countries, adjacent countries. There is a lot of poverty and a lot of deaths because of starvation. Millions of people dying. So this photographer wanted to cover. He wanted to publish a story in leading magazines in the world just to showcase what's happening in Sudan. So he went there and one day he was trying to shoot photographs of all what he was seeing there. People in abject poverty, not having food to eat, dying of starvation. He happened to see a girl who was completely emaciated, weak, so much so that she could not even walk. It was very obvious that she was very hungry and not eaten for days. And because she was so weak, she could not even walk. And knowing that there is so much of poverty and people are dying of starvation, United Nations had set up some relief camps, and they were, you know, there were relief camps few kilometers away. And this girl was crawling, practically crawling to the nearest relief camp, which was few kilometers away, few miles away. She could not walk. On her hands and legs, she was crawling. And this photographer at a distance was capturing her movement, the emotions, the feelings, you know, the face, full of dust, tears gliding out, just like the wildlife photographs, just to get the right shot and wait for hours together. So he was waiting very patiently with his camera focused on this girl, capturing every minute emotion on the face of this girl, the pain, the agony, the hunger, which was quite evident on the face. And after crawling for a while, she didn't even had the energy. On one side, she was hungry, and she knew that she has to reach the relief point for getting the food. She would pause for a while, take little rest, and again crawl. And because she was so lean, so emaciated, there was a chance that she might not be able to reach the relief camp. She might collapse, and she might die. And just at a distance, the photographer noticed a vulture. A vulture was sitting, you know, vultures, you know, they feast on dead corpse, dead bodies. That's their food. There was a vulture sitting, and that vulture also is patiently waiting for this girl to die, waiting when this girl would, just like sometimes, you know, in a kitchen, we are very hungry, something is getting cooked, we are waiting for that cooking to get over so that we can eat. So this vulture is patiently waiting, again, just like that photographer, watching this girl very closely, waiting her to collapse, waiting her to die, so that she can have a nice please. Maybe the vulture itself was quite hungry, starving. So this person captured those photographs, and this very high resolution photographs with a lot of emotions evident on the face of this girl was published in all leading magazines, Time magazines, leading magazines, Newsweek magazines, leading magazines all over the world. And people got to know, although everyone has heard about third world countries and how people, there is a lot of poverty, but first time, you know, this was very evident that person dying of starvation, of malnutrition, so emaciated. So it was a nice article which he showcased what is actually happening in Sudan. And this person because of this article, this story that was published by this person, because of this article, this story, what he published, he got an international award. I forget the name of the award, very internationally acclaimed award. He got it for the pictures he contributed for the story and the entire article. And it said that few days later, this photographer, although he got many accolades, many appreciation for the article, for the hard work he has done, so it has put in few days later, this photographer committed suicide. He could not take what he saw there, so much of poverty, so much of starvation, so many people die.

---
#### suffering, choices, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=781
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Imagine a person, very influential, very powerful, bureaucrat, part of a very big committee, formulate the task, formulate the tax laws for the country, he is going through this."
**Summary:** The speaker shares the story of a very influential and powerful bureaucrat who was suffering from acute back pain and wanted to leave his body, highlighting that even rich and influential people go through immense suffering.

So, I was telling you about this photographer who eventually committed suicide because of the pain, the agony what he went through, all what he saw in Sudan. So, we see people suffering very miserably and it's not just the poor people who are suffering. At times, we see very rich influential people also going through a lot of pain. I think I've shared this article earlier about this gentleman who was staying in Delhi. He's part of a very powerful committee which is into formulating tax laws. And this committee is chaired by the finance minister of the country. In the early days of Akshaya Patra used to help us a lot. He would call some very influential businessmen and fix up the situation. So, he was a very influential businessman. He would give us an appointment where we could go and give us a presentation about what we were doing. And he connected us to many many good people. He himself was influential. He could dial a number of Mr. Mukesh Ambani of Birla's and Tata's of the world and he would be able to speak to them. So, one day this person, we lost touch with him. Our vice chairman Chanchalapati was in touch with him. He's to come to the temple. I have also met him. He exchanged greetings. A very influential person. And we lost touch with him eight years past Akshaya Patra. And one day, all of a sudden, Chanchalapati Poov receives a call from this gentleman and says, Prabhuji, how are you? And a very feeble voice. And he says. He's very kind. So he says, yes, I am planning to come to Delhi next week. He says, So Chanchal Pooja said, sure, next week when I am planning for, I will definitely come to your place. And they fixed the time, date and things like that. So he visited his place and as soon as he rang the bell, the servant opened the house and from a distance, this person was coming to receive. And in a very, very lean, thin, barely able to walk, but still he wanted to receive, he comes towards the door and ushers Chanchal Pooja in and takes him to the drawing room and goes and sits next to him. Chanchal Pooja catches hold of his hand and says, he first little bit inquires about Akshaya Paan, and then he holds his hand and says, you are very dear to the Lord. You all are dedicated your lives for serving the Lord. So I am sure you are very, very close to God. Please pray for me. With pain in his eyes, with a lot of agony, please pray for me. I just want to leave this body. So Chanchal Pooja was startled. What is he saying? Pray to God. Pray to God that I just leave this body at the earliest possible. So he inquired. So he said that for the last few years, I am suffering from such an acute backache, gone to the best of hospitals, best of doctors, taken all possible treatments, but in spite of all that, can't sit, can't lie down, can't sleep. Doctors have prescribed a day he has to take eight or ten days of sleep. He has to take ten painkillers. And because of this painkiller, there is all kinds of gas, gastric problems, all kinds of reactions in the body. And he says that my life has become a hell. I just want to leave this body. Swamiji, please pray to God for it. Imagine a person, very influential, very powerful, bureaucrat, part of a very big committee, formulate the task, formulate the tax laws for the country. He is going through this.

#### choices, consequences, free will
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1052
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every action, every choice we make has a consequence."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that every choice and action has consequences, using the example of choosing to attend the class or relax, and shares a personal anecdote about his past experiences to illustrate this point.

So the choices we make, the actions we do because of these choices have a consequence. Every action, every choice we make has a consequence. Even today, we had a choice to attend this class or to just let back and relax, take an afternoon nap. So basically, the choice is, the consequence will be different. One day, when I was, many years back, when I was, when I used to go out to make life members, I would, I remember I used to go quite often to Manipal Hospital. And I used to go to Manipal Hospital. You know, generally, when a person becomes a life member, he gives references of his friends. So I had many, many doctors who had become life members in the Manipal Hospital. So I knew many doctors, about 40, 50 of them, many specialists. And there was a doctor whom I used to meet quite often. He was also interested in Krishna consciousness. He was a radiologist. So invariably, when I would go, there would be a lot of people waiting for their turns to get their MRI and CT scan done. And I was always given a special access. So whenever I would go, I would call him and then, in spite of people waiting, I would have access to his office and I would go and directly meet him. And all along in my mind, there was a feeling that these all are patients. These all are patients. And I am a devotee. So I am not a patient. And many years later, I also had some backache. And I was advised to get the CT scan done, MRI done. And the doctor had by then got transferred. He had moved to some other city. And one day I was sitting in that same chairs waiting for hours to get my MRI done. So every one of us are acting based on the choices we are making in life and that choice will have a consequence, will have a reaction, good or bad in our lives and what we are going through is what has manifested, the reaction which has manifested in our life. We have talked about Falon Mukham. So it's very important that we make the right choices. Many times our choices are based on what we think is right. So today we are going to discuss about how to make the right choices and also when we end up for whatever reason making wrong choices, what to do.

---
#### mistakes, mind control, forgiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1230
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Mind can be the best of friends or the worst enemy. Mind, if it is controlled, is our best friend. The same mind, if it is uncontrolled, is our worst enemy."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the plan to discuss consequences of choices, and what to do when we make wrong choices, emphasizing the importance of mind control and acknowledging that mistakes are inevitable.

So first we will talk about consequences of choices. We covered briefly. Now we are going to talk about the second aspect is what we do when we make wrong choices. We all know that we have to eat healthy food, a lot of salads, but at times we make a wrong choice of eating some foods which we know for sure is not good for our health. We know that certain principles I should follow, but in spite of that I end up making a wrong choice. I know that I have set up an alarm. I know that I should get up in the morning when the alarm rings, but when the alarm rings, I at times make a wrong choice, press the snooze button and again go back to sleep. Every choice we make has a consequence. Now what to do when we end up making a wrong choice? Krishna in the Gita says mind can be the best of friends or the worst enemy. Mind, if it is controlled, is our best friend. The same mind, if it is uncontrolled, is our worst enemy. And it takes time to tame the mind, to discipline the mind, and till such time we will invariably make some wrong choices. We will make some mistakes, some errors.

#### mistakes, devotion, acceptance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1344
**Verse References:** BG 9.30
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even if a person who is on this path, trying his best to sincerely follow the principles, even if he makes some mistakes, he is considered saintly."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes the Bhagavad Gita, emphasizing that even if a person makes mistakes while sincerely trying to follow the path of devotion, they are still considered saintly, comparing it to a child learning to walk.

Knowing this, even in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says api chet sudhuracharo bhajate mam ananyabha, sadhureva samantavyah, sadhureva samantavyah, even if a person who is on this path, trying his best to sincerely follow the principles, even if he makes some mistakes, he is considered saintly, just like a child is trying to walk. He is barely able to walk. He is just learning how to walk, he will fall down. You cannot expect a child to simply just as he grows up, he simply starts running or walking. So we also while we are treading this path, we will make wrong choices, we will make mistakes, we will make some blunders. Now we have to in a very, very Krishna conscious way, godly way, deal with such blunders, such mistakes. I will just read out few quotes which are very important for us to keep in mind when we make some blunders in life. Never judge a person for his errors. Everyone makes errors. The power is human. So how do we judge a person? Not by the errors he makes, but how he fixes them, how he deals with that errors, what he does after he has made that error. That's more important than the error what a person has made.

#### mistakes, learning, misery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1450
**Verse References:** BG 7.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Every mistake, every error is an opportunity for us to learn and grow."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of learning from mistakes, viewing them as opportunities for growth, and explains that the world is designed in such a way that we will act sinfully and learn from the consequences.

It is okay to make mistakes. Now this may look contradictory. It is okay to make mistakes as long as we learn from them. Every mistake, every error is an opportunity for us to learn and grow. In fact, the whole world has been designed in such a way that we will act in sinful ways, we will break some principles, and nature will retaliate and make us learn the hard way. And Krishna says bahunam janmanam ante, after many many lives of doing wrong things and getting beaten, gyanman, a person who is sufficiently experienced, who has become sufficiently knowledgeable, mam prapadyante, will come to me, vasudevam sarvam iti, sa mahatma durlabha, vasudevam sarvam iti, he is everything and I should surrender to him. I learn from my mistakes. This is another quote. It is very painful way to learn, but without pain, the old saying goes, no pain, no gain. I learn from the mistakes. It's a painful way to learn. Even the miseries which a person encounters is actually a painful way to learn some important aspects of our existence, but without pain, the old saying goes, there is no gain. The greatest mistake a man can make is to be afraid of making one. So when we tread this path, we will make mistakes, we will make wrong choices.

#### mistakes, guilt, comfort zone
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1570
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how to deal with mistakes, emphasizing that what we do after making a blunder is crucial, and warns against rehashing mistakes and making false resolutions, stating that we cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it.

And what we do after having made that blunder, made that wrong choice is what is very, very critical and crucial. We can brood over it. We can feel guilty. Yes, guilt is very much part of life. It is good, but within limits. Ships are very safe at the shore. Ships are very safe at the shore. So we can also leave, live a life in our comfort zone and make no mistakes. We do not set an alarm. We get up whatever time we feel like getting up. You will not make a blunder there. You will not make a mistake there. But that's not what life is all about. We have to, we have to come out of the comfort zone. Brahma Satyabhayam Shudyam. We have to perform tapasya. We have to regulate our senses. We have to regulate our mind. Our senses will sometime overpower us. Our mind will cheat us. We will commit mistakes. Many times when we make mistake, we keep rehashing, why did I do this? Why did next time I will not do this? Why did I do it? Guilty, so miserable, and then we make some false resolution from next time onwards I will not eat this thing, next time onwards I will not do this thing, and we keep thinking about I'll not do it, I'll not do it, I'll not do it. So they're saying that there's a principle which says we cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it. This is applicable to when a man is very much obsessed with thoughts of opposite sex. Here it said that you cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it. Sometimes we make a mistake and then we make even bigger mistake of how we handle that mistake. We cannot stop thinking about something by trying not to think about it. It's a very important principle. It's a very important principle. It's a very important principle.

#### mistakes, sorrow, godly sorrow
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1719
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Godly sorrow is that sorrow which we feel when we feel that by making a wrong choice, we have displeased the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates between worldly sorrow and godly sorrow, explaining that worldly sorrow is concern about one's reputation, while godly sorrow is the sorrow felt when one displeases the Lord.

If we just think a little bit soberly, we'll understand this is a blunder, this is a mistake which most of us make when we commit mistakes. There's a difference between worldly sorrow and a godly sorrow. Worldly sorrow is I make a blunder, I make a mistake, I am more concerned about my name, my fame in the society, what will people think about me, what will the world say about me. It's a worldly sorrow. I'm concerned about me, my ego, my my name, my fame, my eejat. So many times when we make wrong choices or mistakes, we can be overpowered by worldly sorrow. What if I get caught? What if people notice or come to know about my mistake or my wrong choices? That's worldly sorrow. Yes, worldly sorrow can lead to some bit of correction, but in the long run, it is short-lived. It doesn't last for long. The change it brings in us is not long lasting. In contrast, what is what we should strive for is godly sorrow. Godly sorrow is that sorrow which which we feel when we feel that by making a wrong choice, we have displeased ourselves with the wrong choices we have displeased ourselves with the wrong choices we have displeased the Lord. Just like you have kept an alarm that I'll get up in the morning and morning you don't get up, you snooze that alarm. So that sorrow, that pain that I have displeased the Lord, that godly sorrow will purify us rather than worldly sorrow, what will people think about me, what will the people think in this community of God, that God will purify us about my so to protect that my name in that community, that is called the worldly sorrow.

---
#### experience, choices, learning
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1849
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "I am experienced because of making wrong choices, many wrong choices."
**Summary:** The speaker shares an anecdote about a successful billionaire who attributed his success to making the right choices, which were a result of experience gained from making wrong choices.

So there was a successful man, very successful man, and he was a billionaire. So he was asked how did you become so rich, how did you become so successful? So the person replied that it's because of making the right choices in my life. I made many right choices. So the person who was interviewing him asked the second question, what helped you make the right choices? So the person replied experience. And then the interviewer asked the ultimate question, how did you gather experience? How you became so experienced that you made many right choices and because of which today you are successful? He said I am experienced because of making wrong choices, many wrong choices. So yes, life will throw up many choices. When we make choices, we will gather experience and from that experience we will be able to make right choices and that's how we grow in life. So in other words, there's nothing wrong about making mistakes or making errors, but if we keep repeating them and not learning from them, not gathering experience from them, not becoming more mature because of those mistakes and avoid repeating those mistakes, then there's a problem.

---
#### root cause, self-analysis, lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=1956
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ask why five times and if you ask why five times you will get to the root cause."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the "5 Whys" technique to get to the root cause of a problem, and mentions Arjuna's question to Krishna about why we act sinfully, with Krishna answering that lust is the root cause.

So the founder of the Toyota company, he suggested a way to get into the root cause of a problem. He said that if you have to get to the root cause of a problem, let's say you have made a wrong choice and you're making the wrong choice quite often. Every day you sleep and you know you keep an alarm, but morning when you get up, you put the snooze button invariably and you're not able to understand why am I doing this? I want to get up. I make up my mind. I sleep with that resolution that I will get up, but again in the morning why I'm not able to get up. So he suggested that ask why five times and if you ask why five times you will get to the root cause. We'll not get into details. Let's say for example a person wants to get up in the morning and he is not able to get up invariably he pushes us, presses the snooze button. He should ask why am I not able to get up? So the answer would be morning I feel very tired. Why I feel tired? So the answer is I sleep late in the night. Why do I sleep late in the night? The answer is because I have a lot of work. Why I have taken so much work on my head, on my shoulders? If you ask five times these questions why, you will get to know the root cause, the root cause which is leading to make the wrong choices and at times we are not able to understand why. So we can even try this to understand why we end up making wrong choices in our lives. In fact, Arjuna asked this question to Krishna, Krishna, why is it that many times we end up acting in sinful ways as if there is some kind of force much against our wish? We know that it is wrong. We know that we should not be doing it, but why do we do it in spite of knowing that it is wrong as if there is some kind of force pushing us to do it. So then Krishna answers in the Gita kama aesha krodha aesha rajoguna samudbhava. This kama, this lusty desires is a root cause. It's the greatest enemy of the living entity because of which, because of this kama, because of this lust, we end up in sinful ways. We end up making wrong choices, acting in wrong ways because of which we suffer the consequences.

---
#### mistakes, forgiveness, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=2141
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ask for forgiveness, forgiveness includes acceptance."
**Summary:** The speaker suggests a practical approach called FPL (Forgiveness, Prayer, Learn) to deal with wrong choices, emphasizing the importance of begging forgiveness, praying for strength and knowledge, and learning from mistakes.

So when we make the wrong choices, I would suggest another small simple practical thing which we can do rather than beating ourselves up and not learning anything from the mistake. One thing I shared with you, we can always ask five why's, why, so you will get to the root cause and at times you can, if you unless the root is addressed, that's why in medical science also many times, you know, the diseases are treated symptomatically. A person has acne or marks on his face and has some fever, so give a paracetamol, but then the root cause is not addressed and the disease again surfaces up in some other way. So if we are making wrong choices again and again, we have to do some root cause analysis, why? So that's one thing which we all can practically try. And second thing is if we make wrong choices rather than beating ourselves morally, we can, we all have, you know, IPL has become very popular, so I'll give you an acronym that is FPL, we can do FPL. F stands for yes, mistake has done, we can beg forgiveness from the Lord, beg forgiveness from Prabhupada. It's my mistake, accept that mistake. Although the Lord knows that mistake has happened, but acceptance that yes, I have done a mistake, it's my mistake is step one, F, ask for forgiveness. Forgiveness includes acceptance. Many times we are not ready to accept our mistake, forgiveness, F, and then we have P.

---
#### prayer, strength, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/txeoyamWwKs?t=2273
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We can pray, we can beg the Lord, we are we are very small."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining the FPL acronym, focusing on 'P' for prayer, emphasizing the need to beg the Lord for strength, knowledge, and skills, and acknowledging our insignificance compared to the Lord's unlimited power.

P is to pray, to beg the Lord for strength, for jnana, bala and kriya. Jnana means the right knowledge, bala means to strength and kriya means skills. We all are small, insignificant. We have limited knowledge. We have limited strength. We have limited intelligence, but we are I'm sure of the Lord who has unlimited strength, unlimited knowledge, unlimited intelligence. We can pray, we can beg the Lord. We are we are very small. The example is given that when a person a ship has capsized a person is an Atlantic ocean middle of an ocean, he may be an expert swimmer, but he cannot cross the ocean just because he knows how to swim. So we may have some skills, we may have some strength, we may have intelligence, but life will throw up challenges that will not be enough. We may have the knowledge. We may know that it is not to be done, but in spite of that the allurement, the temptation will be so great that we will falter.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### MATERIAL CAREER WITH SPIRITUAL MINDSET | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, duty, spiritual mindset
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** introduction
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The whole Bhagavad Gita was spoken to inspire Arjuna that he should do his duty, he should execute his responsibilities but while doing his duties he should be conscious of the Lord."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of balancing material career with a spiritual mindset, referencing Arjuna's dilemma in the Bhagavad Gita and setting the stage for a discussion on practical application.

A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is material career with spiritual mindset. When we start practicing the principles of Bhagavad Gita, the principles of Krishna consciousness, and also we have material duties to take care of, often we encounter a dilemma: how to balance both. In fact, Arjuna also in the Bhagavad Gita, first chapter, he was reluctant to do his duty as a Kshatriya, which was to fight, and the whole Bhagavad Gita was spoken to inspire Arjuna that he should do his duty, he should execute his responsibilities, but while doing his duties, he should be conscious of the Lord. So how to practically apply that, what is a practical application of this instruction, how to think of the Lord and be conscious of the Lord and execute our worldly duties is what we are going to talk about today in the session. So let's start the session with prayers, let's beg the mercy of Prabhupada, Lord Chaitanya, and Lord Krishna.

---
#### balance, material aspirations, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=264
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "How do we balance? How do we strike a healthy balance? We are trying, but what is an ideal balance which Krishna recommends in the Bhagavad Gita, which Acharyas have recommended?"
**Summary:** The speaker poses the central question of how to balance material aspirations with spiritual progress in Krishna consciousness, seeking guidance from the Bhagavad Gita and the Acharyas.

So how do we balance our aspiration to grow materially? We all have some desires, some aspirations, some goals. It could be material goals, it could be growth in my career, promotions, increments, growth in my business, and simultaneously we want to also progress in Krishna consciousness. How do we balance? How do we strike a healthy balance? We are trying, but what is an ideal balance which Krishna recommends in the Bhagavad Gita, which Acharyas have recommended? So let's start with the first point, again we will be covering only three aspects today.

The first aspect is means and the end. I repeat, the means and the end. We all have some needs. Maslow's law of human needs. We all have heard about Roti, Kapda and Makaan. These are all basic fundamental needs of everyone: food to eat, clothes to wear, and some place where we can stay. Roof on top of our head is basic means and the end.

#### Vedic society, importance of Brahmanas, guidance

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of learned Brahmanas in guiding society towards the purpose of life.

You are talking about this in front of me just like, that is to say, just like if the Brahmanas are compared to the head, the Vaishyas are compared to the belly, the Kshatriyas the arms, and Shudras the leg, although we are not in one sense speaking lowly of any particular class, naturally in a society there are divisions like that, even though Vedic times such divisions were there but it was based on guna and karma according to the activities a person performs and his gunas, not by birth. So now if a person, let's say for some reason, met with an accident and his limbs get amputated, still he can do meaningful work. Let's say a person was amputated, legs gets amputated, he is not able to walk, still the person on a wheelchair can do meaningful work, can do something very meaningful, contribute to the society, contribute to his family, whereas if a person is not together here, mentally off, his mind is not together, then even though he may have a strong and stout body, still he will only create nuisance in the society. So therefore it was very important that there is a section of people in the society who are very learned, educated, knowledgeable, well versed with Vedic literatures, who knew the purpose of life and who would guide the entire section of the society.

#### spiritual perspective, value of actions, spiritual mindset
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=962
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "From spiritual perspective, from the perspective of Atma, if we don't have one in front of those zeros, all the zeros, the value is zero itself."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of zeros to illustrate that material accomplishments without a spiritual foundation are meaningless.

If you remember, we had talked about zeros. String of zeros. If you have two zeros, three zeros, four zeros, string of zeros, the value is zero. No matter what we accomplish materially from spiritual perspective, from the perspective of Atma, if we don't have one in front of those zeros, all the zeros, the value is zero itself. So working with spiritual mindset is ensuring that we have one in front of those zeros. And even though we go about achieving our material goals, fulfilling some material desires, doing our worldly duties, if that one is in place, then we don't have to worry because each of those zeros will also add value. Two zeros becomes hundred. Three zero becomes thousand. So the question then should be how to ensure that we have one very solidly in place in front of those zeros.

#### internal struggle, spiritual mindset, right end
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=1083
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The spiritual mindset is very important for us to be conscious of the right end. Otherwise, the means will become an end."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story of a lady in the mountains who is busy training her senses and ego, emphasizing the importance of a spiritual mindset to stay focused on the right end.

There was once a lady who was staying up in the mountains all by herself, all alone, and she was spending lot of time in meditation, so a person who was mountaineering, who was, you know, happened to meet this lady who was in meditation and she was dancing. The person saw that everywhere, all around, complete silence, no one to be seen, complete solitude. So this person went to this lady, introduced briefly himself, and then said, What are you doing here, all alone? What work are you engaged in? Probably because he did not have any spiritual mindset. He says, what are you doing? Lot of people at times wonder, what do you do Swamiji, whole day? For people, it's understandable, somebody is going, doing business, doing a job. What do you do the whole day? You don't do job, you don't earn money. So whole day, how does it work? How does it go? Lot of people wonder. Okay, prayers, understandable. Okay, doing aarti, understandable. Hearing Bhagavatam class, understandable. But whole day, 24 hours? Lot of people actually ask this question, what do you do the whole day? The fact is that, we all are more busy than most of the people in the world out there. For most of us, our day starts as early as 3 a.m. or 3.30 a.m. and ends by 9.30, 10 p.m. So whole day, we are engaged in activities to take forward this mission, to serve this mission. Janma Sarthaka Kari Karopaka, to purify our life, engage in activities which will purify our lives, solidify that one. And for the service of the humanity, engage in various activities to take forward this message to more and more people. This message is very important. The spiritual mindset is very important for us to be conscious of the right end.

#### self-control, senses, ego
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=1328
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The big ego which thinks that I am great. I am the lord of all I survey. The big ego which makes me think that I am in control of things. I have to purify this ego. I have to tame this lion."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the lady's explanation of training her senses (eyes, hands, legs, tongue) and taming her ego, highlighting the internal struggle for self-control.

The hawks stare at everything that is presented to me, good or bad. I have to work on them, I have to tame them, I have to take charge of them, I have to control them, so that they always look for the good. And here the hawk is compared to our two eyes. Then the lady said, I have two eagles with their claws. Eagles have claws and then they swoop on some target, catch hold of their prey and then eat it. So I have the eagles with sharp claws which can hurt and destroy. I am sure most of you would have guessed by now what she is referring to, which can catch hold of something, hurt someone and destroy. I have to train them not to hurt, to do good. And they are my hands. So we have ten working senses and we have gyanendriyas and karmendriyas. Five knowledge acquiring senses and five working senses. Hands, legs, speech. All these are part of working senses. And then we have eyes, ears, nose, tongue and touch as our gyanendriyas or knowledge acquiring senses. Then the lady said, I have two rabbits who go wherever they want to. They are very very stubborn, at times uncontrollable, and at times it leads me to difficult situations and places. I have to take charge of these rabbits and ensure that they go to the right places and they do not stumble and make me suffer. And they are my legs. We have to control our legs. They are the working senses which can take us to wrong places. They can stumble. And then the donkey is tired, stubborn and does not want to carry the load each time I ask it to carry. It is stubborn. So this she is referring to her body. Stubborn, does not want to carry the load. This lethargy, this laziness. We want to get up in the morning but body won't allow. The mind won't allow. So I have to take care and tame this donkey. And the most difficult thing is the snake. The lady replied, the most difficult thing is the snake. What is this snake she is referring to? Although this snake is locked in a cage of 32 bars. 32 bars refers here to 32 toes. The snake here is referred to the tongue. It is always ready to sting, to bite and to poison anyone nearby. See many times we speak words which can hurt someone. We can speak very negatively. We can really hurt someone very badly if we don't have control over our tongue and the words we speak. I have to discipline it, the lady said. And last but not the least, she said, I have a lion. The big ego which thinks that I am great. I am the lord of all I survey. The big ego which makes me think that I am in control of things. I have to purify this ego. I have to tame this lion. So in that sense,

#### self-realization, mind and senses, happiness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=1577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What profit the man if he wins the whole world but loses his own soul?"
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Jesus Christ and emphasizes the importance of self-control and purifying the mind and senses for true happiness, contrasting it with material success without inner control.

All of us have a big, big, big project at hand. Jesus Christ also made the statement, What profit the man if he wins the whole world but loses his own soul? We can accomplish big, big things. We can become CEO of a big multinational company. But what if we are not in control over our own self, own mind, own senses, own ego? There are a lot of people who have earned a lot of money, materially speaking, become very famous, very rich, but were very egoistic, were very uncontrolled as far as their senses are concerned, embroiled in some scandals. So Jesus said, What profit the man if he wins the whole world? Materially speaking, you grow very, very big, go up, hide the ladder, but loses the soul. So we all have a very, very meaningful end to achieve in human form of life. And that is, Human life is meant for divine tapasya, control the mind, control the senses. Bhagavatam says, If one thinks that he has many enemies, he is mistaken. The real enemy is his uncontrolled mind and uncontrolled senses. If a mind and senses are controlled, we can have practically very little and still be happy and satisfied. And if a mind and senses are not controlled, we can have everything in the world still be dissatisfied and unhappy.

The second point which I would like to explain in this context is the five factors of action. O mighty armed Arjuna, learn from me the five factors which bring about the accomplishment of all actions.

#### five factors, endeavor, Sankhya philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=1975
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Many of us think that endeavor is the only factor. Hard work is the only factor. Our efforts are the only factors. If we put more efforts, sincere efforts, hard work, we will be successful."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Krishna reveals the five factors necessary for success, challenging the common belief that hard work alone is sufficient.

So Krishna here in this verse is saying the five factors which will lead to an accomplishment of an action or success in a particular endeavor. And Krishna is saying, know from me. These are declared as, Bhagavad Gita is so wonderful. It is wonderfully dissecting us and giving us, if we have to be successful, these are the five factors. Many of us think that endeavor is the only factor. Hard work is the only factor. Our efforts are the only factors. If we put more efforts, sincere efforts, hard work, we will be successful. These are declared in Sankhya philosophy to be place of action, the performer, the senses, the endeavor, and ultimately, the super soul.

#### Karanam, instrumental cause, teacher
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=2091
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The teacher was instrumental in me succeeding. Because I had right teacher who inspired me, motivated me, I became successful."
**Summary:** The speaker begins explaining the five factors, starting with Karanam (instrumental cause), using the example of a teacher's influence on a student's success.

So what are these five factors? We will go one by one. The first one is Karanam. Karanam means instrumental cause. For example, if a person becomes successful, let's say PV Sindhu won the world badminton championship or for that matter any sportsman when they won, they win a particular medal. So invariably there is some interview and they asked, how did you do it? What brought about this accomplishment? There are interviews like that. After every match, post-match, there is an interview. So you might find answers like, my teacher was very helpful and very inspiring. He guided me, trained me, he was with me in all the practice sessions and that contributed to my success. The teacher was very, very inspiring, very, very motivating. It's an instrumental cause. The teacher was instrumental in me succeeding. Because I had right teacher who inspired me, motivated me, I became successful. That's called Karanam.

---
#### Adhishthanam, place of action, supportive environment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=2168
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In my home, everyone was encouraging me. The whole environment was very supportive. Everyone allowed me to do my thing the my way. They trusted me."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Adhishthanam (place of action), using the example of a supportive family environment contributing to a student's success in IIT JEE.

The second factor is Adhishthanam. Adhishthanam means the place of action. The place of action. So here, for example, somebody may say that how did you crack ITJE? How did you get first rank? He says, my family was very supportive. In my home, everyone was encouraging me. The whole environment was very supportive. Everyone allowed me to do my thing the my way. They trusted me. And I am very grateful to my family members, to my parents, to my mother, to my father for what I have achieved. So you might get answers like this. So here they are referring to the place of action, my home, my family. That's called Adhishthanam.

#### Karta, performer, determination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=2252
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I saw my brother cracking IIT JEE. From that day, I took a vow. I made a resolution. I took a determination. I was determined that I will also crack it."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Karta (the performer), using the example of a person's determination and resolution to succeed after seeing their brother's success.

So third factor is the Karta, the performer. You might hear a person saying that I saw my brother cracking IIT JEE. From that day, I took a vow. I made a resolution. I took a determination. I was determined that I will also crack it. And I worked very sincerely. I worked very hard. I made a resolute determination. I will also crack the IIT JEE. So that's called the Karta, the performer, the resolution, the determination.

In Soulful Japa talks, Madhupant Puri also is saying about Cheshta, knowing attention and doing attention. We have to give that Cheshta if we have to chant attentively. I'm sure many of you would have heard about Natarajan. He played the IPL this time and then he was spotted and he joined the Indian team. #### Daivam, mercy of the Lord, ultimate cause

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers

His name was included and he was not supposed to play. He was supposed to be on the bench. On the day before the match, one of the fastballers again met with some injury and he was included in the team. He performed quite well. And then, again, he became part of the Indian team. So, you may have lot of people who have worked sincerely, lot of people who have worked very hard, but you have to get the right break. And that is not something which you can logically calculate. It is just, it happens. The mercy of the Lord, the blessings of the Lord is the Daivam, the ultimate cause.

#### Srila Prabhupada, Daivam, mercy of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=2495
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Please make me dance the way you want me to dance. Make me a puppet in your hands. Make me speak in a way whereby they can understand your message. I have no qualification."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Srila Prabhupada's journey to America with limited resources but with complete dependence on the Lord to illustrate the power of Daivam.

I will give another example from the life of Shila Prabhupada. Prabhupada, when he went to America, he went with 40 rupees in his pocket in a cargo ship. From material perspective, a person going to America at the age of 70 with 40 rupees, with all the other factors, Chesta, hard work, this is a limitation what an old man can do. How many contacts he has, how much he is networked, this is all very important for material accomplishment. Lot of business people, they have highly networked and these days they are social media tools to network. Even before you reach the place, you have some contacts in place. Prabhupada went with no idea where he is going to stay. But he had Daivam with him. En route to Boston, in the cargo ship, in Jaladuta, he writes in his diary, Oh Lord! Why are you taking me to a miserable place? I have no idea how I am going to speak to them. Please make me dance the way you want me to dance. Make me a puppet in your hands. Make me speak in a way whereby they can understand your message. I have no qualification. And then he signs the most insignificant beggar and writes his name.

#### karma bank account, Lord's sanction, devotee's advantage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/E-nMXXw1BF8?t=2658
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is not only a witnesser, not only a sanctioner, but he is a director."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concept of a karma bank account and how the Lord sanctions desires based on it, but for devotees, the Lord can override karma.

A living entity can desire. We all can desire. I want to be this. I want to achieve this. But the Lord who is there as Paramatma in our heart, the super soul, He senses our desires. He smells our desires and sanctions after having a look at our karma bank account. Everyone who is playing cricket can desire that I want to play in the Indian cricket team. But there is a karma bank account which the Lord checks. And if the karma is not there, you may have put a lot of hard work, person will not get. So whereas in case of a devotee, if we have Daivam with us, if we have the Lord with us, even karma is not a disqualification. Krishna is not only a witnesser, not only a sanctioner, but he is a director.

---
#### Daivam, chanting, accomplishment of action

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that even for material aspirations, one should not neglect spiritual practices like chanting and reading scriptures, as Daivam is crucial for success.

So therefore, knowing that Daivam is very important, even if we want some material accomplishment, we should not think that we are wasting our time chanting and reading Bhagavatam, hearing Bhagavad Gita. We should understand if we have Daivam with us, even though our karma is not good, that will be taken care of if we have Lord with us. So with this understanding, we can go about leading our lives and even if we have some material desires, some aspirations, some goals, we can go about pursuing them, understanding that ultimately for accomplishment of action, the important cause is Daivam, the Lord.

#### recap, means and end, five factors of action

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

So, a quick recap of what we have covered so far. The first point we covered was the difference between means and an end. Earning money, career growth, engaging in some business activities for making some profit, it's a means to an end. And we should be very clear as to what's the end. The end should always be very clear and prominent in our sight. We should not get lost and think the means as an end. Then we talked about five factors of action. The five factors of action was Karanam, Karta, Chesta, Adhishtanam and finally Daivam. And we talked about how Daivam is very very important.

A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is 'My Previous Life.' Today we are going to talk about the concept of soul accepting many many bodies, Punarapi Janma Punarapi Marana. Krishna extensively in the Bhagavad Gita talks about this process of birth and death, how the soul moves from one body to another, and he also tells Arjuna that both you and me have taken many many births. We are going to talk about this in detail in today's session, so let us start this discussion with prayers. Please join me in offering the prayers. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktiveda Anta Swami Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswati Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Samyavadi Pasyatya Deshatarine. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gatara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrindha. Namo Mahavadanyaya Krishna Prema Pradayate Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gauratushe Namaha. Namo Brahmane Devaya Go Brahmane Hittaya Cha Jagad Hittaya Krishnaya Go Vindaya Namo Namaha. He Krishna Karuna Sindho Dina Bandho Jagat Pate Gopesha Gopika Kanta Radha Kanta Namostute. Tapta Kanta Namaste Anchan Gaurangi Radhe Vrindha Vaneshwari Vrishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Hari Priye. Vansha Kalpatarubhyascha Kripa Sindhubhya Evacha Patitanam Pavnebhyo Vaishnavebhyo Namo Namaha. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

#### reincarnation, Bhagavad Gita, knowledge transmission
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/a1cWlX8SDPI?t=315

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita tells Arjuna that I spoke this knowledge to Sun God Vivaswan."
**Summary:** The speaker begins discussing the first point: our many lives. He references Krishna's statement in the Bhagavad Gita about imparting knowledge to Vivaswan, Manu, and Ikshvaku, highlighting the ancient lineage of this wisdom.

The first point, the first aspect is our many lives. I repeat, our many lives. Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita tells Arjuna that I spoke this knowledge to Sun God Vivaswan. Vivaswan spoke to Manu, Manu spoke to Ikshwaku, and then Krishna says, evam parampara praptam, this knowledge was coming down in time, and in due course of time this knowledge got lost. The original intent, the original purpose for which this knowledge was spoken seemed to have got lost, and the same knowledge Krishna spoke to Arjuna 5000 years ago.
