#### Arjuna's doubt, contemporary, understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/a1cWlX8SDPI?t=379
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It's like someone comes and meets us or meets you and says that yesterday I had gone to Delhi and yesterday I had an opportunity to have a wonderful interaction with Mahatma Gandhi."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Arjuna's doubt about Krishna's claim of teaching Vivaswan millions of years ago, using an analogy of someone claiming to have recently met Mahatma Gandhi.

So in this sense Bhagavad Gita is not some knowledge which is 5000 years old. So when Arjuna heard from Krishna that he spoke to Sun God Vivaswan and Vivaswan spoke to Manu many many millions of years earlier, this Arjuna got a doubt in his mind and he asked this clarification from Krishna, 'O Krishna, you are saying that you spoke to Sun God Vivaswan many millions of years ago, but you are my contemporary, how am I to understand that you spoke to Sun God?' It's like let me give you a crude example. It's like someone comes and meets us or meets you and says that yesterday I had gone to Delhi, I am coming from Delhi, and yesterday I had an opportunity to have a wonderful interaction with Mahatma Gandhi. I spoke to him, in fact he invited me for a dinner, he asked me if I can come down to his place. I was so blessed to interact with Mahatma Gandhi. If a person comes and tells you this, immediately this question will come, 'How are you saying that you met Mahatma Gandhi? We know Mahatma Gandhi is a reality, he was a person but he died long long ago.' So this question is but natural.

---
#### reincarnation, Krishna's response, memory
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/a1cWlX8SDPI?t=487
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Many many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them but you cannot."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Arjuna's doubt and Krishna's response, where Krishna states that both he and Arjuna have had many births, but Krishna remembers them all while Arjuna does not.

So similar question came in the mind of Arjuna that you have spoken this knowledge to Sun God Vivaswan. You are saying that I am seeing that you are my contemporary, we are in this battle field standing next to each other, how am I to understand? So in reply to this question Krishna says, 'Many many births both you and I have passed. I can remember all of them but you cannot, O subduer of enemy.' It's a fact that all of us have taken many many births. Just because we do not remember our previous life does not mean that we have not taken many many births.

#### forgetting past lives, Lord's arrangement, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/a1cWlX8SDPI?t=543
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It's a wonderful arrangement by the lord that we forget our previous life details."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the common question of why we don't remember our previous lives, explaining that it is a merciful arrangement by the Lord to prevent confusion and allow us to focus on the present.

Just like I don't remember what I ate the previous week, I don't remember what I ate the previous year, but that does not mean that since I don't remember I have not eaten. It is lot of people ask me this question, I have encountered this question many many times, why don't we remember our previous births? If we could remember that we did such and such thing or we did these karmas, good karmas and bad karmas and because of which we got this particular birth and because of which we are enjoying or suffering then it's easy for us to reconcile okay, it's my doing. We see good things happening to bad people and bad things happening to good people, but sometimes we are not able to see the correlation. It looks a contradiction, it's very bewildering. But if somehow an arrangement is there good things are happening to bad people we can see that yes in this life, in the past or in the previous lives they have done some good things because of which today apparently they are in a comfortable situation although they are doing bad things now, we won't be bewildered. It's a wonderful arrangement by the lord that we forget our previous life details.

Lord is the lord, he is omniscient he is all knowing he knows the past he knows the present he knows the future he remembers all the births not only his births in fact the births of every one of us. **Summary:** The speaker narrates the incident where Bharat Maharaj witnesses a pregnant deer, startled by a lion's roar, jumping across a stream and dying in childbirth.

So Bharat Maharaj went to the forest with very very wonderful intention to meditate on the lord wholeheartedly without any distraction and he chose a wonderful place a small hermitage he started meditating and one day while he was meditating he saw a mother deer, mother deer who was pregnant was carrying was about to deliver a baby. She was grazing on the bank of a river a stream. The stream had some currents the water was flowing and suddenly this mother deer heard the roar of a lion. It was a forest and she heard the roar of a lion and out of fear she jumped to save her life she jumped across the stream with the intention that she will jump across the stream and she would because maybe the lion was on this side of the stream it will be difficult for the lion to cross that stream. So she jumped across the stream and wanted to save her life and the life of her child baby deer who was about to be born but because of that fear and because of that big leap and because of that thud she collapsed and immediately died on the spot. But before she could land with the thud the baby deer came out and fell in the stream.

And this was the time when the sun had set it was getting darker and Bharat Maharaj opened the eyes just to see where the deer is and he found the deer had not returned. So he became little anxious he tried meditating he tried continuing his meditation but he could not meditate in his mind he was full of anxiety thinking of is the deer safe.

In fact he became even more anxious and started worrying he started all kinds of thoughts like is the deer safe maybe who has lost the way in the forest maybe in the forest there are all kinds of wild animals what would have happened to the deer. So he started in search of the baby deer started going into the forest and the search became very fast by that time the sun had set it had become pitch dark it's all dense forest there is no clear path to tread and he was very very attached to the deer because of that intense attachment in spite of being so dark he kept moving in that forest and started calling for the deer shouting screaming just to maybe the deer can hear him out and respond but nothing was happening there was no reciprocation he kept moving and started screaming started calling out the deer and all the more he became more and more anxious more and more worried and because it was pitch dark dense forest he in search of the deer unfortunately hit a rock could not balance himself fell and his head stuck directly on a rock big piece of rock and because he hit the rock the head hit the rock very very hard impact strong impact the head in the rock he collapsed and spot he died.

Bharat Maharaj died in the Bhagavad Gita Anta Narayana Smriti Krishna talks about this Anta Narayana Smriti. Anta means at the end Yamyam Vapi Smaran Bhavam Tyajati Ante Kalevaram whatever our state of mind our consciousness is our thoughts are at the point of death Yamyam Vapi Smaran Bhavam Smaran means thinking what we think of Tyajati Ante Kalevaram when we give up this body Kalevaram means this body according to our thoughts whatever our consciousness is whatever our thoughts are at the point of death that will determine what body the soul will get in the next life.

Or the alternative could be what if a person is chanting throughout his life getting up in the morning doing Mangal Aarti chanting the names of the Lord following all the principles following Ekadashi Vrata but somehow at the point of death he forgets to remember the Lord what happens to him?

**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

I have heard this many many times in fact devotees can also die in accidents. So what happens? So Anta Narayana Spirit that's the law that determines our next life just like when we join a college finally we are judged by our exams scores the final exams scores. Even when companies come for placement they set a benchmark that students who are above 75% can come and face the interview. That 75% is basically what we have done our final exams. Even in matric in our 10th and 12th we are judged by the exam results and the exams are only for 3 hours into 10 subjects or 5 subjects whatever it may be those 15 hours what we do in those 15 hours actually gives a clear understanding of how well we have studied and done in our academics throughout the year throughout our degree. So likewise what is our consciousness what is our thinking what is what is preoccupying our mind at the point of death is actually a sum total of cumulative effect of what we have thought throughout our life.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Maintaining The Body | HG Amitasana Prabhu
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### detachment, body as a tool, proper maintenance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sCvPoszDro0?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To identify with the car is wrong, but the car can be used very nicely."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that understanding 'I am not the body' should not lead to neglecting the body, using the analogy of a car that needs maintenance even when used for Lord's service.

So how not to get fooled by Maya is what we're talking about. This is no way to say that I am not the body and hence I completely do not take care of the body. Sometimes people go to that extreme. After they come to this knowledge, they stop exercising, they stop going for a walk, stop taking care of the body because I am not the body, which is a wrong understanding. So in the Gita, Krishna talks about Kshetra and Kshetragna. Kshetra means a body, Yantra, and Kshetragna means the real occupier of the body. Kshetra is very important. Just like I gave the example of a car. To identify with the car is wrong, but the car can be used very nicely. And the car being a Yantra follows certain laws. Like for example, even if you are using the car for Lord Seva, from time to time, you have to check the tire pressures, air pressure, you have to send it to the service station. One day you come for a program, Abhishek is going on, you collect your Namrit and you put in that petrol tank and say, 'Charnaamit, you also get a little spiritual.' Will it work? It follows certain laws. Now, if you don't take care of your car, even if you want to use it for Lord service, a time will come if you're not servicing it, if you're not checking it properly, this thing, it will stop functioning properly.

---
#### balance, detachment, body as a tool
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sCvPoszDro0?t=100
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Body is a yantra which has been given to us. I am residing in that yantra. I am not that yantra. I have to maintain that yantra in good shape for me to do the real business."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes avoiding two extremes: neglecting the body and becoming overly attached to it, advocating for moderate exercise and care to maintain the body as a tool for spiritual progress.

So two extremes we have to avoid when you say that I am not the body. One extreme is, I am not the body, so I am reckless about, I want to treat today as anybody. You have to quiet down your Kiaeka with the same kind of belief, that youresser thinks I am a lucky one. So, you have to calm down. Seven steps to take more dynamically, you must understand that the physical touch. Bit of additional side effect on the body, it is the Ehre, all the kinds of things inside, the collection. There are thousands of reasons to use the head, not the body, so I don't care of the body. I'm indifferent to the body. I neglect the body. I ignore the bodily needs. That is wrong. The other extreme is I am the body and then I enroll for a gym and every day two hours, three hours, six pack, that's my goal. That's also wrong. Body is a yantra which has been given to us. I am residing in that yantra. I am not that yantra. I have to maintain that yantra in good shape for me to do the real business. For which, if 15, 20 minutes, 30 minutes of exercise, stretching, Surya Namaskar, all that is required, I do it.

---
#### detachment, sense control, balance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sCvPoszDro0?t=178
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The goal should not become that, my goal is to seek pleasure through my tongue."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies that 'I am not the body' does not mean abstaining from all pleasurable experiences, but avoiding the extremes of uncontrolled indulgence and unnecessary renunciation, particularly in relation to eating.

Yes. So is that clear, first thing? So I am not the body does not mean that, now I am not the body does not mean that Krishna is recommending that we stop eating, we stop seeing. Anything I see, anything I eat which is pleasurable to taste, that is Maya. No. Again, let me avoid two extremes. The goal should not become that, my goal is to seek pleasure through my tongue and I eat. I always seek for all pleasurable things, no matter from which source they come, whether it's veg, non-veg, so long as it is tasting for my taste buds, I take it. That's another extreme. The other extreme is, no, no, I am not the body. Anything which is tasty for my tongue, it has to be shunned. It has to be renounced. That's other extreme.

---

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Meaning of OM TAT SAT | Bhagavad Gita 17.24 - 17.28 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### peace, duty, detachment, well-wishing
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nIJj9zl5HIE?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We should do it with the understanding that I am doing as an instrument in the hands of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines three principles for achieving peace: recognizing Krishna as the enjoyer, proprietor, and well-wisher, emphasizing selfless action as an instrument of the Lord.

If we follow these principles, we will achieve peace. What are the three principles which we have to remember, which we have to follow? First, bhoktaram yagya tapasam: He is the enjoyer of all sacrifices and austerities. He is the bhokta. When we try to be the bhokta, that's why in many places Krishna says you have the right to do your duty, do not be attached to the results. When we become bhokta of the results, forgetting the Lord, peace goes away from our life. So Krishna says bhoktaram yagya tapasam. Then second, Sarvaloka Maheshwaram: He is the true proprietor of everything. Sarvaloka Maheshwaram. When we think that I am the proprietor, I am the enjoyer, then again peace eludes us. And the third formula, bhoktaram yagya tapasam sarva-loka maheshwaram. Third one, suhridam sarva-bhutanam. Suhrid means well-wisher. He is the well-wisher. In fact, we see so many people suffering. At times with good intentions, we try to be the well-wisher. Actually, the Lord is the well-wisher. Even for a person who is going through some misery, the Lord is taking him through this misery because of some correction which he intends to bring about in that person. Just like sometimes a father may even slap a child, may punish a child, or for that matter a government may imprison someone. We have disobeyed the Lord and hence for our wrong actions we are being punished. Even in that punishment, there is an element of intention of reforming that person and also so that that person does not trouble other law-abiding citizens. So isolate him, put him behind bars. So Krishna says one who understands that I am the well-wisher, suhrtumsarva bhuvtana. Now does that mean should we do charitable work, philanthropic work, yes or no? How many of us say we should do? But then we are trying to take the position of Krishna to being suhrtumsarva bhuvtana. Should we do or not do? We should do it with the understanding that I am doing as an instrument in the hands of the Lord, not that I am bhogta, not that I am the karta, not that I am the proprietor, mere kharan woh khana kha rahi hai. I have sponsored three children, unki parvrish mere kharan ho rahi hai. That consciousness is wrong. The Lord has used you as an instrument to provide food to some people. Nimitra matra bhav satsachi, you become an instrument. So that's why your consciousness, it is not the external, it is, what is your bhava, what is your intent, what is your consciousness, what is your thinking behind that act, is that clear? So we'll start the discussion today, we are waiting for the presentation to come. In the meantime, I will start.

#### detachment, materialism, relationships
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nIJj9zl5HIE?t=309
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So more than the fear of death, how will he manage without anyone along with him? He will be all alone."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of Om Tat Sat and begins narrating a story about a merchant with four wives to illustrate the attachments people have in life and their ultimate value.

The topic today is Om Tat Sat. Before we start, I will start with a story. This is a very famous story which most of us would have heard many, many times. But there is some important lesson from this story, more so to the topic what we are going to discuss today. How many of us have heard the story of merchant and poor wife? So not many have heard, I will repeat. Those who have heard also, after the story, when we derive the meaning out of it, it will help. So there was a merchant who had four wives, good news, more so the husbands can say, good news. No good news. Wife is sitting next to you. More is not. One is good. Somebody is saying one is difficult to manage four. So merchant had four wives. So we'll go one by one. The fourth wife, he was very, very attached to. He would spend a lot of time with her, talking to her. And whatever her desires were, whatever her demands were, he always ensured that they were fulfilled. In that sense, she was pampered. You understand pampering? Jo bhi maangna hai usko pura hi karna hai. And he was very attached. Spent a lot of time with her. The third wife, although not very attached, he was very attached to her. But she was not so attached compared to the fourth wife, but she was the most handsome, most beautiful of all the four wives. And whenever this merchant would go to a party or social gathering, he would ensure that the wife comes along. He would take this wife along with him and would take pride in introducing, she is my wife. She was very handsome, very beautiful. But deep within he had a nagging fear. What was the fear? Anyone can help me with an answer? If he had a beautiful wife, what fear would he have? She will leave him somewhere. Who knows, somebody else gets attracted to her and she falls in love. So she may leave. So he had a nagging fear. The second wife, of all the four wives, she was the most loyal. Whenever there was a problem, any difficulties, challenges he would face, she was always there with him to hear him out, to give him the required strength, support. And the first wife, what happened to her? Completely neglected, completely injured, completely indifferent to her need. Even though she may want to spend some time, no time for her. Never ever spent time with her. Never ever fulfilled any of her requirements. So years rolled by, a time came when this merchant became very very old and practically he was on a death bed. And it started dawning on him that, now the time has come. Now I have to leave. Very soon I will have to depart. Now all along, he had four wives. He had the company. And now he has to leave and go all alone. So more than the fear of death, how will he manage without anyone along with him? He will be all alone. So he went to his fourth wife and said, dear, I am very very attached to you. You know this. I cannot live without you. We have spent so much time together. I want you to come with me. So the wife looks at him and says, no way. Very bluntly, without mincing words, no way I can come. Just turns and walks away. He never expected this kind of an answer from a wife whom he was very attached, fulfilled all her demands. And when really the time comes when he wants her company, he is all alone. She says no way and just walks away. So he is quite pained with that answer. He turns to his third wife. Fortunately he had options. So he turns to his third wife and says, Dear, I want you to come with me. Please do not say no. So the wife says, to tell you very honestly, life is very good out here, and I am still young, and my plan is that after you are gone, I will marry someone else. I have plans to remarry because I am still young, I am beautiful, I am handsome, and after you are gone, I will marry someone else. So he was shattered with that answer. And then he turns to his second wife. He says, please, this time he is in a begging mode because two wickets down. So he says, he is in begging mode. Please, can you come with me? I beg you, I plead, please come with me. The name is today. So the wife says, yes, I will come with you, but I too have limitations. Maximum, I will come with you till the graveyard. After that, even if I want to, I cannot. So that's my limitation, unfortunately. I want to come, but maximum I can come till the graveyard. After that, I can't come. So as if someone has pierced a knife in his heart, pain was intolerable for him. And while he was completely shattered, sitting on a chair with too much of pain, grief, hearing these kind of answers from his dear most wives, he hears a feeble voice. And when he turns back and he sees, who was there? The first wife. I will come with you. I will be with you, always. And when he looks at her, she is completely emaciated, thin, lean, as if she had not eaten for days together, completely malnourished. So he says, I will come with you. And that's the first wife. Now, this is an important part of the story. I am going to ask each one of you, please spend little time introspecting, thinking, and let's all apply our mind. Each one of us, each one of us, without exception, including me, although I am not married, have four wives. Each one of us have four wives. We are like that merchant having four wives. Now, let's analyze what does these four wives represent in our lives.

We will go one by one. The fourth wife, the merchant was very, very attached to. He spent a lot of time, whatever her desires were, he fulfilled. She was pampered. What is that in our life? Go one by one, raise your hands, and then we will go to the answers. Material possessions. Senses. Luxury. I repeat, please think a little. Spent a lot of time. What is that which we spend a lot of time, fulfill all the demands, pamper, and finally when the time comes, no weight comes with us? What is that? Yes, Ashwin. Body. Preeti ji, what is the answer? Our body. The first thing is we spend, if you analyze 24 hours in a day, we are spending time to fulfill the demands of the body. We pamper. Shampoos, conditioners, different kinds of clothes, cosmetics, makeup, hairstyles. People these days for keeping the body fit, nothing wrong about it. How much time? I was seeing an interview of Virat Kohli, two hours in the gym daily. Okay, as an international cricketer, he may require it. But how much time we are spending to keep this body fit, young, healthy, good looking. Am I right? Yes or no? Demands of the body, fulfilling it. We are pampering the body, the bodily requirements. Our life has become, the goal of our life has become to satisfy the bodily demands, the sensual demands. That has become a goal. Why we need more money? With more money we can give more comforts to the body. We are the Atma. Body is a dress. But we are fulfilling the demands of the body, not realizing that I am not the body. That's called Dehatma Buddhi. Dehatma Buddhi means, I think, it's a misconception that I am the body. So, the fourth vibe represents the body. So, when a time will come, imagine we have kept the body healthy, good looking, good hairstyle, good, nice, this thing. But the time comes, no way it comes with us. Yes or no? In fact, the body will be burned. In fact, if the body is not burning quickly, they will put some more kerosene, petrol, quickly. It will be burned. All that hairs which we have so painstakingly used conditioners and shampoos and all that, gone. The third wife, very handsome, very beautiful, had a fear that she might run away with someone else. And when the time comes, she says, I will marry to someone else. What does that represent? Wealth and possessions. Wealth and possessions, properties, assets. So, whole life we spend so much time accumulating, accumulating, accumulating and have a sense of pride. And we show off also. Big house, big bungalow, big this thing and you know, there is a sense of satisfaction. We introduce, we call people and then there is an element of pride. How does it get married to someone else? Somebody inherits. How much we take back with us? So, it's all left behind. The second wife, she was very, very loyal. And when the time came, she says, yes, I will come with you. I want to come with you, but I have limitation. I will maximum come with you till the graveyard. What is that? Because family, relatives, friends, near and dear ones, maximum they can come with us till the graveyard. Beyond that, they too have limitations. And the fourth wife, she was very, very loyal. And the first wife, I will always come with you. What does that represent? The soul, the atma, the real self. And is it wrong to spend time taking care of the body? Is it wrong? Is it wrong to take care of our near and dear ones? Is it wrong to spend time with them, socialize, invest in the relation? Is it wrong? Is it wrong to earn money, have some properties, build a new house? Is it wrong? What is wrong? It should not be too much. Not to neglect the soul. It should not be attached to them. So, yes. It is wrong to neglect any of the wife. In a way, we need to take care of them in every aspect of life. A healthy body gives you healthy brain, right? At the same time, our family is alive. Our family is also very important, which keeps us healthy mentally. So, basically all the effects are very important in life. At the same time, we should not take it for the first time. It is a joke. Very true.

#### spirituality, purpose of life, human form of life
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nIJj9zl5HIE?t=1233
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The main thing is to keep the main thing the main thing. And what is that? Taking care of soul."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of spiritual pursuits in human life, distinguishing it from animal existence and highlighting the potential for liberation from the cycle of birth and death.

They say, if the car has to run smoothly, how many wheels the car has usually? Four wheels. They talk about wheel balancing. Imagine the car has three wheels and the fourth wheel is punctured. Will it run smoothly? In today's life, unfortunately, in the pursuit of bodily demands, fulfilling the bodily demands, socializing, building that relationship, so busy earning the money, we have forgotten the main thing. The main thing is to keep the main thing the main thing. And what is that? Taking care of soul. And that's why we see there is so much of anomaly in the society. You are right. Physical health is very important. Mental health is very important. Social relationship is very important. But in maintaining these relationships, in taking care of our survival, roti, kapda, makaan, we should not get so sucked up in this thing that we have no time for any spiritual pursuits. For an average man, if you ask what is spirituality, how much time he is spending? It's five minutes before going to the office. Even that may be missing. In fact, our scriptures say, one of the most important purpose of human form of life, Athato Brahma Jigyas. Now that we have got human birth, because taking care of family, taking care of bodily needs, maintaining relationship in different life forms, we do it. Even a dog has a family and takes care of the near and dear ones. Even the dogs have some relationship. Even the dog fends for food. Even the dog defends. But what the dog cannot do is take care of the soul. It cannot engage in Athato Brahma Jigyasa, Tamaseema, Jyotirgamaya, Satoma, Satgamaya. It cannot do that. And that's the prime purpose of human form of life. In fact, that is what differentiates us from animals because we have that ability to engage in Athato Brahma Jigyasa, to nourish the soul. And eventually, the soul, in human form of life, by performing different spiritual activities, not only gets nourished, eventually can come out of this cycle of birth and death, which is called Mukti or Moksha. Dharma, Artha, Kama, Moksha.

#### absolute truth, om tat sat, supreme lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nIJj9zl5HIE?t=1412
**Verse References:** BG 17.24
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Absolute Truth means it is Truth in the past, Truth in the present, Truth in the future."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to explain the meaning of Om Tat Sat, referencing Bhagavad Gita 17.24, and defining each word: Om as Krishna, Tat as 'that you are', and Sat as the Absolute Truth, which is unchanging and the cause of all causes.

So this is text 24 from the Bhagavad Gita, 17th chapter. Thus the transcendentalist undertakes sacrifices, charities and penances, beginning always with Om to attain the Supreme. From the beginning of creation, the three syllables Om, Tat, Sat have been used to indicate the Supreme Absolute Truth. So what does this word Om, Tat, Sat refer to? It refers to the Supreme Lord. We'll go word by word. What does Om refer to? What does Tat refers to? And what Sat refers to? Om, Krishna talks about in the Gita, Omkara. Akshara. He says, I am that Omkara. I am that Om. That's why all the Vedic mantras start with Om, Tat, Vishnu, Param, Padam, Sada, Pashyanti, Surya. Starts Om, Tat. Om, Vishnu, Ay, Namaha. So Omkara is a representation of Lord Shri Krishna. Krishna says that. I am that syllable Om. Tat. What is Tat? Tat Tvam Asi. Tat means that you are. That Absolute Truth. The Supreme Personality of Godhead. You are. Tat. Tvam Asi. You are that. So Om, Tat and Sat. Sat means the Truth. Supreme Lord Personality of Godhead. Supreme Personality of Godhead is also called Absolute Truth. What is the meaning of Absolute Truth? Param Satya. Absolute Truth. This is a Relative Truth and this is an Absolute Truth. Relative Truth may be Truth in the present, may not be Truth in the past, may not be Truth in the future. For example, I am a man. Is it Truth? Yes or No? I am a man. It is a Relative Truth. Last life, I am a man, may not be Truth. Next life, I am a man, may not be Truth. Right now, it is Truth. It is called Relative Truth. Absolute Truth means it is Truth in the past, Truth in the present, Truth in the future. Krishna is a Supreme Personality of Godhead. Is it Truth in the past? Truth in the present. Is it Truth in the present? Yes. It is Truth in the future? Yes. It is Absolute Truth. Another feature of Absolute Truth is, Truth is dependent on something. I have no existence unless my Father exists. My Father is the cause of my existence, materially speaking. An Absolute Truth is, that Truth which has no cause, which is the cause of all causes. It exists when nothing was there. So Absolute Truth is that Truth which is Truth in the past, Truth in the present, Truth in the future. It is not caused by anything. It stands alone. In fact, it is the cause of all other causes. Govindam Aadi Purusham Tamham Bhajami. It is the original cause. Sarva Karana Karanam. So that Supreme Lord is Sat, Absolute Truth, is Truth, which is everlasting and unchanging. Now, Om Tat Sat. Om refers to Supreme Lord. It is a sound representation of the Lord. Tat means Tat Tvam Asi. And Sat, Sat means Truth. The Lord is Truth. Absolute Truth. In all three phases of time. It is not dependent on anything. It is the cause of all causes. So this Tat Tvam Asi, sorry, Om Tat Sat, refers to the Supreme Lord.

#### yajna, karma, krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nIJj9zl5HIE?t=1665
**Verse References:** BG 9.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Performance of everything on behalf of the Supreme Personality of Godhead assures the perfection of all activity."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that uttering Om Tat Sat before any activity indicates that it is being done for the pleasure of the Supreme Lord, transforming it into a Yajna and leading to liberation, contrasting it with karma that binds one to the material world.

And this word is uttered, Om Tat Sat, for any work we do, any activity we engage in, to offer that activity, to indicate that this activity is being done for the pleasure, for the satisfaction of that Absolute Truth, we use the word Om Tat Sat. In the Vedic Hymns, the word Om is always found because it represents the Supreme Lord. Formerly, when Brahma performed sacrifices, he spoke these three names of the Supreme Personality of Godhead: Om Tat Sat. Any work done should be done from Om Tat Sat, for Om Tat Sat or for the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Performance of everything on behalf of the Supreme Personality of Godhead assures the perfection of all activity. While we are doing that activity, it should be done with the pleasure of the Lord in mind. That is Krishna Consciousness. And the results of that work also, knowing that it is coming from the Lord, should be offered for the satisfaction and pleasure of the Lord. Then, no matter what activity we do, that activity becomes, it becomes what? Anyone can help me with an answer? Yajna. What is Yajna? Any activity done for the pleasure and satisfaction of the Supreme Lord. Krishna in the Gita says, Yajnat Karmano Anyatra. Means, any karma, any activity, which is not done as Yajna. Anyatra means Anya, other than Yajna. Yajnat Karmano Anyatra. Lokoyam Karma Bandhana. That activity binds us in this world, entangles us in this world. Activity which is done for the pleasure and satisfaction of the Supreme Lord, that activity, Yajna, disentangles us, liberates us, takes us closer to God. Yajnat Karmano Anyatra. Lokoyam Karma Bandhana.

#### punya, karma, devotional service, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/nIJj9zl5HIE?t=1824
**Verse References:** BG 9.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The highest is, all our activities should be done as Yagya."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates between Punya (good deeds) and activities done for the Lord's satisfaction, explaining that even Punya can bind one to the material world, while devotional service leads to liberation.

So we have discussed in one of the sessions, what is the difference between Punya and work done for the satisfaction of the Lord. For example, somebody opens an orphanage with all good intentions that I want to please and serve the orphans. Is it a good activity? Yes? No? It is Punya. There is a difference between Punya, work, which has good reaction and activity which is done for the satisfaction and pleasure of the Lord. Even so called Punya binds us in this world. How? Even so called Punya, which we think is all good, binds us to this world. Yajnat Karmano Anyatra Lokoyam Karma Bandhana. It entangles us. Why? Based on good Karma, you get good births. Janma Aishwarya Shrutashri. But you are still in this material world entangled in Janma Mrityu Jara Vyadhi. We are still in so called prison house of this material world undergoing Janma Mrityu Jara Vyadhi may be in better conditions. Just like Janma Mrityu Jara Vyadhi is there even in America, it is there even in Somalia. An old man in America experiencing invalidity or diseases in very opulent conditions vis-a-vis a person experiencing the same conditions in a third world country in a very impoverished condition, the pain is the same. Old age is the same. The eyes will start blurring. May be you have more better gadgets to take care of your health, more amenities, more better hospitals. So Punya gives us a good reaction. Based on that reaction, we get a material body in this material world to enjoy Janma Aishwarya Shrutashri. Good birth, Aishwarya opulence, Shrutah, good education, intelligence and Shri means beauty. Is that clear to everyone? Whereas acts of devotional service, acts which are done for the pleasure and satisfaction of the Lord, disentangles us, has the potency to extricate us from the cycle of birth and death. So Krishna therefore says, yes, better than doing Papa, better to do Punya. But doing Punya is not the highest. The highest is, all our activities should be done as Yagya. And how do we do that Yagya? That act should be dedicated for the pleasure of Supreme Lord. How do we dedicate? Om Tat Sat. Before we do that activity, remember that this activity is meant for pleasure and satisfaction of Lord. I am doing this for the pleasure and satisfaction of Lord. Offer the results of that activity to the Lord. Then that activity becomes Yagya.

#### meditation, yoga, transcendental knowledge, inconceivability of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U5hYPAOacSU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Meditation in the true sense is meditating on the Lord, and that is called yoga."
**Summary:** This segment defines real meditation as focusing on the Supreme Lord, emphasizing His qualities and inconceivable nature, contrasting it with impersonal or void meditation, and highlighting the need for Vedic knowledge to understand Him.

So today's discussion is on meditation as taught by Krishna. What meditation? We all are talking about meditation these days. A lot of people are practicing different kinds of meditation. What is real meditation? What's the objective of that meditation? One should meditate upon the Supreme Person as the one who knows everything. Now Krishna in the previous verses is saying one should remember me, always think of me. Now it's not an abstract concept. He is saying remember Him as what? As one who knows everything, who is omniscient, as one who is the oldest, who is the controller, everything is moving as per His desire, who is smaller than the smallest, who is the maintainer of everything, who is beyond all material conception, who is inconceivable, and who is always a person. He is luminous like the sun, bright, brilliant, lustrous, and being transcendental, He is beyond this material nature. So it's not an abstract meditation. One should remember the Lord, one should think of Him as what? He is a transcendental person, He is luminous, He is all-knowing, He is the oldest, He is a supreme controller, who is beyond material conception. Now here in this particular verse, the process of thinking of the Supreme Lord is mentioned. One cannot meditate on something that is beyond his control. One cannot meditate on something impersonal or void. Yes, these days we have meditation, candlelight meditation, you can burn a candle and watch the flame. Yes, it has some benefit, we can focus our mind, we can concentrate, it improves our concentration, that's all great. But meditation in the true sense is meditating on the Lord, and that is called yoga. We link ourselves with the Supreme Lord. Now the word achintya is used in this verse. Achintya means inconceivable. The Lord cannot be conceived by our blunt material senses. He is transcendental, He is adhokshaja. Adhokshaja means beyond sense perception. He is a supreme person, but the process to see Him is not through our blunt material eyes. Therefore, we need the help of the Vedas, our scriptures, to know more about this person. We are too small and insignificant compared to this gigantic creation. Have you heard of this Hubble telescope? So with this Hubble telescope, they are able to watch galaxies. Milky Way, we are part of Milky Way, this solar system, the sun, family of planets and many more stars, each of them as big as the sun. We are part of a galaxy called Milky Way. And they say there are millions of such galaxies in this universe. And you can see these galaxies through these telescopes. Now, just see this planet Earth. How gigantic it has, huge continents. If you go by a Boeing flying at 900 miles per hour, you know it takes 18 hours, 20 hours, 25 hours to go from one side to the other side of the planet. Huge, mighty oceans. A plane is flying at 900 miles, it takes 8 hours to cross that ocean. Now like this we have other planets and this whole planet we have a solar system, the sun being in the center. And there are millions of stars, each of them as big as the sun. Each of them having its own family of planets. And imagine in that planet Earth, we are sitting in a small city, relatively speaking, and in that city, a small place, Powai, and in that Powai, you know, a small school, and in that school, in the fifth floor, in an auditorium. How small, insignificant we are compared to this Earth, and this Earth when compared to this galaxy and this universe.

#### knowledge acquisition, death, consciousness, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U5hYPAOacSU?t=600
**Verse References:** BG 8.06
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One who at the time of death fixes his life between the eyebrows and in full devotion engages himself in remembering the Supreme Lord will certainly attain to the Supreme Personality of Godhead."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the insignificance of material logic in understanding the Lord, emphasizing the importance of the descending process of knowledge acquisition, and highlights the significance of remembering the Lord at the time of death to attain the spiritual realm.

Now, this Lord, He is the creator of millions of such universes, each of them having millions of galaxies, each of them having so many stars. In fact, through the Hubble telescope, they figured out that these galaxies are moving away. They are not stationary in one place. They are receding, and they are receding at a certain speed. Now, using that calculation, mathematical calculations, they figured out that if all these galaxies are receding, going away, and they are constantly going away, then at one point of time, they all should have originated from a particular point. And if you know the speed at which they are receding, and you work backward that you can calculate. There was a point of time when all these galaxies were exploded from a point. They said there was a point. There is a person who has written, a scientist who has written this article, who says there was a point which had sum total of all the energies, all the matter, and there was an explosion, and this whole thing started expanding. And he being a believer of God says, and God was out of that point. He made that explosion happen. That big bang happened. So there are different theories. So this Lord, who is the creator, who is the maintainer, who is Adhokshaja, who is beyond our sense perception, He is Achintya. Achintya means inconceivable, inconceivable by our material senses. Because He is inconceivable, using material logic, material senses, many people conclude He does not exist, or they use extrapolation. Because we see people who are limited by time and space. If I see you here, I can very confidently say you have not gone to Mayapur, because you cannot be in two places simultaneously. Whereas we know that Lord is all present. So they use that logic and says, how can the Lord who is all present be a person who is limited by time and space, because their experiences, people are limited by time and space. But we cannot use material logic. Lord is not like a material person like us, limited by time and space. We see people, every person whom we come across, we see, they are subjected to old age, diseases, death. And we understand the Lord is all great, all powerful, is not subjected to all this, birth, death, old age. Hence, He cannot be a person. Can you see this logic? Using material logic we come to a wrong conclusion. So therefore if we have to understand about that Lord, we have to follow a different process. Therefore intelligent persons take help of descending process of acquiring knowledge. There are two ways of acquiring knowledge. One is Avarohapanta. Avarohapanta means ascending process. What is ascending process? Going upwards. Where? Going upwards in what way? Going upwards. We build knowledge through observation, through experimentation, by own experience, by gripping more and more data. That's what the science is doing. At one point of time we believed that earth is flat. We observed, no, no, no, earth is not flat. Atom is the smallest particle. We further did lot of research. No, no. Atom is not the smallest particles. Quantum theory came about. So we are building our body of knowledge through observation. It's ascending process. We are going upwards in our search of absolute truth. If we have to understand about the Lord, which process we have to use? Descending process. Just like I gave example, a small ant in this room, some ant hole in this hall, can never figure out using ascending process, what happened to Lalu Yadav yesterday. Can it figure out? Current affairs. You should be up to date about what's happening in the world. Can it ever know? Can it ever know? Think about it. It also has a world view. When it moves around, it also has an understanding of this world, figuring out what's going on. Sunday comes, 4 o'clock, what do we do here? It has a world view. On its own, using ascending process, it can never figure out what's happening in other parts of the world, but it can figure out using the descending process. You can tell Lalul-e-Adab ko jail ho gaya. Achcha, hona chahiye. Okay, text 10. One who at the time of death fixes his life-wear between the eyebrows and in full devotion. Mind you, this is something which all of us will have to go through. Now somebody says, Swamiji, aap death ke baarein bahut bolte ho. Yes, we can always set aside, close our eyes. In fact, there is a time when the rabbit is being chased by the lion. It runs, runs, runs, runs and finally at one point of it realizes the tiger is running very fast and he will not be able to escape. So at one point it just gives up, sits in one place, closes the eyes and thinks. What does it think? There is no tiger. There is no tiger. So, we all have to face this moment of death, and we have faced it many, many times. Now in human form of life, we can face differently. Prabhupada says one is a Kripana, a miserly man, if he dies like a cat and a dog. What is the meaning of dying like a cat and a dog? Born in ignorance, died in ignorance. In between did lot of gymnastics. He is a miserly man who got this human form of life, had an opportunity to realize the self but without perfecting his life, died in ignorance. One who at the time of death fixes his life between the eyebrows and in full devotion engages himself in remembering the Supreme Lord will certainly attain to the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

#### moksha, consciousness at death, karma, free will, divine justice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/U5hYPAOacSU?t=1200
**Verse References:** BG 8.05, BG 9.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate."
**Summary:** This segment explains the concept of moksha, emphasizing the importance of fixing the mind on the Supreme Godhead at the time of death, and discusses how our consciousness at death determines our next life, highlighting the Lord's reciprocal relationship with devotees and the role of free will in experiencing suffering or happiness.

So Krishna in the Gita talks about parastasmatu bhavanyo vyakta-avyakta-sanatana. Beyond this material world, there is spiritual world, which is vyakta-avyakta-sanatana. This world which we are living in is vyakta. Vyakta means manifest. Avyakta means unmanifest. It has come into being and will one day get annihilated. Creation, maintenance and annihilation. But Krishna says parastasmatu bhavanyo beyond this material creation there is a spiritual creation, which is beyond vyakta-avyakta-sanatana and it is sanatana. And here Krishna is saying that one who quits his body remembering me, in full concentration with the devotion will certainly attain to the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Lot of people ask moksha, mukti. You know we all have a weird concept. Moksha means all our hands will go away and suddenly we will start flying in the air. It's not abstract concept. There is a material world where the soul is transmigrating life after life, and we come out of this transmigration, and achieve our spiritual home. At the time of death the mind must be fixed in devotion on the Supreme Godhead. If the mind is not fixed what happens? Whatever we are thinking, whatever our consciousness is, that state we attain in next life. Did we all choose our birth? We will be born in Pavai. Did we choose? We will be born in this family. We will take birth as Indian. No. As human being. No. As a man. No. Who chose? And on what basis? Some atma is born as let's say a cat, as a dog. Many of you may have pets. You throw a piece of stone it immediately responds. There is an experiencer like you and me, who is experiencing pain, who has feelings. That experiencer is the atma. That atma and the atma in me is not different. It's not different. The body is different. The dress is different. So, based on our thinking, we get the next body. The mind must be fixed in devotion. It's not just for time pass, it's not for peace of mind, it is not for relaxation, it is not for, you know, it gives us a good feeling, it's not for all that mundane thing. Human life, there is a higher purpose. Higher purpose is what? To purify our existence, so that we can not die like cats and dogs, die with remembrance of the Lord. If you have to die with remembrance of the Lord, you have to practice remembering the Lord, and to practice remembering the Lord, you have to spend time. Any practice takes time. Saini and Eval practices badminton. How many hours in a day, anyone guess? Eight hours a day. It doesn't come cheaply. The cricketers, I am sure they must be putting lot of time practicing, even though they may be very accomplished, very this thing, still they may be practicing to improve their skills. A pure devotee is the one who is always thinking of Krishna, sees everything as manifestation of Krishna and His energies. A pure devotee can live anywhere and create the atmosphere of Vrindavan, so one thing is yes, Vrindavan which is a spiritual place but if we engage in devotional service, the place where we are staying can also be transformed into Vrindavan. Vrindavan is a place where the Lord is the centre. So even our house can be Vrindavan, if the centre thing which is happening in the house is the Lord, His devotion, His worship, His glorification, His chanting, that is the main thing happening, that house is Vrindavan. A pure devotee cannot forget the Supreme Lord for a moment and similarly the Supreme Lord cannot forget His pure devotee for a moment and this is a reciprocal relationship. It is not that we keep doing devotion, devotion, devotion. A lot of people sometimes says, you know, when people get into problems, difficulties, some people have some ailments, some diseases, incurable, then we tell them, you know, worship God, and then they actually worship God and say, nothing is happening, I have done a lot of Pooja, I have done a lot of Paath, I have gone here, I have gone there, it was 7.30 pm, I have done a Pooja of Shani, it is not happening, because we are disconnected from the Lord. At that point when we need help of the Lord, we are seeking, give me help, give me help, when actually we were in good shape, you know, we were completely forgetful of the Lord. That's why it says, So when we remember the Lord, when we have invested time in the Lord, the Lord is reciprocating. If we are dear to the Lord, Lord is dear to us. As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate.

Iti Shodashaka Naam Naam. Shodashaka means 16. Iti, Iti means this. Shodashaka. Shodashaka means 16. Naam Naam. Kali Kalmasha Naashanam. Naata Parotra Upaya. You will not find a Parotra. Parotra means higher, superior, better, more effective. Naata. Naata means not. Parotra. Parotra means higher. Upaya. Upaya means solution. Sarva Vedeshu Drishyate. If you search scrutinizingly all the Vedas and try to figure out what is the Upaya, what is the solution, what I should be doing, you will not find a better Upaya. So it is like, I was telling about three aspects of absolute truth, Bhagwan, Paramatma and Brahman. They all are referring to the same, just like sunshine, sun globe and sun God. When sunshine comes to your room, you say sun has come. Actually sun has not come. When sun comes, everything will end. Because we use interchangeably, because sunshine is sun, in one sense. Likewise Brahman is an aspect of Bhagwan, and Om chanting is a representation of the impersonal aspect of the Lord, which is Brahman. Whereas Hare Krishna Mahamantra is representing the Lord, representing the Bhagwan aspect of the Supreme Lord. Krishna, Rama, Hare refers to the energy of the Lord. Is that clear to everyone? Three aspects of absolute truth, Brahman, Paramatma, Bhagwan. So Om is representation of the impersonal aspect of the Lord, which is Brahman, all pervading Supreme Lord. He's an invisible man who is up in the sky, who is witnessing all what we are doing. Is it true, yes or no? How is it possible for him to witness? He's within us, yes. And he is? Because he's within us. Because he's within us. Because he's all-powerful. He's all-knowing. Using mundane material logic, we come to understand how can there be a person like that, who is all-knowing, who is witnessing everything. But we saw in this verse, what did we see? He's adukshaja. He's transcendental. He's not a mundane personality. He's all-knowing. Yes, he's a witnesser. Upadrishta and anumanta. He's a witnesser and sanctioner. He's there in everyone's heart. If God is all-good, all-kind, all-loving, then why are they suffering? Huh? Lord has... Just like even in this state, the government is for the people, by the people. Am I right? The government is for the people, by the people. Am I right? The government is for the people, by the people. Am I right? The government wants the election manifesto. We'll serve you in such a way. He doesn't want us to suffer, but if we choose, using our free will, to do something which is not in our self-interest, then we suffer. As you sow, so shall you reap. #### mixed devotion, desire, spiritual progress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lueREOuBsSs?t=0

**Key Quote:** "We all are mixed devotees."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains the concept of mixed devotion, where individuals engage in devotional service with a mixture of spiritual and material desires, seeking personal benefits like peace of mind, satisfaction, or material gain.

So that's what Prabhupada here is mentioned. A mixed devotee engages in devotional service. We all are mixed devotees. Prabhupada, great devotees, there are pure devotees. So mixed devotee, what does he do? Engages in devotional service for the spiritual benefit. There is some aham in that. That aham has not gone, but that aham is relatively pure. It is for my spiritual progress, my advancement. For the spiritual benefit of being eternally engaged in transcendental abode of the Lord in full bliss and knowledge. In the material existence, when a devotee is not completely purified, he experiences, expects material benefit from the Lord. When we are serving, we are expecting some benefit, peace of mind, satisfaction, feeling good, in the form of relief from material miseries or he wants material gain. Lot of people are kamabhaktas. They are serving the Lord because they have some desires to be fulfilled. Advancement in knowledge of relationship between the Supreme Personality of Godhead and these people, and some are serving the Lord or engaged in devotional service to get enlightened, to know Geta Mita Jigyasu. They want to know. When a person is now, this is all mixed devotee. Now, when a person is blessy, he wants Bhajan to come, to know Knowledge, to know coolness of life, to know things of happiness, to know warmness, that is all mixed devotee. He wants Bhajan, he wants lightening, he wants happiness, he wants Wealth, for the Mastra of 절 bile, for the Self. Whole-body habit of service to the Lord. ON service to the Lord is means he needs light and something приходless and kind. SYSTEM IS TO BE SOLVED IN THIS WIDTH OF COVID-19 Sários Now, God is not anxious but he says let another agita in Jainus at that. Surviving will Service to the Lord is a mean And is what? #### pure devotion, love, selflessness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lueREOuBsSs?t=126
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "His one interest is that he loves the Supreme Personality of Godhead and he spontaneously engages in satisfying Him."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts mixed devotion with pure devotion, explaining that pure devotees serve the Lord solely out of love, without seeking personal gain or even liberation, exemplified by the Gopis of Vrindavan.

So Prabhupada says when a person is transcendental to these conditions, he is called a pure devotee. Pure devotee does not engage in devotional service to the Lord because he wants knowledge, because he wants to get enlightened, because he wants pleasure, because he wants satchitananda. Why does he serve the Lord? His one interest is that he loves the Supreme Personality of Godhead and he spontaneously engages in satisfying Him. A devotee, Prahlad Maharaj says, for me, I do not, I am already satisfied. They are already in a position of satchitananda. They already reached that stage because they are engaged in the service of the Lord. What more they have to achieve? For them even going to spiritual world because what we do in spiritual world engage in service of the Lord, glorify the Lord. A pure devotee is engaged doing the same thing here in the material world so he doesn't even have that desire to go back to the spiritual world. So highest example of pure devotional service is that Gopis in Vrindavan, they are not interested in even understanding Krishna but only in loving Him. That platform of love is a pure state of devotional service.

So what are we trying to engage in? We have come here in the material world. We have rebelled against the Lord. We have an impure desire to be the Lord, to be the center of attraction, to be glorified, to be famous, and that is the root cause why we are in this material world. And by Prabhupada's mercy, by the mercy of pure devotee, we have got some understanding what mistake we have done and we are now engaged in devotional service. And right now because the desires cannot be purified suddenly. When a person is diseased, you cannot suddenly get cured. It takes time, you have to take the medicine. A fan is rotating, it suddenly cannot stop. Immediately, it is not possible to get cured. It is not possible to get cured. It is not possible to get cured. So what we are doing is we are taking the medicine. We are chanting the holy names of the Lord. We are engaged in devotional service and slowly our disease is getting cured. Our mixed devotee may desire to enjoy a comfortable life on another planet with a greater span of life such as Brahma Loka. These are material desires but because a mixed devotee engages in the service of the Lord ultimately, it is not possible to get cured. Fortunately, after many

Thank you. For today's Gita Live session, the topic for the day is Overcoming Social Media Addiction. Social media has become a very integral part of our lives. Even if we want to, it is difficult to live without it. We all know there are many benefits, and we also know that it has many bad effects. It can even lead to addictions. So today we are going to talk more about this subject, and we will also share perspectives from the Gita why people are getting addicted to it. What is the root cause? And from the Gita, we will also get some clue as to how we can handle these tools intelligently, effectively, without these tools harming us. So before we get into the discussion, let us all pray together.

#### social media, technology, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/04EA3ny2Bns?t=252
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So knife per se is not good or bad, a lot depends on how we use it, how intelligently or unintelligently we use that knife."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that social media is not inherently bad, using the analogy of a knife to illustrate that its value depends on the user's intention and skill.

This is a very interesting and relevant topic, more so in present times. We all know people in general spend an inordinate amount of time in front of their smartphones, and these days there are many apps, many social media platforms which, if we get into, eat up a lot of our time. Now, is it really bad to be on social media, to be on Facebook, to be on Instagram? Is it wrong? The answer is no. It's not wrong. It's something like asking, is a knife bad? It depends on who is using it and how he is using it. If the knife is in the hand of a surgeon, the same knife can save a life. But the same knife in wrong hands can even take someone's life, can kill someone. So a knife per se is not good or bad; a lot depends on how we use it, how intelligently or unintelligently we use that knife. The same is true with all social media platforms.

#### social media, communication, technology, benefits
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/04EA3ny2Bns?t=341
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Those days the mode of communication was letters."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the benefits of social media and shares a story about communication methods in the past, specifically during his college days in the late 1980s, highlighting the slow pace and limitations of letters and postcards.

They have a lot of benefits. There are a lot of advantages which we can get out of these social media platforms. I'll just share a few of them before we talk about addiction and things like that, something on the negative side. Let us be judicious and also count on some of the good things which these platforms have done. This reminds me of my college days. This was in the year 1988-89 when I was doing my engineering. Those days the mode of communication was letters. I still remember there was a postcard, a 15 paise postcard, and you would scribble something and put it in a post box. There was a post box right next to our hostel, and you post it in, and then after 5 days, 6 days, or 7 days, the postcard will reach the destination. I used to write to my parents and near India ones. It would reach after 4-5 days. So if you have to communicate something, the message will reach the other side after 5 or 6 days. If you have travelled from your home town to the college, they have no clue whether you have reached safely. And invariably we used to write some kind of letters or postcards, and it will take 4-5 days. Till that time, you can assume that yes, the person has reached safely or the person is fine.

---
#### communication, technology, history
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/04EA3ny2Bns?t=444
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "First time we could speak to our near and dear ones, go to an STD booth."
**Summary:** The speaker continues his historical overview of communication technology, describing the use of telegrams for urgent messages and the advent of STD/ISD phone calls, highlighting the cost and limitations of these methods compared to modern communication.

I remember in those days we used to, for some urgent matters or some urgent news, suppose some, let's say there is a death in the family, it has to be communicated, there was something called as a telegram. These days you also have a telegram app, but those days the telegram was you have to go to a post office and you have to write what message you want to send. Each word, for each letter, counts. There is some paisa associated with each letter, so you have to be very judicious because the longer the sentence, the longer the number of words, the more is the money involved. So you are very judicious, you will eat the words, and you will draft the message, and for each word, for a small telegram with 2 or 3 sentences or 4 sentences, in those days it would cost some 30 rupees, 40 rupees, or 50 rupees. And you will go to the post office, write the whole thing, and then there will be a person who will type it out, and through that mechanism of telegraph, the technology, whatever it was, the same message would be typed on the telegraph machine at the recipient town or the city, and the postman would go and deliver it over the telegraph on an urgent basis to the person whom you want to communicate to. So in the best case scenario, you are able to transmit the message, that to one or two sentences, the same day to the person, but at a very, very additional high cost. And then a revolution came; this was in the year 1990, somewhere around that time, STD ISD started becoming very popular. First time we could speak to our near and dear ones, go to an STD booth, and those days since the technology was new, for every second there was a pulse, 2 second pulse, 4 second pulse, and for each pulse there was a certain amount fixed, and there were time zones; in the morning before 6 am it was a 4 second pulse, after 6 to 10 am it was a 2 second pulse, and after day time regular business hours it was a 1 second pulse, depending on the distance. I remember even if you speak for 2 minutes or 1 minute, the bill would be as high as 50 rupees, 100 rupees. In those days when a full semester we used to spend about 4000-5000 rupees per month, around 1000 rupees.
