## HG Amitasana Dasa

### OVERCOME SOCIAL MEDIA ADDICTION | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### social media, addiction, dangers
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4QbIuTyYwCc?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "As I mentioned, it's a double-edged sword, knife in the hand of a doctor, it can save life. The same knife in the hand of a terrorist can take someone's life, can kill someone."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the dangers associated with social media, using examples like a mother neglecting her child, 'WhatsAppitis,' gaming-induced blood clots, 'Selfititis,' and sleep deprivation among pilots.

So, I will continue from where we left, or probably a little before that, because there was a disruption in the internet connection here. So, I was talking about the benefits which social media platforms have given us, the contributions they have made in our lives. But yes, we also have some demerits, so today we are going to talk about it, some of the dangers which are associated with the use of social media platforms if we are not cautious, if we are not careful. As I mentioned, it's a double-edged sword, a knife in the hand of a doctor, it can save life. The same knife in the hand of a terrorist can take someone's life, can kill someone. So what are the dangers? So here we have an example, this was published. This article was published in a website called ddhs.com where it was mentioned a mother by name Alexandria, a 22-year-old mother from Florida, violently shook her innocent infant child to death while playing a game of Formula. She got so absorbed, so involved in the game that the infant was in her hands and shook violently that the infant died. In other words, you can be transported into another world where you are not so much conscious of the real world. There's a term called WhatsAppitis. It's a very funny thing. The term talks about, there's a newly coined term called WhatsAppitis. WhatsAppitis, which has recently become popular, meaning that incessant use of thumb to text and operate cellular phones can damage your thumb muscles causing tenosynovitis, which is a technical term, medical term. Constantly we are on the phones and it can numb the muscles, it can damage the muscles and there's a technical term to that. And this medical condition has been named after WhatsApp, WhatsAppitis, lot of people are on WhatsApp. Man dies from gaming-induced blood clot. Playing video games up to 12 hours a day was a regular part of Chris' life. This is a real-life example. Chris, a person, used to play for 12 hours a day. The cause of Chris' sudden death was a deep vein thrombosis, a blood clot that occurs when sitting in one spot for prolonged periods of time. You're sitting in one spot, and so absorbed in the game that you have completely become unconscious of your surroundings, so much so of your bodily needs. This blood clot happening because there is least movement in the body, sitting in one place for hours together, drinking water, and this has led to death of a person. There is another term which is a funny term which is called just like I talked about Whatsappitis. This is called Selfititis which is a person having a compulsive need, a genuine mental condition and people feels compelled to continually post pictures of themselves on the social media. It is a genuine mental disorder says psychologist. We had gone on a trip and along with us there was a person. I could see that for every place we visited, for every small thing we did, there was a compulsive need to take a photograph and put it on Facebook. And invariably after posting he would check how many people have liked it, how many people have commented, how many people have viewed it and that would give him a kick. This was an article where Air Chief Marshal, Air Force Chief of the Indian Air Force, Mr. B.S. Dhanoya had said that pilots spend long hours on social media at night which is leading to sleep deprivation among them. And sleep deprivation can be dangerous more so if the work involves a lot of attention like pilots. Air Chief Marshal Dhanoya added that a system should be developed which can tell if a pilot is sleep deprived. Imagine a person has not slept enough, has slept for 3 or 4 hours in the night and he is a pilot in a plane. He can doze off while the flight in the cockpit and it can be fatal. We even had a fatal accident in 2013 because of this problem, he added. So it's a serious problem. People get hooked on, people spend inordinate amount of time, even at the cost of losing their sleep, not getting enough sleep, not becoming conscious of their surroundings and not becoming conscious of how it is eating up so much of their valuable time. So these are some of the dangers which we talked about. If we are not cautious, if we are not careful, it can consume us.

---
#### relationships, productivity, health
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4QbIuTyYwCc?t=383
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Digital distraction leads to obesity and cardiovascular diseases."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how social media can damage relationships, cause productivity loss, and lead to health problems like obesity, depression, and poor sleep quality.

At a relationship level, how these apps, these social media platforms can even destroy relationships. We all live in a world where relationships play a very very important role if we have to be happy. We have our near and dear ones, our friend circles and good relationship is very important if we have to lead a happier lives. So it says that social media takes a toll on relationships. How? In a study published in Journal Psychology of Popular Media Culture, researchers found that 70% of women said that smartphone interfered with their relationships. We'll get into more details. How can social media interfere into relationships? They say that 70% of the women had techno interference. Just like we have interference, interference of signals, interference of noise. So techno interference where technology interferes in your life, in your relationships. 62% of women said that technology interfered with their leisure time and the time which they spent with their partners. We have seen people even when they are in a restaurant, each one, although they have gone together, but each one is on their phone catching up with their posts and checking their profile, checking their whatever social media platform they are on. Next, how does it soar the relationships, spoils the relationships? It creates resentment which can easily build if one partner spends too much time. The other person can feel ignored. The other person is not giving enough time, quality time. He is busy with his own phone, spending inordinate amount of time, ultimately, even leading to a breakup. And over and above that, there is lot of productivity loss. This is, for many, it's a reflex, as unconscious as breathing. Just like we breathe unconsciously. Likewise, whenever we hit a stumbling block during some important task, let's say we are thinking about something or we are doing some project, we hit some stumbling block or we need a small mini break or we are writing something which is quite taxing, the hand reaches for the phone and opens a social media network of our choice. It could be Facebook. It could be Instagram. It could be something else. A blur of time passes and half an hour or more of what ought to have been a productive time is gone. We all have experienced this. Just we want to check and we get pulled into it. And by the time we realize 15 minutes, 20 minutes of our prime time has been eaten away. A feeling of regret is quickly displaced by the urge to see what has happened on the Twitter in the past 15 seconds. So, if we are not conscious, a lot of time can get wasted. We can get pulled into it. We can get distracted. The amount of time we spend on actual productive work could be very very less because time and again just like it says breathing is we do it unconsciously. Likewise, unconsciously we may get distracted by these platforms which pulls our attention. At a personal level, it leads to even diseases. Digital distraction leads to obesity and cardiovascular diseases. Several scientific studies have shown that people whose attention is distracted while they are eating their food can eat more than what they require. It's a fact. When people are busy with their phones and while they are eating or while they are watching the television, they are not conscious of the body signals and may eat more and may lead to obesity and related health issues. It leads to depression. Many internet users distort the truth on the internet. Everyone is posting all the good things which is happening to them in their lives, approximately 75% of them presenting only the good in their life. Upon comparing with their peers, what is being posted online with their reality many can get depressed because invariably people compare. When they see someone is enjoying life and someone is you know has gone to a particular place, there could be envy, there could be jealousy, there could be a feeling of depression, feeling of that I am not good enough. Why am I not enjoying like him. Why am I left behind. All kinds of thoughts can crop up in the mind. Insufficient and poor quality sleep. Researchers have concluded that social media users sleep less and their sleep is not as thick and restful as non-users because throughout the day we have been distracted. Time and again we have been pulled. Our attention has been scattered and the mind is agitated and more so if we have used these platforms, spent time before going to bed, it can even interfere with the quality of sleep we get. While we are on the bed.

---
#### addiction, dopamine, habit loop
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4QbIuTyYwCc?t=770
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The net is designed to be an interruption system. It's designed like that. A machine geared for dividing our attention."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how social media is designed to be an interruption system, releasing dopamine and creating a habit loop that leads to addiction, comparing it to other addictions like smoking and alcohol.

Nicholas Kark, author of The Shallows, what the internet is doing to our brain wrote, the net is designed to be an interruption system. It's designed like that. A machine geared for dividing our attention. I was watching a video of ex-founder, co-founder of Facebook. He was sharing that when it was originally designed, it was designed that how, when people get a like or someone comments positively, there is a feel good factor, the release of a hormone called dopamine and which enthuses, inspires a person to post many more such images, many more such pictures, many more such posts and crave for those likes, crave for those views, a feel good factor, the release of a hormone called dopamine and in the process a person gets hooked on to that platform, get addicted to the platform. I'll share with you how this habit loop functions. Whenever there is a trigger or a cue and if we act and as a result of that action which we may call as a ritual, if we experience some kind of a reward or a good feeling or a pleasant feeling then naturally there is a craving set up in our system for that reward, for that good feeling. Just like to give you an example, a person may innocently smoke, just as he is in a party and friends have, you know, been telling him, no, no, why don't you take, why don't you try for one time. He may innocently try it out for the one time. But then first time when he takes, the ritual, the action of smoking the cigarette and the reward which he gets, although initially yes, it can be a very big kick and it may not be actually a reward, but the reward in terms of the kick, the high, some bit of relaxation, some bit of, you know, dealing with that stress which has built up in the system, that caffeine or nicotine, whatever that intoxicant is there can be like a reward and soon a habit loop sets in where unconsciously we start craving for that cue, in that case a cigarette. That's how, that's true with all the addictions, all the intoxicants, be it nicotine, caffeine, cigarettes, alcohol, gambling. We start off innocently and then we get some kind of a reward which could be in term of relief, a feel good factor and then our body starts craving it and a time comes when we are actually wanting that thing to happen. We cannot live without it. Just like in this case, many people cannot live without being on social media platform.

#### addiction, distraction, focus
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4QbIuTyYwCc?t=984
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "How much a person is addicted to even in night time."
**Summary:** The speaker shares personal anecdotes about witnessing social media addiction, including someone checking their phone multiple times during the night on a train and students using their phones for non-educational purposes during class, highlighting the impact on focus and productivity.

I was recently traveling from Jaipur and I had to go through quota. So I was going through a train and night in the compartment. I saw there was another person next to the same bogie, same this thing and three or four times in the night I saw the person got up, checked his phone and there was some kind of Facebook and checked the phone, browsed it and then slept off. I was wondering how much a person is addicted to even in night time. Early I wanted to chant because instead of wasting time I couldn't sleep. So I got up around two thirty, three o'clock and I saw in front of the student use their phone during class for non-educational purposes. According to a study published last month in Journal of Media Education, it takes your brain 15 to 25 minutes from back and be attentive and focus on the task which we were before getting distracted. The result is reduced productivity and engagement. This is a problem world over. All have figured out ways and means to check their post, check their feeds and things like that.

#### social media, addiction, Einstein

**Key Quote:** "I fear the day the technology will surpass our human interaction. The world will have a generation of idiots."
**Summary:** The speaker recaps the dangers of social media, including specific examples, and poses the question of whether it is a form of addiction, referencing an Albert Einstein quote about technology surpassing human interaction.

So quick recap of what we discussed so far, we talked about how technology, the social media platforms can be of great use if used judiciously and if we are not careful, they can consume us, they can consume us. Time, they can consume our lives. And we gave examples how it can be dangerous. We have examples of WhatsApp-a-titus. We have selfie-titus. We have pilots who have can lead to dangerous accidents, if they are sleep deprived because they have not slept enough previous night because they were quite engaged with social media. At relationship level it can lead to resentment. People are busy in their own world and hence do not spend time with each other, with their partners. And in companies, social media, if not properly used, can lead to productivity loss. And at a personal level, at a health level, it can lead to binge eating where a person is so busy, the social media, that he is not getting a heed to the body signals that he has eaten enough. He can overeat and can lead to lot of health related challenges, can lead to depression, can lead to poor quality sleep. Now, the question to be asked is social media some kind of an addiction? Just like tea, coffee, anything with caffeine, nicotine or intoxicants, in course of time can be addictive. People can't live without it. They hanker for it. So, is social media some kind of an addiction? This reminds me of a quote of Albert Einstein who said that, I fear the day the technology will surpass our human interaction. This is Einstein saying. The world will have a generation of idiots. One study of Android users. Now, are we moving in that direction?

#### addiction, dopamine, notifications
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4QbIuTyYwCc?t=2033
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "When a smartphone vibrates with a new notification, the brain starts anticipating the momentary happiness that opening WhatsApp, Facebook provides."
**Summary:** The speaker presents statistics on smartphone usage, highlighting the addictive nature of notifications and the dopamine release associated with social media interactions, comparing it to addictions like tea and coffee.

You will be surprised. This is one study done by a company for Android users. Found that people tapped, swiped and clicked their smartphones. I repeat, people tapped, clicked and swiped their smartphones 2617 times a day. 24 hours, it's about 6-7 hours. You are lying in the bed, you are sleeping. It's almost 200 times in an hour. Isn't it a kind of an addiction? 200 times clicking or tapping or swiping in one hour. When a smartphone vibrates with a smartphone, when a smartphone vibrates with a new notification, the brain starts anticipating the momentary happiness that opening WhatsApp, Facebook provides. These tools are designed that you see something, it releases a feel-good factor, some new post, some new information, some latest news. We get pulled by that and if we have made some post, we are curious to know how it is doing, how many likes, how many comments, how many people have viewed it. So we get pulled into it and the brain starts anticipating that feel-good factor, the dopamine. This is how it was originally designed and to a large extent it is serving that purpose. So as a result, unknowingly, unintentionally, we all are, if we are not very careful, getting hooked on, getting addicted. Just like people may start consuming tea, one cup, two cup a day, but slowly the body starts craving. You start getting headache unless you have it. People can't get out of the bed unless they have had a cup of tea or coffee. So it's an intoxicant, it's an addiction.

---
#### detachment, addiction, gradual change
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4QbIuTyYwCc?t=2182
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "What started off as an innocent curiosity, finally, he started hunting and shooting those animals and birds, and he himself became a hunter."
**Summary:** The speaker tells a story of a sage who becomes a hunter due to curiosity with a bow and arrow, and an analogy of a frog in slowly heating water to illustrate how gradual changes can lead to unconscious addiction.

There was once a sage who was meditating in the forest and he was living in a very simple hut. And one day a king, not a king, sorry, a hunter came to that place and said, I am going to the nearby river to take my bath. I am keeping this bow and arrow with you. Please, this is my request to you, please keep it very very careful because if I am in the river taking a bath, quite possible somebody can come and steal it or somebody can come and take it. So I am leaving it in your custody. Keep an eye on it. I know you are busy with your meditation. But I request you to personally take care, keep an eye on this bow and arrow. I will soon return after completing my, taking my bath. Initially the sage was reluctant, but because the hunter requested him many many times, after all it's a question of few hours, he reluctantly agreed. And while the sage was meditating, he kept his eyes half opened, he also kept an eye, because this is what he was requested, was practically pleading, that personally keep an eye, keep a check. He kept his eye once in a while open and checked whether the bow and arrow was safe. Some hours passed by, and now the sage started wondering why the person, the hunter has not returned back after having completed his bath. And in his eagerness to find out where the hunter is, so that he can go and return that bow and arrow to him, he went in the direction of the river in search of that hunter. And he couldn't find the hunter. And this was Sandhya Vandana time, he himself had to take a bath, so he wanted to go to the river, inside the river, but then he cannot leave the bow and arrow, because he was requested by the hunter to take care, because all kinds of animals are there, and someone can steal that bow and arrow. So he actually went into the river with the bow and arrow in his hand. And while he was taking the dip, while he was having his hand upraised, raised with the bow and arrow in his hand, taking his bath, after completing his bath, he came out, and still he couldn't find the hunter. He became little curious, what is this bow and arrow all about? What is this prized possession, which the hunter is so, so attached to, that he practically pleaded, please take care of it. So he started little bit fiddling with that bow and arrow, he actually pulled that string, and while he released it, there was some kind of a sound made, he became little more curious. Then he put one of the arrows, and he tried stretching it, and released it, and he was amazed, that arrow actually travelled considerable distance, before falling to the ground. And since the arrow had the sharp edges, he again tried few times, and this time he thought, why not aim at some object at a distance, and try it out. After all there is no harm. So he tried with, a small little cute flower at a distance, he aimed at that, and pulled the arrow, and you know, tried hitting that flower, and he could do it. He became quite involved in this exercise, he became interested with this bow and arrow, and then he started aiming at some of the fruits, which were dangling from the tree, some of the fruits which were out of his reach, up in the tree, ripened fruits. So he started shooting arrows, trying to hit those fruits, and get them down. So he could hit few arrows, and he could get those fruits, so he became even more involved in that exercise. And then, he saw at a distance, there was some animals, some deer, some rabbits. So, he thought, why not try it out, why not try, instead of hitting a static object, like a flower or a fruit, which is just stationary, why not try hitting an object, which is actually moving, which is far more difficult. So then he started aiming at, some birds, some rabbits, and, you won't believe, soon, what started off as, an innocent curiosity. Finally, he started hunting and shooting, those animals and birds, and he himself became a hunter. Imagine, a sage who was meditating, became curious with the bow and arrow, and started off innocently, just by, you know, trying to hit those arrows, at some static objects, some flower or a fruit, and eventually started killing animals. And this happened very, at a very very unconscious level, it was not a premeditated, effort that yes, he should shoot down those animals, and become a hunter. After all, he was a sage. It is said that, you know, if you take, a frog, and you put him in a beaker, and put that beaker, on top of a flame or a stove, and you start heating that water, 10 degree, 15 degree, 20 degree, 25 degree, the frog, will start becoming, uncomfortable, as the water gets heated up. But it starts, somehow, adjusting to, the new environment, the changes in the environment. That's how we all adapt, to the changes. So he's adapting, somehow adapting, it's becoming even warmer, and he's adapting, adapting, 60 degree, 65 degree, somehow, although he's feeling very groggy, very uneasy, somehow adapting, to that environment, a time came, when the water becomes, close to 80 degree, 90 degree, it becomes intolerable, for him to adapt, and finally, he dies, in that beaker.

Now in Karma Yoga also, I'll repeat this, what is Yoga? Yoga means, to link or to connect. No doubt it's part of, the Ashtanga Yoga process: Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama, Dharana, Dhyana, Samadhi. These are the eight steps, ultimately leading to Samadhi. So what is Yoga? Yoga means, we, that soul, who is eternally connected with the Lord, have forgotten that relationship, have somehow got separated. Although we are eternally connected, we have a relationship with the Lord, we have got disconnected, forgetful of that relationship. To re-establish that union, to re-establish that connection, is called Yoga.

#### relationships, selfishness, pleasure
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sm4p6C6LUig?t=825
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that many relationships in the material world are based on selfishness and the desire for personal pleasure, leading to their eventual breakdown when those needs are no longer met.

That's why, many of the so-called loving relationships end in divorce. I love you, because I get what I want to get. I want to feel important. I want to feel loved. I want to feel a sense of security. When that is missing in that relationship, that relationship becomes redundant. When I am not getting pleasure from that relationship, then why have that relationship? A real love is, where we are completely focused on giving pleasure to the object of love, with no selfish motives.

**Summary:** The speaker identifies Krishna as Agni among the Vasus and Meru among the mountains, and explains the roles of the Trinity (Brahma, Vishnu, and Maheshwara) as Gunavataras, each in charge of a specific Guna (mode of nature).

Of the Vasus, I am fire, Agni and of the mountain, I am Meru. The eleven Rudras and then Krishna is saying, "I am Lord Shiva, Supreme, the most prominent Rudra." Now, we all heard about Trinity, Brahma, Vishnu and Maheshwara. They are also called Gunavataras. Lord Shiva is in charge of the three Gunas, Satva Guna, Raja Guna and Tamo Guna. Lord Shiva is in charge of Tamo Guna. Brahma is in charge of Raja Guna, creation. Creation requires passion. Destruction requires Tamas and maintenance, it is not easy. So Brahma is in charge of creation, Rajas. Lord Shiva is in charge of Tamas, annihilation and maintenance or Satva Guna in charge is Lord Vishnu. We have heard about the Trinity.

#### Lord Shiva, Vaishnava, Tamo Guna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sm4p6C6LUig?t=1210
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker addresses potential concerns from devotees of Shiva, clarifying that Lord Shiva is a great Vaishnava and that his association with Tamo Guna is for the purpose of delivering those living entities predominantly under its influence.

Now what does that mean? Some of us who are devotees of Shiva might feel, this is not right. Why they are saying Lord Shiva is in charge? Lord Shiva is not in ignorance. In fact, he is one of the greatest Vaishnavas, Vaishnava Naam Yatha Shambhu. If we have to find a most exalted Vaishnava, think of Shambhu. So now, why is he in charge of? As we know, that all of us, forgetful of Lord, are in different stages of our spiritual evolution. Some of us are predominantly in Tamo Guna, some are predominantly in Raja Guna and some are predominantly in Satva Guna. And at different times of the day, different times in our lives, we are influenced by mixture of different modes. Now, those living entities who are predominantly under the influence of Tamo Guna, the Lord has to deliver them also.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### PRACTICAL DETACHMENT | HG Amitasana Das | GITA LIFE Live
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### detachment, misery, religion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "All of us are attached to people, to situations, to circumstances, to things and many of us have realized that there is a linkage to our attachments and the misery which we encounter in our lives."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces the topic of practical detachment, linking attachments to misery and stating the goal of understanding and applying detachment in our lives.

Krishna, a warm welcome to all of you for this Gita live session. Today's topic is practical detachment. All of us are attached to people, to situations, to circumstances, to things, and many of us have realized that there is a linkage to our attachments and the misery which we encounter in our lives. In fact, Lord Buddha said the root cause of all our misery is attachments. So therefore, in every religion, some talk, some understanding about rules, regulations for detaching ourselves from things, from people, from situations. So today we are going to discuss this in detail based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada, teachings of Lord Shri Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita. So before we dive into this discussion, let's start with prayers seeking the blessings of Prabhupada, Lord Shri Krishna, Lord Chaitanya Prabhu, so that we can understand this topic. Not only understand, we can get some insights, how we can practically apply this in our lives and not remain too attached to things, to people, to situations, to circumstances.

---
#### detachment, renunciation, scripture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=307
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Before we even pursue, at least we should have clarity, what is real detachment?"
**Summary:** The speaker defines real detachment and renunciation according to scriptures, contrasting it with common misconceptions about outward appearances and lifestyles.

So, the first principle, the first aspect about practical detachment which we should all thoroughly and clearly understand is what is real detachment? Before we even pursue, at least we should have clarity, what is real detachment? What is real renunciation? What the scriptures are trying, are telling us to pursue? Many people have an understanding, detachment means you should not be wearing good clothes, you should not be living in a good, comfortable house. A person who is renounced is supposed to be in some forest or in some mountains. Very, very matted hairs, tattered clothes, generally we have about a renounced or a detached person. But there is some lacuna, some mistake in this understanding.

---
#### detachment, example, kings
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=383
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** example
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is an example how a person may be living in a palatial house but deep within in his heart, he is not attached."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Raj Rishis and Parikshit Maharaj to illustrate that real detachment is an internal state, not dependent on external circumstances.

We have examples of kings who were also called kings. Raj Rishis, Raja Rishis, Raja Rishis means they were kings, they were living in palaces amidst opulence, married, with lot of so-called guards, lot of comforts, lot of luxuries around them but they were simultaneously called Rishis, Rishis at heart. Example, Parikshit Maharaj, he was the emperor of the whole world, grandson of Arjuna, son of Abhimanyu. He was emperor of the whole world but when he was cursed that he will leave the body or die in seven days, although he was living in a palatial comforts, but he renounced everything in moments notice and went to the bank of the river. And for next seven days heard the Shrima Bhagavatam. This is an example how a person may be living in a palatial house but deep within in his heart, he is not attached. It's there so he is so-called enjoying that comfort but knowing very well that this is not mine.

---
#### detachment, false renunciation, family
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=480
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So today we will be discussing about how to pursue, how to achieve this real detachment and not get deluded or bewildered with false renunciation."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the possibility of true detachment while living within family and fulfilling worldly duties, contrasting it with false renunciation, and uses the analogy of a lotus flower to illustrate detachment from surroundings.

The example is given just like coconut. When it ripens that shell, within that the coconut gets detached, separated out. So today we will be discussing about how to pursue, how to achieve this real detachment and not get deluded or bewildered with false renunciation. And this true detachment is very much possible even while we are living amidst family, doing our worldly duties, executing the responsibilities, it is necessary to be detached. And that is what the Lord is asking us to strive for in the Bhagavad Gita. He gives an example of lotus, although it's living, although it is growing in a dirty pond, but it is detached from the surroundings, it's untouched by the dirt around it.

---
#### detachment, integrity, temptation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=549
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Let's say a person is walking down the road and he sees a wallet, purse fallen on the road, lying on the road."
**Summary:** The speaker presents an analogy of finding a wallet on the road to illustrate different approaches to detachment and integrity, outlining three possible options.

So I'll share with you an example to illustrate what is real detachment. Let's say a person is walking down the road and he sees a wallet, purse fallen on the road, lying on the road. And you can also see these 2000 rupee notes coming out from that purse and it's quite thick, it's quite tempting and alluring even for a person who is of high integrity. Broadly speaking, a person can take one of the three steps or three options. The first option is the person sees left, right, up, down, ensuring that nobody is watching, he can pick that wallet and walk away. That's option one. The second option is a person after seeing the wallet, there is a momentary temptation, allurement. Money is sweeter than gold. Money is sweeter than honey. And right there it's available and nobody is watching. But then a person has good sense, he has values. He says it's not mine, leaves it and walks away. It's not mine. That's option number two. Although there are many combinations of these three options, broadly speaking, there are three options.

---
#### detachment, integrity, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=658
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The best option is where a person understands that it does not belong to me, but it belongs to someone else and strives to find."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the third and best option in the wallet analogy: striving to return the wallet to its owner, highlighting compassion and understanding of another's potential misery.

The first option is the person gets allured, yes, quite valuable, 2000 rupee notes, bundle of them, lying on the road. He understands using his intellect, discerns that it is not mine, nirmama, but he also understands that there is something. There is someone who has lost this wallet. It belongs to someone. One understanding, it doesn't belong to me. It's better than thinking that, okay, I can take it and I can enjoy. But the best understanding is, the best option is where a person understands that it does not belong to me, but it belongs to someone else and strives to find. Find to whom it belongs and goes and gives it back.

---
#### karma, gyana, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=754
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That mentality is called a karmi."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the concepts of 'karmi' and 'gyani' in relation to the wallet analogy, contrasting the karmi's attachment to results with the gyani's detachment and renunciation.

So the first option, the people in, who try to enjoy all the facilities, all the comforts, thinking that it belongs to me. I deserve it. I have worked hard. It's mine. Aham ameti. I have worked hard. I have built this bungalow. I have bought this car. It belongs to me and I have right to enjoy. That mentality is called a karmi. Karmi means who works hard and wants to enjoy the fruits of the result, thinking that this results belong to me. It's because of me. I have worked hard. It's because of my ability. So that's called karmi. And there are some gyanis who understand that this is not mine, nirmama, it's not mine, but I have nothing to do with it. Just like the option number two, just walks away, leaving it aside. I am not the body, Brahma Satya Jagat Mithya, all this Jagat Mithya, it's false, I have nothing to do with it. It's the second option. There are spiritualists who pursue this understanding that this Jagat is Mithya. Money is material. It's Maya. I have nothing to do with money and hence renounce. I have nothing to do with comforts, this luxuries. I just renounce. I am detached from it and to practice the detachment, they stay away from it, they shun it.

---
#### detachment, devotion, abilities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=867
**Verse References:** BG 5.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Knowing that I have been blessed with these abilities, it is not mine, it is gifted to me and the results of my activities also truly belong to the person who has been blessed with these abilities."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the third option, where one recognizes their abilities and possessions as gifts from the Lord and offers the results of their activities for His pleasure, acknowledging Him as the true proprietor.

Option number three, we may be blessed with certain abilities, certain skills. We may be blessed with comfortable house, nice family members. We realize that I have been blessed with these luxuries, these possessions. It does not belong to me. It belongs to the Lord, the true proprietor, Bhokta Ram Yagna Tapasam, Bhokta, he is the real enjoyer. I am just a Shetragna, a knower of the field of activities. I am that Atma. I am not this body, all the skills, the abilities which I have, which this body, I am able to speak well, I am able to sing well, I am able to see clearly, these all are abilities given to me. Because of those abilities, I am able to interact in this world, because of some intelligence, I am able to fetch a good job. Knowing that I have been blessed with these abilities, it is not mine, it is gifted to me and the results of my activities also truly belong to the person who has been blessed with these abilities. Because of certain abilities, somebody is earning a lakh of rupees, because of lack of some abilities, somebody is earning 5000 rupees. So a person understands that these abilities, this body, these skills, the intelligence, the mind, analytical faculties are coming from the Lord. And hence offers the result of all his activities for the pleasure and satisfaction of the Lord. Knowing that it truly belongs to him.

He offers and for his, for those accepts as Prasadam, leads a regulated lifestyle, without being attached. So the real detachment of consciousness, that awareness, nirmama, it is not mine. The begging bowl, it is my bowl. A person in contrast can be living in a palatial house, have a nice beautiful altar with an understanding that this house belongs to the Lord. I am just a caretaker. I am a servant of the Lord and my duty is to please the Lord, to serve the Lord. I go out to work so that I can earn money and serve the Lord nicely. The Lord is the center of my activities, the center of my house. I saw this practical example I mentioned in our previous session also. We had gone to somebody's house, a very devoted lady, very Krishna conscious person and in the hospital where they operate, where they function, one full floor is dedicated to the Lord. They call it Mother's Temple of the Lord and in the house also as you enter one room the Lord, the Archa Vigraha, the deity of the Lord is in that room. It is a very good example of how we can make the Lord the center of our activities. And when one leaves with that consciousness that is called Nirmama and that is real detachment, and that is what we should strive for.

#### false renunciation, mind, Markat Vairagya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=1243
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The first one is called Markat Vairagya. Markat means monkey."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the first type of false renunciation, Markat Vairagya (monkey renunciation), and illustrates it with a story of monkeys who planned to fast but ended up eating before the fast even began due to the mind's whims.

I will share with you four kinds of false renunciation. The first one is although there are many other types also but four of them which I'm sure you all can relate with. The first one is called Markat Vairagya. Markat means monkey. This reminds me of a story. There were group of monkeys who decided that they will fast the whole day. Somehow they got a good sense of it that let us fast. So, all in a group they came together and decided we will fast tomorrow. Then, one of them went and told this group of monkeys, tomorrow when we will be fasting who knows we will be very tired, very weak, at least let us go and sit near the tree with the fruits are here. At least we may not have the strength to climb the tree, so let's sit near the tree. And they all went and sat near the tree, laden with fruits. And then one of them suggested, what you have said is so correct, so true. What if tomorrow when we are fasting, we may not even have the strength What about climbing the tree? It also requires lot of strength, lot of energy. So all of them decided they all climbed the tree and sat, so that they can fast tomorrow, and because they will not have enough energy, they can make arrangements in advance.

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We may not eat it, but we can keep it along with us."
**Summary:** Continuing the story of the monkeys, the speaker describes how they progressively made arrangements to ensure they could eat easily, ultimately breaking their fast before it even started, illustrating how the uncontrolled mind can sabotage good intentions.

Another one, Another one suggested that tomorrow when we are fasting, you know, we may not have energy to pluck the fruits from the tree. Why don't we pluck it and keep it along with us? We may not eat it, but we can keep it along with us. Who knows, we may not have the energy to even pluck the fruits from the tree. So they all plucked the fruits and kept it along with us then. And then another wise monkey said, we have plucked the fruits, why not, what if we don't have energy even to chew the fruits tomorrow, why not we just chew the fruits and keep it in the mouth. We may not ingest it, we may not take it in, but we can chew it and keep it in case. In case, the time the fast gets over, we can take it in. And they were deliberating, the day passed by and before they could even fast, they had already taken the fruits and the fast was broken. So many times Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita says the mind, if it is uncontrolled, it's a worst enemy.

#### false renunciation, mind, Markat Vairagya, Broken Cart Renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=1515
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So the first false renunciation is Markat Vairagya where we give lot of freedom to the whims of the mind, the suggestions of the mind and we fall prey to them."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes Markat Vairagya and introduces the second type of false renunciation, Broken Cart Renunciation, illustrating it with a story of a person who started sleeping on the floor because his cot broke and then philosophized about detachment.

So the first false renunciation is Markat Vairagya where we give lot of freedom to the whims of the mind, the suggestions of the mind and we fall prey to them. The second kind of renunciation or detachment is Broken Caught Renunciation. This is a nice name on some incidences, anecdotes just to make us easy to understand. What is Broken Caught Renunciation? There was a person who had a nice khat, khat means a cot, in Hindi it is called khat. He got a good nice cot in dowry and over the years it became quite old and a time came when one of the legs of the cot broke. And he tried his best to put the leg together, tie it out with some ropes. Could not do it, because the cot was in very bad shape and he realised that since the cot cannot be repaired and he didn't have any money to buy a new cot, he started sleeping on the floor.

---
#### false renunciation, Broken Cart Renunciation, circumstances
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=1604
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is not coming from within, it's not coming out of real understanding."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Broken Cart Renunciation is circumstantial detachment, not arising from genuine understanding, and gives the example of sadhus begging due to lack of options.

So he observed that he is sleeping on the floor, they asked him, why are you sleeping on the floor? So he started philosophizing and preaching to them, don't you know sleeping on cot, we should give up all these comforts and luxuries. Human life is temporary. Human life is meant for detachment. He started preaching to them. Why should we be attached to the cot? Life is meant to cultivate detachment and renunciation. So he started preaching and philosophizing, showing off his detachment to his friends. I am not attached, but the root cause of detachment is not real detachment. It is because he does not, he cannot afford a cot. So a person may be living in an impoverished condition, may be very detached, but quite possible, it is circumstantial. It is not coming from within, it's not coming out of real understanding. It is out of some compulsion, some circumstantial circumstance. And that renunciation is called broken understanding. And that renunciation is called broken understanding. You might have many sadhus in holy places begging, not necessarily because of their church, but because they had no other option, but to beg and to live.

#### false renunciation, Shamshaan Vairagya, futility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=1746
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Once he comes out home, again all that detachment surfaces up, manifest in the life. It is very short lived and that renunciation, that Vairagya is called, Shamshaan Vairagya."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Shamshaan Vairagya, describing the feeling of detachment and futility experienced at a crematorium, which quickly disappears once one returns home and resumes their usual activities.

Sometimes we all have experienced some near and dear one or other place we have gone to crematorium for someone, the cremation ceremony. So you would have seen you know on the walls Ram Naam Satya Hai and people also chant this Ram Naam Satya Hai and when you go to the crematorium for a moment you feel quite blessed. You feel detached. You feel the futility, the uselessness of pursuing material things which will go in no time. The person who is lying dead, whole life he struggled, worked so hard, he had so much of stress, went through so much of anxiety, now he is lying there. It's all become null and void. All his struggles, all his pains, all his anxieties, he struggled for money, he struggled for loans, he struggled for so many things, just like that. It's all become null and void. He had so much of property, so much of assets, it's all left behind. So seeing that a person can experience futility, what is the use? They feel that yes Ram Naam Satya Hai but that renunciation, that detachment, that detachment, does not last. A person, so long as he is in the crematorium, he experiences that detachment. Once he comes out home, again all that detachment surfaces up, manifest in the life. It is very short lived and that renunciation, that Vairagya is called, Shamshaan Vairagya.

They had started in the second year smoking. Generally in the first year there is some bit of regulation, some bit of control. But by the time many of them come to the second year, smoking and drinking becomes quite common. So one of them had started smoking. He started off, he was there for a few days, maybe just in some parties or friends are forcing him, why not try one. So he starts on it and then around 7 o'clock in the evening or something Fehler will happen. He starts smoking again. He will come when he does not like it. So he will eat everyone. #### false renunciation, guilt, addiction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BvZpRjl_uWs?t=1925
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Many times I have seen him raising his hand, this is the last cigarette of my life."
**Summary:** Continuing the story, the speaker describes how his roommate became addicted to smoking, often running out of money and resorting to cheaper alternatives, while repeatedly declaring out of guilt that each cigarette was his last.

So he started off and he didn't realize that very soon he started becoming addicted. It didn't dawn on him, he didn't realize and there was a time when he was smoking about 10 to 12 cigarettes a day. He was my roommate and because there was, in hostel you have always limited money coming in from parents a little cautious, he is all alone, he can get spoiled. So by 15th, by 20th of every month you are broke, all the money what you have received is all over. And this friend of mine won't believe towards the end of the month he would not have money to even buy cigarettes and he even resorted to smoking BDs. BD is a, some of you may be aware, it is the cheaper version of cigarette. And every day I have seen him many, many times, every time he would smoke there was an element of guilt feeling. And I have seen him n number of times, at least 20 number of times saying that this is the last cigarette of my life. Very emphatically. Very emphatically. Very forcefully out of that guilt feeling, out of that feeling of getting trapped, that feeling that I am getting addicted, out of frustration, why am I getting into this habit. I started off as a social kind of fad but I am getting hooked out of that guilt feeling. Many times I have seen him raising his hand, this is the last cigarette of my life.

So, let's chant this verse together. This is the 20th verse from the 8th chapter. #### spiritual world, eternal, Vaikuntha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cI9M9k-V0ic?t=85
**Verse References:** BG 8.20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The speaker explains that beyond the temporary material world, there is an eternal spiritual world with Vaikuntha planets, including Goloka Vrindavan, known by different names in various religions.

We see everything in this world is temporary, including our bodies. So, then Krishna is saying, parastasmatubhavinu, beyond this material world, beyond this material creation, there is another world. And which is that world? It is the spiritual world, which has many, many Vaikuntha planets and the topmost of which is Goloka Vrindavan. So, elaborate description of this spiritual planet, the spiritual world is given in our scriptures. In fact, all the scriptures of the world in different religions, they call it by different names. Somebody call it heaven, jannat, you know, so many other names are there, which are synonyms in that sense. So, Krishna is saying, beyond this material world, there is another world. So, Krishna is saying, beyond this material world, beyond this material world, beyond this material world, there is another world, material world which is vyakta and avyakta, there is another world, and that world, the nature of that world is sanatana.

#### eternal nature, struggle, material body
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cI9M9k-V0ic?t=155
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "We all are looking for permanence because we are part of that world. As spirit soul we are eternal and that world is eternal."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the eternal nature of the spiritual world (sanatana) and the eternal nature of the spirit soul, contrasting it with the temporary material body and world, leading to a struggle like a fish out of water.

Sanatana means eternal, does not cease to be. Yasa sarve bhuteshu and those who go there, yet there is another nature which is eternal and is transcendental to this manifested and unmanifested matter. It is supreme and is never annihilated. When all in this world is annihilated, that part remains as it is. We all are looking for permanence because we are part of that world. As spirit soul we are eternal and that world is eternal. And we are put in a material body which is temporary in a world which is temporary. And hence there is a struggle. It is like a fish out of its natural habitat which is water, when put on land, irrespective of how much comfort we give to the fish, it will wriggle out. Because that is not its natural habitat. Fish out of water. You put the fish back in water, it is back in its natural state. So we right now are inhabiting a material body.

**Summary:** The speaker describes the material body's cycle of birth, death, disease, and decay, leading to the experience of death and the beginning of a new cycle over many lifetimes.

We are living in a material body. We are living in a material body. We are living in a material body. And this material body is going through transformation, janma, mrityu, jarav, yadi. It comes into being one day, we take birth and then much against our wish we encounter different kinds of diseases. And then it decays, deteriorates with time and then one fine day we die. Do we die? Yes? No? No? The body dies. The body dies. But we have identified ourselves with the body, we experience death. And then again the next cycle begins. And this is going on over many many lifetimes.

#### analogy, first aid, solution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cI9M9k-V0ic?t=361
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Just giving first aid is not the solution."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a person walking barefoot on a thorny hill to illustrate that providing temporary relief (first aid) is not a sustainable solution; a better path or protection (slippers) is needed.

Let me give you a very good example. Let's say a person is walking up the hill and that hill has lot of thorns, lot of pebbles, lot of small pieces of stones with sharp edges. And the person is walking barefoot. Now as soon as he is climbing the hill, suddenly a thorn pricks his leg and he feels lot of pain and blood starts oozing out. Now you are by his side, what will you do? You will help him with some first aid, remove the thorn. And then wish him good luck, wish him good health, get well soon and then again the person starts walking up the hill. Because that hill has lot of thorns. And he is walking barefoot and there are lot of sharp stones there. Again he will get pricked with a thorn. And again you rush him to give a first aid. Should we give him first aid, yes or no? Apart from giving first aid, what should we do? Because this will go on. How long you can keep giving first aid? Next moment again he will get pricked with a thorn. So while you give him a first aid, you should also try to find is there another way which is free of thorns, another path which he can tread which is free of thorns where he can go to the destination or you can give him slippers. Just giving first aid is not the solution.

**Summary:** The speaker explains that genuine spirituality involves practicing devotional service (chanting, hearing, etc.) in the material world to qualify for service in the spiritual world, leading to liberation.

And genuine spirituality involves we try practicing that while we are in this material world. So all the devotional services chanting, hearing, paada sevanam, archanam, vandanam, dasyam, sakhyam, atma nivedanam, all this process of devotional service is like qualifying us to do what we are going to do it in the spiritual world. So for a devotee who is engaged, sent person throughout the day in the service of the Lord, in that sense it is not different whether he is in the material world or in the spiritual world. See already he is liberated. Because what he is going to do in the spiritual world he is doing it here.

So Krishna is saying while you are in this material world do that. And at the end of the life you will get qualified to go back to the spiritual world. And what is that dhama? It's chintamani dhama. So we are having some hope. If I achieve this, if I get this, if I have this I will be happy. There we are completely satisfied. Completely contented. And the trees, their example is given in Brahma Samhita by Lord Brahma in our authorized Vedic literatures. The trees are like desired trees. And the cows are like surabhi cows. Surabhi cows means they can give unlimited profuse supply of milk. Cow. And everything is full of spiritual bliss. A vivid description of this is given in the Brahma Samhita. Prayers offered by Lord Brahma to Lord Krishna.

So we will stop here. Anyone has any question, anything like to ask and then we move on to the discussion on chanting. Any questions on what we discussed? Morning we get up and then we have our own things to catch up with our responsibilities, this thing and we have forgotten the overall picture. What is that overall picture? We are that spirit soul. That soul is eternal. This life is a small phase in our eternal existence. It's a small phase. And especially because we have got human birth in this life, it is a very very important responsibility that in this life because of our developed intelligence we can inquire into Who am I? What is the purpose of my life? What happens to me after death? Why am I suffering? Why am I suffering? If we don't ask these fundamental questions as scriptures say we are not doing justice to the human birth.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Prioritizing Character Above Reputation | Sri Amitasana Dasa | SB 3.13.9 | 23.05.2024
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### father-son relationship, guidance, obedience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pu4CVNQHXyc?t=50
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Both the father and the son are well qualified and their example should be followed by all humankind."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the ideal father-son relationship as exemplified by Brahma and Manu, emphasizing the importance of the son's willingness to be guided and the father's eagerness to instruct.

You have without reservation surrendered yourself unto me with your heart for my instructions. The relationship between the father and the son is always sublime. The father is naturally disposed with good will towards the son, and he is always ready to help the son in his progressing life. But in spite of the father's good will, the son is sometimes misguided because of his misuse of personal independence. Every living entity, however small or big he may be, has a choice of independence. If the son is unreservedly willing to be guided by the father, the father is ten times more eager to instruct and guide him by all means. The father and son relationship as exhibited here in the dealings of Brahma and Manu is excellent. Both the father and the son are well qualified and their example should be followed by all humankind. Manu, the son, unreservedly asked the father, Brahma, to instruct him, and the father, who was full of Vedic wisdom, was very glad to instruct. The example of the father of mankind may be rigidly followed by mankind, and that will advance the cause of the relationship of fathers and sons. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Parpat.

---
#### obedience, spiritual master, instruction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/pu4CVNQHXyc?t=127
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This conversation is always going on between spiritual master and disciple, between the superior and the subordinate."
**Summary:** This segment highlights Manu's request for guidance from Brahma and connects it to the relationship between a disciple and a spiritual master, emphasizing the importance of receiving and executing instructions.

In the previous verse, we saw how Manu is requesting Brahma, who is his father, 'O worshipable one, please give me your direction for execution of duty within our working capacity, so that we can follow it for fame in this life and progress in the next.' So very obediently, Manu is receiving instruction is one thing. Intense desire to execute the instructions is another thing. So here he is also saying, 'For the execution of duty within our working capacity, so that we can follow it.' There are two components. One is receiving instruction. Prabhupada has given so many instructions. Prabhupada's spiritual master, Bhaktisiddhanta Saro, gave instruction to him, and he was very very eager to fulfill that instruction. So in this verse, in response to what Manu said, Brahmajai is saying, 'My dear son, O Lord of the world, I am very pleased with you.' Now here, there is a conversation happening between Manu and Brahma, but actually, in the spiritual plane, in the spiritual life, this conversation is always going on between spiritual master and disciple, between the superior and the subordinate.

Spiritual life means austerity, tapo divyam, and austerity means voluntarily accepting some inconvenience for a higher cause. There may be inconvenience, but we accept it for a higher cause. Relationship between the father and the son is always sublime. The father is naturally disposed with good will towards the son, and he is always ready to help the son in his progress in life. Every living entity, however big or small he may be, has a choice of independence. If the son is unreservedly willing to be guided by the father, the father is ten times more eager to instruct and guide him by all means. **Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

Shastras, scriptures, is the real father. All shastras instruct that one should end his material way of life. If one does not know the purpose of his father's order, the shastras, he is ignorant. A parent who does not know how to save his son has no value because such father and mother may be had in any form of life, even among the cats, dogs and so on. Only a father and mother who can elevate their son to the spiritual platform are bona fide parents.

#### moral principles, respect, spiritual master

**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of respecting superiors, elders, and spiritual masters, using the example of Grahaspati's mistreatment by Indra and the demigods to illustrate the dangers of familiarity breeding contempt.

But simultaneously know that, even in the laws, there are gradations. And that maturity comes when it comes over a period of time. Otherwise, sometimes we can be fanatics. By and large, we follow the moral principles. We don't tell lies. We don't cheat. We obey our elders. And yes, we also have examples of higher principles. And if situation arises, we can use those principles. Now what is the general principle? The thing is, our superiors, elders, spiritual master have to be respected. When Grahaspati entered the assembly of the demigods, they, this I have shared in previous earlier class also, they and their king Indra took them for granted. Since he came every day, they thought they did not have to show him special respect. As it is said, familiarity breeds contempt. Being very much displeased, Grahaspati immediately left Indra's palace. Thus all the demigods headed by Indra became offenders at the lotus feet of Grahaspati. And Lord Brahma, being aware of this, condemned their neglect.

But there is a danger also. Can anyone tell what's the danger? What is the danger? Vaishnav Pradhan. Vaishnav Pradhan. And what is the thing here says? Familiarity breeds content. Brahaspati came. They were regularly coming. So, Indra and other demigods came. Why we have to respect them? Next, Prabhupada says, because of Brahma's head, because of your misbehaviour towards Brahaspati, you have been defeated by the demons. Now, you see, the same principle applies. ## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Purpose of Renunciation | Bhagavad Gita 18.01 - 18.03 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai.
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### renunciation, Bhagavad Gita, summarization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This 18th chapter is the summarization of what Krishna has so far instructed in the first 17 chapters."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with an introduction to the 18th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, focusing on the purpose of renunciation and its significance as a summary of Krishna's teachings.

Okay, what's the topic today? The purpose of renunciation. So we are going to start with the 18th chapter. We'll be covering three verses today based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada. Now, broadly speaking, Gita can be divided into three parts: the first six chapters, the second six chapters, and the last six chapters. So total, how many chapters are there? 18 chapters.

---
#### yoga, bhakti, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=120
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "That process which will link us to the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the structure of the Bhagavad Gita, dividing it into three sections, and explains how Krishna discusses various yogas, ultimately emphasizing Bhakti Yoga as the most direct path.

So first six chapters ends with Krishna talks about Karma Yoga, Krishna talks about Jnana Yoga, Krishna talks about Ashtanga Yoga, and then Krishna also talks about Bhakti Yoga. By the way, what is yoga? That process which will link us to the Supreme Lord. In other words, the end objective is to reach Delhi from Mumbai. So Krishna is saying, you can go via Ahmedabad, you can go via Kanpur. You can go by flight, you can go by train. That's what Krishna is saying in all the chapters because some people cannot afford flight, and then if you have to tell different options to people, then he will say the best option is considered to be compete.

---
#### bhakti, devotion, yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=241
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Of all the yogis, one who abides in me, in other words, bhakti yogi, who is always thinking of the Lord, worshipping him with faith and conviction, he is the highest."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Krishna's preference for Bhakti Yoga, highlighting that the devotee who constantly thinks of and worships the Lord with faith is considered the highest yogi.

So yogi naam api sarvesham, of all the yogis, Krishna says, who is the most dear to me? Yoga naam abhi sarvesham api sarvesham madh gatar antaratmana. One who always thinks of me. Shraddhavan bhajate yo mam. With Shraddha, with faith, with conviction, bhajate yo mam, who worships me. See, even we don't have to be very big pandits. These words are very simple. Yogi naam api, api means all. Sarvesham, of all the yogis, madh gatar antaratmana, who abides, who is thinking of me. Madh gatar antaratmana, antarat means in his mind, in his consciousness. Madh, me, is thinking of me. Shraddhavan bhajate yo mam, with Shraddha, with faith, one who worships me. Same tamo, that is my mandate, that is my opinion, that is my conviction, that is my belief. So to say, final judgment, sarvesham, he is the highest. Tamo mataha, in my opinion, in my understanding, he is the highest. Now Arjuna is a kshatriya. He is a family man. He is not a sanyasi, he is not a brahmachari. He is not in the renounced order of life. But Krishna says, become a yogi. So yoginam api sarvesham, the first six chapters end with that. And the next six chapters, having concluded, talked about different yogas, and then concluding the six chapters by saying, of all the yogis, one who abides in me, in other words, bhakti yogi, who is always thinking of the Lord, worshipping him with faith and conviction, he is the highest.

---
#### devotional service, bhakti, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=382
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To engage in devotional service is success in life, is the purpose, is the, so to say, objective."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that devotional service is the ultimate purpose of life, clarifying that bhakti is not mere sentimentality but active engagement in serving the Lord.

The next six chapters, Krishna talks about devotionalism. He is the highest. He is here for devotional service. Shri Lama Prabhupa did not translate bhakti as the sentimental stuff. Sometimes when we talk about bhakti, the understanding we get is, it's more of jnana means, rigorous study of Veda s, pandithya, tarka good understanding of Sanskrit. Bhakti means, you know, simple women, devotees, some old women in the house, they do bhakti, sentimental stuff. So, Srila Prabhupada translates bhakti as not some sentimental worship of the Lord, as devotional service. So, next 6th chapter, that is chapter number 7 to 12, is focused on what is pure devotion, and the nature, what is the nature of pure devotion, and how do we engage in that kind of an activity, devotional service of the Lord. So, next 6th chapter, still 12, it is about devotional service, and the last 6th chapter is about knowledge. Krishna talks about knowledge in goodness, passion, ignorance, and then renunciation. Again, Krishna talks about renunciation in goodness, passion, ignorance, talks about three modes of material nature, and then concludes again with devotional service. So, 18th chapter, we'll, 18th chapter, Krishna says, sarva dharman paritajya mam ekam sharanam raja. And then, he also says, after having spoken the entire Gita, in the 18th chapter, Krishna says, now, I am going to tell you, I am going to tell you my highest instruction, sarva guhyatam bhuya, shunu me paramam vachaha. Paramam vachaha means, my highest instruction. And then, Krishna says, manmana, think of me. Bhav mat bhakto, become my devotee. So, devotional service, the 18th chapter, you will see that how devotional service is the ultimate purpose of life. To engage in devotional service is success in life, is the purpose, is the, so to say, objective.

#### success, human life, consciousness

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the definition of success in human life, emphasizing the importance of remembering the Lord at the time of death as the ultimate test of a successful life.

I have shared this earlier in one of the class. When can we say, when can we say that we have led our life successfully? We achieve success in human form of life. Again, I know all of us have different perspectives of life. All of us have different understanding of what is success. Success definition might vary from person to person. For a businessman, success might be to earn lot of money, to grow in business. For a student, maybe getting into IIT. But according to the Lord, what is success? Can anyone tell me? What is success? When can we say the person has led his life, human form of life? When can we say that we have led our life successfully? We achieve success in human form of life successfully. We have achieved success. Can anyone help me with an answer? Realizing the human form of life. Realizing the human form of life. When we continue to study and go to schools and colleges, we are taking exams at our sleeves. Yes or no? In whatever I studied or those that I have understood through exam, tested, and the results of exam to a large extent shows the person's understanding. To a large extent, although these days exams you can copy, you can use some tricks and things like that. When can we say that human life we have led successfully? Kya aisa exam hoga jo hum log pass kare, to hum kya sakte hain ke hum log jeevan mein successful ho gaye hain? What is that? Anta, Narayana, Smriti. That is the final test. Anta, Anta means end, Narayana, Smriti.

#### death, consciousness, attachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=673
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those thoughts will predominate our consciousness at the point of death."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the thoughts and attachments we cultivate throughout life will influence our consciousness at the time of death, making it crucial to develop love and remembrance for the Lord.

Can we leave this body thinking and our consciousness absorbed in the Lord? It may be a moment's thing but if we have to succeed in that moment, then the whole life has to be engaged in a particular way so that during that time you will remember the Lord. Like for example now there are many people who are studying for IITs. Finally what they write in that 3 hours, 6 hours, 2 years, 3 years, people go to Kota and 5 years, 8 years they prepare for exam. Finally what they do in that 3 hours makes all the difference. A person may study quite well but 3 hours ke exam mein somehow he is not in good shape, in his mind or whatever and he doesn't do well. Nobody is going to see how many hours you studied. Now somebody may say, achha, is that so? Then I will remember. It's not easy. Why it's not easy? What we are attached to. What we have been thinking about. What has sucked our consciousness throughout our life. Those thoughts will predominate our consciousness at the point of death. If we are very attached to our family, our property, money, all these things and we have not cultivated attachment and love and remembrance for the Lord, that is far away. Will not be able to remember.

#### success, material desire, spiritual desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=787
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "To give up material desires and to cultivate spiritual desires."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts worldly definitions of success with the scriptural view, emphasizing that true success lies in remembering the Lord at death and that renunciation involves detaching from material attachments and cultivating spiritual desires.

So Shastra is saying, knowing the purpose of human. Now again I said, all of us have a different understanding of what is success. Somebody may say to be a good human being. Somebody may say to live a life that even though you may die, your acts don't die. People remember you for your acts. See the people like Mahatma Gandhi. They fought for the country. They were so selfless in their actions. All those definitions are there of success. Sacrifice their life for humanity. Mother Teresa. But Acharya's self-realized souls and Krishna himself is saying one who can think of me while living this body. What happens when a person thinks of the Lord while living the body, what happens to him? He goes to the Spiritual world. He doesn't come back to this material world again having a material body. Our material existence is because of fulfilling our material desires, we have got a material body. How many material bodies we can get? 84 lakh species. Atma can get that many types of bodies to facilitate fulfilment of so many permutations and combinations of desires. So long as we have a tinge of material desire, we will get a material body. The Lord will give us a material body through material nature to facilitate fulfilment of those material desires. So Prabhupada also says that this human form of life is an opportunity for us to purify our consciousness. What is renunciation? It is to detach from all the material so-called attachments and develop your attachment to the Lord. To give up is only half of the equation. To give up material desires is only half. Sometimes people think desire is the root cause, so give up desires. That is only half of the solution. But the full solution is to give up material desires and to cultivate spiritual desires. To give up material attachment and to develop attachment to the Lord.

#### action, offering, purification
