**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All your actions should be done for the satisfaction and pleasure of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that all actions should be performed as an offering to the Lord, leading to purification and attachment to Him, and introduces the topic of Arjuna's question about renunciation.

So that's why in the 17th chapter, last chapter we covered Om Tat Sat. Krishna says all activities, yad karosi, yad asnasi, yad juhosi, yad dadasi, whatever you do, yad karosi, yad dadasi, whatever you give away, Kurushwar, you do it, mad arpanam, as an offering to me. Om Tat Sat. All your actions should be done for the satisfaction and pleasure of the Lord. Because slowly that will lead to purification. Slowly that will lead to attachment to the Lord. Now why do we take action? I want the results. And what are the results? We enjoy. And what happens in that? We get trapped. So Arjuna is now asking, what is renunciation? What is the purpose of renunciation? Shri Prabhupada writes, in the introduction of Gita, that while I am writing this Bhagavad Gita commentary, already in the market, there are 650 editions of the Gita. Written by different authors, different philosophers, scholars, politicians, Dr. Radhakrishna, Mahatma Gandhi. So then Prabhupada writes, why another edition of Gita? And when we ask people also, every one of us, I am sure, is there anyone who does not have a copy of Gita in their house? Anyone here? Mostly, no. Everyone will have.

#### essence, meaning, message
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=1084
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The true essence, the true meaning, the true message which Krishna is trying to give to Arjuna, is completely missed out."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a sealed honey bottle to illustrate how many people read the Gita without truly understanding its essence, emphasizing that the true message is often missed.

But, while many people have commented and written on the Gita, the true essence, the true meaning, the true message which Krishna is trying to give to Arjuna, is completely missed out. Sometimes people think, I am reading. I have Gita in my house, I am reading. Yes, you might be reading. Prabhupada gives example of, if there is a honey bottle, and somehow no one has told you and it is closed from the top. It is sealed. The cap is on and it is sealed. And somebody tells, can you lick the honey? You are not feeling it. Try to lick the honey. They say, it is a little bitter. If you suck some honey in your throat, they say. Lick the honey. You might take the bottle and lick the honey. And you might say that I am licking the honey. But you are not actually tasting the honey. Because you have not followed the process for tasting the real honey. It is a very crude example. You might be licking the bottle and saying and believing and thinking that you are licking the honey. But the real honey is not being tasted. You are licking the bottle. So likewise, many people, it is a fact, many people may be reading Gita. Many people may be discussing Gita. But the real essence, the real meaning, the real gist which Krishna is trying to impart to Arjuna, is completely missed out.

---
#### bhakti, knowledge, truth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/lOpYB1KdQoA?t=1166
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only through bhakti, you can know me in truth."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the true meaning of the Gita is confidential and can only be understood through bhakti, emphasizing the difference between superficial and factual understanding.

Why? Now is that some kind of fanatism? That yes, Prabhupada's Gita is everything. Everything else is useless. Is that some kind of fanatism? Krishna himself in the Gita says, who can penetrate into the real meaning of Gita? That's why Krishna says that this is rahasyam hi etat uttamam. Rahasyam means it is a secret. If Bhagavad Gita is available in all book stores, available online, free e-books are available, just type Gita, you will find so many PDFs, free you can download. You don't even have to pay a penny. Why it is confidential? Why it is rahasyam? It is rahasyam because you can lick from outside without penetrating into the mystery of Gita. And Krishna says, bhaktiamam abhijanati. bhaktiamam abhijanati. bhaktiamam abhijanati. Only through bhakti, bhaktia, mam abhijanati, you can know me in truth. Yavan yas chasmi tatvatah, in truth, tatvatah. One is superficial understanding and one is in truth, the actual, factual understanding. Yavan yas chasmi tatvatah, bhaktiamam abhijanati.

Now, I'll give you an example for you to understand what is renunciation. There was a person who was walking on the road and he sees a wallet, a purse with 2000 rupee notes. A bundle of 2000 notes. Sometimes, even a wallet is quite big. Lot of 2000 rupee notes and they are just oozing out, so to say, coming out of that wallet and that wallet is lying on the road and you are passing by. Broadly speaking, broadly speaking, although there are many options what you can do with that wallet, there are three options. You can do three things with that. In such a scenario where you are going and you see a wallet right there on the road full of 2000 rupee notes, you can do three things. What are the three things? We'll go one by one. What is the first thing you can do? Not first thing. What are the three options, broadly speaking? I'm sure if we apply our mind we can think of fourth and fifth and sixth and seventh option also. Broadly speaking, the three options. First option. Okay. Pick up the purse and find out if there is some visiting card. And count the notes also. Depending on that we'll decide visiting card. See or not see. Okay. First option is you just say it's not mine and walk away. It's not mine. It's not mine and just walk away. That's option one.

#### anger management, practical application, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=0
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The devil is in the details; we can talk about theory, but the ground reality when the rubber meets the road, that's where the test really lies."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa introduces a Q&A session dedicated to managing anger, emphasizing practical applications and addressing unanswered questions from previous sessions, followed by an invocation prayer.

Hare Krishna, warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita live session. Today's session is a session which is dedicated to answering questions related to managing anger. We all have covered two sessions. The first session was Hame Gussa Kyun Aata Hai, where we analyzed the root cause of lust, of greed, of anger, and all base qualities. Then we had another session where we talked about some practical tips, some ways in which we can go about dealing with anger in practical day-to-day situations. But there were many, many questions which came in, and we could not do justice to all the questions. We couldn't answer all the questions, so we thought it will be nice if we can have a dedicated session where we can answer all the balance questions and some more questions which people can ask. That we, the devil is in the details; we can talk about theory, but the ground reality when the rubber meets the road, that's where the test really lies to be going to discuss in practical applications in our day-to-day lives. So we'll begin the session with really good person of NOTS do not say Krishna to Prabhu power to large Chaitanya Mahaprabhu seeking their blessings so that we can have a very meaningful discussion today. So please fold your hands, and if possible, you can even close your eyes and repeat these prayers after me.

#### anger management, parenting, practical application

**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa outlines the structure of the session, addressing questions from previous sessions and new inquiries, beginning with a question about managing anger when correcting unruly children.

So today we will cover some of the questions from the session, Hame Gussa Kyun Aata Hai? Some questions which were asked and we could not answer them because of paucity of time. And the last session, unfortunately because we had to edit the video a bit because there were some audio issues, we lost the questions there. So in case your questions were unanswered in the previous session, please type them again and we have enough time today and we will see that all the questions get answered as far as possible. That will be our desire and attempt. So this question is from Rajeshwari Devi Dasi. If we are taken for granted by our children, what if we need to mend their ways? How to do it without getting angry? In other words, the question is, children at times can be very unruly, can act in ways which is not good for them and we have to do it. We have to correct them. Is there a way of handling children? More so if they are acting in a notorious way, acting very naughty, not following our instructions. Is there a way of handling them, handling such situations without getting angry?

---
#### anger management, Gita teachings, practical application
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, there is a very fine difference between anger being used as a tool and anger using us."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that anger has a place and can be used as a tool, but it's crucial not to let anger overpower and control us, leading to regrettable actions.

So if you remember, the goal of life or goal of the teachings in the Gita is not that we should become dumb or inert like a stone. Anger has a place. And we discussed in our previous session, there are situations in which it is justified to become angry. I gave an example that just like when devotees of the Lord or Lord himself is blasphemed, is insulted, it is okay for a devotee to get angry. And we gave an example of Hanuman, how he became angry on Ravana for having kidnapped Sita and burned the whole Lanka. And Hanuman is worshipped as a great devotee. We also gave an example of Lord Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu who taught us to be humbler than a blade of grass, more tolerant than a tree. And he himself became very very angry when Nithyananda Prabhu was hurt by Jagai and Madai. Now, anger can be used as a tool in our day to day life. Just like we have different tools, we can talk sternly, we can give a very strict warning, we can show that such acts are unacceptable, but we should not let anger overpower us. In other words, anger use us. We come under the control of fits of anger. That is not justified. That is not okay. Because when we come under the control of anger, we will lose our discerning ability, our intelligence, and we will act in ways which we might later on regret. So, it is okay to be angry at times if the situation warrants. If let's say a child is not doing his homework, not studying, and time and again is not following the instruction, it is okay to get angry at times. But it should be more of the anger which is used as a tool to correct a person rather than we getting very frustrated with the situation and out of frustration, out of bewilderment, we lose temper and then we get angry. So, there is a very fine difference between anger being used as a tool and anger using us. So, let us be aware of that, be conscious of that and then use anger as a tool in our day to day life situations.

So, the question is sometimes the force is so strong, the urge is so strong that we know on one side we have intelligence which is saying that I should not get angry, I should not see this, I should not indulge in this but at times the force is so strong it seems it's like a battle. On one side we know it is wrong, the other side as if we are compelled, we are forced, we are helpless, we are pushed in that direction of getting angry. #### anger management, positive spirals, negative spirals
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=617
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Because of anger, we can act in ways which can fuel even more anger in us and can aggravate the problem, aggravate the situation."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains the concept of positive and negative spirals, using the analogy of the rich getting richer and the poor getting poorer, to illustrate how anger can lead to a negative loop, fueling more anger and aggravating situations.

There is something called as positive spirals and negative spirals. I am sure you would have heard about this saying called rich becomes richer and poor becomes poorer. We see this in our day to day lives. Like for example, just for a crude example, Mukesh Ambani, the company is doing well, Jio has practically captured the market and now more and more companies are pouring in trying to get a share of Jio platforms. So, he is already rich, already captured the market, already a market leader and more and more companies are coming in and pitching in and investing on Jio platforms. So, rich is becoming richer, and already somebody who is has already lost the market share, already the company is apparently sinking, even the so-called subscriber, even the so-called investors will try to withdraw their money. So likewise, when we become angry, we can get into a negative loop, negative spiral. Because of anger, we can act in ways which can fuel even more anger in us and can aggravate the problem, aggravate the situation. For example, if I get angry and the other person also gets angry and back and forth arguments happen and in that situation, I might be justified that yes, the situation warrant that I have to get angry because he said so and so, he abused me. But quite possible, we got into a negative spiral. We responded wrongly. Because of that wrong response, the person further abused us or maybe spoke wrong words, because of which, we again became more angry. Another example, let's say somebody wrote a nasty email and I reply to it in a very angry mode or a fit of anger, frustration and then comes another email or a reply which is even more, which even more, so to say, triggers anger in me. So the best thing is when we notice

#### anger management, self-awareness, modes of nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So self-awareness is the key."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa emphasizes the importance of self-awareness in recognizing when one is getting angry, linking it to the influence of the modes of nature (tamo guna, rajo guna, and sattva guna).

that we are getting angry, it requires some element of self-awareness and more and more, we will be self-aware if we are under the influence of higher modes. If we are in tamo guna, the self-awareness is practically nil. If we are in rajo guna, relatively, it is higher self-awareness, but again, it is not enough for us to control ourselves. And if we are in sattva guna, under the influence of mode of goodness, the possibility for us to discriminate, for us to be self-aware, to be in the control, to control our emotions is much easier. So self-awareness is the key.

---
#### anger management, self-control, practical tips
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=824
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, if we are in a situation where we realize that we are getting angry, we lose our good sense, our intelligence is bewildered."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa advises extricating oneself from anger-inducing situations, sharing a story about a wife who used Gangajal to manage her husband's anger, highlighting the importance of self-control and mindful responses.

The moment we know that I am losing temper and it can happen, mistakes can happen. Suddenly a situation comes and we lose temper. So moment we realize that it is best, it is best if we can extricate ourselves from that situation. So that's why they are saying that when you are angry, do not reply to your males. So this reminds me of a story. There was a man and a woman who used to fight quite often, husband and wife, and they used to have lot of fights among themselves and family life was not at all going on smooth. So this lady went to a Babaji, Sadhu, and said that my husband is so and so and he always fights with me and for silly reasons he loses his temper and then he starts quarreling and starts abusing and things like that. So can you please help solve the situation? Can you give me some solution? So the Swamiji said, take this bottle of Gangaja, and this will surely help. So the lady very eagerly took that bottle of Gangaja and then she asked, okay tell me when do I give him? Should I give him two times a day, three times a day, before meal, after meal? How should I give this Gangaja to him? Because she thoroughly believed that the problem is out there. He is the one who is abusing, he is the one who is getting angry and the entire circumstances, the situation is because of him. So the Baba said, no, no, no, no, this is not for him, this is for you. Whenever that person gets angry, all you have to do is drink little bit of Gangajal, keep it in the mouth and let it remain in the mouth till the anger in him subsides. So the lady was quite bewildered with this solution. After all he is getting angry and then why I have to take the Gangajal? Nevertheless, maybe Baba Ji is saying, she accepted. She went back home, situation arose where the husband is getting very furious, getting very angry and she remembered that Baba Ji had told about Gangajal, she took that Gangajal and put in his mouth and kept it in her mouth and and after a short while she noticed that the husband when he became angry, in a short while, unlike previous times, the anger started subsiding and he became quiet and went to another room. She thought, this is great, this Gangajal has done a miracle. Earlier he used to get angry and at times there was some 15 minutes, 20 minutes, 25 minutes, there would be heated exchange and here in next 2 minutes the anger subsided and he just went off. So, the point to be noted here is, in such situations, at times we also contribute to the problem by becoming angry. So, if we are in a situation where we realize that we are getting angry, we lose our good sense, our intelligence is bewildered. So, when we realize that we are in a situation like that where I am losing control over myself, anger has overpowered me, I can try to extricate myself from that situation. That's not defeat. Sometimes we may say we are escaping from the situation. May not be. We are temporarily escaping from the situation and we are trying to escape from that situation so that we can handle the situation more mindfully, more intelligently.

---
#### anger management, mindfulness, intelligent response
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1083
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "At times we are reactive. We react in that very moment rather than responding intelligently."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa highlights the difference between reacting and responding intelligently to stimuli, advocating for thoughtful responses over thoughtless reactions driven by urges.

Not necessarily all the situations have to be handled there and then. There are some situations, like for example, email has come. I can wait for an hour. Only when I am in good state of mind, when I am in control of myself, I can respond to such emails. At times we are reactive. We react in that very moment rather than responding intelligently. So there is a difference between reaction and a response. Responding intelligently. In either case, we are responding to the stimuli but then one is reaction which is more like thoughtless behavior driven by the urges, forces within us. We are overpowered by such urges and forces and there is another behavior where I respond to that stimuli intelligently.

---
#### anger management, practical tips, self-acceptance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1139
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Let us not allow the slip to become a slide."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa suggests practical techniques like deep breathing, drinking water, walking, or chanting to manage anger, emphasizing self-acceptance and quick recovery from lapses.

So that way we can try to extricate ourselves from the situation and at times if we have got overpowered by anger, we can try some things like doing some deep breathing, drinking a glass of water, going for a small walk or maybe chanting the names of the Lord. All these might help in that situation. By the way, let us all accept we are not perfect. Even though we may be on the path, we are all trying to become devotees of the Lord. There will be situations where we lose our temper. The point here is how badly we fall. Yes, slip can happen. Let us not allow the slip to become a slide. In other words, we quickly recover and come back to our good sense.

---
#### anger management, medication, health
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1192
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Sometimes there are some medicines which can have side effects or reactions in a person's body. Not every medicine suits every person."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa addresses a question about managing anger and lust induced by medications, advising consultation with doctors to balance disease treatment with side effects.

This question is from Pavan Kumar. There are few medicines due to some diseases which increase anger and lust. So how to tackle that, Prabhuji? In other words, sometimes we may have to take some medications which can result in some kind of anger or lusty feelings within us. It could be because of the chemicals which gets injected into our mind-body system. So how do we control that? We can talk to our doctors. The doctor who has prescribed the medicine. And sometimes we have to balance between the two. On one side we have a disease which has to be cured. The other side we have some because of the medicines it is creating problem within our mind-body system. So we have to discuss with our medical practitioner or the doctor whom we have consulted. And then he can guide us. Sometimes there are some medicines which can have side effects or reactions in a person's body. Not every medicine suits every person. So we can discuss and maybe, who knows, the doctor can either reduce the medicine or he can probably prescribe another medicine. So it's a trade-off. On one side we have a disease because of which we are suffering. Other side we have a medication which also gives us some element of discomfort. Maybe temporarily we have to tolerate that discomfort of the medicine for long-term good of recovering from that disease. So we have to choose among the two.

---
#### happiness, Krishna consciousness, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1296
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But how much that Dukha affects us, that is in our control."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa responds to a question about maintaining happiness at all times, clarifying that Krishna consciousness doesn't promise a problem-free life but offers control over how much suffering affects us.

This question is from Roma Acharyaji. How to become happy at all times including bad times? It's a very good question. Krishna consciousness or for that matter Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita never gives us a false promise that you become a devotee of the Lord, you chant the names of the Lord, you read Bhagavad Gita and your life will become very, very smooth. There won't be any problem whatsoever. You will not face any unfortunate situation, any untoward issue, any untoward incident. Everything will be fine in your life. No. Krishna does not promise. Bhagavad Gita does not say so. In fact Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita says Agama paayino anityas tam titikshu sabharata Just like the seasons come and go winter season, the summer season, the spring season, the autumn season. They come for a while and then the next season comes. It changes. So likewise Krishna says Sukha and Dukha good times, bad times will come and go. More so when we are in this material world which is declared as Dukha alayam ashashvatam Dukhas will come in our lives. But how much that Dukha affects us, that is in our control.

---
#### happiness, Krishna consciousness, consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1398
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So how much that situation affects us, influences us, torments us, rattles us, bewilders us is in our hand."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that while suffering is inevitable in the material world, the degree to which it affects us is controllable through higher consciousness, using Srila Prabhupada's example of maintaining Krishna consciousness even during physical deterioration.

That is what we can control. Not the amount of Dukha and not whether Dukha will come or not. It will come. Just like for example for a devotee also his eyesight with passage of time will get blurred. A devotee also will become old and may have associated old age problems, physical problems. Now there is an infection. A devotee can also get infected. But to the degree we can get disturbed, to the degree we can get affected because of those unforeseen, unpleasant, unfortunate situations or bad times that because of our higher consciousness we can control. I will give you an example. Srila Prabhupada when he was in Vrindavan in 1977 towards his last days before leaving his body his health was deteriorating and he was in Vrindavan and the time came when all his organs one by one started failing. He stopped even eating. And devotees were all around him and many of them knew that yes, his health is deteriorating and very soon he will be leaving. But even during that time although the bodily parts were all failing his consciousness was very very bright. Even till the last day he was dictating Bhaktivedanta purport. When we are in pain we don't feel like doing even so called normal activities. We just want to lie, sleep and take some pain killers and just escape. But Srila Prabhupada even last day was dictating Bhaktivedanta purport. And if you could see those purports full of Krishna consciousness. Pure Krishna consciousness. So how much that situation affects us influences us torments us rattles us bewilders us is in our hand. And it's largely dependent on our consciousness.

---
#### happiness, self-realization, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1536
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "By developing our Krishna consciousness which takes time it's a journey purification is a journey which we all have embarked upon."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains that developing Krishna consciousness helps handle life's ups and downs without bewilderment, referencing Arjuna's question about the symptoms of a self-realized person and Krishna's answer about equipoise in happiness and distress.

The more Krishna conscious we are less we will be affected by ups and downs in life. Good and bad. In fact Arjuna asked Krishna what are the symptoms of a person who is Sthita Pragna who is self-realized. So Krishna answered he is equipoised in happiness and distress pleasure and pain fear and anxiety. He is equipoised. Sthita Pragna Sakabhasha. So by developing our Krishna consciousness which takes time it's a journey purification is a journey which we all have embarked upon. And when we become purified the more and more we become purified the more and more we will be able to handle ups and downs of life without becoming bewildered. So that way yes it is possible to be happy even in bad times. Bad times will come but we can be so to say internally be happy and not get affected by such bad times. This question is from Pranay Pawar. What role does food play in anger management?

---
#### anger management, food, modes of nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1612
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If we eat sanctified food then for us to control our emotions and base quality becomes much easier."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa explains the significant role of food in controlling emotions, classifying food into Satvik, Rajasik, and Tamasik categories, and emphasizing the importance of eating sanctified food (Prasadam) for easier control over emotions.

We covered this in the last session. Yes, food does play a very very big role as far as controlling our emotions are concerned. In fact food can be broadly classified in three categories. Satvik Ahar Rajasik Ahar and Tamasik Ahar. If we eat food which is Rajasik in nature very very in Rajoguna we will come under the influence of the mode of passion. And the symptom of mode of passion is intense hankering. We lose control over our emotions. Intense hankering lamentation greed envy jealousy all these are symptoms of a person who is under the influence of Rajoguna mode of passion. So that's why even in the Gita Krishna extensively talks about the three modes of material nature. He talks about foods in the mode of goodness in the mode of passion in the mode of ignorance. So as practitioners of Bhakti Yoga practitioners of Krishna Consciousness we try as far as possible to eat foods which are Satvik in nature and foods which have been offered to Lord as Prasadam. If we eat sanctified food then for us to control our emotions and base quality becomes much easier.

---
#### detachment, duty, property disputes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1709
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You have the right to do your duty, but do not be attached to the results of your activities."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa addresses a question about reacting when one's possessions are wrongly possessed by others, referencing Krishna's instruction in the Bhagavad Gita about doing one's duty without attachment to results.

This question is from Gopalam How should one react to a situation where in our things if our things are wrongly possessed by others and they are delaying imparting of them to me. In other words somebody has taken away something and it has to be returned back to me and somebody is delaying it is not willing to to give back. There is a very nice instruction of the Lord in the Bhagavad Gita where many many places he says you have the right to do your duty but do not be attached to the results of your activities. For example let's say there is a property feud between two brothers or for that matter two parties somebody has let's say subbed the property or taken away the property and and unlawfully and you have filed a case. The question is should you get angry the question is should you fight should you engage in a legal tussle how to get it back these are all practical day to day life questions which can arise in a person's mind. So that's why Krishna many many places in the Bhagavad Gita says you have the right to do your duty as a matter of duty if a person has unlawfully taken your share of property or thing which belongs to you as a matter of duty you can try to get it back even if it requires to take a legal route it may be justified as a matter of duty.

---
#### detachment, duty, Krishna's grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1877
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "You have the right to do your duty as a matter of duty what needs to be done please do it but do not be attached to the result."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa contrasts two extremes: doing nothing versus getting it back by hook or crook, advocating for doing one's duty without attachment to the outcome, acknowledging the five factors of action and the importance of Krishna's grace.

So there are two extremes one extreme is you don't do anything nothing is mine so I don't care one can take that kind of a approach in life the other approach is by hook or crook come what may I will get it back that's another approach by hook or crook a person may have sleepless night high blood pressure is not able to take it in stride and not able to sleep spoils the health there are people like that they get into some kind of family disputes and you know even takes a toll on their health so that's the other extreme what is the right approach Krishna says you have the right to do your duty as a matter of duty what needs to be done please do it but do not be attached to the result come what may by hook or crook it has to happen Krishna says the five factors of action which contributes to the result of any endeavour we think that our endeavour is the only factor which contributes to the result and there are many books which says I worked hard and that's how there are lot of people who work hard but they do not get the results what they are wanting in life we may hear some success stories that you know so and so person you know he dreamt of being a cricketer and he used to practice six hours eight hours a day and then finally he landed in the Indian team there are examples like that we may come across some case studies like that but there are many many people who have worked six hours eight hours have sacrificed their family have moved into Mumbai and practiced hard but they could not even get into an IPL or Ranji Trophy so we do as a matter of duty maintain our balance maintain our consciousness we put in the efforts and do not get very attached to the outcome it has to happen that way

#### anger management, counseling, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rcgQZA5aLT0?t=1964
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "With regular counseling or regular advice depends on what's the relationship that can make a difference but not at the time when he is angry at other times when he is in good mood we can speak to that person."
**Summary:** HG Amitasana Dasa addresses a question about an 11-year-old nephew with uncontrollable anger, suggesting counseling, introducing Krishna Consciousness principles, and consulting a psychologist if needed.

If required we can express our feelings to the Lord this is what I want please help me beyond a thing we leave it to the Lord we do our duty and then do not be attached to the results this question is from Sunil Balachandrani I have a nephew who is about 11 years old he tends to be very angry and at the peak of his anger he becomes uncontrollable as an example he threw the remote at the television what is your advice? So here we have an example of a person who is very very angry an 11 year old boy and at times when he is overpowered by anger he acts in a ways which can be one very for example throwing a remote on the television can lead to lot of breakage and lot of nuisance so how do we manage that? Yes some element of counseling will help and counseling many times what happens is when we try to counsel a person when he is angry or in that particular situation it won't help for example when there is an argument happening between two persons at that time you try to tell that person no you are wrong this is not the right way of doing things probably that's not the right time to advise a person it's always good if we can counsel the person or speak to that person when he is in good mood for example this child quite possible with regular counseling or regular advice depends on what's the relationship that can make a difference but not at the time when he is angry at other times when he is in good mood we can speak to that person and at times we can even we can maybe recommend that child to take up some of the principles of Krishna Consciousness like chanting the names of the Lord like taking Prasadam like visiting and associating with the devotees we have many many classes now for even children

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### REAL INDEPENDENCE | HG Amitasana Das | Independence Day Special | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### introduction, independence, bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** introduction
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So let's understand what is real independence."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of real independence from the perspective of the Bhagavad Gita and offers prayers.

Hare Krishna! A warm welcome to all of you for today's Gita Live session. The topic for the day is Real Independence. Today we are celebrating Independence Day. There is a lot of festive mood all around. A lot of patriotic songs are being sung and being heard. So let's understand what is real independence. Are we really free? And how to achieve that real independence, which we all are actually seeking, we all are wanting. Let's understand from the perspective of Bhagavad Gita. And before we plunge into this discussion, let us all pray together and seek the blessings of Srila Prabhupada, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, and Lord Sri Krishna so that we can have a very meaningful understanding today.

---
#### prayer, guru parampara, Krishna

**Verse References:** —

Namo om vishnupadaya... Krishna preshtaya bhutale... Srimate Bhaktivedanta... Svamin Nithinamine... Namaste Saraswate Deve.... Gauravani Pracharine... Nirviseshashunya... Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya, Prabhu Nityananda, Shri Advaita Gadadhara, Shri Vasadi Gaur Bhakta Vrinda, Namo Mahavadanyaya, Krishna Prema Pradayate, Krishnaya Krishna Chaitanya, Namne Gauratuse Namaha, Namo Brahmane Devaya, Go Brahmane Hitayacha, Jagadhitaya Krishnaya, Go Vindaya Namaha.

---
#### independence, material nature, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=227
**Verse References:** BG 13.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Real independence is how we can come out of the clutches of janma, mrityu, jara, vyadi."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the illusion of independence, emphasizing that we are under the stringent laws of material nature and the cycle of birth and death, and that real independence is freedom from this cycle.

So we will approach the topic today and categorize our understanding in three buckets, three aspects. So the first aspect which we are going to cover is, are we really free? I repeat, the first bucket of our discussion is, are we really free? Or are we really free? Are we really independent? So today we are apparently celebrating the 75th anniversary of achieving independence. Are we really independent is a very fundamental question. Yes, we can celebrate Independence Day year after year, feel that yes, we are free. We are free from the clutches of the British. We are free from the clutches of the Britishers who were ruling us. But are we really free? Are we really independent? The fact is, we are not. What does independence mean? Independence means you are under the clutches, under the grip of someone. Just like before, pre-1947, we were under the rule of the Britishers. We fought for independence and achieved that independence and got free from the rule of the Britishers. Likewise, our scriptures say that we are under the clutches of stringent laws of nature. Bondage means what? You have caught in the clutches of someone means what? You don't have the freedom to act the way you want to act. Your choices are restricted. Likewise, if you see, even we are under the clutches of material nature, the laws of material nature, and they are very stringent. Someone cannot say that I want to be free. I will not get old. They are laws and one has to surrender to those laws. Someone cannot say I will not get diseased. One will fall victim to one kind of disease or another. One cannot say I will not die. Stringent laws of material nature, they are acting on us and whether we want it or we don't want it, one day we will have to surrender and we will have to die, leave this present body which we are very attached to. So our scriptures say that we are caught up in this cycle of birth and death. Punarapi janma, punarapi marana. And real freedom or real intelligence or real freedom of thought, real independence is how we can come out of the clutches of janma, mrityu, jara, vyadi. In fact, yes, it is a wonderful gesture that we should look for, we should aim for, we should strive for independence, but real independence which our scriptures are pointing out, which is possible to achieve in human form of life, is to achieve independence from janma, mrityu, jara, vyadi. Krishna also in the Bhagavad Gita says, janma, mrityu, jara, vyadi, dukha, dosha, anu, darshanam. One who can perceive that these are the real challenge from which we have to find a solution in this human form of life, that person is in knowledge.

---
#### science, immortality, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Very soon immortality will be a scientific reality."
**Summary:** The speaker shares an anecdote about genetic scientists releasing a book called 'Death of Death' and their claims about achieving immortality through science, highlighting the materialistic desire to overcome death.

There were two genetic scientists and they were releasing a book in Barcelona. One of them was Mr. Parker and another fellow scientist, his name was Gilman. And they had written a book, they were genetic scientists and they had written a book called Death of Death. The name of the book was Death of Death. And the book was being released and a lot of luminaries, authors and people from various backgrounds were gathered in a big auditorium and this book was being released. And when they spoke about the book, the scientists and what we have achieved through science, one of them said that very soon immortality will be a scientific reality. And then another person said that aging will now be termed as a disease for which we will find a cure. Just like many diseases are there and we have medicines, we have cure for them. Likewise aging also will be termed as a disease very soon and we will find a cure for it. And one of them very emphatically declared that I refuse to get old. In fact 30 years from now I will be much younger than what you see me today.

---
#### desire, death, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=594
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "A person who realizes that, understands that, that person is in knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the scientists' claims with the story of Hiranyakashipu's desire for immortality and emphasizes that despite attempts to conquer death, it is an inevitable reality, and true knowledge lies in understanding our dependence on material nature.

In fact this mentality has been there since ages. Even Hiranyakashipu did a lot of tapasya and he wanted to be immortal. And that's how he got a boon that I should not die in the day time or in the night time, in the sky or on the earth. Different kinds of permutation combinations so that he can become immortal. Because as spirit soul, as atma we are deathless, we are eternal, never dying. Therefore we have that inherent desire not to die. But we don't know that so long as we are in this material body which is subject to 6th transformation. It's a material body and anything material undergoes 6th transformation. It comes into being, stays for some time, procreates, produces by-products and then grows, and dwindles and finally it ceases to exist, it dies. And so long as we are identified with the body we will also have to experience these 6th transformation or changes, although as soul we are eternal and deathless. And sorry to say although they claimed so vehemently, very emphatically that death of death in their book, but today they are no more. It's a fact that we cannot come out of the clutches of material nature, although a lot of research is happening, a lot of attempts are being made to become immortal, to not die. But it's a fact that big, big empires, so many eminent personalities have all come and gone. It's a reality and we are under the clutches of those stringent laws. In fact Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita says that if someone does not surrender to me, I come as death and take away everything, and a person has no option but to surrender to death. So to recognize, to understand that we are under the clutches of material nature, we are not really free, although yes we have an inherent desire to be independent, to be free. A person who realizes that, understands that, that person is in knowledge.

#### spiritual life, questioning, freedom

**Summary:** The speaker states that spiritual life begins with questioning our entanglement and suffering, seeking answers from self-realized souls, and emphasizes the importance of understanding true freedom.

In fact Prabhupada says that our spiritual life begins when a person starts questioning. Why am I entangled? Why am I suffering? In fact there is an Upanishad called Kena Upanishad, which is all about asking these fundamental questions. And that is, and when we get answers to these fundamental questions from self-realized soul, a bona fide, authorized spiritual master, coming in Parampara, enlightenment begins, our spiritual life begins. Although we are looking for independence, we very fervently celebrate Independence Day, today I could hear a lot of songs in the morning, and different societies and different buildings, there was some kind of a program organized, hoisting a flag, and which is understandable.

---
#### freedom, attachment, ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=861
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Although we are talking about independence, although we are talking about freedom, we are reluctant to move in that direction."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates a story about a man who tries to free a parrot from its cage, but the parrot is reluctant to leave, drawing a parallel to our own reluctance to seek true freedom despite longing for it.

People long for, people cherish, people look for to be independent, to be free. But are we genuinely, genuinely understanding what is freedom? And are we truly striving for that freedom? It's a very fundamental question. Once there was a person who was passing through a forest, and overnight he decided to stay in a small cottage, some kind of a traveler's inn, some kind of a motel, very rustic, old building, a decent building, and he stayed overnight there. And while he was trying to sleep, he heard some kind of a sound, and when he peeped out of his window, and he saw the sound was coming from a bird, a parrot, was encaged in a cage, kept in a cage, and he was in his own way trying to speak out the word freedom, freedom, freedom. So he was quite surprised, it's quite understandable, nobody likes to be captive, nobody like to be encaged. We also because of this lockdown, we had to stay in a particular place. There were travel restrictions. So nobody likes to be bound or so to say restricted in a particular environment. So quite understandable for this man that yes maybe the parrot is finding himself restricted and is looking for freedom freedom that's how he's saying this freedom freedom. So he tried, he thought that let me help this parrot and then he slowly without making any sound sneaked out of his room knowing that this poor parrot although is wanting freedom this mortal owner has caught him and put in a cage so let him be freed to give his freedom he'll have that satisfaction that yes he has done some good work for some soul and given what it really wanted was really looking for freedom. So this person sneaked out of the room and went to that cage and he saw that the parrot slowly without making sound less he should wake up some owner or create some commotion he opened the door of the cage fortunately it was not locked he opened the door and now the door of that that cage was wide open and he spoke to that parrot very very feebly now it's open now it's open go go sky is the limit now go fly you are free now. But the parrot quite surprisingly was holding on to the cage in fact when this person insisted now you are free go get out he more tightly clinged on to the rods of that that cage but this person in fact was a freedom fighter he was all the more determined he was quite surprised all the parrot is saying freedom freedom even now he was saying freedom freedom now the cage is open but he is reluctant to go out. So this person put his hand inside the cage somehow so that he can pull out that parrot and let it achieve that real freedom what it's longing but moment he puts his hand inside the cage this parrot started pecking on his hand started pricking on his hand and it was quite painful but still since he was determined he caught hold of the parrot and took him out of the cage and threw him up he had a sigh of relief that at last one soul he could do some good some wonderful thing which he achieved today and that was to give freedom to this the soul who was engaged in this cage and then he came back to his room there was a lot of pain in his hand but still there was some bit of satisfaction finally it could give freedom to the bird who was longing for it and next day morning when he woke up he heard the same parrot speaking freedom freedom freedom and thought maybe he's sitting on something some branch of some tree nearby or something and when he peeped out of the window, the parrot was in the same very cage, inside the cage with the door open and speaking, freedom, freedom, freedom. Our case is something similar. Although it may look that this bird is so foolish. We all long for independence, we all long for freedom, we are striving for that freedom but unfortunately we don't know what is real freedom. What is real independence and how to achieve it. Although our scriptures, spiritual masters, great acharyas are teaching us, telling us how we are engaged and giving us the wonderful instructions how we can come out of that engagement although we are talking about independence although we are talking about freedom we are reluctant to move in that direction just like this example of this parrot is talking about freedom is speaking freedom freedom but when the door is being opened is reluctant to move out.

#### independence, regulations, permissive society
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=1244
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Generally the understanding of independence of freedom means that we should be free to do what comes in our mind."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second point: independence versus regulations, explaining that the common understanding of independence is to be free to do whatever one wants, leading to a permissive society.

So what is real freedom what is real independence this brings us to the second point the second aspect which we're going to discuss today the second aspect is independence versus regulations independence versus regulations generally the understanding of independence of freedom means that we should be free to do what comes in our mind. In other words, eat whatever we like eating, sleep whatever time we feel like sleeping. Maja karo is the general understanding. Eat, drink, be merry. There shouldn't be any rules, regulations, restrictions imposed on us. Some people are not fond of leading a disciplined life. Why discipline? Why regulations? Whenever you feel like sleeping, you sleep. Whenever you feel like waking up, you wake up. Whatever you feel like eating, whatever these days, you know, you have so many options. Swiggy, Zomato, whatever comes in your mind, you can order. Next 30 minutes hot hot things will be delivered at your doorstep. That is real freedom. You can watch a cricket match, an IPL match with a hot pizza on your plate right in front of you and you can enjoy. That's freedom. Eat what you want, as much quantity as you want, whenever you want. Actually, although we are talking that we are freedom. In fact, the whole society is moving in that direction. Thinking that this is real freedom.

#### permissive society, freedom, slavery
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=1359
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In the name of freedom, in the name of independence, we have become slaves."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of a permissive society, where restrictions are relaxed, leading to problems like teenage pregnancies and the illusion of freedom, ultimately resulting in becoming slaves of the mind and senses.

We are living more and more in a society which is becoming permissive. The society is called permissive society. Permissive society means it is okay, it is permitted to act in a way you want to act. And that's how you see all the restrictions over a period of time are getting a little bit liberalized, relaxed. Abortions, now it's legalized in some countries. You can go out to the court. You don't feel like having a child, you had unsafe sex and you didn't want to have a child. You can go to the clinic and get it aborted. You don't like a particular, you got married somehow you're not getting along. Okay, go and sign some documents in the court and you are free. You can, you can marry someone else. So we think that yes this is liberal society the less and less laws which are restricting us we are free to do what comes in our mind but actually if we analyze although we say that we are free in fact in the US it's a very big problem this called teenage pregnancies 13 year old 14 year old girls getting pregnant imagine there was a girl was still in school in the 10th class and she had a baby all in the name of freedom all in the name of enjoyment without having an understanding of what is real enjoyment enjoyment is a birthright Anandamayo Bhyasat nobody is saying that we should not enjoy but not enjoy like animals not enjoy in any other way and that's why we should not enjoy like animals not enjoy in any other way and that's why we should not enjoy like animals not enjoy in ignorance what is use of that enjoyment yes for a short while few seconds few minutes few days we enjoy but rest of the life we suffer actually lot of people are entangled are miserable facing one kind of problem another many do not know the cause of that misery is their own actions which is stemming out of ignorance that enjoying tendency which is brainwashing tolerance and means really weak but it is the right Indeed The responsibility of an animal is not absolute our job cause law-abiding life, like a genuine good citizen. So, today's world is more or less a permissive society. But actually, if we analyze, in the name of freedom, in the name of independence, we have become slaves.

#### slavery, senses, regulation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=1555
**Verse References:** BG 3.41, BG 6.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Human life begins when we understand these principles that, yes, tapo, divyam, shudhyet, human life is meant for regulations."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in the name of freedom, we have become slaves of our minds and senses, and true freedom lies in regulating the senses, as taught in the Bhagavad Gita, to achieve real happiness.

Slaves of what? Slaves of our mind, slaves of our senses. Actually, it's a fact. The senses are dictating. Just like earlier, pre-1947, the Britishers were dictating. They were enacting the laws. The laws were governing us. We were not free. And 1947, when we got free, there was sigh of relief. Now we are independent. Yes, we are independent of the laws which were enacted by Britishers, but there are many laws, the stringent laws of nature which are acting on us. Are we really free? Do we know how to get freedom from those laws? So, we, in the name of independence, in the name of freedom, have become slaves of our minds, slaves of our senses. The tongue is dictating, eat this. Although I know it is not good for my health, although I know I am already obese, I am already overweight, but still the tongue is dictating, no, eat it. Yes, sir. And we follow. So, in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna is saying, from the very beginning, regulate the senses. The senses, mind and intelligence are the sitting places of lust. From the very beginning, regulate your senses and slay this destroyer of knowledge and self-realization. So, human life begins when we understand these principles that, yes, tapo, divyam, shudhyet, human life is meant for regulations. Regulations for what? Shudhyet brahma saukhyam to anantam, so that we can get purified and we can achieve real freedom, real happiness. Brahma saukhyam to anantam. We can achieve that happiness which is anantam, which is unending, which is lasting, which is eternal. So, all of us can realize that how we all are slaves, our senses and minds are dictating us. Each one of us know that, yes, we should exercise, but when the time comes, the mind says, no, not today, it's raining. We keep an alarm that morning we'll get up at 6 o'clock or 5 o'clock, and when the alarm rings, the mind says, no, another 5 minutes. Yes, sir. And I again sleep. So, we become slaves of our senses. They are controlling us. We are not the mind, we are not the body, but they are controlling us. I have given this example of a chariot driven by five horses, where these horses represent the mind. The reins of the horses represent, the horses represent our senses. The reins represent the mind. The chariot driver represents our intelligence. And the passenger in the chariot represents the soul. In normal sense, in a chariot, the flow of command is from the passenger to the driver to the horses, and then the horses move in the direction in which the passenger wants to move. Unfortunately, if you see, in our present life, it's the other way around. It's the horses which are dictating where we should go. And passenger is completely bewildered, having no sense of what is the ultimate goal of life. So, it's a very sorry state of affairs if we truly understand what is happening to us. Why are we entangled?

#### attachment, lust, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=1780
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you had such intense desire, the same desire for Hari, for Lord Shri Krishna, your life would have been done."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Bilwa Mangala Thakura, who was deeply attached to a prostitute, Chintamani, and how her words awakened him to the futility of his lust and inspired him to renounce and seek Krishna.

There was once this very now an exalted devotee, we all have heard about him, his name was Bilwa Mangala Thakura. When he was a young man, he fell in love with a prostitute, and he would daily visit her and spend time with her. He was very attached to her, and every day he would long for the time when he would reach, he would go and spend time with her. There was a longing. One time it so happened that his father expired and there was the cremation happening. And as part of cremation, the pandits were there, the pujas were happening, and the whole ritual, the ceremonies were happening. And this person was very, very eagerly waiting when the ceremonies would get over, so that he can go back to this prostitute called Chintamani. In fact, he was so anxious, so eager that he told the pandits, can you please speed up? Can you please do it fast? So the pandits obliged, and very soon that there was some, they took some shortcut and finished the ceremony in a shorter duration of time. Instead of chanting full mantras, they chanted few mantras and somehow finished the ceremony as per the request made by this person, Bilwa Mangala Thakur. So now, when he stepped out of his house to go to Chintamani, in fact, her house was across the river, he found that on that particular day, it was pouring very badly. It was very, very, like a stormy day, pouring very badly, and the river was in spate, lot of currents. There was no boatman where he could, you know, take him in the boat and across the river. That was the normal way he would travel and go to her place. And it was quite late in the night, there was no boatman, the river was in spate, lot of currents, it was pouring very badly, it was, you know, the winds were blowing, dead in the night. So intense was his death, desire to go to Chintamani that he decided, come what may, I will go there. And he jumped into that river. And since the river was in spate, he was practically getting blown away with that current. But somehow, he caught hold of something so that he could save himself. He thought that it was some kind of a log, some kind of a wooden log, which was floating in that river. But later on, when he came closer to it and felt it, actually it was a dead corpse, a dead body, which was floating in that river. Somehow he caught hold of that dead corpse, saved himself, otherwise he would have flown with that currents in that river and somehow managed to reach the other side of the shore. He was completely drenched and in the night time, Chintamani thinking that it's quite late, now he is not going to come, it's too late and it's also pouring very badly, for sure he will not come, she closed the door, switched off the light and went off to sleep. Otherwise, every day he was coming. So knowing that today it's quite late and he will not come, it's pouring so badly, he has to come all the way from that village, from the other side of the river, she just closed the door and went off to sleep. This Bilwa Mangala somehow crossed the river, as I said, holding on to the dead corpse and when he reached the house, he saw that the house was locked and only way for him to, so he knocked the door but there was no response, only way for him to now go inside the house was to climb over that wall and it was quite high, but he somehow, because it was so intense, this saying that where there is a will, there is a way. He was so determined, so passionate, so much desirous of meeting Chintamani that he decided that he will climb that wall. The wall was quite high, how does he manage to climb and cross over? He caught hold of the wall and started climbing. Luckily, he found some kind of a rope dangling from that wall and caught hold of that rope and somehow managed to leap across that wall. And as soon as he leapt across the wall, he saw that actually that was a live snake and it slithered away after he had jumped to the other side of the wall. So imagine, having crossed the river which was in spate with lot of currents, holding on to a dead corpse and finally crossing that wall, holding on to a snake. Such was his intense desire. And finally, completely drenched, he reached the house, he knocked the door and Chintamani was quite surprised who has come dead of the night. When she opened the door, he found it was Bilwa Mangala. And when Chintamani opened the door, she asked, Bilwa Mangala, in dead of night, how did you manage? How did you come here? So he explained and started telling that actually it was, the ceremonies were on, I did this, I did this, I jumped into the river and there was a corpse like this and somehow I managed. He wanted to show that how intense was his desire for her, how intense was his love for her, although it was not love, it was lust. He explained the whole thing. Chintamani patiently heard the whole thing and said, alas, alas, if you had such intense desire, the same desire for Hari, for Lord Shri Krishna, your life would have been done. It's unfortunate that you have that same lusty desire for, for this body made of flesh and bones. Alas, in fact, these words pierced through the heart of Bilwa Mangala and he realized that, how fallen he is. On the day when his father's death ceremony was happening, the rituals were happening, he rushed to her place, taking all kinds of risks, just because of that lust which was burning in his heart. He realized these words pierced in his heart and there was some kind of awakening. And hearing the words of Chintamani, he decided, enough is enough. Now I should go to Vrindavan and rest of the life, rest of my life, engage in serving Lord Hari, Lord Shri Krishna.

#### senses, control, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=2238
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Real independence, real freedom is somehow we can subjugate them."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Bilwa Mangala, describing his struggle with lust even after renunciation, leading him to blind himself and fully dedicate himself to devotion in Vrindavan, emphasizing that true freedom is controlling the senses.

He decided to go towards Vrindavan. But habits die hard. Senses, mind, they're all, we all are, have been slaves of our senses and mind for ages, for many, many lives. The habitual tendencies, we get allured, we get tempted. So on his way to Vrindavan, he again got attracted to a woman. She was very beautifully dressed and she was moving into a house and he followed her. The moment the woman realized that a person is following, she told her husband that so and so person is following me and he is coming behind me. So the husband came out of the house and this Bilwa Mangala approached him and he was quite upfront. He told that somehow, I've got attracted to your wife, to this lady and I'm sorry. So husband said, you are our guest and guest is like Athito Devo Bhava. You are our Athiti, you are our guest. Please, you are most welcome. You can come. Please stay with us overnight. It's quite late in the night. What can we do to serve you? And this person again, because of the lust burning in his hand, in his heart, he opened his heart and said, can I somehow spend time with your wife? My desire is to embrace her. And this person also, he is a guest, he is requesting, guest cannot be said no. Somehow agreed. He went in the room of that lady with the desire to enjoy and the same words of chintam, chintamani started ringing in his ears. He started thinking how fallen I am. I am on my way to Vrindavan. I have renounced everything. But my eyes, my senses are forcing me to do such an abominable act. I have become slaves of these senses. So he asked that lady, can you please give me your hairpin? So the lady obliged. She gave her hairpin. He took that hairpin and pierced his eyes. And that very moment he became blind. And this Bilbo Mangala eventually reached Vrindavan and day and night he would be glorifying the Lord. He composed many songs, many poets, many things he had written glorifying the Lord. Although he was blind, but he was completely steeped in devotion to Lord Shri Krishna. So this is our state. We think that we are free. We think we are independent. But actually we are slaves of our senses. Time and again we get tempted. Time and again we get, so to say, cheated by our senses, cheated by our mind. And real independence, real freedom is somehow we can subjugate them. Somehow we can win over them. That's why there is two words. One is called Godasa. And one is called Goswami. Godasa means we are servant of God. God means senses. And Goswami. Goswami means one who is master of his senses, master of his mind. And real freedom or real independence is how we can learn to control the urges. Vacho vegam, Manasa vegam, Jeeva vegam, urges of tongue, urges of belly, urges of genitals. These are vegams, the urges, and we can win over them. That is real freedom. That is real independence.

#### recap, freedom, regulations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Mjw2NevLBRo?t=3197
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** summary
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In present times we think that independence means we should be free to do what comes in our mind."
**Summary:** The speaker recaps the first two points: the illusion of freedom under material nature and the importance of regulations for achieving real freedom, drawing an analogy to traffic lights and quoting Krishna on the benefits of regulation.

So to recap what we have so far discussed. The first point we discussed was Are we really free? We talked about how we are under the control of stringent laws of nature. We are caught up in this cycle of Janma, Mrityu, Jara, Vyadi. And in human form of life we can achieve real freedom and that is to extricate ourselves from the cycle of birth and death. And then second point we covered was Independence versus restrictions. In present times we think that independence means we should be free to do what comes in our mind. Fulfil the demands, the desires of our senses thinking that that will make me free, that will make me truly happy. But that is not real freedom. In fact just like even in our daily lives there are some restrictions which are good for us. For example the traffic lights. We don't like if we are going from place A to place B suddenly there is red light and we are made to stop. But such restrictions

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of spiritual health, based on the Nectar of Instruction, emphasizing its practical application in daily life for sincere seekers.

Oum Agyanati Mirandhasya Gyananjana Shalakaya Chakshurunmi Litam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Namahum Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshthaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Svamin Iti Namine. Namaste Sarasvate Deve Gauruvani Vacharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Sri Advaita Gadadhar Srivasadi Gauru Bhakti Vrinda. Namo Mahavadanya Krishna Prem Pradayate Krishna Krishna Chaitanya Namne Gaurat Vishay Namaha. Namo Brahmanya Deva Go Brahman Hitayacha Jagad Hitay Krishna Govinday Namoh Namaha. Krishna Krishna Karunasindhodina Bandha Jagadpate Gopesh Gopikakanta Radhakanta Namostate. Tapta Kanchana Gaurangi Radhe Vrinda Vaneishvari Virishabhanu Sute Devi Pranamami Haripriye. Vansha Kalpatarubhyascha Kripasindhubhyaevaca Patitanam Pavanibhyo Vaisnavibhyo Namoh Namah. Namah. Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare. Hare Krishna. Um grande bem-vindo a todos vocês. Então, nas últimas leituras, nós discutimos os shlokas muito importantes, explicados por Srila Rupa Goswami, Prabhupada. Então, vamos continuar hoje. Então, a discussão de hoje, revivendo uma saúde espiritual, vai ser baseada no texto número 7 de Urdesha Amrita, Nectar of Instruction. Como discutimos antes, Nectar of Instruction compreende 11 versos muito importantes, que são destinados a dar a um iniciante em Krishna, consciência espiritual, direção prática em seguir o caminho espiritual. Nós lemos teoria, mas como aplicar praticamente em nossa vida? Então, é por isso que para qualquer pessoa sincera que quer não apenas manter conceitos espirituais no livro, mas quer implementar em sua vida praticamente, ele ou ela deve ir através deste livro muito importante, Nectar of Instruction.

#### urges, association, spiritual advancement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wS1puVFh_mQ?t=196
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The state of our life is because of the association that we have had."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of controlling bodily urges and the impact of association on one's life, emphasizing that our current state is a direct result of the company we keep.

Então, a instrução mais importante, se precisarmos executar a vida espiritual, Srila Rupa Goswami explica isso. A primeira coisa é que nós temos várias urges em nosso corpo. Então, um tem que controlar essas urges. Então, nós temos que ser muito cuidadosos em nossa vida. Temos tantas urges, desejos, e nós nos levamos a ser levados por essas urges. Então, eu deveria praticar tolerância, melhorar aquelas urges, e não ficar abastecido de elas. Como praticar? Para isso, mais sugestões são criadas pela Srila Rupa Goswami, nas versões seguintes. Mas nós temos que entender que todas essas coisas estão lá para nos libertar destes seis urges do corpo. Então, Srila Rupa Goswami explica sete atividades que destroem um avanço espiritual. Estudar o Dr. Santiago de Almeida. Depois disso, o Dr. Mr. Jhon J Rubio. Swami explica seis princípios de avançamento espiritual. Todos estes tópicos nós já falamos anteriormente nas sessões anteriores de domingo. Então você pode voltar e procurar seis princípios de avançamento espiritual, seis coisas que destroem o Bhakti, seis princípios da Associação Satsanga, e talvez nós possamos criar uma playlist também, e então você pode procurar por isso em alguns dias, nós faremos isso. Então Rishila Rup Goswami explica, depois de explicar seis princípios de avançamento espiritual, o fator mais importante, e que é a Associação dos Devoteis, Satsanga. O estado atual de nossa vida é por causa da associação que nós tivemos. Alguma pessoa está lá, que está sentada em prisão agora, por quê? Por causa da errada de associação. De alguma forma ele se associou com pessoas que tinham uma mentalidade similar, deixe-me de alguma forma mentir. Pegue, roube, seja o que você quiser, aproveite a vida. E por causa dessa associação, ele desenvolve um certo desejo, e a reação desses desejos é que ele está sentado em prisão. Outra pessoa, ele se encontrou com algumas pessoas que o levaram a drogas, intoxicantes, e agora sua vida está misturada. Outra pessoa que teve a associação de pessoas sábias, pessoas com controle de si mesmas, ele está em paz na sua vida. Alguma alma muito afortunada recebe a associação dos Devoteis e alcança a perfeição da vida espiritual. Então nossa vida inteira, felicidade, desespero, tudo depende de uma única associação, é isso.

#### satsanga, devotee types, spiritual master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wS1puVFh_mQ?t=352
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Uma pessoa é muito, muito afortunada em encontrar um devoto muito avançado, mas ele se torna infeliz imediatamente se percebe o devoto puro de ser um homem comum."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of satsanga, the association of devotees, and the importance of applying its six principles with the right person, emphasizing the need to avoid seeing advanced devotees as ordinary individuals.

Então é por isso que a associação dos Devoteis é um fator muito importante. Durante a vida, nós temos que manter isso. Agora, o que é a satsanga? Devo simplesmente ir e sentar com os Devoteis, vê-los, ouvi-los? Sim, todas essas coisas são importantes, mas há seis princípios de satsanga também, que também nós discutimos. E então, Rupa Goswami explica mais adiante. Você tem que ter associação. Aplique esses seis princípios. Há seis princípios de satsanga, mas você tem que aplicá-los com a pessoa certa, o mais avançado espiritualista, o mais avançado Devotee. Então os Devoteis são de três tipos, Kanistha Adhikari, Madhyam Adhikari e Uttam Adhikari. Um neofita que está cantando os nomes de Krishna, mas então ele está na plataforma material, e ele tem outros desejos materiais também. Então, a plataforma intermediária, Madhyam Adhikari. E depois de Madhyam Adhikari, vem a Satsanga. A Satsanga é uma plataforma extraordinária, que é raramente atingida pelos espiritualistas, e é chamada Uttam Adhikari, o mais avançado Devotee, Mahabhagavata, o devoto puro de Krishna. E nós devemos tentar nos proteger de um devoto tão puro e Uttam Adhikari, e aceitá-lo como mestre espiritual, e aplicar esses princípios de satsanga. Então isso também nós já falamos nas leituras. E na última sessão, que eu fiz, claro que o Amitássara Cláudio tomou entre as duas. Então foi sobre o maior risco da vida espiritual. Isso o Rugu Swami explica ainda mais. Então uma pessoa é muito, muito afortunada em encontrar um devoto muito avançado, mas ele se torna infeliz imediatamente se percebe o devoto puro de ser um homem comum, Gurushu Narmatir. Então é por isso que o Rugu Swami afirmou que, por favor entendam que nós todos temos sensos materiais agora. Então nós não podemos entender as questões do assunto espiritual e ter visão espiritual. Dhrishtai Svabhava Janitair Vapuchascha Doshai Vapu significa corpo. O corpo terá muitas doxas ou defeitos. Até mesmo o corpo de um devoto muito avançado. Mas todos esses defeitos não são materiais, eles são realmente espirituais na natureza. E isso é o que Rugu Goswami afirma.
