**Summary:** The speaker refutes the idea of the Lord being impersonal, explaining that the Lord's senses are transcendental, not material, and therefore He is called Nirguna, untouched by material qualities, and poses the question of whether the Lord knows the future, leading to a discussion on free will versus destiny and the dangers of fatalism.

But here it is said, The Supersoul is the original source of all senses. Yet, He is without senses. Now, how do we understand? The real understanding is, His senses are transcendental. It is not material. And hence, the Lord is also called Nirguna. Guna means, with material qualities. We have material senses made of gross elements, Earth, Air, Water, Fire, Ether. The Lord's senses are all spiritual. They are not material. They are untouched by the modes. And hence, the Lord is also called Nirguna. This is not to say that, He does not have eyes. He does not have a form. He does not have legs. He has no material eyes, but sees everything, Past, Present and Future. He has no material eyes. Material body means limitation. Material eyes means, I can see only what is within my... Spiritual Lord is transcendental sense. Sat Chit Ananda Vigraha. With His transcendental senses, He can see everything. He can know everything. Now, does that mean, The Lord knows past, Lord knows present. This question I have asked earlier. Earlier also. Again, let me repeat. Some of you are not there. Or there. It could be a revision. The Lord knows the past. Lord knows the present. Lord knows the future. Next week in Gita Live class, Will you come or not come? Does the Lord know? Yes or No? How many of you say, Lord knows? So, in other words, You don't have a free will. It is hard coded. It's already frozen. You don't have a choice. Next Sunday, Who knows, Something interesting comes up in your life. Is it, The Lord knows the past. Lord knows the present. Lord knows the future. So, is the future hard coded? Yes or No? How many of us feel it is hard coded? Hard coded means, That is what the church in the 7th century AD believed that, All this understanding of Karma, The Lord makes a person fatalistic. It's all fate. Everything is written here. Working hard, All this is no meaning, Because it's all fate. Ultimately fate, Destiny is the one which overrules everything. And at times it made a person lazy. Lazy. If it is written in my destiny to pass, Then I will pass. If God wants me to come to Gita Life, Then I will come. If God wants me to watch TV, Then I will watch TV. Very nice philosophy, isn't it? So, does the Lord know the future? And is it hard coded?

---
#### Free will, Destiny, Karma, Choice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7M-mLHpRhfk?t=1552
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Based on the choices we make every moment, our future destiny is changing."
**Summary:** The speaker answers the question of whether the Lord knows the future with both yes and no, explaining that while we are bound by certain limitations, we also have free will to make choices that can alter our destiny, using the analogy of a cow tied to a pole with a rope of varying lengths to illustrate the degree of freedom we possess.

Yes or No? What is the right answer? The answer is both Yes and No. Why? The example is given that, When a cow is tied to a pole with a rope, If the rope which is tying the cow to a pole is 5 meters long, The cow has freedom of moving in a 5 meter radius freely. But beyond 5 meter, the rope restricts that cow. Beyond 5 meters. Am I right? Yes or No? All of us are bound. And all of us have an invisible rope tying us. It could be 3 meters. It could be 5 meters. It could be 10 meters. It could be 0 meters. If the rope length is 0 meters, What does that mean? You are so restricted. The example is given like a tree. It's just standing in one place. Cannot move. It's a living entity like you and me. It's an Atma. It's just standing there. Cannot move. It's raining. It's thunderstorms are coming. It cannot move. And so here we have example of a living entity where length of the rope is practically 0. Cannot move. Cannot do anything. So we also are bound and free to different degrees. Some people are so bound, so bound, the Lord can very clearly say, He will not come. He cannot come because He is so bad. And some of us, just like the cow, Has a freedom within 5 meter radius. We are also free. We can exercise our free will. And choose to come or not come. And the Lord knows. So the answer is both yes and no. It is not that fatalistic. Means everything is hard-coded. We have a free will. Based on the choices we make every moment, Our future destiny is changing. Everything is not written in destiny. So I am just a puppet. I have no choice. Nothing I can do. It's all written in my fate. That's wrong. Every moment we have a choice. If we make the right choices, The length of the rope increases. If we make wrong choices, The length of the rope decreases. If we continuously make wrong, wrong, wrong choices, The length of rope becomes practically 0. So the more the length of the rope, The Lord has given that exercise of free will to the living entity, He can choose.

#### Free will, Choice, Duty, Dependence on the Lord, Karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7M-mLHpRhfk?t=1770
**Verse References:** BG 2.47

**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a father giving pocket money to a child to illustrate how the Lord grants us free will, and quotes George Washington's secret to success as working and praying as if everything depends on oneself and the Lord, and emphasizes the importance of doing our duty with enthusiasm while depending on the Lord, quoting the verse 'Karma ne eva adhikar, Ma phaleshu kadachana, Ma karma phalahetur bhur' to highlight the balance between action and detachment from results.

Just like even in the father, And the Lord, Just like a father gives some pocket money to a child, If he uses it properly, The father will give more money. If he knows that the son has taken that money, And gone to a bar, And spoiled that money, And got into a bad habit, He will restrict that supply. So George Washington, Who was the President of the United States, Was asked, What's the secret of your success? He was very successful, President of the United States. So somebody asked him, What's the secret of your success? You become President of the United States, Almost like the number one man in the world. So he said, I work, I pray, As if everything is dependent on, The Lord. And then, With a smile, He says, I work, As if everything is dependent on, We sometimes act, In the pendulum, Bhoga, Tyaga. Everything is dependent on me. And sometimes, We go to the other extreme. Everything is dependent on, God. The right understanding is, We depend on Lord in our activities, But, We do our duty. That's why there's a verse which says, Karma ne eva adhikar, Ma phaleshu kadachana, Ma karma phalahetur bhur. Do not think yourself, The cause of the result of your activities, And further Krishna says, And do not be a devout, And do not be attached to, Not doing your duty, Because we, What motivates us to do our actions, Is the results. If the results, You are saying, Don't be attached to results, Then what is the motivation to act? Therefore Krishna says, Do not be attached to, Not doing your duty. So, We have to do our duty, With lot of, Enthusiasm. But simultaneously, Depend on, The Lord. Have that feeling that, Not a blade of grass moves, Without his sanction. That doesn't mean, Not a blade of grass moves, Without, Let me go and sleep. He will move the grass. Not a blade of grass moves, Without his sanction, But I will have to do my, Duty.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### The Ultimate Benediction I HG Chanchalapati Dasa I SB 5.3.13 I 18.06.2021
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### pure devotional service, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oyHxOJquSjU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maharaj Nabhi is so desirous of having a son that he is worshipping You for a son although You can offer him any exalted position including elevation to the heavenly planets or liberation back to Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse and explains that King Nabhi desires a son like Vishnu, which is a form of sense gratification, while a pure devotee desires only to engage in the Lord's loving service.

OM NAMO BHAGAVATE VASUDEVAYA. Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 5, Chapter 3, Text No. 13. KINCHAYAM RAJAR SHRIR APATTYAKAMAHA RAJAM BHAVADRISHIM AASHASANAM EESHVARAM AASHISHAM SWARGAPAVARGAYORAPI BHAVANTAM UPADHAVATI PRAJAYAM ARTHAPRATTYAYAHA DHANADAM IBADHANAH PALIKARANAM. Translation and purport by H.R. B.K. By His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada. Dear Lord, here is the great King Nabhi whose ultimate goal in life is to have a son like You. Your Lordship, his position is like that of a person approaching a very rich man and begging for a little grain. Maharaj Nabhi is so desirous of having a son that he is worshipping You for a son although You can offer him any exalted position including elevation to the heavenly planets or liberation back to Godhead.

---
#### pure devotion, material desire, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oyHxOJquSjU?t=92
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The principle of pure devotional service as taught by Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is to have no desire of our own except to please Krishna and to serve Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the priests were ashamed that King Nabhi was performing a sacrifice just to ask for a son, and contrasts this with Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's teachings, emphasizing the importance of pure devotional service without material desires.

Purport by Srila Prabhupada. The priests were a little ashamed that King Nabhi was performing a great sin and a sacrifice just to ask the Lord's benediction for a son. The Lord could offer him promotion to the heavenly planets or the Vaikuntha planets. Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu has taught us how to approach the Supreme Lord and ask him for the ultimate benediction. He said, He did not want to ask the Supreme Lord for anything material. Material opulence means riches, a nice family, a good wife and many followers. But an intelligent devotee doesn't ask the Supreme Lord for anything material. His only prayer is marjanmani. He wants to be engaged perpetually in the loving service of the Lord. He does not want promotion to the heavenly planets or mukti, liberation from material bondage. If this were the case, Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu would not have said, Mama Janmani Janmani, it doesn't matter to a devotee whether or not he takes birth life after life, as long as he remains a devotee. Actually, eternal liberty means returning home back to Godhead. A devotee is never concerned about anything material. Although Nabhi Maharaj wanted a son like Vishnu, wanting a son like God is also a form of sense gratification. A pure devotee wants only to engage in the Lord's loving service. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purpose. So we are reading in this chapter, the priests in the sacrificial arena of King Nabhi, offering prayers to Lord Vishnu, who has made his personal presence there. And these priests, in their prayers, have been bringing up very wonderful concepts and topics of pure devotional service. And in today's verse also, they are addressing the Lord and talking about very important principles of pure devotional service. Prabhupada mentions in the purport, the priests were a little ashamed. This is what was in their heart. They were ashamed. Kinchayam Rajarshir Apatyakamaha. Apatya, offspring, children. Apatyakamaha. Our great king. He is Raja Rishi, but he is Apatyakamaha. He has a desire to have a son. Prajam Bhavadrishim. And he wants a son like you. Bhavadidrishim Bhavadrishim. Bhavadidrishim Asha Asana. That is his desire. And he has approached you, O Lord. Ishwaram, Ishwara. And who are you? Ashabashwara. You are somebody who can give. You for Apatyakamaha. Bhavantam Upatiyakamaha. Bhavantam Upadayateyakamaha. Bhawantam Upadayatyakamaha. Bhavantam Upadayatyakamaha. Bhavantam Bhaavantam idesya upadhavati and he is worshiping you organize this big sacrifice for him to get a son is the highest achievement in life regarding as the ultimate goal of life so we think this is like means to give away like we say bhakti tulasi maharani is one who can give bhakti so dhanadham so a rich man who can give dhanada who can give dhana a poor man who has no dhana so a poor man has gone to a rich man who can give anything and he is asking him please give some husk husk has no value so this is the prayer of the priests dear lord here is the great king whose ultimate goal in life is to have a son a son like you your lordship his position is like that of a person approaching a very rich man and begging for a little grain maharaj nabi is so desirous of having a son that he is worshipping you for a son prajayam artha pratyayaha bhavantam upadhavati for a son prajayam artha pratyayaha bhavantam upadhavati for a son prajayam artha pratyayaha bhavantam upadhavati you can offer him even though you can offer him any exalted position including below elevation to the heavenly planets swarga or liberation liberation back to god live him with me so srimad bhagavatam is setting a very high device is meal that will create a Ef assumed oscillation spectrum and happiness for all of them a very high standard. He is teaching a very high standard of devotional service, which is very rare. Generally, people approach God, people take to religion, some kind of religiosity with an aim to get some material benefit. We see that happening in our country. There are people who go, they start a business and then they pray to God and they go to a temple. My business should expand. I should become successful in my business. Sometimes people go to some temple or some worship to get a son to get a child. Sometimes they do some Ratha to get married, to get a good wife, to get a good husband. #### Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, detachment, materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oyHxOJquSjU?t=739
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the teachings of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu and Srila Prabhupada, highlighting the importance of detachment from material desires and focusing on the loving service of the Lord, using the analogy of asking a rich man for husk.

To accept this is important. That is why Srila Prabhupada has quoted Chaitanya Mahaprabhu has taught us how to offer a prayer and he has quoted this famous Shikshashtakam verse, na dhanam, na janam, na sundarim, kavitam vajagadishakam. Kavitam, kavitam means poetry. Vedic literatures are full of beautiful poetry which is describing fruitive activities, dhanam, janam, sundarim and mukti. So Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is saying na dhanam, na janam, na sundarim, kavitam vajagadishakamayain, I'm not desiring any of these nakamayain. Mama janmani janmani isvare bhavata bhakti rahe itukitvayain. Life after life which means not even liberation, I just want to engage perpetually in the loving service of the Lord. This is the highest law. This is the highest level of devotional prayer that Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is teaching. And Prabhupada often gave this example. If you go and ask Krishna, give me this, give me that, material things, it is like going and asking a dhanadham, a rich man who is very charitably disposed. He is not only dhani, he is not a dhanadham. He wants to give. And a poor man, adhanaha, he goes and he asks, phalikaranam eva, please give me some husk. Prabhupada also gave some examples, please give me some ashes from your house. So this is not considered very intelligent when that man is so rich and he can give so many things. He can ask for something very insignificant.

So the principle of chatushpurushartha is this. You practice some religiosity, dharma, with that you will get artha. And with artha, economic development, you will get karma, fulfillment of your desires. And when your desires are fulfilled, gradually, you can even plan to get mukti, freedom from all material problems. This is dharma artha kama moksha, is considered purushartha, purusha, human beings, and artha, intent, the purpose of our life is these things. But Chaitanya Mahaprabhu teaches differently. In fact, when Chaitanya Mahaprabhu teaches differently, he teaches in a different way. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu describes this in Kashi. He says, when I met my spiritual master, Eswara Puri, in gaya, he told me this instruction. Krishna Vishayika Prema Parama Purushartha To develop love for Krishna, Krishna Vishayika Prema Parama Purushartha. Purushartha. This is the highest Purushartha. Yara Aage Trunatulya Chari Purushartha. In front of this Krishna Vishayika Prema, that Chari Purushartha, the four Dharmartha Kama Moksha, Yara Aage Trunatulya. They are like insignificant grass, straw in the street. See the same principle, like husk. So the Bhagavata's principle, and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu composed his Shrikshashtakam. And coming in the parampara of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Srila Prabhupada taught us that this is not what we should be asking.

Our living, our activities, We should be absorbed in Krishna Vishaya, our living, our activities, everything, our goals, this is important. everything, our goal of life, nothing but Krishna Vishaya. We want to please Krishna. We want to develop devotion to Krishna. We want to understand Krishna. We want to read Krishna's teachings. We want to remember Krishna's Leela. So many institutions are there. This institution is for Krishna Vishaya. We are so fortunate. In 1971, Srila Prabhupada wrote to one of the GBCs. He had just formed the GBC a few months ago. So like this I was recently preaching to somebody that why we say Krishna Krishna Vishaya because Krishna is the source of everything Krishna Vishaya Krishna Vishaya Krishna Vishaya Vishaya Krishna Vishaya Vishaya Vishaya So I was telling this to an intelligent person, Krishna is the source of everything. And then he asked me, oh, if Krishna is the source of everything, is Krishna the source of the intelligence in a criminal's mind? So if a criminal is getting some idea that I should commit some crime, Krishna is the source of that also? Is he the source of everything you are saying? I said, yes, Krishna is the source of everything. Krishna gives the intelligence to a criminal also, because the living entity wants to commit that crime. When the living entity is so much desirous of committing the crime, Krishna will provide all arrangements. So how can Krishna allow that? That is why we must understand Krishna's position. What is Krishna's position? Krishna explains in the 9th chapter, creation, destruction, all this is happening again and again. And I am situated, Udāsīnavat āsīnaḥ I am situated indifferent to it. So much of creation, destruction, this is continuously happening. So much death is happening. Not only human death, we get so many around us. So many little, little insects and bugs and birds and animals, all dying. Rebirth is happening. Udāsīnavat āsīnaḥ Not only the Lord is like that. You see, this is Krishna's characteristic. In 9th chapter, Krishna is describing His characteristics. And He is saying, Udāsīnavat āsīnaḥ

### The Ultimate Knowledge | BG 7.01 - 7.03 | HG Amitasana Das
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

By the time the rice boiled, the brown rice had become black rice. None of you would have, so to say, eaten black rice. Brown rice I can understand. It became black rice. All that what was sticking to the pan, the black thing which has never been washed, all got into the rice. Now we had an option to eat or not to eat. You know which option we took? Eat. And believe me, first few bites were so relishable. And after eating few bites, I said, my God, what it is. It tastes so good. Because when a person is hungry, he will eat anything. The same rice if we had an option, you know, where we had plethora of things available to us, none of us would have touched it. So same is the case when we have no vocabulary of higher form of happiness, which is so full of joy, mind full of bliss. #### Lord Shiva, Benedictions, Demoniac Mentality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wzgxKs3DjXY?t=2970
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "I want to touch your head"
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about a devotee of Lord Shiva who asked for a boon to kill anyone he touched, illustrating the dangers of asking for boons in ignorance and the demoniac mentality of wanting to harm even the one who granted the boon.

Lord Shiva is a great devotee of the Lord, and he is very, very liberal in giving benedictions, giving boons. So one time, there was a devotee of Lord Shiva. He prayed and prayed and prayed to Lord Shiva, and eventually Lord Shiva became pleased. He says, "What do you want?" So he says, "Give me a boon that whosoever's head I touch, he should get killed, he should get destroyed." So Lord Shiva, again, he is ashutosh, he is very liberal. He gave him that benediction, and the moment he got this benediction, this boon, you know what did this person do? He went towards Lord Shiva. He says, "Hey, what are you doing? What are you up to?" He says, "I want to touch your head." And Lord Shiva, now he is already given, he is already released, so he got up from his seat and started running. So he went to another person, he was a great devotee, he understood the whole thing what has happened, and he asked him, "What are you up to?" He says, "I have got this benediction that whosoever I touch the head, he will be destroyed, he will be killed." So this person understood, now Lord Shiva is fleeing and this person is behind him, he wants to kill the very person from whom he has got the boon. This is all demoniac mentality. So then this person says, "Come on, how can Lord Shiva give this boon to you?" He says, "No, he has given the boon. Come on, how can that be possible? Touch your head and see." So he touched his head and he himself got destroyed. So many times, you know, people get boons, but that boon may not be in their self-interest, sometimes it can lead to self-destruction because the boons are asked in ignorance without knowing what is really important for us, what is really our self-interest. So 25% are religious, but it is predominantly for give me this, give me that.

Now, first chapters of the Gita which we have covered talks about the knowledge, transcendental knowledge, knowledge about soul, knowledge about God, knowledge about yoga systems which can elevate the soul towards God. And this balance six chapters now which we are going to cover which will enable us to understand Lord in tatvataha, in truth, which Krishna says is very difficult to know. It's very difficult to know Krishna in tatvataha. Tatvata means in truth. This we have covered and he says bhaktiya maam abhijanati, only through devotion, only through bhakti can I be known as I am. Janma karma cha may divyam evam yo veti tatvataha, janma karma cha may divyam even yo veti tatvataha, one who understands my janma, janma means birth, karma means activities, cha means and, me divyam, divyam means transcendental, divine, even yo veti tatvataha, one who understands in truth, tyaktva deham, after he gives up his body, when he dies, He will not get birth again in this material. So in other words, if we understand Janma Karma Chame Divyam, lot of people ask, you are talking about Krishna, you are saying he is great, he is this thing, he danced with Gopis, something similar to what happened with the Dehrasoda, how can he be God? He is breaking his promises, he is stealing butter, that's why Krishna says Janma Karma Chame Divyam, Divyam means divine. What meets the eye, what we see is not the truth. One who understands that Janma Karma Chame Divyam, evam yo veti tattvata, one who understands in truth, tyaktva deham, after he gives up this body, na eti so eti arjuna, he doesn't

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that following the path of devotion with strict adherence to rules and regulations, as recommended by acharyas and authorized Vedic literatures, is not easy but necessary for achieving the desired results.

get birth again in this world. So non-devotees declare. They declare that this path of bhakti or devotion, service is an easy thing, it's a sentimental stuff, bhakti, ashtanga yoga with rigid postures, all this yama, niyama, asana, that's really something which is difficult to do but all this bhakti, you chant the names of Krishna, you do some aarti, you worship him, you remember him, all this is sentimental stuff, it's easy. So here it is said, to follow the real process with all the rules and regulations, strictly, as per the recommendations given by great acharyas, it's not easy. And there's a verse which says, Shruti Smriti Purana Adi Pancharatri Ki Vidhim Vina, Vidhim means method, prescription, Vina means without, the prescription given in Shruti Smriti, Shruti means the Vedic literature. So here it is said, to follow the real process with all the rules and regulations, strictly, as per the recommendations given by great acharyas, it's not easy. traitorous, they are also called shruti. Smriti means the puranas. Purana Adi Pancharatri Ki Vidhim Vina, the method shown by the acharyas in this from Shruti Smriti Purana Adi Pancharatri Ki Vidhim Vina, aikantikhir hrikakti, if someone just speculates and comes with his own process of worship, aikaantikhir means in a lonely place, harirbhakti ut patiaiva kalpate, means it only creates disturbance. That's it. It doesn't get the right result, it just creates nuisance. So, if we have to follow the right process, authorised process, follow the rules and regulations, and practice the processes of devotional service, we will get the desired results. The devotional service of the Lord that ignores authorised Vedic literatures like Upanishads, Puranas, Narada, Pancharatra, etc. is simply an unnecessary disturbance in the society.

We will stop here. Anyone has any question? It looks very discouraging when you say there are millions and millions of people, one attains perfection. So, common person might say, oh my God, it is not possible for me. So, do I give up or try to be that one? Just like, you get an Olympic gold medal. Or for that matter, I was reading an article, Wimbledon, tennis. You know how many people world over are playing tennis? Lakhs and lakhs. I met a person today, one boy, he was in dilemma. He says, I want to be professional cricketer. You know how many people would be aspiring to become professional cricketer in India? Millions. How many get into Indian cricket team? Who will become like Virat Kohli and earn some crores of rupees? That is a competition. Here, it should be that anyone who comes to me. Unfortunately, unfortunately, in that case, it is here Krishna is saying, in fact, as we move forward, there is going to be a question by Arjuna. He says, what is the state of a person who treads this path but does not complete the process? He does not achieve perfection. Then he is neither here nor there. Then Krishna says, there is no loss for him because whatever he has achieved is his permanent credit. So in other words, when we strive for perfection and tread this path, Krishna also is not expecting that you will achieve perfection and this knowledge in one life. But whatever you have done is your permanent credit. We start from where we have left in the previous life.

**Key Quote:** "Chanting and reading every day."
**Summary:** The speaker recommends starting with two simple daily practices: chanting and reading, emphasizing that these activities accumulate spiritual credits that are eternal assets.

Yes. What are the simple ways to do on a daily basis? I mean, there could be four or five things on a daily basis to do. I will say, start with two things only. Four things I will tell you, mind will get little this thing. Only two things you start. Only two things. What are the two things? Chanting and reading every day, or hearing every day. Two things, simple things. I would say, even if you start with half an hour a day, guaranteed, guaranteed, you will see a tangible transformation happening very shortly. Chanting for half an hour. Chanting, ideally for half an hour. Last time I said four rounds. How many of us started? It's very good. Chant for half an hour. We all find time for so many things. Let's find time for the real thing. It will really benefit us. And with the conviction, with the faith, that whatever credit I am getting by this chanting, is my eternal asset. When you leave this body, money will not come with you. Your degrees will not come with you. Your relatives will not come with you. What will come with you is your spiritual credits. And that spiritual credit will help you to get a good birth, where you can continue from where you have left in this previous life. So we had a session called Permanent Bank Account. If you know there is a bank account where the bank can get, so to say, the money is not safe, or the bank can get closed, you will be reluctant to deposit money there. But here we are investing a lot of our time and energy for this body, which is anyway going to dismantle one day. So spiritual activities are our permanent credit. Let us accumulate as much as you can while we have that ability.

---
#### Chanting, Morning Time, Satva Guna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wzgxKs3DjXY?t=3940
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Morning time is ideal for chanting?"
**Summary:** The speaker answers a question about the best time for chanting, explaining that morning time is ideal because it is associated with Satva Guna, which is conducive to spiritual activities.

Any other question? Are we supposed to do on a specific time only, or we can do on the, where we are travelling? Chanting, ideally, you should do in the morning time, ideally. Although you can do it in the evening also. There is no hard and fast rule. But morning time is... Anyone can tell me why morning time is ideal for chanting? Or for that, any spiritual activity? Yes. Because people are sleeping. Because people are sleeping. Therefore it is ideal time. Okay. Focus and concentration. Anybody else? Less distraction. Yes. 4 and 6 am is considered to be the Brahma Murta which is conducive for spiritual activity and the blessings come more equally. Yes, you are right. Brahma Murta time. Another thing is morning time is... Satva Guna is predominant. As the day passes, Raja Guna becomes predominant. And in the night time, Tamo Guna becomes prominent. So in night time, you know, you have all these bars, pubs, disco clubs. You don't have temples opening at 11.30 in the night. You know. So morning time, that Satva Guna, Stitam Satve Prasidati. Remember? Bhagavad Prasanna Manoso Stitam Satve Prasidati. Automatically that time is is full with Satva Guna. So when we chant in Satva Guna, it helps us. Otherwise, Raja Guna and Tamo Guna influence is there.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### The importance of a genuine spiritual master I Sri Amitasana Dasa I SB 1.1.8 I 17.03.2023
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### hearing from authority, spiritual intelligence, evidence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B524TpV5tyY?t=209
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The first class, the highest, the most intelligent are people who hear and realize, but not hear from ordinary person, hear from authorities, exalted personalities like Suta Goswami."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the different classes of intelligence based on how one learns and accepts knowledge, emphasizing the importance of hearing from authoritative sources rather than relying solely on sensory perception and mental speculation.

The first class, the highest, the most intelligent are people who hear and realize, but not hear from ordinary person, hear from authorities, exalted personalities like Suta Goswami. That's first class. We don't have to make mistakes and then learn. We don't have to make costly mistakes. Life is very short. Human form of life which is very rarely achieved, after many, many lifetimes, it's also very short. Therefore, Shastras say, 'anuvrttih yavān', we have got human birth, but it will not last, 'tūrnam yateta', so our scriptures, those who are wise, those who are intelligent, those who are self-realized, they are expressing some kind of urgency, 'tūrnam', 'tūrnam' means very quickly, 'yateta', 'nipateta anuvrttih yavān', before we again fall into the clutches of death, we have some very important goal to accomplish. So first class is, who hear, and hear from authorities, not common man, and accept what they say, submissively hear. Sometimes people also hear, but there is an element of, how can we, what is the proof, what is the evidence, when initially people come, then they speak about soul, what is the evidence, this life after death, may be true, may not be true. So in other words, they are very much, that's the stage, they are very much wanting an evidence, what they can perceive with their senses, they can fathom with their mind, then only they will accept.

#### spiritual intelligence, experiential learning, acceptance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B524TpV5tyY?t=409

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker continues to describe the different classes of intelligence, explaining that second-class individuals learn by observing others' experiences, third-class individuals learn only through personal experience, and the fourth class, the 'mudas', fail to learn even from their own experiences; he emphasizes the importance of striving for first-class intelligence by eagerly hearing from sages and approaching scriptures with a submissive attitude.

So, second-class intelligent people are people who don't hear and understand, they have to see others experiencing that, and then this may be what was told is true. See experience, see others and then they understand material world is miserable, they see that there is lot of misery and some miserable things happen in the life so understand yes what Shastra is saying is correct. So in second class, third class he hears, does not understand, sees all around, does not understand, it has to happen to him, has to happen to him like for example the father is telling a child don't play with fire it will burn, doesn't understand then puts his hand in the fire and then he understands so that's third class intelligence he has to experience that misery seeing the world miserable he will not understand otherwise all doctors will be self realized especially those who are working in cancer hospitals or the people who are doing post mortem. So third class intelligence is you have to experience and then you realize and the fourth class is mudas it happens they experience still they don't realize so we should be humble enough to accept we belong to sometime we come to third class, very rarely to second class and what is expected is we have to be first class intelligence like the sages they are very eager to hear from. So that goes forward and hearing and reading is same even when we read Prabhupada's books, we have to with that mood with that feeling, let me hear and read submissively, let me realize what, let me realize what, let me realize what.

#### animalistic tendencies, human purpose, introspection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B524TpV5tyY?t=735
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If in human form of life, we also end up doing these activities only, but maybe in a very refined, sophisticated, polished way, then the purpose of human form of life is defeated."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on the four basic activities of eating, sleeping, mating, and defending, common to both animals and humans, and argues that if humans only engage in these activities, even in a refined manner, they miss the purpose of human life, which is to inquire into the absolute truth.

Morning to night, they are only busy with these four activities. What are the four activities? Eating, sleeping, mating, procreation and defending. So I could see that there was nothing which was happening in that 15 to 20 minutes which was out of the purview of these four activities. It was all about eating, it was all about sleeping, defending and Lord has also been very kind, each animal has been blessed with some kind of defence mechanism. Even delicate animals like tortoise, it's very delicate tortoise, it has by Lord's arrangement a very thick shell. Although it can move very slowly, but to defend itself whenever there is some kind of an attack, it just withdraws the senses, the limbs and the shell is very very thick. So Lord has made that arrangement. There is a requirement, eating, sleeping and every animal, some animals with a very delicate body, they can run very fast. So if in human form of life, we also end up doing these activities only, but maybe in a very refined, sophisticated, polished way, then the purpose of human form of life is defeated. Prabhupada uses the word dvipada, which means animal, animals are moving around four legs, we are maybe sophisticated, refined, cultured animals, but the difference is we walk on two legs. Now this is, if we have first class intelligence, there is a very very important point being made in scriptures. Why they are saying? We have plethora of activities, we have sports, we have games, we have adventures, we have so many things going on, we get educated, we go to schools, we go to colleges, we have hobbies, we have so much. You know, activities going around, today's world, events, debates, politics, it's all, if we do little bit of introspection, all these broadly get classified in these four categories.

#### human potential, spiritual inquiry, miserliness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B524TpV5tyY?t=1186
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Likewise, our scriptures point out that if our activities predominantly morning to night, we are using our intelligence to improve these four activities and is utilized in engaging in these activities, we have missed the important point."
**Summary:** The speaker defines 'Kripana' as one who doesn't utilize their human life to understand the Supreme Lord, despite being equipped with the intelligence to do so, and contrasts this with a 'Brahmana' who understands the value of human life and uses it for spiritual inquiry.

So likewise, our scriptures point out that if our activities predominantly morning to night, we are using our intelligence to improve these four activities and is utilized in engaging in these activities, we have missed the important point. We have missed the purpose of human form of life. And such a person, in the scriptures it is called as Kripana. Kripana means? The Kripana is, the opposite is? Brahmana. Brahmana means who understands the value of human life and utilizes that human life for inquiring. Adhato Brahma Jijnasa, which is what the sages are doing here, wanting to inquire from a person who is self-realized. That is the purpose of human form of life. And a person who does not utilize his life to understand about the Supreme Lord, about the transmutable reality, which we are qualified to, which we are entitled to, which we are equipped to. We have been blessed with that kind of an intelligence. That's how we are different from animals. If we don't utilize our intelligence for inquiring into the absolute truth about the Supreme Lord, we are called Kripana.

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/B524TpV5tyY?t=1490
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Surrender means, yes, we may take time to identify a bona fide, authorized person, but because what he is speaking is beyond the realm, what I can judge based on my senses, it is not blind faith, that we accept submissively what he is saying."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the 'Kripana' with the sages who eagerly hear from Suta Goswami, emphasizing the importance of submissive hearing from a bona fide spiritual master, and clarifies that accepting the words of such a master is not blind faith but a reasoned faith based on the master's authenticity and lack of ulterior motives.

So, now here in contrast, we are seeing how the sages, first class intelligence. They have selected Suta Goswami, and they are saying, because you have simply heard from your spiritual masters, we want to, eagerly, they are eager to hear from him. And, they are also saying that, you have been blessed with this realization, because of, anyone can help me with an answer? He has been blessed with the realization, because of being a gentle disciple. None of us are gentle, we are not born submissive. These are things which, as we practice the principles of Krishna consciousness, we will develop, be gentle, be humble, be submissive, we have to cultivate that. More so, okay, we all have doubts, we should not blindly believe anything, we should not put blindly faith. But, once we have realized that this person is authorized, is bona fide, is coming in a bona fides, then we should not every word, every instruction, every statement, we judge. That is not the right. Surrender means, yes, we may take time to identify a bona fide, authorized person. But because what he is speaking is beyond the realm, what I can judge based on my senses, it is not blind faith, that we accept submissively what he is saying. That is what spiritual life is. Now Prabhupada in his book is talking about the spiritual world. We cannot in that sense give evidence and go and verify and then believe. Having heard that he is pure, he has no ulterior motive, he doesn't have any hidden agenda, he is not here to cheat us, he is not here with some any selfish agenda. He is submissively here. And first class intelligence is whatever he says, we accept it. And when we do that, it is not called blind faith. This is the reason why we are putting faith.

## HG Amitasana Dasa

### Theory of Everything | Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9 Verses 1 to 3 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
**Speaker:** HG Amitasana Dasa

#### confidential knowledge, non-envy, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=19
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "I shall impart to you this most secret wisdom, knowing which you shall be relieved of the miseries of material existence."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the ninth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, focusing on the confidential knowledge Krishna imparts to Arjuna because of his non-envious nature, promising liberation from material miseries.

We are going to start with the 9th chapter of Gita today, verse 1 to 3. Anyone know what is the name of the chapter? Yes, the most confidential knowledge. So, based on the teachings of His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada, we will also chant this verse together. This is the first verse from the 9th chapter, please repeat after me. Shri Bhagwan Vacha. Idam tu devu yadamam. Pravakshyami anasuyave. Gyanam vijnanam sahitam. Yajnatva mokshate shubhah. The Supreme Lord said, My dear Arjuna, because you are never envious of Me, anyone know the Sanskrit word used in these words? Anasuyave. Anasuyave means non-envious. I shall impart to you this most secret wisdom, knowing which you shall be relieved of the miseries of material existence. Mokshate. Mokshate means to free. Moksha means freedom.

---
#### material existence, suffering, envy of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=102
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All of us, all of us are envious of God."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of climbing a mountain full of thorns to illustrate the miseries of material existence and emphasizes that everyone is, to varying degrees, envious of God.

Ashubha. Now this material existence has a lot of miseries. We discussed in our previous session that if a person is trying to climb a mountain which is full of thorns, pebbles, sharp rocks, and he is getting pierced with those thorns, one thing is, yes, we can help him with first aid when the thorns get pierced. But then, if he continues like this with barefoot, again and again it is going to pierce with thorns. So we talked about two things which a person can do. What are the two things? Wear slippers. Wear slippers, yes. And try for a different path. So while we are in this material world, if we take shelter of the Lord, which Arjuna has taken, because he is non-envious of the Lord, Krishna is saying, now Arjuna is not a brahmana, Arjuna is not a renunciant, he is not a celibate, he is a kshatriya, he is a warrior, but Krishna chose him to be the recipient of this most confidential knowledge, the secret of all secrets. The qualifying factor is, Arjuna is non-envious of Krishna. Don't feel bad, I am asking this question. How many of us are envious of God? I repeat, I think you haven't understood the question. I repeat, how many of us are envious of God? All of us, all of us are envious of God. Can anyone tell me why or how? How are we envious?

---
#### envy of God, desire, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=226
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When we develop desire and dvesha, envy, then we come to this material world, where we are trying to be the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that envy of God manifests as the desire to be the center of attraction and enjoyer, rather than glorifying the Lord, and that the process of purification involves becoming less envious.

In this world, we are trying to be like God. We are trying hard to be the centre of attraction, to be glorified. Our constitutional position is, we glorify the Lord. We are His amsa. The Lord is centre of attraction. But we see, in gross and subtle ways, we are, it requires some element of introspection, some deep reflection. We all are, again, not to feel bad about it, that's our present state, we all are to different degrees envious of God. When we develop desire, and dvesha, envy, then we come to this material world, where we are trying to be the Lord, trying to be the enjoyer, trying to be the centre of attraction, trying to be glorified, rather than glorifying the Lord, rather than keeping the Lord in the centre, we are attempting to be the centre. We want fame for us. We want people to glorify us. And this will always result in unhappiness. Now Krishna is telling Arjuna, I am telling this science to you because it is non-envious. So this is the purification process, which we are undertaking. As we purify ourselves, we will become less and less envious of the Lord.

---
#### confidential knowledge, pure devotion, alloyed devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=350
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whereas in this chapter, we are going to discuss about pure unalloyed devotion to the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the confidential nature of the knowledge in the ninth chapter, contrasting pure, unalloyed devotion with devotion mixed with material desires.

Now the name of the chapter is, The Most Confidential Knowledge. This is the book what we read the previous cycle, Rajvidya, was based on the 9th chapter. Rajvidya was the king of knowledge. Now this chapter deals with pure unalloyed devotion, or surrender to the Lord. What is the meaning of alloy? We all heard about this word alloy. What is alloy? Mixture of metals. So, just like you have an alloy in which two metals get mixed, likewise, there could be devotion to the Lord, surrender to the Lord, mixed with so many things. Somebody could surrender to the Lord, but it is mixed with desire to be famous. Somebody could surrender to the Lord, but it is mixed with desire to be happy. Lord is a means to an end. End is what my happiness. So called religiosity, what we see in this world, is all mixed. Even though it is service to the Lord, even though we see there is an element of bhakti, people go and stand in queues, they do some different sevas, they do different abhishekas, all that thing, but ultimately the end objective is me and mine. Lord is a means to an end. And end is me. Whereas in this chapter, we are going to discuss about pure unalloyed devotion to the Lord. And the word used in this verse is which means non-envious. That is one of the very very important factor for us to understand Lord in Tatvataha.

#### devotional service, transcendental consciousness, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=531
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Unless we elevate and purify the consciousness, we become qualified to understand the science of Krishna, science of God."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the nine processes of devotional service, emphasizing how they elevate consciousness from a material to a transcendental state, ultimately leading to purification and understanding of Krishna.

Now there are nine processes of devotional service. Starting with, anyone remembers? Shravananda. Hearing. Hearing about the Lord. We come to Gita life to hear about the Lord, His instructions, His words. Now, when we follow this path, starting with Shravananda, Kirtananda, Smarananda, we elevate our consciousness towards transcendental consciousness. I will come to this word transcendental. What is the meaning of the word transcendental? So there is material world and there is spiritual world. So right now our consciousness is trapped. Material consciousness. Rooted in I and mind. Aham, Mumevi. And gradually as we purify ourselves, we transcend this bodily consciousness. Rooted in I and mind. So that consciousness called transcendental, that consciousness called transcendental consciousness. And when we elevate our consciousness, right now we are at the very base level of consciousness. In one sense, all the activities what we are doing, even animals do. Maybe not in a very sophisticated way. Eating they also do. Sleeping they also do. Mating they also do. May not be in a very elaborate, gorgeous way. Defending they also do. May not be through intercontinental ballistic missiles. May be through claws or barking. So our consciousness is more or less like animal consciousness. Unless we elevate and purify the consciousness. And when we elevate and purify that consciousness, we become qualified to understand the science of Krishna, science of God. And then what happens is, moksha se ashubha. All that clutter, all that dirt, all that meshes of actions and reactions which have entangled us. Bhagavatam gives example of a cocoon, silk worm. It builds a cocoon and gets entangled. So our state is like that. So we get freed from all this unwanted contaminations.

#### king of knowledge, secret knowledge, pure knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=691
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This knowledge is the king of education, the most secret of all secrets. It is the purest knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to explain the second verse of the ninth chapter, emphasizing that the knowledge of the Gita is the king of education, the most secret, and the purest knowledge.

And purify our consciousness. Text 2. Raja Vidya Raja Guhyam. This knowledge is the king of education, the most secret of all secrets. It is the purest knowledge. And because it gives direct perception of the self by realization. It is the perfection of religion. It is everlasting. And it is joyfully performed. We will go word by word. Raja Vidya. Raja Vidya means the king of education. We have so many departments of knowledge. Every university has different departments of knowledge. But this knowledge is the topmost. It talks about who we are. What the focus of this world is. Who that Lord is, who is the creator of this world. Why this world is created. Where were we before we came in this world? Where do we go after death? These all appear to be questions which apparently have no clear answers. But believe me, Gita gives complete clarity to these questions. That's why it is called Raja Vidya. It is the king of education.

#### confidential knowledge, body vs soul, realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This confidential knowledge, this Raja Vidya, enables us to perceive the soul."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the knowledge of the Gita is confidential because it reveals the difference between the body and the soul, leading to direct perception and realization.

And it is most confidential. Most confidential because it helps us understand the difference between the body and the soul. Who we really are. We are that soul. We are not the body. The body is like a dress. It is a covering. When a person dies, we all cry, he is gone, he is gone, he is gone. Who is gone? The soul is gone. The body is there. All the cells which constitute the body, just fragments are still there. But we say he is gone. So this confidential knowledge, this Raja Vidya, enables us to perceive the soul. Although we may start with theoretical understanding, a time comes when it is no longer theoretical, it becomes Pratyaksha. Pratyaksha means evident. Pratyaksha. It is no longer theory. It is no longer some kind of belief or a faith. It is very, very evident. It is very, very clear. Just like when the sun rises, it is all foggy, the fogs dissipate, everything is clear. You can see things clearly. When it is fog, it is not very clear. So likewise our consciousness also is very foggy, clouded with ignorance.

#### modern civilization, understanding the soul, pure knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=873
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "This is the defect of modern civilization. You have missed the main point."
**Summary:** The speaker criticizes modern civilization for lacking a dedicated focus on understanding the soul, despite having numerous departments of knowledge, and emphasizes that the knowledge of the Gita is pure and transcendental.

Now we have, Prabhupada explains in this particular purport that we have different departments of knowledge in many, many universities all over the world. But in none of the universities you will find, you may have departments of religion, but you may not have department which is wholly and wholly focused on understanding of soul. This is the defect of modern civilization. You have missed the main point. The important thing, the most important thing minus which the body is a dead body, we cremate the body, we burn the body, we bury the body. That body which we value so much, minus the soul, when a person dies, we cremate the body. Unfortunately we have big, big departments of knowledge but there is no department which is dedicated to understanding of the soul. So this knowledge is also pavitra. Pavitra means purest. It's pure knowledge. And uttamam. Tama means, what is the meaning of tama? It's darkness. It transcends the darkness. It transcends the darkness which clouds our consciousness. That's why this knowledge is also called transcendental knowledge. Transcendental knowledge is also called spiritual knowledge. Our literature, Vedic literature is also called transcendental literature because they transcend material existence. Now the result of this knowledge is pratyaksha. Pratyaksha means it becomes evident. One can easily perceive it but one has to follow the process. And susukam kartum abhyayam. Susukam means susukam, it is joyfully performed.

#### joyful performance, devotional service, confidential knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=992
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This process, it's rajvidya, rajguhyam, it's the most confidential knowledge. It's pavitram, it's purest. Uttamam, it helps us transcend material existence."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the process of devotional service is joyfully performed, contrasting it with severe austerities, and reiterates that the knowledge of the Gita is confidential, pure, and helps transcend material existence.

So Prabhupada gives an example, just like shravanam, we all come, the next one and a half hours we'll hear, it's not very painful. Chanting is not painful. Kirtan is joyful. We dance, we sing, we can enjoy music. So this process, it's rajvidya, rajguhyam, it's the most confidential knowledge. It's pavitram, it's purest. Uttamam, it helps us transcend material existence. A time comes when the knowledge which is mentioned by the Lord becomes pratyaksha, evident. It is no longer some kind of a belief or a faith. And susukam karthum ahyayam, susukam means it is joyfully performed.

#### devotional service, joyful performance, cultural differences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=1039
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "They get up at 4 o'clock in the morning and they start the day with singing and dancing."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a story about a devotee in the US whose daily schedule of early morning devotional practices was met with curiosity and amusement, highlighting the joyful nature of the process.

I think I shared with all of you, one time we were making a documentary in the US. We have a very big auditorium in Bangalore. So we designed some animation and some animatronics. Some pastime, I've got a maja vina story to depict in animatronic form. So one of the devotees goes to the US, for about six months, working with different media people and all the people who make movies and things like that. So, they asked him, by the way, what's your typical schedule? How does your day start? Because they could see that from the dress we amount and have a different hairstyle, shaved heads. So different species. Not ordinary, not normal. So he says, typically how does your, so CP, his name was Chandrapati Das, he is the Vice Chairman of Akshaya Pada. So, in short, he was called CP. CP Das. So CP, how does your day start? So he says, well, we get up around 3.30 in the morning. 3.30? You've got to show off his life. 3.30? Then he says, yes, we get up at 3.30 and then we take a bath. So early in the morning, taking a bath, 3.30? There's something wrong with these people. Okay. And then he said, okay, then what happens? He says, then we all assemble in the temple hall. We have a temple hall, we have the deities, we have beautifully adorned deities of the Lord, Radha, Krishna and all that. Okay. Then he became very curious. Getting up in the middle of the night, taking a bath, going to the temple, what do you do then? He says, then they see a beautiful saw, glorifying the spiritual master. He became even more curious. Then what happens? He says, while taking the saw, we dance. He says, very typical people, you know, get up at 3.30 and then 4 o'clock in the morning, they start dancing. Typically, before the Mangala, you know, at some point of time, they would start dancing, glorifying the Lord. And then, so he heard all that song, somehow digested everything. And next day, there was another meeting and then he tried introducing CP Das to his colleague. And he says, yesterday I spoke to CP Das, these guys are fantastic. He says, what happened? He says, they get up at 4 o'clock in the morning and they start the day with singing and dancing.

#### joyful performance, conditioned state, pure religion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=1203
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Initially, when we start chanting, when we start cave dancing, we may not have taste because we are in a dizzy state right now."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the joyful performance of devotional service with the severe austerities of the past, acknowledging the initial difficulty in developing a taste for it due to our conditioned state, and emphasizing the importance of starting with the purest form of religion.

So, the point here is, this process is, unlike here in India, where people used to go to the forest and do very severe tapasya, you know, renounce everything, eat only barely and unlike that, joyfully perform, sing, dance, hear about the Lord. Initially, yes, because we are conditioned, it's, initially, there is an escape velocity, just like the rocket lifts off, the amount of fuel it consumes in the initial few seconds is much higher than the amount of fuel it consumes in the initial few seconds. So, you know, the amount of fuel it consumes in the initial few seconds is much, much more than hours of travelling in the orbit. So, initially, when we start chanting, when we start cave dancing, we may not have taste because we are in a dizzy state right now. We have got taste for different things right now. So, then there is a shift happening, it is a little difficult in the beginning and then once we get taste for this, then it is difficult to go back to the original state. So, we have to start with the purest form of religion and it can be executed joyfully without difficulty. Now, there are different forms of religion. Srimad Bhagavatam begins that this Srimad Bhagavatam is purest of religion. There are many forms of religion, but there are also many forms of religion that are not purest of religion. So, there are many forms of religion that are purest of religion. So, there are many forms of religion that are purest of religion. So, it is joyfully performed without difficulty.

#### pure religion, faith, stages of devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=1334
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Faith is the most important factor in the progress of Krishna Consciousness and as we tread this path, our faith crystallizes, becomes more and more mature."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that anyone can practice pure religion without material impediments, emphasizing the importance of faith and outlining the initial stages of devotional progress, starting with tender faith (Komala Shraddha).

And the next part is anyone can do it. There is no material impediments. In other words, if someone says, I don't have money, still he can do it. Where there is a will, there is a way. So, there is no time for any effort. So, if someone says, I want to do something that is pure, then I will tell you that that it is a very birth and death in this material world. So those who don't have faith, those who do not practice this process will remain in this material world which is a world of Janmabhrityu samsara, birth and death. Now process of development of faith, when we tread this path, we start with Shraddha. Shraddha means? It is Komala Shraddha. Komala Shraddha means our faith is very tender. Just like when a seed is planted with saplings, initially when it comes, it is very very tender. Anybody stands on it, it's gone. It's crushed. So initially our faith also, when we start, is very tender. That's called Shraddha.

#### stages of devotion, faith, attachment to the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6xUouJ96i2I?t=1437
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Faith is the most important factor in the progress of Krishna Consciousness and as we tread this path, our faith crystallizes, becomes more and more mature."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to describe the stages of devotional progress, from initial faith (Shraddha) to attachment to the Lord (Aasakti), emphasizing the importance of firm faith (Nishtha) and association with devotees (Sadhu Sangha).

Then, the next step, anyone knows, when we start treading this path, what's the next step? Shraddha. Shraddha leads to what? Adho Shraddha. Shraddha leads to what? Adho Shraddha tatha Sadhu Sangha. Sadhu Sangha means? Association of devotees. That Shraddha will... motivate you to associate with devotees. Adho Shraddha tatha Sadhu Sangha. And when we do this Sadhu Sangha, we will elevate to the next stage, which is Bhajan Kriya. Bhajan Kriya means? We start putting into practice some of the things which we have heard. Like many of us have started chanting or reading. That's Bhajan Kriya. Then when we do Bhajan Kriya, the next stage is called Anartha Nivriti. Anartha Nivriti means? Anartha means all the misgivings, all the unwanted things, all the bad things which are clinging to our consciousness, slowly starts dissipating. Some bad habits, some bad company, all that slowly starts. That's called Anartha Nivriti. Nivriti means we start getting freed from all these Anarthas. The next stage is Nishtha. Nishtha means? Nishtha means? Firm faith. Start with Adho Shraddha. Adho Shraddha tatha Sadhu Sangha. Bhajan Kriya. Anartha Nivriti Sya. Tato Nishtha. After Nishtha, what happens? When you develop firm faith, after Nishtha, what happens? Aasakti. Aasakti means? Attachment. Attachment to? Lord. After Aasakti, what happens? Some of you will see that later because we will start with Bhajan Kriya. So firm faith is the conviction, just like the conviction we have when we water a plant by pouring the water in the root of the plant, we are very very sure that the entire plant is getting nourished. Am I right? If we see a plant wilting, the leaves are getting dried, it's completely getting shriveled, you pour the water in the root, you have the conviction, you have the faith that the water is reaching each and every part of the plant. Likewise, Nishtha means firm faith just by serving the Lord. Just by being a devotee of the Lord. That's it. I am doing the highest service. I'll come to that. How we can say that by serving the Lord is one of the highest things we can do. Faith is the most important factor in the progress of Krishna Consciousness and as we tread this path, our faith crystallizes, becomes more and more mature.
